# Architecture

Every block in this catalog runs through one shared patch-graph engine. The full signal and control path - from the host buffer, through the layered engine and the per-voice patch graph, onto the shared bus, and back out - is below. Signal flows top to bottom; control and modulation enter from the right; the engine's CPU optimizations are called out where they apply.

![Full signal \& control architecture of zpwr-patch-core](diagram_arch.pdf){width=100%}

# Performance

Every block in this catalog runs through one shared patch-graph engine, so every optimization in that engine speeds up every patch. The full list of CPU work shipped to date is below, grouped by what it attacks. The cost of one audio buffer is *voices x samples x modules*: the *multiplier* (voices x per-voice work) is what pins a chord; the *slope* (per-sample dispatch and coefficient recompute) is the constant overhead. The phases below attack each in turn. Measured figures are arm64, -O3, from interleaved before/after benchmark runs.

\textbf{1. Engine hot path} - shaves the per-sample slope; together ~18\% off per-sample DSP:

- **Relaxed atomic ordering** on every hot-path load (params, sync-divisor, gain, bypass, bus-send, modulation), dropping the arm64 acquire barrier on the inner loop.
- **Reused compute-context scratch** - a single member buffer reused each sample instead of constructing one per sample.
- **Tempo-sync scan skipped** for nodes that have no syncable parameter.
- **Per-block parameter snapshot** - on the host's `beginBlock` call, unmodulated nodes do zero atomic parameter loads per sample; a safe per-sample fallback runs when a host never calls it.

\textbf{2. Coefficient \& transcendental caching} - block-rate values that used to be recomputed every sample are now computed once per block and reused, recomputed only when an input parameter changes (all bit-identical):

- **CachedSVF** caches the state-variable (TPT) filter coefficients, skipping a per-sample `tan`; one subclass fixes every SVF-based block with no call-site edits. ChoirPad 3-voice: 106 to 76 ns/sample (~30\%).
- **Additive partial-amplitude cache** - a dense additive voice was running up to 70 `pow` per sample (14 partials x 5 internal voices); recomputed only on a Partials / Rolloff / Odd-Even change. 1140 to 600 ns/sample (~48\%).
- **Unison detune-ratio cache** - a `pow` plus a `sqrt` per voice per sample, cached once; benefits every unison oscillator. Osc 5-voice: 246 to 180 ns/sample (~27\%).
- **Per-voice detune-ratio cache** for Screech, Hoover, Reese, Wt, SynthFM and SynthSync (the per-voice `pow(2, spread x detune)` ratio).
- **Risset decay coefficients** - the seven-partial `exp` decay loop cached.
- **Additator amplitude-table cache** - up to 32 `pow` per sample on block-rate Partials / Tilt / Balance, cached like the additive table.

\textbf{3. Voice multiplier} - the real fix for chord cost; turns N copies of an effect back into one:

- **Linear FX kept on the shared bus.** Reverb and delay are linear (`fx(v1)+fx(v2) == fx(v1+v2)`), so running them per voice is N-times waste for a bit-identical result. They live on the shared aux / master bus; only synthesis and per-note timbre stay in the voice patch.
- **Every factory preset audited clean** - no linear FX sits in any voice layer.
- **Regression gate** - the preset validator rejects any reverb placed in a voice layer, so the multiplier cannot creep back in.

\textbf{4. Host \& parallelism} - removes wasted per-voice work in the synth host:\n
- **Metering gated on editor-open** - the per-sample O(nodes) metering scan is skipped entirely while the editor is closed.
- **Parallel voice loop** - voices render across CPU cores through a persistent worker pool, bit-identical to serial, opt-in.

\textbf{5. Block (node-major) processing} - each node processes a whole buffer in one call instead of one sample at a time, amortizing the per-sample cable-gather, context-build and dispatch overhead (~50\% on block-safe graphs; a per-sample-capture fallback covers graphs with buses or modulation):

- **11 FX blocks converted** to whole-buffer processing - the medium FX. Each mirrors its per-sample body over the buffer with block-rate coefficients hoisted out of the loop, and is verified bit-identical to the per-sample path. Converted so far: Bitcrush, Chorus, Comb, Drive, EQ, Filter, Flanger, Gate, Lofi, Tremolo, Waveshaper.

# Reading this catalog (badge key)

An icon glyph precedes each block name; then its badges, then `in:` its input jacks and `out:` its output-jack type, then the description:

- \textcolor{accentPink}{[CAT]} \textbf{category} - **AMP** Amp, **ANA** Analog, **CTL** Control, **DLY** Delay, **DST** Distortion, **DYN** Dynamics, **ENV** Envelope, **FLT** Filter, **FX** Effect, **HRM** Harmony, **MOD** Modulation, **OSC** Oscillator, **PIT** Pitch, **PRB** Probability, **REV** Reverb, **RTE** Routing, **SEQ** Sequencing, **SPC** Spectral, **STR** Stereo, **TUN** Tuning, **UTL** Utility, **VOI** Voicing.

- \textcolor{neonCyan}{[CV]} a \textbf{control / modulation source} (outputs CV, not audio).

- \textcolor{neonGreen}{[CIRCUIT]} a \textbf{component-level analog-circuit model} (per-sample nodal / Newton solve).

- \textcolor{neonYellow}{[PHY]} a \textbf{physical-model instrument} (waveguide / modal / friction synthesis of strings, membranes and resonant bodies).

- \textcolor{neonCyan}{[FX]} \textcolor{neonMagenta}{[SYNTH]} \textcolor{neonYellow}{[MIDI]} which \textbf{plugin(s)} ship the block - zpwr-fx, zpwr-synth (gets the whole audio pack) and/or zpwr-midi-fx.

- \textbf{in:} the block's input jacks, and \textbf{out:} its output-jack signal type - \textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}, \textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI} (note stream) or \textcolor{neonGreen}{out: CV} (control / modulation).

- \textbf{icon:} the glyph before each block name shows its kind at a glance:

# Audio (zpwr-fx / shared pack) (3366 blocks)

## Amp (24)

\phantomsection\label{blk:AC30}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AC30}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vox AC30 Top Boost-style amp: two cascaded Koren 12AX7 stages into a chimey top-boost tone stack (flat ~800 Hz mid, bright treble) and a class-A EL84 push-pull power stage that compresses and sags early - the jangly British chime. EL84 (lower max current) + class-A bias distinguish it from the EL34 Marshalls.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bandmaster}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bandmaster}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fender Bandmaster-style amp: two Koren 12AX7 stages into a deeply scooped blackface tone stack and a class-AB 6L6 push-pull power stage - a piano-clean ~40 W blackface head with big headroom and a glassy, scooped top, breaking up only when pushed hard.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bass410}\blockicon{cabinet}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bass410}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ampeg-style 4x10 bass cabinet: a deep extended low end, a gentle low-mid bump and an early treble roll-off for a round, punchy bass tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bassman}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bassman}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fender 5F6-A tweed Bassman / JTM45-lineage tube amp: two cascaded 12AX7 gain stages modeled with the Koren triode plate-current equation (real self-bias, grid-conduction asymmetry - not a tanh) into a Fender-style tone stack (wide ~650 Hz mid), then a class-AB EL34 push-pull power stage whose plate currents subtract through the output transformer (even harmonics cancel, odd-harmonic clip + supply sag emerge from the physics), 4x oversampled.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Blue212}\blockicon{cabinet}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Blue212}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vox AC30-style 2x12 with Celestion Blue alnico voicing: an open low end, an extended top and a sweet chimey presence - the jangly British combo cab.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bluesbreaker}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bluesbreaker}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Marshall 1962 Bluesbreaker-style amp: two Koren 12AX7 stages into a Marshall tone stack and a class-AB KT66 push-pull power stage - the JTM45-lineage combo behind the classic British blues-rock 'Beano' tone, with a warm, bloomy, KT66 break-up and more low-end body than the later EL34 Plexi.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bogner}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bogner}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bogner Ecstasy-style amp: three cascaded Koren 12AX7 stages into a tight, focused tone stack and a class-AB EL34 push-pull power stage - smooth, singing, high-gain modern British-voiced lead with a refined, liquid sustain.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Brit412}\blockicon{cabinet}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Brit412}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Marshall 1960A-style 4x12 with Celestion Greenback voicing: a tight low-cut, a roll-off around 4.5 kHz and a forward upper-mid presence - the definitive British rock guitar cabinet.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DeluxeRev}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DeluxeRev}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fender Deluxe Reverb-style amp: two Koren 12AX7 stages into a blackface tone stack and a class-AB 6V6 push-pull power stage - the warm, touch-sensitive American combo that breaks into a sweet, compressed overdrive when cranked. Lower headroom than the Twin.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EVH5150}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EVH5150}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

EVH 5150III-style amp: three cascaded Koren 12AX7 stages into a scooped, aggressive tone stack and a class-AB EL34 push-pull power stage driven hard - the tight, saturated, percussive modern metal/shred standard with massive gain and a fast attack.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JCM800}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JCM800}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Marshall JCM800 2203-style high-gain amp: three cascaded Koren 12AX7 stages (the extra gain stage = hot-rodded crunch) into a scooped Marshall tone stack and a class-AB EL34 push-pull power stage. The 80s rock/metal standard - tight, aggressive, harmonically rich.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JTM45}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JTM45}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Marshall JTM45-style amp: the original Marshall, two cascaded Koren 12AX7 stages into a Marshall tone stack and a class-AB KT66 push-pull power stage (more headroom and a rounder break-up than the later EL34 Plexi). Bluesy British crunch with bloom.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Jubilee}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Jubilee}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Marshall Silver Jubilee 2555-style amp: three cascaded Koren 12AX7 stages into a scooped Marshall tone stack and a class-AB EL34 push-pull power stage - a hot-rodded JCM800 with a tighter, more compressed high-gain crunch. The 80s/90s rock-lead Marshall.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MarkV}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MarkV}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mesa/Boogie Mark V-style amp: three cascaded Koren 12AX7 stages into a deeply scooped Mesa tone stack and a class-AB power stage - the smooth, compressed, mid-scooped American high-gain lead (the 'Santana/Metallica' voice) with singing sustain.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Matchless}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Matchless}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Matchless DC30-style boutique amp: two Koren 12AX7 stages into a chimey top-boost-style tone stack and a class-A EL84 push-pull power stage - the AC30 chime with tighter low end and a more focused, hi-fi breakup. Boutique British class-A.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Orange412}\blockicon{cabinet}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Orange412}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Orange-style 4x12: a thick, dark, low-mid-forward voicing with an earlier treble roll-off - the heavy, woody stoner/doom cabinet.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Plexi}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Plexi}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Marshall Plexi 1959 Super Lead-style amp: two cascaded Koren 12AX7 stages into a scooped Marshall tone stack (~500 Hz mid) and a class-AB EL34 push-pull power stage - the bright, crunchy British roar, with real push-pull even-harmonic cancellation and supply sag. Same physics engine as the Bassman, Marshall voicing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Princeton}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Princeton}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fender Princeton Reverb-style amp: two Koren 12AX7 stages into a blackface tone stack and a small class-AB 6V6 push-pull power stage - the low-power (~12 W) blackface combo that breaks into a sweet, touch-sensitive overdrive earlier than the Deluxe, the studio recording favourite. Lower power-tube headroom = earlier sag and bloom.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Recto412}\blockicon{cabinet}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Recto412}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mesa/Boogie Rectifier-style 4x12 (V30 loaded): a big low end, a slightly later treble roll-off and an aggressive 3-4 kHz bite - the modern high-gain metal cab.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rectoverb}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rectoverb}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mesa/Boogie Rectoverb-style amp: three cascaded Koren 12AX7 stages into a loose, scooped Recto-voiced tone stack and a class-AB EL34 push-pull power stage - thick, saturated, sagging modern high-gain with a huge low end. The nu-metal/modern-rock rhythm crush.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Showman}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Showman}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fender Dual Showman-style amp: two Koren 12AX7 stages into a scooped blackface tone stack and a big, stiff class-AB 6L6 push-pull power stage - the loudest, cleanest blackface (~85 W), enormous headroom and a tight, sparkling, scooped clean that barely breaks up. Stiffer power supply (less sag) than the Twin.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SuperReverb}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SuperReverb}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fender Super Reverb-style amp: two Koren 12AX7 stages into a blackface tone stack and a class-AB 6L6 push-pull power stage voiced bright and chimey through its 4x10 cab - a livelier, more touch-responsive blackface clean than the Twin, singing with pick dynamics and breaking up sweetly when cranked.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Twin}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Twin}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fender Twin Reverb-style amp: two Koren 12AX7 stages into a deeply scooped blackface tone stack and a big class-AB 6L6 push-pull power stage with huge clean headroom - the loud, sparkling, scooped American clean. Breaks up only when pushed hard.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Vibrolux}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Vibrolux}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[AMP]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fender Vibrolux Reverb-style amp: two Koren 12AX7 stages into a blackface tone stack and a class-AB 6L6 push-pull power stage voiced bright and chimey - a smaller, livelier blackface clean that sings with pick dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Analog (155)

\phantomsection\label{blk:Acido}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Acido}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TB-303-style 4-pole diode ladder: per-stage diode soft-clip and a diode-shaped feedback stage for high-resonance acid squelch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Ampeg}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Ampeg}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ampeg SVT-style bass amp: two triode stages, thick low-mid focus, power-supply sag and an output-transformer stage with a dark cab - built for bass.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Ampex}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Ampex}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ampex ATR-102-style mastering tape: gentle magnetic hysteresis (B/H + remanence), a soft wide head bump and smooth head-gap HF roll-off - subtle mix-bus polish (pro deck: flutter negligible by design).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Aphex}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Aphex}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Aphex Aural Exciter-style enhancer: a tuned high-pass side chain into an asymmetric class-A harmonic generator (2nd+3rd) and a phase-shift network, blended back for air and presence.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:API2500}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{API2500}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

API 2500-style VCA bus compressor: a Thrust sidechain tilt with Old (feedback) or New (feedforward) detection, through a dB-linear VCA cell with control-port slew and gain-cell THD.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:API550}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{API550}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

API 550-style 3-band EQ with proportional-Q (bands widen at low boost, tighten at high boost) and op-amp soft-clip bite.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:API560}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{API560}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

API 560-style graphic EQ: fixed octave bands with proportional-Q so adjacent bands sum smoothly (4-band).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ApiPre}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ApiPre}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

API 312-style preamp: a discrete 2520 op-amp (level-dependent knee) into an API output transformer with LF-flux iron, plus a bright top; the harmonic tilt shifts upward as level rises.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ARP2600}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ARP2600}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

ARP 2600-style 4072 four-pole ladder: aggressive, clean self-oscillating resonance with a brighter, wetter bite than a Moog ladder.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Audimax}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Audimax}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

CBS Audimax-style broadcast AGC: a slow, bidirectional gain-riding automatic level control that holds a target output loudness - boosting quiet material UP and pulling loud material DOWN over seconds, unlike a compressor (downward-only, fast). Its 'platform' gate FREEZES the gain when the program falls silent so it never rides room noise up in the pauses. Target sets the held level, Speed the gain-ride time, Range the maximum boost, Gate the platform threshold.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Avalon}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Avalon}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Avalon VT-737-style tube channel EQ: clean, musical bell and shelf bands with gentle tube warmth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Baxandall}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Baxandall}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Baxandall negative-feedback tone network: the classic hi-fi Bass/Treble - a gentle low shelf (~100 Hz) and high shelf (~10 kHz) that boost or cut smoothly and symmetrically, with none of the midrange scoop of a passive stack. Bass and Treble in dB.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BBE}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BBE}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

BBE Sonic Maximizer-style processor: a phase-alignment network advances the highs ahead of the (allpass-delayed) lows and adds exciter-style HF harmonics for clarity and punch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BiasTrem}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BiasTrem}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tube bias tremolo: the LFO shifts the tube grid bias (operating point), so the throb modulates both amplitude and 2nd-harmonic content - a richer, harmonically-moving wobble than plain amplitude tremolo.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BigMuff}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BigMuff}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Big Muff Pi-style fuzz: two cascaded silicon diode clipping stages (Newton-solved Shockley pairs, 4x oversampled) that square up into a wall of sustain, with a scooped-mid tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BiPhase}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BiPhase}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mu-Tron Bi-Phase dual phaser: TWO complete 6-stage OTA phasers with independent LFO rates (RateA / RateB), whose two moving notch combs beat against each other for a swirling, three-dimensional sweep no single-sweep phaser (Phase 90, Small Stone) can reach. Depth sets the sweep width, Feedback the resonant emphasis of the notches, Mix the wet blend. Six 1st-order all-pass stages per side.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Blackface}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Blackface}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fender Blackface-style amp: a triode stage, scooped-mid clean headroom (~500 Hz notch), a stiff (low-sag) power section and an output-transformer stage with a bright, glassy top.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlueBox}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlueBox}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

MXR Blue Box-style octave fuzz: cascaded flip-flops divide the pitch down two octaves into a gritty square sub-octave, blended with a fuzzed dry signal - the synthetic, glitchy -2-octave fuzz.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BluesDriver}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BluesDriver}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boss BD-2 Blues Driver-style overdrive: a JFET-buffered asymmetric soft-clipper that stays bright, dynamic and amp-like - cleans up with pick attack, breaks up when pushed. Gain sets the drive, Tone the top-end tilt, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BodeRing}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BodeRing}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bode / Moog 6401-style ring modulator: the input multiplied by an internal tunable sine carrier through a diode-ring nonlinearity, giving the metallic sum-and-difference sidebands of the classic sci-fi/bell sound. Freq tunes the carrier, Drive sets the diode-bridge bite, Mix blends modulated against dry.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BusSat}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BusSat}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

SSL bus-style VCA saturation into an output transformer: a thin 'glue' veneer for the mix bus - subtle thickening, not obvious distortion.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BX20}\blockicon{reverb}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BX20}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

AKG BX-20-style torsional-spring reverb: a dispersive modulated all-pass tank models the spring chirp smeared smooth - long, dark, no boing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cassette}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cassette}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tascam Portastudio-style 4-track cassette: the lo-fi tape end - a slow narrow capstan gives heavy wow (slow pitch drift) plus flutter (fast wobble) through a modulated delay, a restricted bandwidth (HF gap loss + LF rolloff) and early-saturating tape hysteresis, the bedroom-demo cassette character versus the clean Ampex/Studer mastering decks. Wow sets the pitch instability, Sat the tape drive, Tone the HF rolloff, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CE1}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CE1}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boss CE-1-style BBD chorus/vibrato: a triangle-LFO chorus or a wet-only vibrato through a companded bucket-brigade line, with preamp coloration.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CEM3320}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CEM3320}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Reso Mod, Cutoff Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Curtis CEM3320-style 4-pole OTA filter (Prophet-5 rev3 / SH-101 / Pro-One): four OTA integrator poles with per-stage soft clip and a bass-compensated resonance path, so it stays fat where a Moog ladder thins out - cleaner, brighter, more aggressive into self-oscillation. Cutoff, Reso to self-osc, Mod-CV depth (in 3), Drive pushes the cells.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Chamber}\blockicon{reverb}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Chamber}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chamber reverb: higher modal density than a plate with a diffuse onset and warm, non-metallic tail - between a plate and a room.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Chandler}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Chandler}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chandler Curve Bender-style EQ (EMI/Abbey Road): musical stepped bands with transformer/valve harmonic colour.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChandlerPre}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChandlerPre}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chandler TG2-style preamp (EMI/Abbey Road): an input stage into an iron-core transformer (LF flux saturation) - punchy with a distinctive forward presence.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CryBaby}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CryBaby}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dunlop Cry Baby / Vox inductor wah: a resonant inductor-cap band-pass swept by Pedal over the throaty 350 Hz - 2.2 kHz range, with the Q peaking through mid-sweep the way a real wah inductor does and falling at the heel/toe. Pedal sets the position (modulate it for auto-wah), Q the bite, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CS80}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CS80}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Yamaha CS-80 dual resonant filter: a 2-pole resonant high-pass in series with a 2-pole resonant low-pass, each swept independently for the lush, vocal, brass-and-strings voice (Vangelis / Blade Runner) a single multimode filter cannot make. HP Cutoff lifts the bottom, LP Cutoff closes the top, Reso peaks both corners (soft-saturated, musical -- not a screaming self-oscillator), Drive pushes the input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CultureVulture}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CultureVulture}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Thermionic Culture Vulture valve distortion: Triode mode is an asymmetric, even-harmonic soft saturation (warm, musical thickening); Pentode mode is a harder odd-harmonic clip with a push-pull crossover dip near zero for the snarling, aggressive 'P' destruction. Bias shifts the valve operating point (asymmetry / gate), Drive pushes it from fattening to full disintegration, Tone tilts the output, Level trims it. A valve character/destruction unit, not a pedal or amp.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Dbx160}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Dbx160}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

dbx 160-style VCA compressor: true-RMS detection and an 'over-easy' soft knee through a dB-linear VCA cell with control-port slew and gain-cell THD; punchy and musical.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Dbx165}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Dbx165}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

dbx 165-style VCA compressor: true-RMS detection into a dB-linear VCA with the over-easy soft knee, faster and more aggressive than the 160, plus the PeakStop clamp - a click-free output brickwall that stops transients dead. Threshold/Ratio/Knee shape the compression, Makeup the makeup gain.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:dbxNR}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{dbxNR}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

dbx Type II-style companding noise reduction: a 2:1 RMS-compress encode and complementary 1:2 expand decode with HF pre-emphasis - run as one box the two halves track at slightly different speeds, so steady tone passes clean but transients pump and HF content (cymbals) modulates the floor: the signature dbx 'breathing'. Amount sets the companding depth, Emphasis the HF-triggered breathing, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Diezel}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Diezel}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Diezel VH4-style amp: three cascaded triode stages, ultra-tight aggressive high-gain with a scooped low end, reservoir sag and an output-transformer stage.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DimensionD}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DimensionD}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Roland Dimension D-style BBD chorus: two anti-phase bucket-brigade lines (compander + clock-tracked reconstruction filter) for a wide, 'motionless' thickening with no audible wobble.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Distressor}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Distressor}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Distressor-style aggressive compressor: a FET gain cell whose bite grows with gain reduction, switchable harmonic distortion (Dist2 = genuine 2nd, Dist3 = 3rd) and a British-mode all-buttons slam.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Drawmer}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Drawmer}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Drawmer DS201-style noise gate: opens fast above Threshold and closes after Release, with hysteresis so it doesn't chatter on the edge. Range sets how far it ducks when closed (hard gate vs gentle expander), Attack the open speed - the studio-standard gate for tightening drums and killing hum between phrases.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DS1}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DS1}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boss DS-1-style distortion: a Newton-solved symmetric silicon diode pair (4x oversampled - the real shunt clipper, not a hard jlimit) with an active tilt-tone control - brash and cutting.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Dumble}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Dumble}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dumble Overdrive Special amp (full circuit): smooth, singing high-gain with a spongy supply for bloom and sustain -- the holy-grail boutique lead voice. Gain, Tone, Master, Level. Same cascaded-triode + voiced-tone-stack + supply-sag model, voiced for Dumble's compressed sustain.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DynaComp}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DynaComp}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

MXR Dyna Comp-style OTA pedal compressor (CA3080): a full-wave-rectified envelope drives a high-ratio OTA gain cell for the squishy, sustaining 'country squeeze' - fast clamp, slow bloom, and the signature high-end softening. Sensitivity sets how hard it squeezes, Attack the detector speed, Output the makeup.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Echoplex}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Echoplex}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Maestro Echoplex EP-34-style tape echo: warm, wow+flutter-modulated repeats with magnetic hysteresis (asymmetric saturation + remanence) and head gap-loss HF roll-off.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Echorec}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Echorec}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Binson Echorec-style magnetic drum delay: several fixed playback heads give rhythmic multi-tap echoes with rotating-drum wobble and magnetic hysteresis in the feedback.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EF86}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EF86}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

EF86 pentode preamp (Vox AC15/AC30, Matchless front end): a pentode gain stage modeled by the 3/2-power Child-Langmuir plate law with a sharp cutoff (ckt::pentodeIp) - far more gain and a harder, faster-compressing grind than the triode stages in the amp models, with the asymmetric even-harmonic colour of a single tube stage. Drive sets the grid swing, Bias the operating point (edge of breakup / asymmetry), Tone a post treble tilt, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ElectricMistress}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ElectricMistress}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

EHX Electric Mistress-style flanger: a swept companded bucket-brigade line forms moving comb notches; Filter Matrix freezes the sweep for a fixed metallic tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EMS}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EMS}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

EMS VCS3-style 3-pole (18 dB) diode ladder: asymmetric diode shaping gives a sharp, acidic resonance.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EMT140}\blockicon{reverb}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EMT140}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

EMT 140-style plate reverb: a dispersive modulated all-pass tank (the plate shimmer where HF and LF propagate at different speeds), instant dense diffusion, bright sheen, HF decaying faster than the low tail.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EMT250}\blockicon{reverb}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EMT250}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

EMT 250-style early digital reverb: a dense tank with first-generation converter quantization grain - bright and unmistakably early-digital.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ENGL}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ENGL}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

ENGL-style amp: three cascaded triode stages, tight scooped modern-metal high-gain, reservoir sag and an output-transformer stage with a dark cab.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EP3}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EP3}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Echoplex EP-3 preamp: the Echoplex's FET preamp section, famous as an always-on tone boost (Page, Van Halen). A touch of FET saturation plus a presence bump and slightly tightened lows make whatever runs through it fuller, clearer and more present -- a buffer/boost with character, not a distortion or amp. Gain sets the boost, Bright the presence bump, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Fairchild670}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Fairchild670}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fairchild 670-style vari-mu tube limiter: a remote-cutoff 6386 twin-triode is the gain cell, its mu falling as control bias rises so the ratio is soft and program-dependent, and the 6-position time-constant switch sets attack/release from snappy to molasses. Threshold leans it in, TimeConst picks the program-dependent ballistics, the vari-mu adds gentle glue and tube harmonics, Output is makeup.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FATSO}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FATSO}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Empirical Labs FATSO-style warmth/comp: a soft-knee compressor into a tape-style saturation stage (a tilting HF soft-clip) that rounds transients and adds harmonic 'warmth', the way the hardware glues and thickens a mix. Comp sets the squeeze, Warmth the saturation drive, Output the makeup.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FET}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FET}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

1176-style FET compressor: ultra-fast attack, fixed high ratios, and an asymmetric FET gain cell whose harmonic bite grows with gain reduction (max ratio = all-buttons slam).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Friedman}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Friedman}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Friedman BE-100-style amp: hot-rodded Marshall - two cascaded triode stages, tight low end, singing mids, reservoir sag and an output-transformer stage.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Fulldrive}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Fulldrive}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fulltone Fulldrive-style overdrive: a TS-derived circuit opened up with less mid-hump and more gain on tap, asymmetric soft clipping that stays articulate when pushed, plus a cleaner Boost stage. Gain sets the drive, Tone the brightness, Boost stacks gain, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FuncGen}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FuncGen}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

H3000-style function generator: a modulation source with 19 selectable waveshapes plus random sample-and-hold, smooth random and noise (no audio input).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FuzzFace}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FuzzFace}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fuzz Face-style germanium fuzz: two cascaded germanium common-emitter transistor stages (Ebers-Moll, 4x oversampled) whose asymmetric cutoff/saturation gives the gated, spitty fuzz; Bias starves the second stage toward the splatty/gated voicing, and it cleans up as you lower Input - just like rolling back the guitar volume.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FuzzFactory}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FuzzFactory}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Z.Vex Fuzz Factory-style two-transistor fuzz: massive asymmetric gain into a hard clip, with Gate choking the decay into a sputtery rip and Stab starving the bias toward a self-oscillating squeal that ducks under the note - the pinched, gated, velcro fuzz. 4x-oversampled.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FZ1}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FZ1}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Maestro FZ-1 Fuzz-Tone: the first fuzz pedal (the 'Satisfaction' buzz). Three germanium stages give a thin, splatty, ripped-speaker square-wave fuzz that GATES hard as the note decays into the famous sputtering decay. Thinner, harder and more gated than the thick Tone Bender or the FuzzFace. Attack sets the gain, Bias starves the supply (more gate/sputter), Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GML}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GML}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

GML 8200-style parametric EQ: ultra-clean, transparent constant-Q bands with no saturation - the surgical reference where boost and cut cancel exactly.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Guvnor}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Guvnor}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Marshall Guv'nor-style distortion: hard symmetric diode clipping after a Marshall-voiced mid push with an active treble tilt - the 'amp in a box' crunch-to-roar. Gain sets the clipping, Tone the brightness, Body the low-mid push, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:H3000}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{H3000}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Eventide H3000-style micro-pitch doubler: two slightly detuned, short-delayed voices thicken the signal into a wide, lush double-track.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Helios}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Helios}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Helios Type 69-style console EQ: a low bell, a switchable peak-or-trough mid at stepped frequencies, and a 10 kHz high shelf - punchy and musical.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HeliosPre}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HeliosPre}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Helios console-style preamp: punchy class-A transistor saturation into a console transformer (LF-flux iron) with an asymmetric Bias and a forward ~1.5 kHz midrange push. Drive sets the grit, Tone rolls the top, Mix blends dry/wet - aggressive British-console colour.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Hiwatt}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Hiwatt}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hiwatt DR103 amp (full circuit): three could-be-cleaner cascaded triode stages with a stiff power supply and a bright, high-headroom voice -- the clean-but-LOUD British military-grade tone (Townshend / Gilmour) that stays articulate where a Marshall breaks up. Gain drives the preamp, Tone tilts the stack, Master pushes the power stage, Level trims. Cascaded triode transfer + voiced tone stack + supply sag, not a single tanh + fixed EQ.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HM2}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HM2}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boss HM-2 Heavy Metal ('Swedish chainsaw'): a high-gain hard clipper into the HM-2's scooped dual-band Color EQ. Turn Color up and it guts the mids while pushing a resonant low end and a fizzy top into the unmistakable buzzsaw wall of Swedish death metal (Entombed). Dist sets the gain, Color sweeps flat -> full chainsaw scoop, Level the output. Distinct from the mid-scoop MetalZone by the dual-band buzzsaw voicing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JC120}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JC120}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Roland JC-120 Jazz Chorus (solid-state clean): the transistor clean platform -- a stiff power supply (no sag), big bright headroom that stays glassy and articulate, and a harder, more brittle clip when finally pushed, unlike the soft, compressing tube amps. Drive sets the gain, Tone the brightness, Level the output. Pair with a Chorus block for the full JC stereo shimmer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JfetStage}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JfetStage}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

JFET common-source overdrive (true square-law device model, ckt::jfetId): the signal swings the gate of a J201-style JFET and its drain current Id = Idss(1-Vgs/Vp)^2 forms the output, which clips ASYMMETRICALLY - compressing toward zero-bias, cutting off at pinch-off - for the even-harmonic, dynamic, 'amp-like' warmth of a FET preamp stage. Drive sets the gate swing, Bias the operating point (asymmetry / edge of breakup), Tone a post treble tilt, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Jup-8}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Jup-8}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jupiter-8-style 4-pole OTA ladder: per-stage OTA transconductance saturation with smooth resonance that keeps the low end (no Moog bass loss).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Klon}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Klon}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Klon Centaur-style overdrive: a Newton-solved germanium diode pair (4x oversampled) summed back with the clean signal - 'transparent' via the dual-path blend, not soft clipping.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Korg35}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Korg35}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Korg MS-10-style Korg35 Sallen-Key low-pass: hard diode-clipped resonance that screams and squares up at high settings - more aggressive than the later MS-20 OTA revision.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LA2A}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LA2A}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Teletronix LA-2A-style opto leveling amplifier: a T4 electro-optical cell compresses slowly and smoothly with the cell's two-stage, program-dependent release - fast for the first part of recovery, then a long gentle tail - so it sits musically without surgical control. Peak Reduction sets how hard it leans, Gain the makeup, Emphasis tilts the detector toward highs (de-essing).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LA3A}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LA3A}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Teletronix LA-3A-style optical compressor: the LA-2A photocell gain law but a fast-attack, brighter solid-state amp - punchier optical leveling with program-dependent release.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Lex224}\blockicon{reverb}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Lex224}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lexicon 224-style digital hall: a long, lush, slowly-modulated reverb tail (chorusing avoids metallic ringing) with bright, dense diffusion.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Lex480}\blockicon{reverb}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Lex480}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lexicon 480L-style digital reverb: a brighter, denser random-hall/plate with a modulated tail and fast build-up.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Lockhart}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Lockhart}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lockhart-style transistor wavefolder (west-coast): the signal reflects off the rails through several folding stages, multiplying harmonics as Fold rises - the Buchla/Serge timbre. 4x-oversampled.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Maag}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Maag}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Maag EQ4-style EQ: musical low and mid bands plus the famous Air Band - a very high shelf for open, silky top.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Manley}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Manley}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Manley Massive Passive-style EQ: wide bandwidth limits the available gain (opposite of proportional-Q), passive LC curves into an output transformer with LF-flux iron harmonics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MarkIIC}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MarkIIC}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mesa/Boogie Mark IIC+ amp (full circuit): tight, scooped, high-gain lead with a stiff supply for fast attack and a singing sustain (the 80s metal lead voice). Gain, Tone, Master, Level. Same cascaded-triode + voiced-tone-stack + supply-sag model, voiced for the Mark's tight low end and scooped mids -- distinct from the looser, fatter Rectifier.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MassivePassive}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MassivePassive}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Manley Massive Passive-style EQ: broad, gentle passive-network shelf and bell curves with wide, interactive Q - boosts feel airy and musical rather than surgical, the reason it's a mastering staple. Low shelf, a wide high bell at HiFreq, and an Air shelf on top, each in dB.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Matrix12}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Matrix12}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Oberheim Xpander / Matrix-12 multimode VCF (CEM3372): a 4-pole OTA ladder whose Mode taps a binomial mix of the four pole outputs to produce low-pass, high-pass and band-pass responses at 1-, 2- and 4-pole slopes from a single filter -- the signature Oberheim pole-mixing the SEM's 2-pole SVF cannot do. Per-stage tanh models the OTA soft clip and Reso feeds the 4-pole output back for resonance up to self-oscillation. Cutoff sweeps it, Drive pushes the stages.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MemoryMan}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MemoryMan}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

EHX Memory Man-style analog BBD delay: a companded bucket-brigade line whose clock-tracked reconstruction filter darkens each repeat with delay length, gently chorused, with line-level regeneration.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MEQ5}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MEQ5}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pultec MEQ-5-style midrange passive EQ: a low-mid boost, a mid dip and a high-mid boost for vocal/guitar presence.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MetalZone}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MetalZone}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boss MT-2 Metal Zone-style high-gain distortion: two 4x-oversampled clip stages into an active dual-peak mid scoop with a sweepable parametric mid.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
