\phantomsection\label{blk:Mini}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Mini}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Minimoog-style 4-pole transistor ladder: per-stage saturation thins the bass as resonance climbs to self-oscillation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MS-20}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MS-20}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

MS-20-style 2-pole Sallen-Key low-pass: input op-amp saturation and a saturated resonance integrator plus diode clipping in the resonance path - screams and distorts at high resonance.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MuTron3}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MuTron3}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mu-Tron III-style envelope auto-wah: an OTA state-variable filter whose cutoff is swept by an envelope follower of the playing dynamics. Sens sets the sweep depth, Direction flips up/down, Peak sets resonance, Mode picks LP/BP/HP.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MXRDist}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MXRDist}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

MXR Distortion+-style overdrive: a Newton-solved ASYMMETRIC germanium diode pair (4x oversampled) - mismatched diodes give the even-harmonic, woolier-than-a-DS-1 crunch - with an output low-pass.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MXRFlanger}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MXRFlanger}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

MXR Flanger-style BBD flanger: a companded bucket-brigade line swept for a deep, metallic comb with line-level regeneration for jet-plane sweeps.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:N1073}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{N1073}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Neve 1073-style EQ: fixed 12 kHz HF shelf, sweepable mid bell and LF shelf, into an iron-core transformer whose flux saturates the low frequencies first for genuine LF harmonic warmth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Neve1081}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Neve1081}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Neve 1081-style 4-band EQ: the 1073's bigger brother with a fixed HF and LF shelf PLUS two independently sweepable mid bells (the 1073 has only one), into an iron-core transformer whose LF flux saturates first for genuine low-end warmth. High/Low set the shelves, LoMid/HiMid the two bell gains, LoMidFreq/HiMidFreq their centres.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Neve33609}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Neve33609}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Neve 33609-style diode-bridge bus compressor: smooth glue through a diode-bridge gain cell with control-port slew, a dual-time-constant release and transformer saturation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NevePre}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NevePre}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Neve 1073-style preamp: a class-A triode gain stage (grid conduction) into an iron-core output transformer whose LF flux saturates first - the bass 'sings' with iron warmth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OCD}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OCD}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fulltone OCD-style overdrive: 4x-oversampled asymmetric MOSFET hard clipping (smooth-edged) with a bright/dark voicing switch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Octavia}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Octavia}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Octavia-style octave-up fuzz: 4x-oversampled full-wave rectification doubles the pitch, then heavy fuzz - the ring-mod-like upper-octave scream.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OD1}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OD1}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boss OD-1 OverDrive-style pedal: the original asymmetric soft-clipper (two diodes one way, one the other) for a smooth, warm, even+odd-harmonic break-up - lower gain and rounder than a Tube Screamer, with no mid hump. Drive sets the overdrive, Level the output (the real OD-1 has no tone knob).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ODR1}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ODR1}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nobels ODR-1-style natural overdrive: an op-amp soft-clipper with the signature Spectrum control that scoops the mids while bumping bass and treble together for a transparent, open, amp-like break-up - the Nashville session staple. Drive sets the gain, Spectrum the bass+treble vs mid balance, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Odyssey}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Odyssey}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

ARP Odyssey 2-pole filter (4023): brighter and reedier than the 4-pole ladders, with a loud, aggressive resonance that bites and sings -- the thin, vocal ARP lead voice. A soft-saturated 2-pole state-variable filter tapping LP / BP / HP via Mode. Cutoff sweeps it, Reso peaks the corner toward self-oscillation, Drive pushes the input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Opto}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Opto}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

LA-2A-style optical compressor: a T4 photocell whose photoresistor gain law gives a soft knee and a ratio that rises with level, plus program-dependent release (recovers slower after sustained compression).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OptoTrem}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OptoTrem}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fender optical tremolo: a lamp drives a photocell whose asymmetric lag (cools slower than it warms) and sublinear I-V curve round the throb; Shape sets the cell response speed (smooth to choppy).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Orange}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Orange}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Orange OR-series amp (full circuit): mid-forward, thick and woody with more preamp gain and a spongier supply than the Hiwatt -- the chunky British crunch. Gain, Tone, Master, Level. Shares the cascaded-triode + voiced-tone-stack + supply-sag circuit model, voiced for Orange's mid push.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Peavey5150}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Peavey5150}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Peavey 5150 / 6505 amp (full circuit): tight, aggressive, high-gain crunch with a stiff supply for percussive attack -- the metalcore / Van Halen rhythm and lead standard. Gain, Tone, Master, Level. Shares the cascaded-triode + voiced-tone-stack + supply-sag model, voiced for the 5150's tight low end and biting upper mids.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Percolator}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Percolator}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Interfax Harmonic Percolator-style fuzz: mixed germanium/silicon asymmetric clipping generates strong even-order harmonics for a wooly, octave-tinged thickness unlike symmetric fuzzes. Balance skews the asymmetry from even-rich to odd-rich. 4x-oversampled.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Phase100}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Phase100}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

MXR Phase 100-style 6-stage phaser: six JFET all-pass stages give three evenly-tracking notches - deeper and more resonant than the 4-stage Phase 90 - with a 4-position Intensity that adds feedback resonance for the thick, swirling, peakier sweep. Rate sets the LFO speed, Intensity the notch depth/feedback, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Phase90}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Phase90}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

MXR Phase 90-style 4-stage phaser: four JFET all-pass stages (bias-dependent frequency shift + transfer-curve saturation) give two evenly-tracking notches; Script (clean) or Block (resonant feedback).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Polivoks}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Polivoks}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Soviet Polivoks 2-pole OTA filter: its resonance path clips like a comparator, so at high Reso it does not ring sweetly -- it slams into a hard square-wave self-oscillation and screams. A 2x-oversampled two-integrator SVF whose band-pass feedback is hard-clipped (not soft tanh) gives that brutal, buzzy Eastern-bloc character no Moog/SEM filter has. Cutoff, Reso, Drive into the core, and Mode taps LP / BP / HP.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Polysix}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Polysix}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Korg Polysix-style SSM2044 four-pole low-pass: gentle OTA saturation gives a smooth, creamy resonance that stays musical right up to self-oscillation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Pultec}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Pultec}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pultec EQP-1A-style passive EQ: low boost and cut use offset corners so they do not cancel (the boost-with-dip trick), plus gentle tube warmth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rangemaster}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rangemaster}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dallas Rangemaster-style treble booster: a high-pass into a real germanium common-emitter transistor stage (Ebers-Moll, Newton-free closed form, 4x oversampled) - its asymmetric cutoff/saturation adds the bright, singing even+odd harmonics on top of the dry signal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RAT}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RAT}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

ProCo RAT-style distortion: an LM308-slewed gain stage into a Newton-solved silicon diode pair clamping to ground (the real shunt clipper, 4x oversampled - not a hard jlimit), tamed by a single low-pass Filter knob.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RectifierSag}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RectifierSag}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tube-rectifier power-supply sag: a model of the B+ supply drooping under load and recovering with the reservoir cap's RC time. Loud transients momentarily collapse the headroom (duck/compress) then BLOOM back as the supply recovers - the springy, touch-sensitive 'sag' of a tube amp with a 5U4/GZ34 rectifier, distinct from a feed-forward compressor. Drive sets the stage gain, Sag the droop depth, Recover the supply recovery time, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Recto}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Recto}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mesa Rectifier-style high-gain amp: three cascaded asymmetric triode stages with tight inter-stage high-passing, a deep mid scoop, selectable reservoir sag and an output-transformer stage.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RMX16}\blockicon{reverb}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RMX16}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

AMS RMX16-style digital reverb (Nonlin program): a dense, gated tail that holds then cuts off instead of decaying naturally - the classic 80s gated sound.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RossComp}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RossComp}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ross Compressor-style OTA pedal (CA3080): the smoother, longer-sustaining cousin of the Dyna Comp - a softer knee and slower release give singing sustain with less squash and a more open top end. Sustain sets how hard it squeezes, Attack the detector speed, Level the makeup.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SansAmp}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SansAmp}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tech 21 SansAmp-style amp/cab DI: a Character tilt into cascaded asymmetric soft-clipping (4x oversampled) and a speaker-cabinet low-pass, giving the recorded amp tone direct with no mic. Drive sets the gain into the stages, Character tilts presence vs warmth, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SCComp}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SCComp}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: In, Sidechain}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sidechain compressor: compresses the signal (in 0) by the level of a key input (in 1) through a dB-linear VCA cell with control-port slew and gain-cell THD - cable the host sidechain (SC L/R) to the key for ducking/pumping.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SD1}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SD1}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boss SD-1 Super Overdrive: an op-amp gain stage into an ASYMMETRIC soft clipper (two diodes one way, one the other) for a more open, even-harmonic crunch than the symmetric Tube Screamer, with the familiar mid-hump tone that cuts through a band. Drive sets the gain, Tone the mid/treble balance, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SDD3000}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SDD3000}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Korg SDD-3000-style delay: a characterful input preamp into a modeled converter (ADC anti-alias + 13-bit quantization) - clean and bright with subtle digital grain.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SEM}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SEM}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Oberheim SEM-style 2-pole state-variable filter: VCA-transistor saturation in the resonance integrator, continuously morphing low-pass through notch to high-pass.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShadowHills}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShadowHills}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shadow Hills Mastering-style compressor: a photocell opto stage (soft-knee, rising-ratio law, program-dependent release) into a discrete fixed-ratio VCA stage, with a transformer-flavour select (nickel/iron/steel).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SmallStone}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SmallStone}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

EHX Small Stone-style 4-stage OTA phaser: nonlinear OTA all-pass stages; the Color switch adds feedback, drives the OTAs harder and widens the notch sweep for a more vocal, resonant swirl.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Soldano}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Soldano}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Soldano SLO-100-style amp: three cascaded triode stages for a smooth, saturated lead with singing sustain, reservoir sag and an output-transformer stage.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Solina}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Solina}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Solina/string-machine-style ensemble: three BBD taps on 120-degree-phased dual LFOs (slow + fast) through one companded bucket-brigade line for a lush, moving multi-voice swirl.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sontec}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sontec}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sontec MEP-250-style mastering parametric EQ: clean Class-A bell bands with a musical, selectable Q - precise without sounding clinical, the mastering standard. Two parametric bells (Lo/Hi freq + gain) sharing the Q control.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SpaceEcho}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SpaceEcho}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Roland Space Echo-style tape delay: wow+flutter-modulated tape transport with magnetic hysteresis (asymmetric saturation + remanence) and tone-shaped head gap loss in the feedback repeats.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SSLBus}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SSLBus}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

SSL bus-style VCA glue compressor: a dual-time-constant program-dependent auto-release for pumping-free glue, through a dB-linear VCA cell with control-port slew and gain-cell THD that grows with gain reduction.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SSLE}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SSLE}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

SSL E-series-style 4-band parametric EQ: constant-Q (bandwidth stays put as you boost or cut) for a clean, surgical response.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SSLG}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SSLG}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

SSL G-series-style 4-band parametric EQ: proportional-Q (the more you boost or cut, the narrower the band) for a musical, self-limiting curve.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StaLevel}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StaLevel}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gates Sta-Level-style tube vari-mu limiter: a very slow program-dependent auto-release and gentle ~3:1 leveling through a remote-cutoff tube cell whose 2nd-harmonic warmth (Warmth) grows with gain reduction.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Studer}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Studer}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Studer A800-style tape: magnetic hysteresis (B/H saturation + remanence), a firm low-frequency head bump and gentle head-gap HF loss - punchy track-level glue (pro deck: flutter negligible by design).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Summit}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Summit}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Summit TLA-100-style tube/opto leveler: photocell gain law (soft knee, rising ratio) with program-dependent release, transformer warmth and tube makeup.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SunnModelT}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SunnModelT}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sunn Model T amp (full circuit): fat, dark, high-headroom and loose -- the wall-of-low-end doom / sludge / stoner voice, the opposite of a tight bright amp. Gain, Tone, Master, Level. Same cascaded-triode + voiced-tone-stack + supply-sag model, voiced for huge bass, dark top and a spongy, blooming low end.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SuperFuzz}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SuperFuzz}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Univox Super-Fuzz-style octave fuzz: 4x-oversampled full-wave rectification for the octave-up into hard germanium square clipping, with a switchable 1 kHz mid-scoop notch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Supro}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Supro}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Supro 1624T-style octal amp: 6973 power tubes on a tube-rectifier supply with no negative feedback, so loud transients sag the rails - the amp ducks then blooms, breaking up asymmetrically and early. Sag sets how hard the supply droops.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Synthacon}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Synthacon}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Steiner-Parker Synthacon-style diode multimode filter: an asymmetric diode-clipped resonance path gives a snarly, vocal bite, and Mode sweeps the output continuously LP -> BP -> HP - rougher than a clean SVF.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TimeCube}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TimeCube}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cooper Time Cube-style delay: a short, dark acoustic-tube delay (sound piped through a garden hose between transducers) for tight doubling and slapback. Time sets the short delay, Feedback the repeats and Mix the blend. Naturally band-limited and woody - characterful on vocals and snare, not a clean digital echo.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Timmy}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Timmy}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Paul C Timmy-style transparent overdrive: low-gain op-amp soft clipping with cut-only Bass and Treble shaped BEFORE the gain (so it tightens and de-fizzes without adding colour) - clean, dynamic, amp-like. Gain sets the drive, Bass/Treble cut, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ToneBender}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ToneBender}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tone Bender MkII germanium fuzz: three cascaded germanium transistor stages, each asymmetric (germanium clips harder on one rail) with interstage coupling-cap high-pass between them, for the thick, sustaining, slightly-broken vintage fuzz. Bias starves the supply so quiet/decaying notes GATE into the famous spitty sputter; Drive sets the gain, Tone the output roll-off, Level the volume. Distinct from FuzzFace (2 transistors) and Big Muff (4 silicon stages + diodes).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Trident}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Trident}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Trident A-Range-style console EQ: four fixed musical bands (LF shelf, two mid bells, HF shelf) with an aggressive, forward voicing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TriStereo}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TriStereo}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dytronics Tri-Stereo Chorus-style ensemble: three bucket-brigade voices (compander + clock-tracked reconstruction) at staggered rates for a lush, wide three-dimensional chorus.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TubePre}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TubePre}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vintage tube console-style preamp: two cascaded triode stages with an interstage coupling cap (2nd-harmonic-dominant asymmetric clipping) and a warm low shelf.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TubeScreamer}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TubeScreamer}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ibanez Tube Screamer-style overdrive: a real op-amp feedback diode clipper - the mid-focused clean gain is clamped by an antiparallel Shockley diode pair, Newton-solved and 4x oversampled (not a tanh) - with the famous ~720 Hz mid hump. Asym mismatches the two diodes for the asymmetric-clip mod, adding the even harmonics a symmetric clipper can't.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TubeTech}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TubeTech}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tube-Tech CL 1B-style opto compressor: a photocell gain law (soft knee, rising ratio) with program-dependent release and tube makeup warmth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tweed}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tweed}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fender Tweed 5E3-style amp: two asymmetric triode stages into a physical B+ reservoir sag (droops and blooms under pick attack) and an output-transformer stage - warm, loose, early breakup.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:UA610}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{UA610}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Universal Audio 610 tube preamp (Bill Putnam): a 12AX7 valve gain stage between input and output transformers. Push Drive to overdrive the tube into warm asymmetric saturation while Level pads the output -- the '610 move' of cranking the valve and taming the volume -- with transformer low-end thickening and the 610's Low/High program EQ. Distinct from the generic tube preamp by the transformer saturation and the gain/level interaction.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:UniVibe}\blockicon{circuit}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{UniVibe}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Uni-Vibe-style 4-stage phaser: staggered all-pass frequencies driven by an asymmetric lamp-filament LFO through a power-law photocell I-V curve, with nonlinear stages - the watery, throbbing swirl. Chorus or Vibrato.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Urei1176}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Urei1176}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

UREI 1176LN-style FET peak limiter: a field-effect transistor as the variable-gain cell gives the near-instant attack (20 us-800 us) no opto or vari-mu matches, with fixed program ratios and the all-buttons-in mode that slams every ratio at once for the aggressive, distorted British crush. Input drives it into reduction, Attack/Release are the (reversed on the real unit) time constants, Ratio selects 4/8/12/20:1 or All, Output is makeup.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:V76}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{V76}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Telefunken V76-style tube preamp: two cascaded tube stages with interstage coupling into an output transformer - rich, warm 2nd-harmonic saturation with a low-mid body.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VactrolLPG}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VactrolLPG}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Buchla 292-style low-pass gate: a vactrol (LED + photocell) opens a 2-pole low-pass and a VCA together, and the photocell's slow nonlinear light-decay gives the plucky 'bongo' ring as transients fade. Mode picks gate (VCF+VCA), filter-only, or amp-only.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VaractorFilter}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VaractorFilter}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Varactor-tuned filter: a varactor (voltage-controlled capacitance) sets the cutoff, so the cutoff RISES with the instantaneous signal voltage - modulating at audio rate, not via an LFO or envelope. The filter intermodulates the signal with itself, adding gritty, phase-coherent partials no static filter or envelope-wah makes (a clean linear SVF whose tuning is the only nonlinearity). Cutoff sets the base, Depth the varactor sensitivity (self-modulation amount), Reso the resonance, Drive the level into the varactor, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VariMu}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VariMu}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fairchild/Manley-style tube vari-mu compressor: program-dependent ratio rises with level, and a remote-cutoff tube transfer whose genuine 2nd-harmonic warmth grows as gain reduction biases the tube toward cutoff.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Wasp}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Wasp}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

EDP Wasp-style 12 dB multimode filter: the CMOS-inverter gain-stage outputs hard-clip at the rails for a fizzy, gritty bite. Mode 0 LP / 1 BP / 2 HP.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WoolyMammoth}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WoolyMammoth}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

ZVex Wooly Mammoth-style gated fuzz: a huge, square-wave synth-bass fuzz with the signature Pinch control - a starve/gate that chokes the low-level tail into a spluttery, ripped-speaker gate while keeping a massive low end. Wool sets the fuzz, Pinch the gate/starve, EQ the tone, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Zendrive}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Zendrive}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hermida Zendrive-style overdrive: a smooth, mid-focused 'Dumble-in-a-box' with singing sustain and a touch-sensitive, vocal break-up. Gain sets the drive, Tone the top end, Voice the upper-mid focus (the Dumble cut), Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZenerClip}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZenerClip}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ANA]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Back-to-back Zener shunt clipper (Newton-solved, 4x oversampled): two Zeners anode-to-anode clip symmetrically at about +/-3.3 V - far more headroom than a silicon diode pair - so the signal passes clean until it slams into a hard knee. Louder and punchier than soft diode overdrive, the 'stays clean then cracks' character of high-headroom Zener/LED clipping stages. Drive sets the gain into the clipper, Tone a tilt around 1.2 kHz, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Delay (53)

\phantomsection\label{blk:AlignDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AlignDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Alignment delay (Ableton Align Delay-style): a clean dry delay used to time-align tracks or push a signal later by a precise amount - no feedback, no filtering, no colour. Time sets the delay in milliseconds; leave Mix fully wet for pure alignment, or blend for a simple slap double.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BBDDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BBDDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bucket-brigade style echo whose delayed tap is passed through a one-pole low-pass and a tanh saturation in the feedback path, so each repeat grows darker and grittier. Time sets the delay length (1-8000 ms), Feedback sets regeneration up to 0.9, Tone sets the high-frequency rolloff of the repeats, and Mix blends the filtered echo with the dry signal. Lower Tone yields the murky, decaying character of analog BBD delays.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BBDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BBDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bucket-brigade delay: emulates an analog BBD chip with input companding, a darkening low-pass on each repeat and gentle saturation, for the warm, lo-fi, increasingly-murky echoes of a vintage analog delay.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Beatmasher}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Beatmasher}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Beat-repeat: while the gate on in1 is held it records a slice of audio on the first pass then loops that captured slice; releasing the gate passes the dry signal through. Length sets the size of the captured/looped slice (20..1000 ms) and Mix blends the looped slice against the dry input. Hold the gate to freeze and stutter a fragment, release to drop back to live audio.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BeatSlicer}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BeatSlicer}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Captures a buffer while the gate on in1 is held, divides it into 8 slices, and replays them in one of several reorder patterns; releasing the gate passes dry audio through. Length sets the buffer/loop size (100..2000 ms), Pattern selects the slice reordering (0..4, e.g. straight, swapped pairs, doubled, reversed, interleaved), and Mix blends the sliced output against dry. Hold the gate to scramble a phrase into a rhythmic rearrangement.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bouncer}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bouncer}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

A feedback delay whose delay time continually shrinks, so the echoes speed up toward a stutter like a bouncing ball; a gate on in1 re-arms the delay back to its start time. Time sets the initial delay (20..600 ms), Accel sets how fast the time shrinks, Feedback sets the echo regeneration (up to 0.95), and Mix blends wet against dry. Higher Accel collapses the repeats into a rapid buzz, while a gate retrigger resets the bounce.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Chorus}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Chorus}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Single-voice chorus: an LFO (Rate, Depth) modulates a short base Delay (1-30 ms) and the detuned copy is blended back via Mix to thicken and widen the source. Short delays with light depth give subtle doubling; longer and deeper settings approach vibrato and flange. See Ensemble for a three-voice string-machine variant.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Comb}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Comb}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tuned comb resonator: a short feedback delay set by Freq (20-2000 Hz) with a damping low-pass, which doubles as a Karplus-Strong plucked string when excited by a transient. Feedback sets resonance and sustain while Damp darkens the decay. High feedback rings at the tuned pitch; pair it with noise or an impulse for string and membrane tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrossFeedbackDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrossFeedbackDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cross-coupled delay: two delay lines at incommensurate times each feed the OTHER line's input through a damping filter, so echoes bounce between the two streams and interleave into a denser, evolving pattern than a single tap can make.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Delay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Delay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Feedback Mod, Time Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Feedback delay line (1-8000 ms) with a damping low-pass in the feedback path that progressively darkens each repeat. Time is modulated by in 3 (scaled by Mod) for chorus and flange-style movement, Feedback sets regeneration up to 0.99, and Mix sets dry/wet. Damp at 0 gives bright digital echoes; higher values emulate tape/BBD high-frequency loss.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DiffuseDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DiffuseDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Feedback delay whose tap is smeared through a 4-stage all-pass diffusion chain, pushing echoes toward reverb. Time sets the delay (1-1000 ms), Feedback sets regeneration up to 0.9, and Diffuse sets the all-pass coefficient that controls how much each repeat is blurred. Mix sets dry/wet; low Diffuse gives clean echoes, high Diffuse dissolves them into a diffuse wash.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DubDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DubDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dub-techno echo: a feedback delay whose repeats are progressively band-limited and tape-saturated inside the loop, so each echo gets darker, warmer and more degraded - the endless, dubby Basic-Channel chord-stab echo of dub techno and dub house. Time sets the delay, Feedback how many repeats (high = near-infinite), Tone darkens each repeat (low-pass in the loop), Drive the in-loop tape saturation, and Mix the wet/dry blend. Long Time + high Feedback + low Tone gives the classic sinking dub tail. Distinct from the clean Delay and the wow/flutter TapeDelay - the feedback path is filtered and saturated.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DubEcho}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DubEcho}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dub echo: each repeat passes through a low-pass and soft saturation in the feedback loop, so echoes get progressively darker and warmer as they decay - the classic dub/reggae spring-into-the-mix delay.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DuckDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DuckDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Feedback delay line whose wet output is attenuated by an envelope follower tracking the dry signal, so repeats drop out while you play and swell up in the gaps. Time sets the delay length (1-8000 ms), Feedback sets regeneration up to 0.9, Duck sets how hard the dry level suppresses the echoes, and Mix blends wet and dry. Keeps echoes from cluttering busy passages while letting them bloom in the silences.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DuckingDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DuckingDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ducking delay: a feedback echo whose wet output is pulled down by an envelope of the dry input, so the repeats stay out of the way while you play and swell up in the gaps - the classic technique for delays that add space without cluttering the mix. Time sets the delay, Feedback the repeats, Depth how hard the dry ducks the wet.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Ensemble}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Ensemble}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Three-voice string-machine chorus: one LFO modulates three delay taps at evenly spread phases, summing into a lush, wide thickening richer than the single-voice Chorus. Rate and Depth set the swirl and Mix the blend. Classic for pads, strings and synth-brass.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FilterDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FilterDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Filtered multi-tap delay (Ableton Filter Delay-style): three delay taps at spreading times, each band-passed at Color before feedback, so the echoes are tuned and coloured rather than full-range. Time sets the first tap, Spread the spacing of the other two, Color the band-pass centre, Feedback the regeneration, and Mix the wet blend. (Mono sum of the three taps; the device's L/C/R panning needs a stereo rack.)

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Flanger}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Flanger}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Swept-comb flanger: an LFO sweeps a very short delay (0.5-10 ms) mixed with the dry signal to create moving comb notches, with bipolar Feedback resonating the peaks (negative values invert for a hollow tone). Depth sets the sweep width and Mix the blend. Short delays with high feedback give the classic metallic jet-whoosh.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FoldDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FoldDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Folding echo: a long delay whose feedback path is wavefolded each pass, so every repeat grows brighter and more harmonically dense instead of darker - an inverted, ever-spikier echo tail that a damped delay never produces.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FractalEcho}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FractalEcho}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fractal echo: five taps spaced at successive golden-ratio fractions of the base time, each quieter than the last, so one hit scatters into a self-similar cluster of echoes that bunch up as they fade - a fractal stutter, not an even repeat.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Freeze}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Freeze}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Freeze Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Audio freeze / looper: while in1 is high it captures the recent input and loops a window of it indefinitely (an instant sustain/pad-freeze); while in1 is low it passes the dry signal and keeps recording. Size sets the loop window length, Mix the wet level.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FreqEcho}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FreqEcho}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Frequency-shifted echo: a delay line whose feedback is single-sideband frequency-shifted via a polyphase Hilbert pair, so every repeat slides up or down in Hz rather than in pitch. Time sets the delay (1 to 1000 ms), Shift sets the linear frequency offset (-500 to +500 Hz), Feedback (0 to 0.95) sets repeat count, and Mix blends dry/wet. Because the shift is inharmonic, the tail detunes and smears with each pass for metallic, ever-evolving echoes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Glitch}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Glitch}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Probabilistic buffer glitcher that records the input and, at each slice boundary, randomly jumps to a captured slice and may play it forward or reversed. Slice sets the segment length in milliseconds and Chance sets the probability that a given slice is repeated or reversed rather than passed through cleanly. Mix blends the glitched playback against the dry signal for stutter, repeat and reverse artifacts.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GrainDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GrainDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Granular feedback delay that reads overlapping windowed grains from the delay line, pitch-shifting them and scattering their read positions. Time sets the base tap (10-8000 ms), Pitch transposes the grains +/-12 semitones, and Scatter randomizes each grain's offset for a smeared, diffuse cloud. Mix sets dry/wet; the shifted grains are fed back at reduced gain, so repeats drift in pitch and position.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GrainReverse}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GrainReverse}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Reverse-grain smear: continuously records the input and reads each short grain backwards through a Hann window, so the sound is chopped into little reversed snippets - a stuttering, sucking, backwards texture from a live signal, no buffer-capture needed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LagrangeDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LagrangeDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lagrange fractional delay: reads the delay line at a sub-sample position using 4-point (cubic) Lagrange interpolation rather than the usual linear interpolation, so sub-sample delays keep their high-frequency content instead of being dulled - the interpolation used in high-quality tuning, chorus and physical-modeling delay lines. Delay in ms, wet/dry Mix.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Looper}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Looper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Circulating-buffer looper that re-reads a fixed-length loop and either overdubs new input onto it or recirculates it unchanged. Length sets the loop time (100-2000 ms) and Record switches between overdub (input mixed with the looped buffer at half gain) and pure recirculation when off. Mix sets dry/wet; leave Record on to layer takes, turn it off to freeze and play back the captured loop.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Morphagene}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Morphagene}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tape-splice granular (Make Noise / Soundhack Morphagene): continuously records the in0 input onto a virtual tape, then plays back a Gene (grain) from it. Slide scans the playback position along the reel, Vari sets the varispeed (pitch + direction, reverse below centre), Gene the grain length, Morph the grain overlap/smear, and Mix the blend. Scrub, stretch, reverse and granulate live audio - distinct from the fixed-loop Looper and the cloud-style granular blocks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Multitap}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Multitap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Multi-tap delay: the input is written into a circular buffer and read back at several time-spaced taps that are summed, averaged, and fed back into the buffer. Time sets the base delay in ms, Taps chooses 1 to 6 read points, Feedback recirculates the averaged taps for repeats, Spread pulls the taps toward the longer end for uneven echo timing, and Mix crossfades dry input against the wet sum. Low Spread gives evenly stacked echoes while high Spread spaces them out, and higher Feedback builds longer rhythmic delay trails.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MultiTapDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MultiTapDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Multi-tap delay: reads four taps at 1/4, 1/2, 3/4 and 1x of the base time off a single delay line with decreasing levels, building a rhythmic pattern of echoes from one knob.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoiseModDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoiseModDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Random-warble delay: the delay time wanders on a smoothed random-step modulator that picks a new target at the set rate, giving an unpredictable detuned warble between a chorus and a broken-tape pitch drift.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PeakFlanger}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PeakFlanger}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

A comb flanger whose delay time is swept by a peak envelope follower on the input, so transients drive the flange motion (delay ranges ~0.5..9.5 ms). Soften sets the envelope release time, Feedback sets the comb regeneration (+/-0.95, negative inverts the comb), Amount scales how far each peak sweeps the delay, and Mix blends wet against dry. The Traktor Flanger Pulse character: the jet sweep follows the music's own dynamics rather than a fixed LFO.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PingPong}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PingPong}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ping-pong delay for a mono source: a single line is read at time T or 2T so the two outputs hear staggered taps that appear to bounce between speakers, with regeneration fed back through the longer tap. Time sets the base delay, Feedback the number of repeats up to 0.9, and Mix dry/wet. Out picks L or R, where R hears T and L hears 2T to create the alternating bounce.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PitchEcho}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PitchEcho}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pitch-locked echo: the delay time is set to a whole number of the input's detected pitch periods, so the repeats land exactly in phase and reinforce the tone instead of smearing it.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rainmaker}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rainmaker}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rhythmic tapped delay (Intellijel / Cylonix Rainmaker): spreads up to Taps echoes across the delay Time in a decaying rhythm, then runs the result through a tuned Comb resonator for ringing, pitched repeats - rhythmic delays, resonant reverbs and string-like tones in one. Time sets the span, Taps the number of echoes, Feedback the regeneration, Comb the resonator pitch/amount, and Mix the wet blend. A 16-tap rhythm engine, distinct from the fixed-tap Multitap.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RectDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RectDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Octave-rising echo: the feedback is full-wave rectified (DC-removed) each pass, so every repeat jumps an octave and the tail climbs into a shimmering high cloud - a pitched, ascending echo unlike any flat-pitch delay.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Reverse}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Reverse}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Reverse delay: snapshots a Window-length slice of input and plays it backward on a loop, a glitchy pre-echo/reverse texture, blended via Mix. Short windows give fluttering grains; longer windows reverse recognizable phrases. See ReverseGrain and ReverseVerb for related effects.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ReverseDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ReverseDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Captures the input into a delay buffer and reads it back newest-to-oldest within a repeating window, so each chunk plays in reverse. Time sets the window length (50-8000 ms) that defines how much audio is flipped per cycle, and Mix blends the reversed playback against the dry signal. Useful for backward-tape swells and disorienting rhythmic stutters.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ReverseGrain}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ReverseGrain}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Captures a buffer while the gate on in1 is held, then plays it backward at a pitch-shifted read rate for a reversed, stuttered texture; releasing the gate passes dry audio through. Length sets the captured grain size (50..1500 ms), Pitch transposes the reverse playback rate (+/-12 semitones), and Mix blends the reversed grain against dry. Hold the gate to suck a phrase into a backward swell.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rotary}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rotary}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Leslie rotary speaker: combines a doppler pitch wobble (modulated short delay) with a phase-offset amplitude tremolo so the tone swirls as the virtual horn spins. Speed sets the rotation rate and Depth its intensity. Iconic on organ, electric piano and guitar - automate Speed for the slow-to-fast chorale ramp.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Scratch}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Scratch}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Turntable-scratch effect where a raised-cosine LFO sweeps the delay read head back and forth through recent input. Rate sets the sweep speed (0.1-8 Hz) and Depth sets how far back the head travels (1-200 ms). Mix sets dry/wet; faster Rate and deeper Depth give more pronounced vinyl-scratch wobble.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Shuffler}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Shuffler}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Beat-shuffler that records the input and, at each segment boundary, may jump its playback to one of a few earlier segment offsets. Segment sets the slice length (50-500 ms) and Chance sets the probability that a new boundary triggers a jump to a randomly chosen prior offset. Mix sets dry/wet; low Chance plays mostly straight, high Chance stutters and rearranges the beat.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Slapback}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Slapback}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}
