Slapback echo: a single short delayed repeat (typically 60-150 ms) layered under the dry signal - the snappy rockabilly/vocal doubling slap, no feedback wash. Time sets the delay, Mix the level of the repeat.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlapDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlapDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slapback echo: a single short delayed repeat (with optional feedback for a few taps) - the rockabilly/vocal slap that fattens a sound without turning into a wash.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SliceShuffler}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SliceShuffler}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Continuous buffer-shuffler (Max-for-Live Buffer-Shuffler style): records the incoming audio one bar at a time and, while recording the next bar, plays the last bar back as Steps slices re-ordered by a Seed-driven permutation, with a fraction of slices (Reverse) played backwards and a 16-step on/off Gate mask muting chosen slots. Length sets the bar in milliseconds, Steps the slice count (4/8/16), Seed the rearrangement (every value a different reproducible shuffle), and Mix blends against dry. Unlike BeatSlicer's five fixed patterns this is a fully addressable per-slice remap that runs without a trigger.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SpectralTiltDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SpectralTiltDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spectral-tilt delay: each pass through the feedback loop tilts the spectrum around a pivot, so successive echoes get progressively darker (or brighter) - tape-like high-end decay or a reverse 'opening up' tail, set by one bipolar Tilt knob.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Stutter}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Stutter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Auto-rhythmic buffer repeat: at each Rate cycle it snapshots a Grab-length window and loops it for the cycle, creating stutters and beat-repeat artefacts, with Mix blending against the live signal. Short grabs at high rates give granular buzz; longer grabs repeat recognizable slices. See Beatmasher and BeatSlicer for tempo-locked glitching.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TapeDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TapeDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vintage tape echo: a ~1.5 Hz wow modulates the delay time while a Tone low-pass and tanh tape Saturation in the feedback path darken and thicken each repeat. Feedback sets regeneration and Mix the blend. Unlike the clean Delay, repeats degrade and warble musically - raise Wow and Sat for worn-tape character.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TapeFlutter}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TapeFlutter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tape flutter: a short fractional delay whose read head wobbles under three stacked LFOs - slow wow, mid flutter and fast scrape - reproducing the drifting pitch waver of worn tape; no echo, just the wobble.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TapeStop}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TapeStop}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Stop Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tape-stop effect: while in1 is held the playback slows from full speed to a halt over Time, dropping the pitch and smearing into silence - the classic turntable/tape brake. Releasing in1 returns to normal speed. Mix blends against the dry. A performance effect, not a fixed pitch shift.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThiranAllpass}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThiranAllpass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Thiran fractional delay: an integer delay line followed by a first-order Thiran all-pass that adds the sub-sample remainder with a maximally-flat group delay, resolving the delay to a fraction of a sample without the high-frequency loss of linear interpolation. A recursive all-pass method distinct from the FIR Lagrange delay - flat magnitude at the cost of frequency-dependent group delay. Feedback recirculates, Mix blends.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Vibrato}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Vibrato}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pitch vibrato: an LFO modulates a short delay's read position to bend pitch, output fully wet (no dry blend) for true vibrato rather than chorus. Rate sets the wobble speed and Depth its pitch range. Gentle settings add expression; deep settings give a seasick warble.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WowDelay}\blockicon{delay}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WowDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DLY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wow echo: the delay time drifts under a slow sub-1 Hz wow LFO, so the repeats sag and rise in pitch like a worn tape echo - the warbling, detuning character of an old Echoplex, distinct from the fast flutter of TapeFlutter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Distortion (174)

\phantomsection\label{blk:AbsShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AbsShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Odd/even harmonic blender: crossfades between a tanh stage (odd harmonics, symmetric) and a rectifying stage (even harmonics, octave-up), letting you dial the exact odd-vs-even harmonic balance of the distortion.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AdaaCubic}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AdaaCubic}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Anti-aliased cubic clip: the classic cubic soft-clip (1.5x - 0.5x^3, flat past unity) with 1st-order antiderivative anti-aliasing, giving warm odd-harmonic overdrive without the aliasing of the naive cubic.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AdaaFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AdaaFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Anti-aliased wavefolder: a sinusoidal folder with 1st-order antiderivative anti-aliasing, so hard driving into many folds generates far less of the harsh aliasing that plagues naive digital folders.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AdaaHardClip}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AdaaHardClip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Anti-aliased hard clip: applies 1st-order antiderivative anti-aliasing to a brick-wall clipper, so the brutal clipping edges generate far less aliasing than a naive hard clip - aggressive yet clean digital clipping.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ADAAsym}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ADAAsym}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Anti-aliased asymmetric drive: a diode-like curve that saturates the two polarities differently (even + odd harmonics) with 1st-order antiderivative anti-aliasing, so the asymmetry's bright harmonics do not fold back as aliasing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AdaaTanh}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AdaaTanh}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Anti-aliased tanh: applies 1st-order antiderivative anti-aliasing (ADAA) to a tanh saturator, evaluating the average of the nonlinearity's integral across each sample step - hot drive without the harsh aliasing naive tanh produces.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ALawShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ALawShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

A-law companding: the telecom A-law transfer (linear below 1/A, logarithmic above), which heavily compresses level and lifts quiet detail - a gritty, lo-fi logarithmic saturation distinct from mu-law, with a hard knee at the 1/A breakpoint.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AlgSigmoidShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AlgSigmoidShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Algebraic-sigmoid waveshaper: the rational soft-clip x/sqrt(1+(Drive*x)^2), which saturates faster than softsign but more gradually than a hard clip and is cheap to compute (no transcendentals) - a clean, neutral overdrive with a smooth rail approach.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AmpScan}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AmpScan}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Amplitude wave-scanner: the instantaneous level indexes a position in a single-cycle waveform, so louder samples read further through the cycle - a 1-D wave-terrain shaper with offset scanning, distinct from a direct sine folder.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AmpSim}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AmpSim}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Guitar amp model chaining an asymmetric tanh preamp, a tone-tilt filter and a speaker-cabinet low-pass with output compensation. Drive sets preamp gain (0-48 dB) into the asymmetric clipper, Tone tilts between darker and brighter response, and Cab sets the cabinet cutoff (brighter at higher values). Mix blends the modeled tone against the dry; ranges from edge-of-breakup grit to saturated lead tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AnalogClip}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AnalogClip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Variable clipper: one knob morphs the clipping knee from a smooth soft-clip to a hard brick wall, with an asymmetry/bias control for even harmonics - a general-purpose analog-flavored clipper covering the whole soft-to-hard range.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Array}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Array}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Drawable waveshaper: maps the input at in0 (folded from bipolar into a 0 to 1 index) through a transfer curve drawn in node config via linear interpolation, then back to bipolar, blended by Mix against the dry input. The drawn breakpoints define the input-to-output mapping and Mix sets the wet/dry balance. A diagonal curve is a clean pass-through while steeper or folded curves act as custom distortion and waveshaping.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AsinShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AsinShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Arcsine waveshaper: maps the clamped input through asin(x)/(pi/2), the inverse of a sine - so it steepens toward the rails and expands the mid-level (the opposite curvature of a sine soft-clip), brightening and adding odd harmonics as Drive pushes into the clamp.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AsymFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AsymFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Asymmetric triangle wavefolder: positive and negative excursions fold against a biased threshold for even-harmonic content, with a leaky DC servo that recenters the folded output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AsymFuzz}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AsymFuzz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Asymmetric fuzz: a bias offset pushes the signal into a hard clip that ceilings the two polarities at different levels, the gated, sputtery, even-harmonic snarl of a starved-bias fuzz pedal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AsymSat}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AsymSat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Asymmetric saturator: positive and negative half-cycles are tanh-driven by independent amounts, producing even-harmonic, tube-like asymmetry you dial per polarity.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AsymWrap}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AsymWrap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Asymmetric wrap/fold: positive swings WRAP around a ceiling (sawtooth wraparound -> a brash, octave-rich buzz) while negative swings soft-clip with tanh. The deliberate lopsidedness throws strong even harmonics and a glitchy upper octave that symmetric folders never produce. Drive pushes it, Ceiling sets the positive wrap point, Mix blends.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AuralExciter}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AuralExciter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Aural exciter: high-passes the signal, generates new upper harmonics through a soft nonlinearity, high-passes again and adds them back - restoring air and presence without an EQ boost.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BentIdentityShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BentIdentityShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bent-identity shaper: passes the signal through (sqrt(x^2+1)-1)/2 + x, a curve that is almost a straight line but bends slightly and asymmetrically around zero - so it stays near-transparent while adding a touch of low-order (mostly even) harmonic warmth. A subtle console-style colour rather than an overt distortion. Drive sets the bend amount.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BEOD}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BEOD}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Friedman BE-OD-style high-gain overdrive: a hot op-amp gain stage into a tight asymmetric silicon diode clipper, voiced like the BE-100 amp's lead channel - saturated, focused, amp-in-a-box drive.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BiasBreath}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BiasBreath}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Breathing bias saturator: the signal envelope drives a DC offset into the input before a tanh stage (and removes it after), so the harmonic asymmetry swells with playing dynamics - quiet stays clean, loud goes asymmetric.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BiasStarve}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BiasStarve}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bias starve: a starved-plate, dying-battery fuzz - low supply voltage chokes the gain, so notes splat, sputter, gate and sag with a sick, broken-up 'velcro' decay that collapses as it starves. Starve sets how low the supply runs, Drive the gain into it, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bitcrush}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bitcrush}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Digital degradation: Bits quantizes the amplitude to 2^Bits levels (quantization noise) while Downsample holds each sample for N frames (sample-rate reduction and aliasing). Mix blends against the dry signal. Low Bits gives gritty lo-fi crunch; high Downsample adds metallic aliasing for retro and 8-bit textures.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BitLogic}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BitLogic}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bitwise logic shaper: quantizes the sample to an integer and combines it with a bit-shifted copy of itself via AND/OR/XOR (the bytebeat trick), producing harsh digital staircase artifacts no analog circuit makes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BitParity}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BitParity}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Parity fuzz: quantizes the sample and inverts its polarity whenever the parity (XOR of all its bits) is odd - so the waveform is flipped on a fine, signal-dependent bit pattern, generating a dense, dirty, ring-mod-like fuzz that tracks the amplitude in a chaotic, digital way. Bits sets the resolution, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BitReverse}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BitReverse}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bit-reversal scrambler: quantizes the sample and reverses the order of its bits, so the most and least significant bits swap roles - a violent, structured digital distortion with no analog analogue.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BitRotate}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BitRotate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bit rotate: quantizes to a byte then circularly ROTATES the bits, so the most-significant (loudest) bits wrap down into the least-significant places - a violent, non-monotonic remapping that turns smooth ramps into buzzing digital staircases. Distinct from bit-crushing: no information is dropped, it's scrambled. Bits sets the rotation amount, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BitStick}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BitStick}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stuck-bit glitch: instead of quantizing every sample, the output randomly STICKS at its previous quantized value and only refreshes with probability (1 - Stick) - like a failing converter with stuck pixels, freezing little plateaus of the waveform into a jittery, broken digital texture. Steps sets the resolution, Stick the freeze probability, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bitwise}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bitwise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bitwise mangler / databender (a first as a synth block): converts each sample to a 16-bit integer and applies a raw bit operation - XOR, AND, OR or bit-rotate - against Mask, then back to audio. Where Bitcrush only quantises the amplitude, this rips into the actual bit pattern: flipping a high bit folds the waveform, toggling low bits adds fizzing digital grit. Op picks the operation, Mask the bits to act on, Mix the blend. Pure circuit-bent digital destruction with no analogue equivalent.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CabSim}\blockicon{cabinet}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CabSim}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Generic speaker-cabinet voicing: a 90 Hz high-pass trims sub rumble, a high-frequency rolloff tames fizz, and a peak around 1.5 kHz adds cabinet body, all blended by Mix. Body sets how much of the resonant midrange peak is added and Bright raises the rolloff corner for a more open top end. A lightweight tone-shaping voicing for amp signals, not an impulse-response capture of a real cabinet.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CapLeak}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CapLeak}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Capacitor leak: a slow DC bias leaks in proportional to the recent signal and bleeds away in silence, drifting the operating point so the clipping turns lopsided and wanders - the bias-creep of a leaky coupling capacitor that never quite settles. Amount sets the leak, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChaosClip}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChaosClip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaos clipper: a hard clipper whose ceiling is jittered every sample by a logistic chaos map, so the clip level dances unpredictably between tight and loose - the distortion shimmers and crackles with a living, never-repeating edge instead of a static wall. Drive sets the push, Chaos the ceiling jitter, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChaosCrush}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChaosCrush}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaotic bitcrusher: a logistic-map chaos generator wobbles the quantizer's bit depth on every decimated sample, so the crush noise floor wanders unpredictably instead of sitting at a fixed grit - a living, never-repeating lo-fi.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChaosDrive}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChaosDrive}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaos-modulated drive: a logistic-map iterator nudges the saturation gain every sample, so the distortion shimmers and never sits perfectly still - controlled instability between clean and crushed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChaosFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChaosFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaotic wavefolder: the fold density is steered every sample by a logistic chaos map running in its turbulent regime, so the harmonics shimmer and scramble unpredictably - a folder that never repeats, between a controllable warble (low Chaos) and a fully scrambled metallic roar (high Chaos). Drive sets the input gain, Chaos the strangeness of the modulation, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Character}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Character}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Character drive with five voicings (Mode): Push (soft clip), Heat (S-clip), Soar (sine fold), Howl (triangle fold) and Shred (wrap fold), each driven by Drive and blended via Mix. One module spanning warm saturation to wild folding distortion. Modulate Mode or Drive for evolving textures.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChebyBank}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChebyBank}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chebyshev harmonic bank: generates harmonics 2..5 simultaneously via Chebyshev polynomials and mixes them by a spread control, sculpting a precise additive harmonic spectrum from a single waveshaper.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChebyShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChebyShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chebyshev harmonic shaper: a Chebyshev polynomial of order N maps a sine input to its Nth harmonic exactly, letting you dial a specific overtone (2nd..6th) instead of a generic distortion smear.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Chebyshev}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Chebyshev}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chebyshev-polynomial shaper that synthesizes a single chosen Harmonic (2nd-8th) from the input via the T_n recurrence, blended by Amount and Mix. Unlike broadband clipping it adds one targeted overtone - the 2nd for octave thickness, higher orders for metallic ring. Pairs well with a clean sine input for precise harmonic generation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Clip}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Clip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clipper that morphs from soft (tanh) to hard clipping at the Threshold via Hardness, with Output trim and dry/wet Mix. Soft adds rounded saturation harmonics; hard adds bright odd harmonics and caps peaks dead. Use as a tone shaper or a transparent peak ceiling ahead of a limiter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Corrode}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Corrode}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Corrode: a bit-depth that DROPS the longer the signal is sustained loud, so a held note progressively decays into a coarse, quantized, corroded version of itself, then recovers resolution in the gaps. Rate sets how fast it corrodes, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoshShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoshShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cosh expander: sign(x)*(cosh(|x|*drive)-1), normalized, which grows faster than a square so loud signal is pushed up hard while quiet signal is suppressed - an aggressive upward-expansion shaper that adds odd harmonics and exaggerates dynamics, the opposite of a soft clip.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrossDist}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrossDist}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Multiband distortion: two crossovers split the signal into low, mid and high bands that are each saturated independently before recombining, so you can grind one band hard while keeping the others clean.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrossFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrossFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: In A, In B}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cross-modulated wavefolder: the fold threshold is set live by a second input, so one signal carves the folding ceiling of another - audio-rate fold-threshold modulation for spectra that move with the modulator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrossoverDist}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrossoverDist}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crossover distortion: introduces a dead-band notch around zero like an underbiased class-B push-pull amplifier, adding the gritty, buzzy artefact that appears on low-level signal near the zero crossing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrushFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrushFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crush-then-fold: the signal is bit- and sample-rate-crushed, then the stepped result is driven into a triangle wavefolder, so the quantization steps become hard fold edges - a harsh, aliased, digital-destruction timbre neither stage makes alone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Decimator}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Decimator}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bit-crusher + sample-rate reducer: quantises the in0 signal to Bits resolution and holds each sample for Downsample steps, for everything from gentle lo-fi grit to full 8-bit / telephone destruction. Mix blends against the clean signal. Distinct from Bitcrush in adding the sample-rate-reduction (aliasing) stage.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DeltaSigma}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DeltaSigma}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Delta-sigma crush: re-quantizes the audio to a single bit with first-order noise shaping (an error-feedback modulator), turning the signal into a dense +/-1 pulse-density stream like a 1-bit DSD converter - harsh, grainy, with the quantization noise pushed up high. Then a low-pass recovers a gritty version. Tone sets the recovery filter, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Diode}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Diode}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Asymmetric diode clipper that drives the input into an exponential soft-knee saturation, shaping the positive and negative halves differently. Drive sets the input gain in dB into the nonlinearity, while Bias increases the curvature of the negative half for stronger asymmetry and added even harmonics. Mix blends the clipped signal against the dry input for germanium/silicon-style warmth and grit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DiodeClip}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DiodeClip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Antiparallel-diode clipper: the exponential Shockley curve (1 - e^-|x|) soft-clips with the smooth, rounded knee of a real diode pair rather than a hard or tanh limiter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DomainFlip}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DomainFlip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Domain flip: a hysteretic staircase quantizer modelling magnetic domains that only flip once the input pushes far enough past the current level - so the output sticks on a step then jumps, tracing the jagged Barkhausen staircase rather than the smooth signal. Step sets the flip threshold, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Drive}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Drive}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Waveshaping overdrive with three curves (Type: tanh soft-clip, sine fold, or hard clip) driven by up to 48 dB of gain plus a Bias offset for even-harmonic asymmetry. A post Tone low-pass tames fizz, Mix blends dry/wet, and Output trims the final level. Spans subtle warmth to aggressive distortion depending on Drive and curve.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DrumBuss}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DrumBuss}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Drum-bus processor (Ableton Drum Buss-style): a one-stop chain for drum groups - Transient sharpens or softens the attack, Drive adds tube grit, Crunch a harder harmonic edge, and Boom adds a tuned resonant low-end thump at BoomFreq. Mix blends it back. Glues and beefs up a drum bus in one block.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DualBandDrive}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DualBandDrive}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two-band saturator: a single crossover splits low and high bands that are tanh-driven independently before recombining, so you can grit the highs without muddying the lows (or the reverse).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DynSat}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DynSat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dynamic saturation: the drive amount tracks the input envelope, so louder passages distort more and quiet ones stay clean (Sens sets how strongly). Adds harmonics on transients and peaks without muddying sustained low-level material. Good for program-dependent grit on drums and bus.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EluShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EluShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

ELU waveshaper: the exponential-linear-unit transfer passes positive signal linearly but bends negative excursions through alpha*(e^x-1) toward a soft floor at -Alpha - a strongly asymmetric saturation that adds even harmonics and a tube-like one-sided compression.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EnvBitcrush}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EnvBitcrush}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dynamic bit-crusher: the quantizer's bit depth rides the input envelope, so loud passages stay clean and quiet tails crumble into lo-fi grit (or the reverse) - level-dependent digital decay.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EnvCrush}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EnvCrush}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Envelope-driven bit-crusher: the bit depth tracks the input LEVEL, so loud passages stay clean and quiet passages dissolve into gritty quantization - the opposite of how digital normally behaves, like a dying battery or a sample-player running out of headroom on the tails. Floor/Ceil set the bit-depth range, Sens how fast the level pulls it, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EnvFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EnvFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Envelope-driven wavefolder: the drive into a triangle folder rises with the input envelope, so quiet passages stay clean and loud ones fold into ever-brighter harmonics - dynamics-dependent folding with a DC servo.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Erosion}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Erosion}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Noise-modulation distortion: sprays band-limited noise (Freq sets its colour) onto the signal by multiplying it with the input, eroding the tone into gritty granular texture. Amount sets intensity and Mix dry/wet. From subtle dirt to harsh destruction - a creative degrade rather than a clean overdrive.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Exciter}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Exciter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Harmonic exciter: splits off the band above Freq, generates new upper harmonics from it with a tanh Drive, and blends them back via Amount for added air and presence. Subtle settings add sheen and intelligibility without raising broadband level. Most effective on dull vocals, drums and full mixes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ExpShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ExpShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Exponential waveshaper: maps the signal through an exponential transfer curve so small signals stay small and large ones swell sharply, an aggressive convex distortion unlike the concave tanh.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FeedbackSaturator}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FeedbackSaturator}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Feedback saturation: the saturated output is low-passed and fed back into the saturator's input, so the distortion compounds on itself and the harmonics bloom and self-bias the way a cranked amp with a feedback loop does - richer and more compressed than a single tanh stage.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FeedbackShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FeedbackShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Self-modulating waveshaper: the block's OWN previous output is fed back into the shaping curve - y = tanh(Drive*in + Feedback*y_prev) - so the distortion bends itself every sample. From warm saturation up into chaotic screaming as Feedback rises; a living, unstable fuzz no static waveshaper can make. Drive sets gain, Feedback the self-modulation, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FloatCrush}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FloatCrush}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Floating-point crusher: quantizes the MANTISSA at the signal's own exponent, so the quantization step scales WITH the level (like low-bit floating-point audio) - the noise floor rides up and down with the signal instead of sitting at a fixed level as in a normal (linear) bit-crusher. A subtler, more 'alive' digital grit. Mantissa sets the kept bits, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FoldCascade}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FoldCascade}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cascaded wavefolder: drives the signal through several fold stages at progressively tighter thresholds, multiplying the fold density into a dense, West-Coast complex-oscillator timbre.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FoldSine}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FoldSine}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phase-warped sine folder: folds through sin() like a sinusoidal folder but pre-warps the phase with a second-harmonic term, breaking the pure odd-harmonic symmetry to give a brighter, asymmetric fold spectrum.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FractalFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FractalFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Recursive folder: the folded output is fed back into the folder's input, so each sample folds against a memory of the last fold - the harmonics build into a self-similar, evolving, fractal-like spectrum that a single-pass folder cannot make.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Fuzz}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Fuzz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

High-gain fuzz: heavy drive soft-saturates then hard-clips into a square wave for a thick, sustaining buzz, with a Tone low-pass to tame the fizz and Level to set output. Mix blends dry/wet. Aggressive and compressed - see the BigMuff and FuzzFace analog models for circuit-specific fuzz voicings.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GatedFuzz}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GatedFuzz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gated fuzz: a hard fuzz clip followed by a fast noise gate, so the fuzz collapses into stuttering, velcro-ripping decay as notes die - the dying-battery / starved fuzz sound.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GeluShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GeluShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

GELU waveshaper: the Gaussian-error-linear-unit transfer 0.5x(1+tanh(sqrt(2/pi)(x+0.0447x^3))), which gates the signal by an approximate Gaussian CDF - a soft, slightly dipping asymmetric saturation that fades small signals and passes large ones, a gentler-than-Mish self-gate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GeoFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GeoFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Geometric folder: a wavefolder whose fold spacing is logarithmic rather than linear, so the harmonics it adds are spread geometrically (octave-like) instead of evenly - a brighter, bell-ier, more musical fold than the usual triangle/sine wrap. Drive sets how deep it folds, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GompertzShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GompertzShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gompertz waveshaper: shapes the signal through the asymmetric double-exponential sigmoid 2*e^(-e^(-Drive*x))-1, which approaches its lower and upper rails at different rates - unlike the symmetric tanh, this lopsidedness injects strong even harmonics and a tube-like asymmetry. A distinctly coloured saturation; a transfer-curve use of the Gompertz growth function.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GravityQuant}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GravityQuant}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Magnetic quantizer: instead of hard-snapping to a grid, the output is PULLED toward the nearest grid level by a continuous spring (Strength). At low Strength it just nudges the signal toward the steps for a subtle pitch/level magnetism; at full Strength it becomes a hard quantizer. The in-between gives a soft, gravitational stair-step. Levels sets the grid, Strength the pull.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GrayFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GrayFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gray-code folder: quantizes the sample and XORs it with its own Gray code (v ^ (v>>1)), refracting the waveform into a self-similar staircase of digital harmonics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GridConduction}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GridConduction}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Grid conduction: when the drive swings positive past the bias the tube's grid starts drawing current and clamps that half hard, while the negative half stays soft - the asymmetric blocking distortion of an overdriven valve grid that gives tube amps their compressed, sagging bite. Drive sets the push, Bias where conduction starts, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GrowlBox}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GrowlBox}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Growl box: at random zero crossings the polarity of an added sub-octave FLIPS, so the period randomly doubles and the tone breaks into a gnarly, sputtering sub-harmonic growl/multiphonic - the controlled chaos of a fuzz on the edge of tracking. Amount sets the sub level, Chaos how often it flips (smooth octave-down at 0, chaotic growl high), Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GudermannianShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GudermannianShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gudermannian waveshaper: maps the input through the Gudermannian gd(x)=2*atan(e^x)-pi/2, a transcendental soft-clip with a gentler knee than tanh and a softer approach to the rails - a smooth, transparent saturation that adds subtle odd harmonics without harsh edges.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HarmonicMul}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HarmonicMul}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Harmonic multiplier: passes the signal through the Nth Chebyshev polynomial, which maps a sine to its exact Nth harmonic - a precise single-harmonic generator (octave at N=2, fifth-of-octave at N=3, etc.) for additive coloring.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HermiteShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HermiteShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hermite waveshaper: maps the input through a probabilist's Hermite polynomial He_n of selectable order, generating a specific harmonic (like Chebyshev shaping but on the Hermite basis), then tanh-limited - a precise, order-selectable harmonic adder.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HysteresisQuant}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HysteresisQuant}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hysteresis quantizer: a stepped quantizer with a DEADBAND - it holds its current step and only jumps to a new one once the input moves past the deadband, so dithery, wandering signals stop chattering between adjacent steps. Glitch-free stair-stepping with a sticky, lagged digital character. Steps sets the resolution, Deadband the stickiness, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HystShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HystShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Backlash distortion: the output only moves once the driven input pulls beyond a deadband around its last position, modelling mechanical play / relay hysteresis - it sticks then jumps, adding memory-dependent grit unlike any clip or fold.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LangevinShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LangevinShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Langevin waveshaper: shapes the signal through the Langevin function L(x)=coth(x)-1/x, the saturation curve of paramagnetic and ferrofluid magnetization - a sigmoid that approaches the rails more gently than tanh and with a softer knee, giving a smooth, magnetic-style saturation. Normalized so unity input maps to full scale at the chosen Drive.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LockhartFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LockhartFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lockhart wavefolder: the closed-form transfer curve of the Lockhart transistor folding cell (the heart of many West-Coast folders), giving smooth, analog-accurate fold harmonics rather than a piecewise reflection.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Lofi}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Lofi}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lo-fi degrader: sample-rate reduction (holds each sample for Rate frames) plus a Tone low-pass and added tape Hiss, blended via Mix. Unlike Bitcrush it quantizes in time rather than bit-depth, layering filtering and noise for a warmer vintage-sampler grime. Push Rate for aliasing; lower Tone and raise Hiss for cassette/SP-style character.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Logarithmic shaper: a log transfer curve lifts quiet detail steeply and compresses loud peaks, the opposite curvature of the exponential shaper - a bright, dense, low-level-emphasizing distortion.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LorenzDrive}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LorenzDrive}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lorenz-modulated drive: the Lorenz attractor's wandering x coordinate continuously bends the gain of a tanh saturator, so the distortion character drifts chaotically and organically as the signal passes through.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LPCWhiten}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LPCWhiten}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Linear-prediction whitener: a one-tap adaptive predictor (NLMS) continuously guesses the next sample from the last one and outputs only the prediction ERROR, so the predictable, resonant part is subtracted away and what's left is the flat, whitened, edgy residual - instant presence and de-resonance. Amount sets how much residual replaces the signal, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Magnetize}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Magnetize}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Magnetize: a saturator with magnetic HYSTERESIS - the transfer curve lags differently on the rising and falling edges, tracing a loop like tape magnetization, which adds warmth, a soft memory smear and even-order colour the way a real magnetic medium does. Drive sets the level into the loop, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MishShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MishShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mish waveshaper: the self-gated neural activation x*tanh(ln(1+e^x)) used as a transfer curve - nearly linear for loud positive signal but with a smooth non-monotonic dip just below zero, adding a soft asymmetric saturation with subtle even harmonics distinct from tanh.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MorningGlory}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MorningGlory}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

JHS Morning Glory-style transparent overdrive: a low-gain symmetric LED-clipper drive that stays open, dynamic and amp-like, cleaning up with the guitar volume - a pedalboard always-on.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MuLaw}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MuLaw}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mu-law compander: the logarithmic mu-law curve from telephone codecs squashes the dynamics nonlinearly, adding the gritty, level-dependent quantization grain of an 8-bit phone line.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Mulholland}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Mulholland}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tanh overdrive feeding a resonant Chamberlin state-variable lowpass, with a slow internal LFO wobbling the cutoff. Drive sets the input gain into the saturator (1..30), Cutoff sets the filter frequency (~80 Hz..14 kHz), Reso sets the filter Q, Mod sets how much the LFO modulates cutoff, and Mix blends wet against dry. Goes from gentle filtered grit to a screaming self-resonant squeal as Drive and Reso climb.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MultibandFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MultibandFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two-band wavefolder: a crossover splits the signal into low and high bands that are wavefolded with separate drive amounts, so the bass can stay clean while the highs shred (or vice versa) - frequency-selective folding that a full-band folder cannot do.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MultiDist}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MultiDist}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Three-band distortion: one-pole filters at 300 Hz and 3 kHz split the input into low, mid and high bands, each saturated through its own tanh stage before being summed. Low, Mid and High set the drive into each band's saturator and Mix blends the result against dry. Per-band drive lets you grind the highs without muddying the lows, or add weight to bass alone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Munge}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Munge}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Munge: a digital mangler that flips between a small bank of per-sample operations (clean, invert, full-wave rectify, sine fold) on a random clock, so the waveform is mauled into a glitchy, ever-shifting destruction. No two passes mangle the same way. Rate sets how fast the operation switches, Amount the wet level, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoiseShapeQuant}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoiseShapeQuant}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Noise-shaped quantizer: crushes the sample value to N levels but feeds the quantization error forward into the next sample, pushing the crush noise up out of the band a plain bit-crusher leaves it in.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Nroot}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Nroot}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Signed nth root: sign(x)*|x|^(1/N), which lifts low-level signal toward unity while leaving the sign intact - so quiet detail is brought up (the opposite of squaring), brightening and thickening as N rises, a continuously-tunable root-law shaper.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OctaveDivider}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OctaveDivider}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Analog-style sub-octave: a flip-flop toggles on every rising zero crossing of the input, producing a square one octave below that tracks the pitch; scaled by the input's own envelope so it follows dynamics, then blended under the dry signal for a thick sub.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OctaveFuzz}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OctaveFuzz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Octave fuzz: blends a fuzz-distorted signal with a DC-corrected full-wave-rectified upper octave, the classic Hendrix/Octavia scream. Drive sets gain, Octave the upper-octave blend and Mix dry/wet. Tracks best on clean single notes near the 12th fret; chords intermodulate, by design.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OctFuzz}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OctFuzz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Octave fuzz: blends a straight hard fuzz with a full-wave-rectified fuzz (an octave above), the classic Octavia/octave-up fuzz voice that screams an octave over the note when pushed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OverdriveOS}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OverdriveOS}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Oversampled overdrive: linearly upsamples 2x, applies tanh saturation at the higher rate and averages back down, so the alias products generated by the distortion fall mostly above the audible band - cleaner high-gain tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ParabolicSatShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ParabolicSatShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Parabolic-saturation waveshaper: passes the signal through x*(2-|x|) up to unity then hard-limits, so the approach to the rails is a smooth parabola rather than a tanh curve - a punchy soft-clip with a distinct second-harmonic-tinged knee, then a clean ceiling.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhaseBurn}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhaseBurn}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phase burn: a first-order all-pass whose coefficient is driven by the SIGNAL ITSELF (a shaped, level-dependent phase shift), so the waveform's phase smears and self-warps more as it gets louder - a frequency-dependent, dynamic phase distortion that adds a hollow, vowel-like movement and grit on transients without changing the amplitude curve. Drive scales the modulation, Amount the burn depth, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Polarize}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Polarize}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Polarize: the asymmetry of a soft clipper is slowly swept by an LFO, so the bias drifts and the generated even harmonics wax and wane over time - the tone breathing between symmetric (odd-only) and lopsided (even-rich) saturation. Rate sets the sweep, Depth the bias swing, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PowerShape}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PowerShape}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Power-law shaper: raises the signal to a variable exponent (preserving sign), so below 1 it expands quiet detail and rounds the peaks while above 1 it contracts, hollowing the body and sharpening the spikes - a continuous control over the whole transfer curve's shape from soft-and-fat to hard-and-thin. Exp sets the exponent, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PowShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PowShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Power-law shaper: raises the signal's magnitude to a variable exponent (preserving sign), sweeping the transfer curve from expanding/concave below 1 to compressing/convex above - continuous control of harmonic content.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PreisachHyst}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PreisachHyst}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Preisach hysteresis: drives a parallel bank of 16 relay hysterons - each flips to +1 above its up-threshold and to -1 below its down-threshold and latches in between - whose sum is a saturating transfer that REMEMBERS where the signal has been (the Preisach wiping-out property). Distinct from the catalog's Jiles-Atherton tape model: it gives a sticky, history-dependent grit whose loop width you set directly. Drive pushes the operating point across more relays, Width the hysteresis lag.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
