\phantomsection\label{blk:Pulverize}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Pulverize}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pulverize: the signal is run through a stack of sine wavefolders in series, each one pulverizing the last, so a clean tone is ground into a dense, buzzing cloud of high harmonics. The more stages, the finer the powder. Drive sets the input level, Stages the grind depth, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RectFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RectFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rectify-then-fold: full-wave rectification followed by triangle folding stacks octave-up rectifier content with fold harmonics for a dense, aggressive shaper unlike either stage alone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RectFuzzGate}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RectFuzzGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gated rectifier fuzz: full-wave rectification feeds a hard tanh fuzz (octave-up, even harmonics) that only opens when the envelope crosses a threshold, with a touch of crackle on the gate - a spitty, gated octave-fuzz.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rectify}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rectify}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rectifier waveshaper: full-wave (Mode 0) folds the negative half upward to add strong octave-up and even harmonics, while half-wave (Mode 1) keeps only the positive half. Mix blends with the dry signal. The classic octave-fuzz colour - aggressive alone, useful in small amounts for harmonic lift.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RectMorph}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RectMorph}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rectifier morph: sweeps continuously from half-wave to full-wave rectification, generating octave-up and even-harmonic content; dry blend keeps the fundamental.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ResonantFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ResonantFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Resonant folder: a state-variable low-pass with adjustable resonance feeds a triangle wavefolder, so the resonant peak is driven into folding and rings into a screaming formant of fold harmonics - a vocal, whistling distortion that tracks the cutoff.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RingFuzz}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RingFuzz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ring fuzz: ring-modulates the input against an internal sine, then slams the result into a hard fuzz clip, stacking clangorous inharmonic sidebands with gated fuzz spit for a destroyed, robotic tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Riot}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Riot}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Suhr Riot-style distortion: a high-gain asymmetric-clipper drive that nails a tight, saturated, amp-like high-gain rhythm and lead - JCM800-into-a-box aggression with a smooth top.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Roar}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Roar}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Feedback waveshaper (Ableton Roar-style): drives the signal through a selectable saturation curve, but wraps it in a resonant feedback loop - the distorted output is band-passed at Color and fed back into the input, so it rings, blooms and self-oscillates into the gnarly 'roar' that plain saturation can't make. Drive sets the push into the Shape (soft sine / tube / soft clip / diode / fold), Tone tilts the spectrum before the shaper, Feedback how much output returns, Color the feedback resonance frequency, and Mix the dry/wet. (Single-shaper core; the full device's serial/parallel/multiband/mid-side routings need a rack.)

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rumble}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rumble}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Techno rumble bass: the input on in0 (a kick) drives an envelope follower that swells two slightly detuned sub-sines running underneath it, so a low rolling sub locks to the kick pattern and the slow Release lets the rumble roll on between hits - the Drumcode-style rumble built by sub-under-kick rather than a reverb send. Tune sets the sub pitch, Detune beats the two oscillators against each other for movement, Release sets how long the rumble tail rolls, Drive saturates it for weight, and Mix blends the rumble under the dry input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SagClip}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SagClip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sag clipper: the clipping ceiling DROPS as the running level rises, modelling a power supply that sags under load - so sustained loud passages compress and clip harder while transients punch through before the rail collapses. A living, dynamic distortion that breathes, unlike a fixed clipper. Drive pushes it, Sag how far the rail collapses, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SampleRot}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SampleRot}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sample rot: the effective sample rate decays the longer a note sustains - the decimation deepens over a held tone so it grows progressively more aliased and gritty, then refreshes in the gaps. A time-axis companion to bit-rot, on the rate axis instead of the depth axis. Rate sets the rot speed, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Saturator}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Saturator}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Multi-mode saturator: drives the in0 signal into one of four waveshaping curves - tanh, soft tube, sigmoid or hard clip - with Tone tilting the result darker/brighter, output-level compensation, and a dry/wet Mix. A clean, controllable distortion stage distinct from the modeled fuzz/amp circuits.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Schmitt}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Schmitt}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Schmitt-trigger fuzz (a first as a synth block): a comparator with memory - the output snaps fully high once the driven input rises past +Hyst and stays high until it falls past -Hyst, squaring the signal into a hard two-level wave that resists chattering near zero. The hysteresis band makes it 'stick', cleaning the buzz of a raw square-up while adding a gated, brutal edge. Drive sets the gain into the trigger, Hyst the dead-band width, Level the output, Mix the blend. A hysteretic square fuzz no clipper or fold reproduces.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SergeFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SergeFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Serge wave multiplier: six folding cells at staggered offsets sum into the dense, bright, vocal-formant-like spectrum of a Serge VCM, a richer fold than a single threshold reflection.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Shaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Shaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

SynthMaster-style 12-mode waveshaper: Shape selects soft/hard clip, tube, diode, sine and triangle fold, full/half rectify, sine, crush, sync-wrap and Chebyshev modes, each driven by Drive with Bias for symmetry. Mix and Output blend and trim. Modulate Shape to morph between distortion characters - a one-stop tone-shaping distortion.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShotNoise}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShotNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shot noise: adds grit whose loudness follows the PHYSICAL shot-noise law - its amplitude scales with the square root of the instantaneous signal level (sqrt(|x|)), the way photon/electron shot noise grows with current. So the noise tracks the signal's contour instead of sitting flat: a self-masking, signal-dependent fizz that disappears in the gaps. Amount sets the noise gain, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SignedSquare}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SignedSquare}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Signed square: sign(x)*x^2, which squares the magnitude while keeping the sign - so loud parts are pushed louder and quiet parts quieter (a downward expander curve) without the octave-up of a plain square, adding odd-harmonic edge as it expands.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SinhExciter}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SinhExciter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sinh exciter: shapes the signal through sinh(Drive*x)/sinh(Drive), an expanding transfer curve that is the mirror image of tanh saturation - instead of squashing peaks it pushes mid and high levels outward, exaggerating dynamics and adding bright odd harmonics. A de-compressing exciter rather than a soft-clipper. Normalized so unity input stays unity.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sizzle}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sizzle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sizzle: an HF exciter that generates fizzy new top end from the signal's OWN high frequencies (it shapes the highpassed content, so it only sizzles where there is already brightness, never on bass). Adds air, presence and a metallic crackle. Drive sets the harmonic heat, Amount the added sizzle, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlewChew}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlewChew}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slew chew: a slew limiter with DIFFERENT rise and fall rate caps, so the waveform is chewed asymmetrically - sharp on one edge, rounded on the other - bending the shape and pumping in even harmonics, the lopsided cousin of a symmetric slew. Rise sets the up-rate, Fall the down-rate, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlewClip}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlewClip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slew-rate distortion (a first as a synth block): limits how fast the output can move between samples, so when Drive pushes the input past that maximum slope the block cannot keep up - fast transients are sheared into ramps and the waveform is dragged toward a buzzing triangle. Where a clipper flattens the peaks, SlewClip mangles the slopes: a harsh, gritty, distinctly digital lo-fi grind that also drops level as it limits. Rate sets the maximum step per sample, Drive the input gain, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlewCrush}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlewCrush}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slew + crush: a slew-rate limiter rounds the fast edges, then a noise-shaped quantizer drops the bit depth - the combined softening and grit of a cheap converter feeding a slow line.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlewFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlewFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tracking wavefolder: the fold threshold slews toward the signal level, so the fold density pumps and breathes with dynamics rather than staying fixed - a self-adjusting fold.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlewFuzz}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlewFuzz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slew-then-fuzz: a slew-rate limiter rounds the fast edges (program-dependent triangular softening) before a hot tanh fuzz, so the fuzz bites on sustained energy but the rounded transients keep it from fizzing - a thick, controlled gnarl.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlewShape}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlewShape}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slew-rate distortion: separate rise and fall step limits soften fast edges asymmetrically (TIM-style), generating triangular, program-dependent harmonics; mix blends the slewed and dry signals.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlewStep}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlewStep}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slew step: quantizes to a coarse staircase but then GLIDES toward each new step at a limited rate instead of jumping, so you get the lo-fi stepping of a bit-crusher with smooth ramps between treads - a soft, gooey digital quantization rather than a hard one. Steps sets the resolution, Slew the glide rate, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlopeOverload}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlopeOverload}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slope overload: a slew-rate-limited follower that can only change so fast, so when the input moves quicker than the limit it lags behind and overshoots - the slope-overload and granular-noise distortion of a delta / CVSD modulator. Slow signals stay clean, fast ones tear. Slew sets the rate limit, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SmoothClamp}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SmoothClamp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Smooth clamp: passes the signal linearly until it reaches the Knee, then smoothly tanh-saturates the excess toward the +/-1 rails - a soft-knee limiter with a genuinely flat (undistorted) centre, distinct from a plain tanh that curves everywhere.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Snarl}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Snarl}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Snarl: a high-resonance band-pass parked in the low-mids feeds a saturator, so the signal grows a vicious vocal-formant growl - a snarling, throaty bite midway between a wah stuck in place and a fuzz. Freq sets where it snarls, Drive the aggression, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SoftplusShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SoftplusShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Softplus waveshaper: the centred softplus ln(1+e^x)-ln2 as a transfer curve - it passes positive signal almost linearly but smoothly compresses negative excursions toward a floor, an asymmetric soft half-wave shaper rich in even harmonics (octave-up colour).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SoftsignShaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SoftsignShaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Softsign waveshaper: the rational sigmoid x/(1+|x|), a soft clipper that saturates far more gradually than tanh and never quite reaches the rails - a smooth, gentle overdrive with a softer knee and longer transition than transcendental saturators.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SovtekMuff}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SovtekMuff}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sovtek (Green Russian) Big Muff-style fuzz: cascaded transistor gain into two symmetric silicon-diode clipping stages for the thick, smooth, mid-scooped wall of sustain - darker and bassier than the US Muff.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SpectralClip}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SpectralClip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Band clipper: splits the signal into low and high bands and clips ONLY the highs, leaving the lows clean - so you get crunchy, distorted top end riding over an undistorted, solid bottom, the inverse of the usual full-range fuzz that turns bass to mush. Drive sets the high-band clip, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StochasticResonance}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StochasticResonance}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stochastic resonance: the counter-intuitive effect where adding NOISE to a weak, sub-threshold signal HELPS it through a nonlinearity instead of burying it. The signal drives a bistable double-well system (x' = Barrier*x - x^3 + Drive*in + noise) that hops between its two states; a tuned amount of noise makes those hops follow the weak input, recovering and amplifying detail a gate or expander would simply cut. Too little noise and nothing crosses, too much and it is random - the sweet spot in between is the resonance, so SWEEP Noise to find it. A gritty, alive texture/recovery tool with no equivalent in the catalog. Drive scales the input into the wells, Noise the assist level, Barrier the well depth/threshold, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SubEnhance}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SubEnhance}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sub-bass enhancer: a flip-flop toggled per rising zero crossing generates a half-frequency square tracked by the input envelope and low-passed, added under the dry signal at Sub. Reinforces weak low end on kicks, bass and 808s. Monophonic by nature - best on clean low-register sources.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Subharmonic}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Subharmonic}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Subharmonic generator: zero-crossing detection on the input drives a divide-by-four flip-flop, multiplying the input's rectified amplitude to synthesize a tone two octaves below, then smooths it with a one-pole low-pass. Sub sets the level of the generated sub, Tone opens the low-pass cutoff (darker to brighter sub), and Mix sums the sub onto the dry signal. Adds weight and low-end body to bass, kicks and leads.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TanhLadder}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TanhLadder}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cascaded tanh stages: several tanh gain stages in series compound their soft saturation, building the progressive, harmonically dense overdrive of a multi-stage tube preamp rather than a single clip.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TapeMachine}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TapeMachine}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tape machine: combines soft tape saturation, a slow wow pitch wobble via a modulated delay, and a hint of hiss into one block - the glued, warm, slightly-unstable character of analog tape.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TapeSat}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TapeSat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tape-style saturation: a soft tanh stage with a touch of linear blend emulates magnetic-tape compression, with Warmth rolling off the highs for a rounder tone and Mix blending dry/wet. Gentle glue and harmonic thickening rather than overt distortion. See the Studer and Ampex analog models for machine-specific tape.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TiltDistort}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TiltDistort}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tilt-weighted saturation: the spectrum is tilted before a tanh stage and tilted back after, so the distortion bites hardest on the boosted band - dial Tilt positive to fizz only the highs, negative to grind only the lows, while the overall balance is restored.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TransformerSat}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TransformerSat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Transformer saturation: a hysteresis loop (the output lags and remembers, like a magnetized iron core) plus soft clipping models an audio transformer's low-end thickening and gentle history-dependent distortion.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TransientExciter}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TransientExciter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Transient exciter: harmonics are added ONLY during attacks - a fast/slow envelope split detects each onset and injects bright odd harmonics that fade as the note settles, so picks, hits and consonants get crisp bite while sustains stay clean. The opposite of smearing. Amount sets the bite, Sharp the harmonic edge, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TransientFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TransientFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Transient-keyed folder: a fast-minus-slow envelope detector measures the attack of each event and opens a wavefolder only on those transients, so the body of a sound stays clean while every hit cracks with a burst of fold harmonics - percussive brightening, not sustained grind.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TriState}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TriState}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tri-state quantizer: collapses the signal to just three levels - full positive, full negative, or zero inside a central dead band - so it squares up like an infinite clipper but with a silent gap around zero, giving a gated, telegraph-like buzz. Dead sets the size of the zero zone, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TriToSine}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TriToSine}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Triangle-to-sine shaper: the soft-clipping transfer that classic analog VCOs use to round a triangle into a near-perfect sine, useful for warming any signal or deriving a sine from a triangle core.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tube}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tube}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tube-style saturation: an asymmetric transfer curve (offset by Bias) adds the even and odd harmonics of a valve stage, tamed by a Tone low-pass and blended via Mix. Warmer and rounder than the harder Drive/Waveshaper - subtle settings thicken and glue, higher Drive gives singing tube grit. See the TubePre and VariMu analog models for circuit-specific versions.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tumnus}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tumnus}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wampler Tumnus-style transparent boost/overdrive (Klon-lineage): a clean op-amp boost summed with a mild germanium-diode clip stage for a touch of grit while keeping the dry signal intact - the famous transparent mid-push.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TurboRAT}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TurboRAT}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

ProCo Turbo RAT-style distortion: a high-gain op-amp stage into hard LED clipping for a sharper, louder, more aggressive RAT with a fierce midrange bite.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VHS}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VHS}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cassette/VHS tape emulation: the input runs through a short modulated delay whose time wobbles via an internal 0.7 Hz LFO, then a one-pole low-pass rolls off highs and random pseudo-noise hiss is summed in. Wow sets the LFO delay-modulation depth (pitch wobble), Hiss scales the added noise level, and Dropout raises the probability of brief signal dropouts driven by a per-sample random envelope. Mix blends the degraded signal against the dry input for subtle aging or full lo-fi destruction.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Vinyl}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Vinyl}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vinyl character that reads the signal through a slowly modulated delay for wow pitch-drift, injects random impulses for surface crackle, and pushes the result through a tanh saturator for groove distortion. Wow scales the pitch-drift depth, Crackle sets how often surface pops appear, Drive sets the saturation amount, and Mix sets dry/wet. Use it to age a clean source with turntable wobble, dust noise and warm grit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VirtualBass}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VirtualBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Virtual bass / missing-fundamental enhancer: isolates the sub-bass band, rectifies it to synthesize a harmonic series (2f, 3f, 4f...) of the fundamental, then mixes those upper-bass harmonics back with the dry signal. The ear fuses the harmonics into the residue pitch of the absent fundamental, so a small speaker that cannot reproduce 50 Hz still seems to - the MaxxBass/psychoacoustic-bass principle. Distinct from a sub-octave generator, which adds a lower octave the speaker still cannot play.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveCrush}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveCrush}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crush-then-fold: a low-bit quantizer's staircase is fed straight into a triangle folder, so the quantization steps themselves get folded - a layered digital-plus-fold timbre neither stage makes alone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Wavefolder}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Wavefolder}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

West-coast wavefolder that multiplies the signal by Fold and reflects it through triangle folding boundaries, adding bright harmonics that bloom as level rises. Bias shifts the fold symmetry for asymmetric timbres, Mix blends dry/wet and Output trims gain. Unlike clipping, folding keeps energy moving, so the tone stays lively even at extreme settings.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveMirror}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveMirror}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Reflecting waveshaper: excursions past a slowly DC-tracked threshold are mirrored back down by an adjustable amount, folding ring-like overtones that recenter as the signal's bias drifts.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveMix}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveMix}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Blended waveshaper: one knob crossfades the transfer curve from soft tanh saturation to sinusoidal folding, sweeping the harmonic character from warm clip to bright fold in a single stage.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Wavemult}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Wavemult}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

West-coast wave multiplier (Serge/Buchla-style): cascades several triangle wavefolders so the signal is folded again and again as Drive rises, multiplying the harmonics far beyond a single Wavefolder for ringing, metallic, formant-rich timbres. Drive sets the fold depth, Stages the number of cascaded folders (more = denser), Bias the fold asymmetry, and Mix the dry/wet blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Waveset}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Waveset}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio In}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Waveset distortion (Trevor Wishart, 'Audible Design'): slices the in0 signal into wavesets - the segments between upward zero-crossings - and plays each one Repeat times before moving on, dropping the input in between. Repeating every waveset time-stretches and drops the pitch in gritty, vowel-like steps that track the signal's own period, not a fixed grid - the classic granular-but-pitch-synchronous Wishart sound. Repeat sets how many times each waveset loops, Mix blends dry/wet. in0 = Audio In.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveshapeMorph}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveshapeMorph}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Morphing waveshaper: one knob crossfades the drive curve from a tanh saturator (odd harmonics, soft clip) to a sine wavefolder (dense fold harmonics), sweeping continuously from warm overdrive to bright metallic folding on the same input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Waveshaper}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Waveshaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Memoryless waveshaper with four transfer curves (Shape: tanh, atan, cubic soft-clip, sine fold) driven by Drive and blended via Mix. Each curve adds a different harmonic signature, from smooth saturation (tanh/atan) to a foldback character (sine). Lighter-weight than Drive when you just want one fixed shaping curve.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveWrap}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveWrap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sinusoidal wavefolder: drives the signal through sin() so that increasing index folds it through multiple sine lobes (Serge-style), staying perfectly bounded while adding rich symmetric harmonics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:XformerSat}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{XformerSat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Transformer saturation: asymmetric soft-clipping knees (different for positive and negative swings) add even-harmonic colour, plus a low-passed component for iron-core low-end thickening. Subtle weight and warmth on bass, drums and the mix bus. See the NevePre analog model for a transformer-preamp variant.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:XorFold}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{XorFold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

XOR fold: bit-wise exclusive-ORs the current sample against a delayed copy of itself, a digital comb that carves jagged, metallic, glitch-ridden notches no analog filter can make - the harmonics interfere at the BIT level rather than the amplitude level. Time sets the delay, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZenerClamp}\blockicon{amp}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZenerClamp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zener clamp: a voltage-reference clamp that limits asymmetrically - a soft forward knee one way, a harder reverse-breakdown wall the other - so the waveform is squashed lopsidedly the way a Zener-diode protection circuit clips, adding strong even harmonics. Forward sets the soft side, Reverse the breakdown wall, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZeroCrossGlitch}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZeroCrossGlitch}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zero-cross sample-hold: tracks the signed peak within each half-cycle and freezes the output to it until the next zero crossing, rebuilding the waveform as flat plateaus that snap on every crossing - a PWM-like, octave-rich square reconstruction.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZeroCrush}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZeroCrush}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DST]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zero-cross sample-hold: latches the input value at each rising zero crossing and holds it until the next, reducing the waveform to one stair-step per pitch period - a pitch-synced lo-fi staircase.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Dynamics (63)

\phantomsection\label{blk:AmpHold}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AmpHold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Peak-hold envelope: captures the loudest recent peak and holds it, then decays slowly - a control-rate 'freeze the loudness' source for driving other parameters, distinct from a plain release-only follower.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AsymComp}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AsymComp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Asymmetric compressor: independent envelope followers level the positive and negative half-waves with separate ratios, so unequal polarity gains introduce even harmonics and a tube-like asymmetry on top of the leveling - part compressor, part saturator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AutoGain}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AutoGain}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Automatic gain control: tracks the signal's average level and slowly scales it toward a target, evening out long-term loudness swings (quiet passages come up, loud ones come down) the way a broadcast leveler does.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Choke}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Choke}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Choke gate: every new onset slams the gain shut for a brief window, killing whatever tail was still ringing - the hi-hat choke, applied to anything. Successive hits clamp each other off into a tight, staccato, gasping rhythm. Time sets the choke window, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CompProg}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CompProg}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Program-dependent compressor: blends a fast and a slow release envelope so brief peaks recover quickly while sustained loud passages release slowly, the auto-release behaviour of classic bus compressors - transparent glue.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Compressor}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Compressor}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Peak-following compressor: tracks the input envelope with Attack/Release ballistics and, above Threshold, reduces gain by the set Ratio, with Makeup to restore level. Fast attack tames transients while slow release glues sustained material. A clean general-purpose dynamics stage - see the analog pack (FET, Opto, VariMu) for coloured compression.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CompUpward}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CompUpward}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Upward compressor: instead of pulling loud parts down, it pushes signal below the threshold up, raising low-level detail and ambience - density and presence without limiting the peaks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ContrastEnv}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ContrastEnv}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Amplitude contrast: a power curve applied to the signal's envelope around a reference level pushes loud parts louder and quiet parts quieter (positive) or evens them out (negative), preserving waveform shape.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrestFactor}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrestFactor}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crest-factor follower: tracks the running peak-to-RMS ratio of the input (about 1.4 for a steady sine, ~1 for a square, high for spiky/transient or sparse material) - a control signal measuring how 'peaky' the dynamics are, for transient-aware modulation or auto-dynamics. Time sets the averaging window.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DeEsser}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DeEsser}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tames sibilance by splitting off a high band with a one-pole low-pass, tracking its level with a fast-attack envelope, and applying gain reduction to that band only when it crosses the threshold before recombining it with the lows. Freq sets the sibilance band, Threshold sets where reduction begins, Amount scales how hard the high band is pulled down, and Mix sets dry/wet. Because only the highs are compressed, it removes harsh esses without dulling the rest of the signal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Deflate}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Deflate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Deflate: the opposite of an inflator - a cubic curve that sucks the body out of low-level signal so quiet detail shrinks back and the sound feels smaller, thinner and pulled-in. Use it to deflate an over-full source or for a claustrophobic effect. Amount sets the deflation, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DeSibilance}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DeSibilance}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

De-esser: detects energy in a high band (the 's' and 't' sounds) and ducks only that band when it exceeds a threshold, taming harsh sibilance on vocals without dulling the whole signal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Ducker}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Ducker}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Sidechain}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sidechain ducker that follows the level of the key input (in 2) with a release-smoothed envelope and pulls down the main signal whenever that key exceeds the threshold. Threshold sets the key level that triggers ducking, Amount sets how far the main signal drops, and Release sets how quickly it recovers once the key falls back. Use it for music-under-voice ducking or rhythmic pumping driven by a separate trigger.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DynEQ}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DynEQ}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dynamic EQ band: an SVF band-pass at Freq/Q tracks an envelope follower, and when that band's energy crosses Threshold the band is subtracted from the signal in proportion to Amount. Freq and Q place and narrow the band, Threshold sets where reduction begins, and Mix blends the result against dry. It only acts when the band gets loud, so it tames resonances and harshness without static EQ cuts.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EntropyGate}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EntropyGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Entropy gate: measures zero-crossing density (how noisy vs tonal the signal is) and gates on it, so you can pass tone and mute hiss/noise, or the reverse - a timbre-keyed gate, not a level gate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EnvClip}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EnvClip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Adaptive clipper: the soft-clip ceiling follows the signal envelope, so the amount of clipping stays proportional as levels change - consistent grit from quiet to loud passages instead of a fixed wall.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Envelope}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Envelope}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Envelope follower that rectifies the input and smooths it with independent Attack and Release times into a control signal. Gain scales the output for driving a filter cutoff, VCA or any mod input (auto-wah, ducking, dynamics). Fast attack tracks transients; a longer release gives a smoother contour.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EnvFollow}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EnvFollow}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Envelope follower: tracks the rectified amplitude of the input with separate Attack and Release times, outputting a smooth control signal that rises quickly on transients and falls slowly back - the standard way to turn audio into a modulation source. Patch its output into any parameter's mod input so a filter, VCA or effect responds to playing dynamics; Gain scales the result. Unlike the duckers (which bury the follower inside one effect) this exposes the envelope itself.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EnvGateAR}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EnvGateAR}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: In A, In B}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gated AR envelope VCA: a second-input gate opens an attack/release envelope that shapes the audio input's level - a self-contained note-on amplitude envelope without a full synth voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EnvSlope}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EnvSlope}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Envelope slope: outputs the per-sample rate of change of the amplitude envelope - strongly positive on attacks, negative on decays, near zero on steady sustain - a bipolar onset/transient detector and dynamics-derived modulation source. Gain scales the (small) derivative to a usable range.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EnvSwell}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EnvSwell}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Auto volume swell: a slow-attack envelope suppresses each note's initial transient and fades the body in (the violin bow-swell / no-pick effect), with no pedal or LFO.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Expander}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Expander}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Downward expander: attenuates signal that falls below Threshold by the set Ratio (the inverse of a compressor), tightening quiet passages and reducing bleed and hiss between notes. Attack/Release set the ballistics. Gentler than a gate - it fades low-level material rather than hard-muting it.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FeedbackCompressor}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FeedbackCompressor}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Feedback-topology compressor: the level detector watches the already-compressed output instead of the input (the classic 1176/opto behavior), so the gain reduction is self-referential - this softens the knee, makes the effective ratio program-dependent, and gives the smoother, 'rounder' character feedback designs are prized for. Threshold, Ratio, Attack/Release in ms, Makeup.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Gate}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Gate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Noise gate: when the detected level falls below Threshold it attenuates the signal toward Range (the floor), opening and closing with Attack/Release ballistics. Range at -80 dB fully mutes between events; smaller values leave a residual for gentler ducking. Use it to silence hiss and bleed between phrases or for rhythmic gating.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GateBounce}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GateBounce}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Contact bounce: when the gate opens it chatters - a few fast random on/off bounces before it settles, the way a mechanical relay or switch contact bounces on closing. Adds a glitchy, machine-gun stutter to every note onset. Bounce sets how much it chatters, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HystGate}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HystGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hysteresis gate: separate open and (lower) close thresholds give Schmitt-trigger immunity to chatter near the threshold, with a smoothed gain ramp so the gate fades rather than clicks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Inflate}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Inflate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Inflate: a psychoacoustic loudness exciter - a gentle cubic curve that lifts the body and harmonics of the signal so it sounds bigger and louder without raising the peak level. The 'inflator' move for making a mix feel fuller. Amount sets the inflation, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Limiter}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Limiter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brick-wall peak limiter: instantly clamps gain so peaks never exceed Threshold, then releases smoothly back to unity over Release, with Output trimming the post-limit level. Set Threshold as the ceiling and drive the input for transparent loudness. See Maximizer for loudness-optimized limiting.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LookaheadLimit}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LookaheadLimit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lookahead limiter: the signal is delayed through a short buffer while the gain is computed from the (still-undelayed) incoming peak, so the gain reduction is already in place when the peak reaches the output - catching transients a feed-forward limiter would overshoot. Release sets the recovery, Thresh the ceiling.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MaskGate}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MaskGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Temporal masking gate: a loud event briefly DEAFENS the block to what follows, modelling forward auditory masking - quiet detail in the wake of a transient is pushed down (the ear can't hear it anyway), tightening busy material and emphasising the hits. Distinct from a normal gate: the threshold is set by the recent loudest peak, not a fixed level. Amount sets the masking depth, Hold the masking time, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Maximizer}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Maximizer}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Loudness maximizer that applies a gain boost, tracks the peak envelope, and divides back any sample exceeding the ceiling before hard-clamping to it, acting as a brick-wall limiter. Boost sets how hard the input is driven up and Ceiling sets the absolute output peak in dB. Push Boost while keeping Ceiling near 0 dB to raise apparent loudness without overshooting the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MBDynamics}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MBDynamics}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Multiband dynamics processor that splits the input into low, mid and high bands (crossovers near 250 Hz and 2.5 kHz) and applies independent compression and gating to each. Threshold sets the level above which a band is compressed at Ratio, while Gate sets the level below which a band is attenuated downward. Mix blends the processed sum against the dry signal, useful for tightening or de-noising specific frequency regions at once.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MultiComp}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MultiComp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Three-band compressor: crossovers split the signal into low, mid and high bands (Low/Mid and Mid/High), each compressed independently against a shared Threshold and Ratio with fast-attack/slow-release ballistics, then summed with Makeup. Tame a busy mix's bands separately - control boomy lows without dulling the highs. See the SSL/dbx analog models for coloured bus compression.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MultiCompress}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MultiCompress}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two-band compressor: splits the signal at a crossover and compresses the low and high bands independently before recombining, so a loud low end stops pumping the highs (and vice versa) - the core of multiband dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoiseGate}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoiseGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Noise gate: passes the in0 audio only while its level is above Threshold, otherwise silences it - cleans up hiss, bleed and tails between notes. A peak follower with a Release time smoothly opens and closes the gate so it doesn't chatter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoiseGateChop}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoiseGateChop}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chopping gate: while the input sits above threshold it passes clean, but as it falls below the gate chops it with a fast square on/off instead of fading - so decaying tails break into a rhythmic glitchy stutter rather than smoothly muting. Rate sets the chop speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoiseGateHF}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoiseGateHF}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

High-frequency-keyed gate: triggered by high-band energy (a one-pole high-pass detector), it clamps hiss and cymbal bleed without choking sustained low-frequency tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Omnipressor}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Omnipressor}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Extreme dynamics on one knob: Function sweeps from gating expansion through limiting compression to full dynamic reversal, where loud passages turn quiet and quiet turns loud.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OTT}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OTT}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Over-the-top multiband compressor (Xfer OTT-style): splits the signal into low / mid / high bands and applies BOTH downward and upward compression to each at once - loud parts are pulled down and quiet detail is pushed up - for the dense, controlled, minimal-dynamic-range sound on modern EDM / dubstep / future-bass synths and drums. Depth sets how hard the up + down compression bites (the OTT % knob), Time the attack/release across all bands (fast = tight, slow = more transient), Low / Mid / High trim each band's output in dB, and Mix blends against dry. Unlike MultiComp / MBDynamics this also compresses upward (raises the quiet parts), which is what gives OTT its signature.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ParallelComp}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ParallelComp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Parallel (New York) compression: a heavily-compressed copy is blended back UNDER the dry signal, so the transients and peaks stay intact while the body and low-level detail are lifted - punch and density without squashing the dynamics (the drum-bus / mix-bus trick). Distinct from a normal compressor, which replaces the signal: here the crush sits in parallel. Threshold/Ratio set the parallel crush, Attack/Release its ballistics, Makeup the parallel gain, Blend how much of the compressed copy is mixed under the dry.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PeakLimiter}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PeakLimiter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Peak limiter: gain drops instantly whenever the signal would exceed the ceiling and recovers gradually, holding output below a hard threshold without the slow ratio behaviour of a compressor - transparent loudness control.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PercentileGate}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PercentileGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Percentile gate: the threshold is not fixed - it slowly tracks the signal's own loud peaks, so the gate passes only the top fraction of dynamics and adapts as the material gets louder or softer over time. Self-calibrating, unlike a fixed-dB gate. Open sets how much of the top passes, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Pump}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Pump}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tempo-synced sidechain-style volume pump that ducks the input each cycle and recovers using a sample-clock phase for coherent timing. Rate sets the duck frequency in Hz, Depth sets how far the level drops, and Curve shapes the recovery ramp from snappy to gradual. Mix blends the pumped signal against the dry input for the classic EDM ducking effect without an external sidechain.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RateGate}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RateGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slew gate: opens on MOTION, not loudness - it passes signal only while its rate of change (slew) is high, so fast transients and bright content get through while slow, steady tones are gated out. A movement-sensitive gate that does the opposite of a sustain pedal: keeps the attacks, drops the drones. Threshold sets the motion needed, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RmsComp}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RmsComp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

RMS compressor: detects level with a root-mean-square window instead of peaks, so gain reduction follows perceived loudness smoothly and ignores brief spikes - the gentle, musical compression of classic RMS-detector units.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sidechain}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sidechain}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: In A, In B}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sidechain ducker: the audio level is pulled down in proportion to a second input's envelope, the pumping kick-ducks-bass / broadcast voiceover effect, driven by an external key.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlewGate}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlewGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Self-adjusting gate: the threshold slews toward a slow running average of the level, so the gate auto-tracks a drifting noise floor and opens only on signal that rises above its own recent average.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SoftExpand}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SoftExpand}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Soft downward expander: below the threshold the gain falls off by the set ratio (smoothly, not a hard gate), widening dynamic range and pushing low-level noise/bleed down without chopping.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SoftKnee}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SoftKnee}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Soft-knee compressor: a smooth knee region around the threshold eases gain reduction from 1:1 into the set ratio, so compression blends in gradually instead of switching hard at the threshold.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
