\phantomsection\label{blk:SoftThreshold}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SoftThreshold}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Soft thresholding: the wavelet-denoising shrinkage operator sign(x)*max(|x|-T,0), which subtracts the threshold from the magnitude rather than hard-gating - so signal below T is removed and signal above is passed but pulled down by T, a continuous deadband distinct from a hard noise gate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Splat}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Splat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Splat: a transient that crosses the threshold briefly blows the gain into hard saturation, then recovers - so big hits 'splat' with a momentary overdriven smear before settling clean, the overshoot artifact of a slamming converter or a maxed preamp. Thresh sets the trigger, Amount the splat heat, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Squash}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Squash}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Aggressive parallel smash: a fast peak-following envelope drives heavy gain reduction above a fixed threshold, and the gain-reduced signal is pushed through a tanh saturator for crushed, distorted dynamics. Amount steepens the gain-reduction curve (harder squash), Tone scales the saturated output level, and Mix blends the smashed signal in parallel with the dry input. Useful for adding punch and grit to drums and bus material.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SubBoost}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SubBoost}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sub-bass synthesizer: isolates the low band and drives it through a soft nonlinearity to regenerate and reinforce the fundamental, adding felt low end on small speakers without just boosting EQ.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThyratronZap}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThyratronZap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Thyratron latch: a gas-tube trigger that FIRES when the input crosses a strike level and stays latched on - passing full signal - until the level falls below a lower hold point, then snaps off. A latching, hysteretic on/off zap that holds a note open after a hit. Strike sets the fire level, Hold the drop-out level, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Transient}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Transient}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Transient shaper: a fast vs slow envelope follower separates the attack (fast > slow) from the sustain (fast < slow), letting Attack and Sustain each be boosted or cut independently, with Output trimming the result. Add punch to drums, or tighten/lengthen sustain - all independent of input level, unlike a compressor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TransientDesign}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TransientDesign}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Transient designer: a fast and a slow envelope track the signal; their difference isolates the attack while the slow part is the sustain, so you can punch up or soften each independently - add snap or glue without a threshold.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TransientGate}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TransientGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Transient-only gate: passes the signal while the fast envelope outruns the slow one (the attack) and mutes the sustain - isolates clicks / picks / hits, the inverse of a sustain ducker.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TransientSculpt}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TransientSculpt}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Transient designer: a fast and a slow envelope follower track the signal; their difference is the attack and the slow one is the body, letting you boost or cut the punch and the sustain of a sound independently.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TransientSplit}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TransientSplit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Transient designer: a fast minus slow envelope isolates attack vs sustain energy, scaled independently so you can sharpen or soften hits and swell or dampen tails without a threshold.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TruePeakLimit}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TruePeakLimit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

True-peak limiter: estimates the inter-sample peak with a 4-point band-limited reconstruction of the midpoint between samples and limits against that, catching the over-0 dBFS overshoots a sample-peak limiter misses and that clip a downstream converter (the ITU-R BS.1770 true-peak concern). Output is one sample delayed so the gain aligns with the reconstructed peak - an inter-sample-aware ceiling, distinct from a plain lookahead limiter. Ceiling sets the ISP ceiling, Release the recovery.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:UpComp}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{UpComp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Upward compressor that tracks the input envelope and, when it sits below the threshold, applies makeup gain proportional to how far below it is and to the ratio, lifting quiet passages toward the threshold. Threshold sets the level below which boosting begins, Ratio sets how aggressively the deficit is filled, Makeup adds a fixed output gain, and Mix sets dry/wet. Use it to raise low-level detail and tighten dynamic range from the bottom up rather than taming peaks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VarianceGate}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VarianceGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Variance gate: opens not on loudness but on COMPLEXITY - it tracks the short-term variance (the energy of the fluctuation around the local mean) and passes only when the signal is busy/noisy, muting steady tones and DC-like calm. A texture-sensitive gate. Threshold sets the activity needed, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZeroCrossGate}\blockicon{dynamics}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZeroCrossGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[DYN]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zero-cross gate: a noise gate that only ever opens or closes AT a zero crossing of the signal, so it never produces the click a normal gate makes when it slams shut mid-waveform. The gate decision is latched at each crossing from the envelope vs Threshold. Click-free gating for harsh, percussive, or already-distorted material. Threshold sets the open level, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Effect (51)

\phantomsection\label{blk:AMRadio}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AMRadio}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

AM-radio lo-fi: band-limits in0 to a tinny midrange, adds a faint carrier hum and a wash of static, the sound of a distant transistor radio. Band sets the midrange tightness, Static the noise level, Mix the blend. in0 = Audio.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Avalanche}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Avalanche}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Avalanche: a feedback line whose gain briefly exceeds unity after a loud trigger, so the sound builds on itself into a swelling roar before a tanh ceiling and a decay tame it back down - a self-amplifying cascade that snowballs and settles. Trigger sets the trip level, Decay the avalanche length, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BarkhausenBuzz}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BarkhausenBuzz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Barkhausen buzz: magnetic-domain jumps emit a crackle whose intensity tracks the RATE of magnetization change (dB/dt) - so fast-moving signal hisses and buzzes with domain noise while steady levels stay quiet. The audible signature of a magnetizing core. Amount sets the buzz level, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Boomerang}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Boomerang}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boomerang: a reverse delay that catches the signal and throws it back REVERSED through a sliding window, with feedback so each throw comes back again - swelling, backwards-shimmer echoes that arc out and return. Time sets the window length, Feedback how many returns, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClothRustle}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClothRustle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cloth rustle: soft high-frequency noise bursts triggered by how fast the signal MOVES, like fabric brushing past a microphone - it whispers and rustles on motion and falls silent when the signal holds still. Amount sets the rustle level, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Creak}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Creak}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Creak: a stress-driven stick-slip squeak - the input level winds up tension that periodically slips, sweeping a thin resonant tone upward in little jerks, like a creaking door, rope or floorboard. The louder you push, the faster and higher it creaks. Tune sets the squeak pitch, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Crumble}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Crumble}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crumble: the longer a note sustains the more of its samples drop out and hold, so a held tone progressively crumbles into a stuttering, gap-riddled fragment - then knits back together in the silences. Rate sets the crumble onset, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Crystallize}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Crystallize}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crystallize: each transient seeds a high shimmering partial locked to a tuned pitch grid (so the sparkle is always musical, never random), ringing out and accreting into a crystalline upper layer. Tune sets the grid root, Amount the shimmer level, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DiodeClipper}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DiodeClipper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Diode clipper: a symmetric pair of clipping diodes giving a soft-knee crunch that rounds into hard saturation as it's pushed - the core of countless distortion pedals. Drive sets the gain, Bias skews the symmetry toward even harmonics, Level the output. in0 = Audio.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Dropout2}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Dropout2}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tape dropout: random brief mutes, as if the oxide flaked off the tape - the signal cuts out for a few milliseconds at random, with a quick fade so it ticks rather than clicks. Sparse and subtle for vintage wear, dense for a glitchy, failing-media stutter. Rate sets how often dropouts happen, Length their duration, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Foldback}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Foldback}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Foldback distortion: instead of clipping, any signal past the threshold is reflected back on itself, generating bright, ring-mod-like upper harmonics that grow chaotically with drive - harsher and more digital than soft clipping. Drive sets the level into the fold, Thresh the fold point, Level the output. in0 = Audio.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FreqShifter}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FreqShifter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Frequency shifter: builds an approximate quadrature (Hilbert) pair of the input through two allpass paths and ring-modulates them with a complex exponential, shifting every partial by a fixed Hz amount (not a pitch ratio) - the inharmonic, Bode-style shifter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GeigerNoise}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GeigerNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Geiger counter: emits sharp random CLICKS whose density tracks the input level - loud input is a frantic crackle of clicks, quiet input an occasional tick, like a Geiger counter responding to radiation. Each click is a fast-decaying impulse fired by a Poisson process whose rate is driven by |signal|. A novel level-to-texture sonifier. Sensitivity sets the click rate, Decay the click length, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GravelCrunch}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GravelCrunch}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gravel crunch: dense bursts of low-passed noise grains gated by the signal level, like boots grinding on gravel - the more signal, the more it crunches underneath. A grounded, gritty texture rather than a hiss. Density sets the crunch amount, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Groan}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Groan}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Groan: the input level wakes a deep, slowly wavering sub-tone that moans underneath the signal - a wobbling low resonance whose pitch drifts on a slow random LFO, adding a creaking, living, beast-like undertone to drones and pads. Tune sets the groan pitch, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:IntegrateDump}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{IntegrateDump}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Integrate-and-dump: the input is accumulated into a bucket and, the moment the bucket overflows past Threshold, it DUMPS its whole contents as one spike and resets to zero. Loud or sustained input fills it fast (rapid spikes), quiet input fills it slowly (sparse spikes) - a charge-pump rhythm generator whose pulse rate tracks the signal energy. Rate sets the fill speed, Threshold the dump level, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Ionize}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Ionize}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ionize: transients ignite bursts of high, crackling electric sparks - bandpassed noise gated into random arcs, like a plasma discharge or a Tesla coil snapping. Distinct from a tonal sparkle: this is pure electric noise. Amount sets the arc level, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JitterClock}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JitterClock}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clock jitter: the sampling instant is dithered by a tiny random delay each sample, so the signal acquires the smeared, noisy edge of a jittery sample clock - subtle phase-noise grime that frosts transients and high frequencies. Amount sets the jitter, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MagnetostrictRing}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MagnetostrictRing}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Magnetostrictive ring: a magnetic core changes shape with the SQUARE of the drive (magnetostriction), exciting a high metallic resonance an octave up - so the signal grows a singing, ferromagnetic ring that tracks its envelope, the hum you hear from transformers and tape heads. Freq sets the ring pitch, Amount the ring level, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Megaphone}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Megaphone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Megaphone / bullhorn: forces in0 through a harsh, peaky midrange band and clips it, the distorted, honking voice-of-authority sound. Honk sets the resonant peak, Drive the clipping, Mix the blend. in0 = Audio.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MetalStress}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MetalStress}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Metal fatigue: the input's energy accumulates as 'stress' in a metal part; when the stress crosses a building threshold the metal CRACKS - a bright inharmonic ping - and partially relieves, so sustained loud input produces irregular, escalating metallic snaps and creaks, like a stressed sheet or cooling engine. Buildup sets how fast stress accrues, Threshold the crack point, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Overdrive}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Overdrive}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tube-style overdrive: a smooth tanh soft-clip with a midrange-focused drive, warming and compressing the signal the way a pushed valve preamp does - the Tube Screamer voicing. Drive sets the gain into the clip, Tone the post brightness, Level the output. in0 = Audio.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhaserStages}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhaserStages}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Multi-stage phaser: a cascade of all-pass sections swept by a built-in LFO, with feedback for deeper notches - from a subtle shimmer to a vocal, resonant sweep. Rate sets the LFO, Stages the notch count, Feedback the resonance, Mix the depth. in0 = Audio.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PlosivePop}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PlosivePop}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Plosive pop: each transient triggers a short low-frequency thump under the signal - the 'p'/'b' plosive blast or a mic-pop, a dull pressure pulse that fattens hits with sub-bass weight. Tune sets the pop pitch, Amount the thump level, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PrintThrough}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PrintThrough}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tape print-through: on a wound reel the magnetic field of a loud passage slowly imprints a faint GHOST of itself onto the tape layers pressed against it - so on playback a dull copy appears both one layer BEFORE the sound (the eerie pre-echo that creeps in just ahead of a loud transient on old tapes) and one layer AFTER it (the post-echo). A plain delay cannot make a pre-echo; this does, at the cost of one print-spacing of latency. Spacing sets the reel-layer time between the ghosts and the original, Amount the print strength, Tone how dull the ghosts are, Mix the print intensity. A vintage-tape character and a uniquely 'impossible' pre-echo effect.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RatDist}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RatDist}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

RAT-style distortion: a hard op-amp clip into a sweepable lowpass 'filter' control, giving the aggressive, fuzzy-yet-focused ProCo RAT grind. Drive sets the gain into the clip, Filter rolls off the top (counter-clockwise = darker), Level the output. in0 = Audio.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rattle}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rattle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rattle: while the signal is loud enough a loose part buzzes against it - fast random noise bursts gated by the input envelope, like a snare wire, a blown speaker or a rattling panel that only sounds when driven hard. Goes quiet the instant the signal drops. Thresh sets the drive needed, Amount the rattle level, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RectifierHum}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RectifierHum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Power-supply hum injector: adds mains-frequency hum (and its octave, the way a full-wave rectifier ripples at 2x mains) whose level GROWS with the signal - modelling a sagging power supply that hums harder when the amp is pushed. Distinct from a static hum: it ducks away in silence and blooms on transients. Freq sets the mains frequency, Amount the worst-case hum, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Robotize}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Robotize}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Robot voice: ring-modulates the input against a fixed carrier and imposes a vowel formant, turning speech or any signal into the classic talking-robot timbre. Pitch sets the carrier, Vowel the formant, Mix the wet. in0 = Audio.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RotarySpeaker}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RotarySpeaker}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rotary speaker (Leslie): a spinning-horn cabinet that adds pitch-doppler vibrato and amplitude tremolo, sweeping between slow (chorale) and fast (tremolo) - the swirling organ and guitar cabinet. Speed morphs slow to fast, Depth the swirl, Mix the wet. in0 = Audio.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rust}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rust}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rust: a corrosion that ACCUMULATES the longer the signal is present - a crackle/grit bed that grows as the contact oxidizes, then slowly cleans up in silence. Unlike a static crackle, it gets worse over a held passage. Speed sets the rusting rate, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ScrapeFlutter}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ScrapeFlutter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Scrape flutter: the fast, nervous high-frequency wobble of tape scraping past a fixed guide or head - a rapid (tens of Hz) random micro-pitch and amplitude jitter plus a faint signal-gated scrape hiss, the gritty motion noise distinct from the slow musical wow. Rate sets the flutter speed, Depth the jitter intensity, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Shatter}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Shatter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shatter: the signal is smashed into tiny grains that play back from random points in a short buffer at random gains (some silent), so the sound fractures into a jagged spray of shards - a stochastic granular shatter, not a tidy looped stutter. Size sets the shard length, Chaos how often and how violently it re-shards, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sigh}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sigh}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sigh: when a note releases (the level falls away) it triggers a downward filter sweep, so phrase endings trail off into a soft darkening sigh. A release-keyed motion you can't get from a static EQ. Fall sets how long the sigh takes, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SignFlip}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SignFlip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sign flip: at a steady clock it randomly inverts the polarity of the whole signal for that window, scrambling the phase into a harsh, fizzing, broken texture - a pure waveform-polarity destroyer with no level change, just sign chaos. Rate sets the flip clock, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sparkle}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sparkle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sparkle: every transient triggers a brief random high bell 'ting', so hits and picks throw off glittering, fairy-dust overtones at random pitches above the note - an onset-driven shimmer quite unlike a reverb or a fixed octaver. Amount sets the sparkle level, Decay the ting length, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sproing}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sproing}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sproing: each transient launches a cartoon spring 'boing' - a sine whose pitch DROPS rapidly after the hit (the opposite of WaterDrop) with a dispersive, comedic wobble. Turns drum hits and clicks into bouncy spring-toy sounds. Tune sets the start pitch, Drop how far it falls, Decay the bounce length, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sputter}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sputter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sputter: the signal cuts in and out at irregular intervals with little bursts of crackle at each break - a failing cable, a dying tube, a loose connection. The randomness of the timing is the point: it never settles into a rhythm. Rate sets the average break rate, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Stammer}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Stammer}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stammer: an onset captures a tiny grain and replays it a handful of times with falling level before the sound continues - a quick involuntary repeat that trips over itself, the verbal stammer made audible. Rate sets the repeat speed, Decay how fast the stammer dies, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SteamHiss}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SteamHiss}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Steam hiss: a high, airy band of noise that swells and breathes like escaping steam or a hi-hat wash, with the input level pushing the pressure up. Tone sets how bright/sharp the jet is, Amount the pressure, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SubBass}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SubBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sub-octave generator: tracks the in0 signal and synthesises a clean sine/square one octave below, fattening basslines and kicks with weight a normal octaver can't. Tone shapes the sub from pure sine to growl, Mix blends it under the dry signal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TapeHiss}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TapeHiss}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tape noise floor: adds a band-limited hiss that behaves like real tape - it is loudest in the SILENCES and ducks down under the signal (self-masking), instead of the constant flat hiss of just summing in noise. Tone shapes the hiss colour, Amount the floor level, Mix the blend. Pairs with the tape/print-through blocks for an authentic worn-tape bed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tinnitus}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tinnitus}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tinnitus: a thin high sine that you only hear in the GAPS - it ducks away while the signal plays and rings out in the silences, building up after loud passages and slowly fading, exactly like the ear-ringing after a loud show. An eerie, fatiguing ambience or a sound-design unease tool. Freq sets the ring pitch, Amount the loudest ring, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ToneStack}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ToneStack}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Passive guitar-amp tone stack: the interacting Bass / Mid / Treble shelving network of a Fender/Marshall preamp, with the characteristic mid scoop when the controls interact. Bass boosts the lows, Treble the highs, Mid cuts or boosts the middle. in0 = Audio.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tumble}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tumble}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tumble: a feedback echo whose delay time slowly GROWS after each onset, so the repeats droop downward in pitch and spread apart - the sound of something tumbling away down a flight of stairs. Time sets the starting gap, Feedback the tumble length, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Twang}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Twang}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Twang: each transient excites a tuned resonator whose pitch droops as it decays, so picks and hits bloom into a springy, boinging twang - the wobble-board / saw-blade / loose-spring voice. Tune sets the resonant pitch, Decay the twang length, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Vaporize}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Vaporize}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vaporize: the attack passes clean but the sustain dissolves into a cloud of filtered noise driven by the signal's own envelope, so notes evaporate from solid tone into airy vapor as they hold. Amount sets how much vaporizes, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VinylSim}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VinylSim}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vinyl record emulation: passes in0 through a wow/flutter pitch wobble (a slowly-modulated delay), a band-limiting filter and added surface crackle - the warm, worn character of a played record. Wow sets the pitch wobble, Crackle the surface noise, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Warps}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Warps}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Carrier, Modulator}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cross-modulator (meta): blends two inputs through one of four cross-modulation algorithms - crossfade, amplitude mod, ring mod, or sine wavefold of in0 by in1 - with Amount morphing from the clean carrier into the modulated signal. One knob from subtle movement to clangorous metallic timbres.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaterDrop}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaterDrop}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Water drop: each transient in the input strikes a resonator whose pitch RISES as it rings out - the unmistakable 'bloop' of a drip into water (the cavity shrinks as the bubble collapses, so the frequency sweeps UP). A percussion-to-droplet converter. Tune sets the base pitch, Rise how far the pitch sweeps up, Decay the ring, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaxCylinder}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaxCylinder}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wax cylinder: the 1900s phonograph sound - a steep midrange band (everything below ~250 Hz and above ~3 kHz gone), heavy slow wow from the hand-cranked mandrel, constant surface crackle, and soft horn-driven saturation. The most extreme lo-fi in the box, older than tape. Age scales the bandlimiting + saturation, Wow the pitch drift, Surface the crackle, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Envelope (6)

\phantomsection\label{blk:AD}\blockicon{envelope}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AD}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ENV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonGreen}{out: CV}

Attack-Decay envelope: a rising edge on the in0 gate fires a one-shot ramp up over Attack then back down over Decay, ignoring how long the gate is held - the classic percussive/pluck envelope. Patch its CV output into a VCA, filter cutoff or any param.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ADSR}\blockicon{envelope}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ADSR}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ENV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonGreen}{out: CV}

Full ADSR envelope: a held in0 gate ramps up over Attack, falls to the Sustain level over Decay and holds there; releasing the gate fades to zero over Release - the standard four-stage envelope for any voice or modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AR}\blockicon{envelope}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AR}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ENV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonGreen}{out: CV}

Attack-Release (ASR) envelope: while the in0 gate is high the output ramps up to full over Attack and holds; when the gate falls it ramps back to zero over Release - a smooth sustaining envelope for pads and swells.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DADSR}\blockicon{envelope}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DADSR}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ENV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonGreen}{out: CV}

Delayed ADSR: like ADSR but with a Delay stage before the attack - the gate opens, nothing happens for Delay, then the four-stage envelope runs. Useful for staggered swells and per-voice timing offsets.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EnvADSR}\blockicon{envelope}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EnvADSR}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ENV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: In A, In B}\quad\textcolor{neonGreen}{out: CV}

ADSR envelope: a gate drives the classic attack/decay/sustain/release contour, applied as a VCA to the audio input - the fundamental synth envelope as a self-contained block.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HoldDecay}\blockicon{envelope}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HoldDecay}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ENV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonGreen}{out: CV}

Peak-hold envelope with linear decay: latches the loudest recent peak, holds it for a set time, then ramps down at a constant slope (not exponentially) - a control source with a straight-line fall a plain release follower cannot make.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Filter (166)

\phantomsection\label{blk:Acid303}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Acid303}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Cutoff CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TB-303-style acid filter: a smooth, squelchy 18 dB resonant lowpass that screams and 'wows' as Reso climbs - the liquid, rubbery acid-bassline character. Drive overdrives the input for grit, Reso self-oscillates near max. in1 modulates cutoff for that classic envelope-swept squelch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AdaptiveNotch}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AdaptiveNotch}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Adaptive notch: a constrained second-order IIR notch whose centre frequency is steered by normalized-gradient descent on the output power, so it hunts down and removes a single steady whistle, hum or feedback tone wherever it sits - no reference input or manual tuning. Distinct from the reference-based LMS/NLMS cancellers (which need a copy of the noise) and from a fixed notch (which can't track). InitFreq seeds the search, Width the notch sharpness, Adapt the tracking speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AirAbsorb}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AirAbsorb}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Air absorption: models how the atmosphere swallows high frequencies over DISTANCE - a gentle, physically-shaped low-pass that gets progressively darker the farther away the source is, the cue your ear uses to hear depth. Stack it for distance/perspective without reverb. Distance sets how far, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AllpassFilter}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AllpassFilter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cascaded allpass: passes all frequencies at unity gain but shifts their phase, building the swirling notches of a phaser when mixed with the dry signal or smearing transients on its own. Freq sets the centre, Stages the number of allpass sections (steeper phase), Mix the dry/wet for phasing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AlphaTrimFilter}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AlphaTrimFilter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Alpha-trimmed-mean filter: sorts a 7-sample window, discards the Trim lowest and Trim highest values, and averages what remains - Trim=0 is a plain moving average, Trim=3 is the median, and values in between blend Gaussian-noise rejection with impulse rejection. A tunable bridge between linear smoothing and rank filtering.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AutoWah}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AutoWah}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Envelope-following resonant band-pass (auto-wah): the input level sweeps a band-pass from Base upward by Range, with Sens setting how strongly playing dynamics open it and Attack the follow speed. Reso sets the peak sharpness and Mix the blend. Funky on guitar, clav and bass; raise Reso for a vocal quack.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AWeighting}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AWeighting}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

A-weighting filter: the standardized IEC 61672 / ANSI S1.4 frequency response that approximates the ear's sensitivity at moderate levels - a steep low-cut, a gentle presence rise around 2-4 kHz and a high roll-off - built as the exact 6th-order analog prototype (poles at 20.6, 107.7, 737.9, 12194 Hz) bilinear-transformed and normalized to 0 dB at 1 kHz. Thins a signal to its 'perceptually loud' band; the classic SPL-meter weighting as an audio filter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BarReson}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BarReson}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bar resonator (a first as a synth block): rings the input through the modes of a free-free metal or wooden BAR - the widely-stretched overtones (1, 2.76, 5.40, 8.93...) of a marimba, glockenspiel or music-box tine. Because the partials leap apart so fast it imposes a clear, bright, struck-mallet pitch on whatever passes through, turning drums or noise into tuned-percussion tones. Freq sets the bar's pitch, Sharpness the ring time, Mix the wet/dry blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BellReson}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BellReson}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bell resonator (a first as a synth block): rings the input through the partials of a cast BELL - the hum, prime, tierce, quint and nominal tones (0.5, 1, 1.2, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3) whose deliberately-tuned MINOR THIRD gives a bell its dark, mournful clang. Feeding a source through it casts that bronze, struck-bell colour over it. Freq sets the strike tone, Sharpness the ring time, Mix the wet/dry blend. A tuned-bell spectrum distinct from the bar and plate resonators.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BesselLowpass}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BesselLowpass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bessel lowpass: a 2nd-order IIR filter tuned to the Bessel family Q of 1/sqrt(3), which gives maximally flat group delay - so transients pass through with the least possible phase smearing, at the cost of a gentler magnitude rolloff than Butterworth. The filter family of choice when waveform shape matters more than steep cutoff.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BilateralSmoother}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BilateralSmoother}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bilateral smoother: averages a 7-sample window but weights each neighbour by how close its amplitude is to the centre sample (a Gaussian range kernel of width Range), so smooth regions are denoised while sharp transients - whose neighbours differ a lot - are left intact. A transient-preserving lowpass, unlike a plain moving average. Adds 3 samples latency.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Biquad}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Biquad}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

RBJ biquad: the textbook second-order filter with a type selector (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, notch) and a resonance/Q control - the general-purpose EQ/filter primitive computed from the standard cookbook coefficients.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BodyResonator}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BodyResonator}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Acoustic body resonator: three high-Q bandpass modes scaled by a Size knob stamp the boxy, woody resonant signature of an instrument body onto any input, like playing it through a guitar/violin shell.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Buchla292}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Buchla292}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Gate CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Buchla 292-style vactrol lowpass gate: a combined lowpass filter + VCA where a control voltage on in1 opens both together through a slow, organic vactrol response - the soft, bongo-like 'bonk' and gentle plucks of West Coast synthesis. Response sets the vactrol attack/decay sluggishness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BuchlaLPG}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BuchlaLPG}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: In A, In B}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Low-pass gate: a gate input charges a simulated vactrol whose slow, asymmetric response simultaneously opens an amplitude VCA and a low-pass filter, the plucky, organic 'bongo' dynamics of a Buchla LPG.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ButterworthLowpass}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ButterworthLowpass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Butterworth lowpass: a true 4th-order maximally-flat-magnitude lowpass built from two cascaded biquads at the Butterworth Q pair (0.541, 1.307) - the flattest possible passband with a clean 24 dB/oct rolloff and no peak at cutoff. Completes the IIR filter family alongside the Bessel (flat group delay) and Chebyshev (rippled, steeper) lowpasses, for tone shaping where any resonant bump would colour the sound.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CapSag}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CapSag}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coupling sag: an AC-coupling high-pass whose corner frequency rises with level, so loud low-end momentarily loses its bottom and then 'sags' back as a thump when the signal drops - the bass pumping of an under-sized coupling capacitor. Amount sets the sag, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChaosLfoFilter}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChaosLfoFilter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaos-swept filter: a logistic-map chaos generator (updated at the set rate) drives the lowpass cutoff, so the tone lurches through deterministic-yet-unpredictable jumps - more structured than random noise modulation, more alive than an LFO sweep.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChebyshevLowpass}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChebyshevLowpass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chebyshev type-I lowpass: a 2nd-order IIR whose pole Q is derived from the chosen passband Ripple (in dB), trading a flat passband for a steeper rolloff than Butterworth - more ripple buys a sharper knee and a more resonant edge. The Q comes from the real Chebyshev pole formula, so it is a true filter-family response, not a waveshaper.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChromaticReson}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChromaticReson}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chromatic resonator (a first as a synth block): rings the input through resonators tuned to all twelve equal-tempered SEMITONES of the octave - a comb of every chromatic pitch, so it imposes a fixed twelve-tone pitch grid on whatever passes through, quantising noise or percussion toward an equal-tempered cluster. Root sets the lowest note, Sharpness the ring time, Mix the wet/dry blend. The densest scale resonator, a chromatic pitch-grid distinct from the consonant major/minor/pentatonic banks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CicFilter}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CicFilter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

CIC filter: two cascaded boxcar averages of length Taps, giving the sinc^2 magnitude response of a 2-stage cascaded-integrator-comb - a steep, multiply-free lowpass with deep spectral nulls at every multiple of the sample rate over Taps. Sharper rolloff and stronger notches than a single moving average; the workhorse anti-alias filter of decimators.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cluster}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cluster}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cluster filter (Pigments-style): places up to five resonant band-pass filters around a single Cutoff, fanned outward by Spread, and sums them into one animated, vowel-like comb of peaks. Sweeping Cutoff glides the whole cluster; widening Spread pulls the peaks apart from a single resonance into a chord of formants; Reso sharpens each peak toward self-oscillation; Mix blends against dry. Distinct from the harmonic SMR bank (whose bands track a fundamental's overtones) - here the peaks are symmetric octave-spread satellites of one cutoff.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CombBank}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CombBank}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tuned comb filter: feeds the input through a short delay with feedback so it resonates at a pitch (and its harmonics), turning noise into tuned tones and adding a metallic, flange-like comb colour to audio. Freq sets the pitch, Feedback the resonance/ring length, Mix the wet. Distinct from the single Comb in its long-feedback resonant tuning.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CombChord}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CombChord}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chordal comb: three feed-forward comb taps tuned to a triad of pitches place harmonically-spaced notch sets at once, coloring the input with a chord's worth of comb resonance.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CombFlutter}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CombFlutter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fluttering comb: a tuned feedback comb whose delay length wobbles a few percent under an LFO, detuning the resonant peaks continuously so the metallic comb tone shimmers and choruses instead of sitting at a fixed pitch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CombKeytrack}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CombKeytrack}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pitch-tracking comb: detects the input period and tunes a comb filter's notches to a chosen harmonic of it in real time, so the comb coloration locks to the note being played rather than a fixed frequency.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CombMorph}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CombMorph}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Morphing comb filter: one control sweeps continuously from a feed-forward (FIR, notch) comb to a feedback (IIR, resonant) comb at the same tuned delay, with fractional-delay tuning in Hz.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CombResonator}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CombResonator}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tuned comb resonator: a damped feedback comb makes any input (noise, clicks, drums) ring at a chosen pitch, the Karplus-Strong damping filter applied as an effect to turn percussive sources into tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CombSweep}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CombSweep}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Envelope-swept comb: the comb's tuned frequency rides the input envelope between two limits, so transients sweep the notches automatically - auto-flange driven by dynamics rather than an LFO.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ComplexResonator}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ComplexResonator}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Complex resonator: a one-pole filter with a complex coefficient R*e^(j*omega), iterating z = p*z + x so the state spirals as a phasor - the real part is a clean resonance and the two state variables are inherently in quadrature. The building block of the sliding DFT and Mathews' phasor filter, distinct from real-coefficient biquads. Resonance sets the pole radius.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ContactMic}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ContactMic}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Contact mic: the boxy, midrange-honk colour of a piezo disc stuck to a surface - a narrow band-pass plus structure-borne rumble that swells with how fast the signal moves (handling noise), so everything sounds like it was picked up through the body of an object rather than the air. Body sets the rumble, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ConvolveCab}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ConvolveCab}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Short convolution: convolves the input with a fixed 16-tap impulse response (a tiny cabinet/early-reflection signature), the genuine FIR-convolution method real cabinet/IR loaders use, in miniature.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrossComb}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrossComb}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: In A, In B}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cross-fed comb: the comb taps a delayed copy of a second input rather than itself, so one signal carves tuned notches into another (cross-synthesis), with the delay set in Hz.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Crossover}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Crossover}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two-band splitter that derives a low band from two cascaded one-pole low-passes (an LR2 response) and forms the high band by subtracting it from the input, outputting one band at a time. Freq sets the split frequency and Band chooses whether the low band or the high band is passed. Use it to route or isolate a frequency region, or to feed separate processing chains per band.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CryWah}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CryWah}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Pedal CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wah pedal: a resonant bandpass swept by the Pedal control, the vocal 'wah' of a guitar pedal - rock the pedal (or in1) for the talking sweep. Pedal sweeps the peak, Reso the sharpness, Mix the wet. in1 = Pedal CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CWeighting}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CWeighting}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

C-weighting filter: the standardized IEC 61672 / ANSI S1.4 response for peak and high-level sound measurement - nearly flat across the midband with gentle -3 dB roll-offs at 31.5 Hz and 8 kHz (a double pole at 20.6 Hz and 12194 Hz, the same outer corners as A-weighting but WITHOUT its midrange emphasis). Keeps the low end A-weighting throws away, so it reads bass-heavy material far closer to flat - the cinema/peak-level counterpart to the A curve.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DCBlocker}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DCBlocker}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

DC blocker: the standard one-pole/one-zero high-pass (y = x - x_prev + R*y_prev) that removes any constant offset or sub-sonic drift while leaving the audband untouched - essential after rectifiers and asymmetric distortion.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DericheGaussian}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DericheGaussian}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Recursive Gaussian filter: the Young-van Vliet 3rd-order IIR whose impulse response approximates a true Gaussian of width Sigma samples, giving a maximally smooth lowpass with no ringing - distinct from one-pole and biquad lowpasses, which have exponential or resonant impulse responses. Sigma sets the smoothing radius. Unity DC gain.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DiodeLadder303}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DiodeLadder303}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Acid diode ladder: a four-stage filter with the asymmetric diode-ladder feedback and gain compensation of the TB-303, giving the rubbery, squelchy resonance that defines acid bass lines.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DispDelay}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DispDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dispersive resonator: an all-pass in the feedback loop makes high partials travel at a different speed than lows, so the comb rings with the stretched, inharmonic, metallic timbre of a stiff string or bar.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DJFilter}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DJFilter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Reso Mod, Cutoff Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

One-knob DJ filter: with Cutoff at centre the signal is open, turning down sweeps in a low-pass and turning up sweeps in a high-pass, while Reso adds bite at the corner. The Mod input (in 3) offsets the knob for automation. The classic build-and-drop sweep on a single control.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DrumheadReson}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DrumheadReson}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Drumhead resonator (a first as a synth block): rings the input through a bank tuned to the modes of a circular DRUM MEMBRANE - the inharmonic Bessel-function overtones (1, 1.59, 2.14, 2.30, 2.65...) that give a struck skin its pitch-ambiguous, tom-like 'boing' rather than a clear note. Drop a drum loop, voice or noise through it and the source takes on the body of a tuned membrane. Freq sets the membrane pitch, Sharpness the ring time, Mix the wet/dry blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DynamicComb}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DynamicComb}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dynamic comb: a tuned comb whose resonant feedback rises with the input's loudness, so soft passages pass nearly dry and loud hits ring into a sharp metallic resonance - a level-dependent comb intensity, distinct from a comb whose pitch is swept.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DynamicEQ}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DynamicEQ}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}
