\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AiryOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AiryOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Airy oscillator: each cycle traces the Airy function Ai(-t) over its oscillatory region via the asymptotic form, so the waveform sweeps upward in frequency and tapers in amplitude within every period - a built-in intra-cycle chirp no static waveform has. Range sets how far up the tail it reads.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AizawaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AizawaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Aizawa attractor: a chaotic flow that wraps around a torus and punches through its axis, producing a complex orbit with both rotational and spiking character - a richly-structured strange attractor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AkashOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AkashOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Akash oscillator: each cycle is the Akash PDF proportional to (1+x^2)e^(-Theta*x), a lifetime distribution whose quadratic factor gives a more pronounced hump before the exponential tail than Lindley - a rounder, later-peaking skewed bump. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AkgulFlow}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AkgulFlow}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Akgul flow: a representative three-dimensional chaotic system from the chaos-based-encryption literature, with an exponential nonlinearity in one equation folding an asymmetric scroll - a hardware-RNG-oriented chaotic source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Alarm}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Alarm}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Alarm - an insistent two-tone or pulsing siren for warnings and tension. Rate sets the pulse speed, Pitch the tone, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AlarmBeepOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AlarmBeepOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Alarm beep: the urgent insistent beeping of an alarm or alert (clock, smoke detector, warning), a piercing tone hard-gated into fast even beeps, the wake-up / attention-required sound; the beep rate sets the urgency.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AllpassPdOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AllpassPdOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Allpass phase-distortion oscillator: a sine is passed through a first-order allpass whose coefficient is itself modulated by a second oscillator at a set ratio, warping the phase at audio rate to generate FM/PD-like sidebands - a distinct distortion-synthesis path between phase distortion and through-zero FM. Ratio sets the modulator pitch, Depth the warp amount.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Almglocken}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Almglocken}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Almglocken (tuned cowbells): pitched alpine bells with a metallic, clanking attack between a cowbell and a bell, and a medium decay. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AmariField}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AmariField}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Amari neural field: a continuum model of cortical activity with local excitation and broader inhibition, reduced to one bump-amplitude mode that pulses and (with adaptation) oscillates - working-memory/attention dynamics as a slow source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Angklung}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Angklung}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Angklung: shaken Indonesian bamboo tubes - a soft, woody pitched tone with the rattling buzz of the tubes knocking in their frame. Tune sets the pitch, Rattle the shake noise, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AngleGrinderOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AngleGrinderOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Angle grinder: a power grinder cutting metal, a very high-pitched motor whine over a gritty abrasive grind, throwing a crackle of sparks, the metal-shop scream; the spark knob sets the sparking.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AnishchenkoOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AnishchenkoOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Anishchenko-Astakhov oscillator: a self-sustained chaotic generator from radiophysics with a switching nonlinearity, producing noisy-periodic signals on the boundary between an oscillator and a noise source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AntPheromone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AntPheromone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ant pheromone trail: an ant walks a ring biased toward stronger pheromone, deposits scent on each cell it visits, and the whole field slowly evaporates and diffuses - the stigmergic feedback that lets ant colonies self-organize trails; a probe cell's concentration is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Anvil}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Anvil}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal anvil: a trigger on in0 strikes iron - a hard, bright, dense burst of high inharmonic metal partials with a fast attack and a ringing metallic clang, the blacksmith's hammer or industrial-percussion hit. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Ring the metallic richness, Level the output. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Applause}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Applause}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Applause - a dense shower of random claps with a soft noise wash. Intensity sets the clap density (from polite to thunderous ovation), Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ApplauseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ApplauseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Applause: a roomful of clapping hands, a dense random scatter of short broadband clap transients whose rate sets the size and enthusiasm of the crowd, the wash of an audience ovation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AradhanaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AradhanaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Aradhana oscillator: each cycle is the Aradhana PDF proportional to (1+x)^2 e^(-Theta*x), a lifetime distribution whose squared-linear factor gives a stronger, later hump than Lindley before the exponential tail. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ArcFurnace}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ArcFurnace}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Arc furnace: the chaotic V-I characteristic of an electric arc (a negative-resistance plasma whose length wanders) feeding a reactive supply, producing the broadband voltage flicker that arc furnaces inflict on the grid.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ArchimedeanSpiralOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ArchimedeanSpiralOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Archimedean-spiral oscillator: the projection theta*cos(theta) of the spiral r=theta over several turns per cycle - a cosine whose amplitude rises linearly from zero across the period, a ramped swell with a clean zero start distinct from the Involute and Fermat spirals.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ArcsineDistOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ArcsineDistOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Arcsine oscillator: each cycle is the arcsine PDF 1/(pi*sqrt(x(1-x))), a U-shaped curve that dips in the middle and rises toward both edges (the long-run fraction-of-time distribution of a random walk) - a hollow, edge-weighted waveform unlike the centre-peaked bumps.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ArcWeldOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ArcWeldOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Arc welder: the harsh buzzing of an electric welding arc, a gritty mains-frequency-pulsed broadband noise spitting random bright spatter clicks as molten metal flies, the fierce sizzle of a struck arc; the density sets the spatter rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ArneodoOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ArneodoOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Arneodo attractor: a jerk system with a cubic term whose orbit spirals through a screw-like attractor, a smooth chaotic source related to the Coullet/Genesio family but with its own fold.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ArnoldCat}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ArnoldCat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Arnold's cat map: a classic area-preserving toral automorphism that stretches and re-wraps the state every step, a maximally-mixing chaotic source whose two coordinates scramble into hard, modular sawtooth jumps.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ArnoldTongue}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ArnoldTongue}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Arnold tongue: the sine circle map theta -> theta + Omega - (K/2pi) sin(2pi theta), whose average rotation locks onto rational winding numbers over ranges of the drive (the Arnold tongues), so sweeping the bias traces the devil's-staircase of mode-locking.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AssocLegendreOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AssocLegendreOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Associated-Legendre oscillator: each cycle is an associated Legendre function P_n^m(x) (the angular part of spherical harmonics) - polynomials multiplied by powers of sqrt(1-x^2), giving lobed waveforms that taper to zero at the edges, distinct from the plain Legendre/Chebyshev families. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AsymLaplaceOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AsymLaplaceOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Asymmetric-Laplace oscillator: each cycle is a two-sided exponential cusp whose left and right decay rates differ by Skew - a sharp peaked bump leaning to one side, the skewed cousin of the Laplacian, with a hard cusp at the centre and exponential tails.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AsymSechOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AsymSechOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Asymmetric-sech oscillator: each cycle is a hyperbolic-secant bump whose left and right skirts decay at different rates set by Skew - a smooth, click-free pulse leaning to one side, the soft-tailed counterpart of the asymmetric-Laplace cusp. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AuroralOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AuroralOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Auroral pulsation: the slow magnetic-field oscillations (Pc continuous pulsations) that ripple through the magnetosphere during auroral activity, modeled as two near-equal frequencies beating into a waxing-and-waning quasi-sine - the magnetosphere's gentle ring.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AutocatalatorChem}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AutocatalatorChem}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Autocatalator: a two-variable cubic-autocatalysis chemical model (a precursor decays into an activator that catalyses its own production), giving sharp relaxation oscillations and, when driven, chaos - a minimal self-accelerating reaction clock.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AvalancheOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AvalancheOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Avalanche: a mountainside of snow letting go, a low broadband rumble that builds and roars to a thunderous peak with grinding debris before slowly subsiding, looping at the slow event rate; a force-of-nature wall of noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AYChip}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AYChip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

AY-3-8910 / YM2149 oscillator: a square plus a noise channel shaped by the chip's hardware sawtooth envelope - the classic ZX-Spectrum / Amstrad chiptune voice. Noise sets the noise mix, EnvRate the hardware-envelope speed, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AYToneOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AYToneOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

AY tone: the General Instrument AY-3-8910 / Yamaha YM2149 PSG (ZX Spectrum, Atari ST, MSX) square tone shaped by its hardware volume envelope, which can repeat fast enough to act as a buzzy sawtooth-like timbre - the chip's signature 'buzzer bass' voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BackEaseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BackEaseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Back-ease oscillator: each cycle is the easeOutBack animation curve, which shoots past the top then settles back - an S-ramp with a single overshoot whose size Overshoot controls, giving a waveform between a sigmoid and a damped step, distinct from any plain ramp.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bagpipes}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bagpipes}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bagpipes: a constant droning reed under a buzzy chanter melody - the fixed low drone plus a pitched reed voice that never stops between notes. Drone sets the drone level, Reed the chanter buzz, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BakerMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BakerMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Baker's map: the dough-kneading stretch-cut-and-stack transform that is the archetype of chaotic mixing, iterated at a chosen rate for a hard, fractal, modular-jump texture.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BakSneppenEvo}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BakSneppenEvo}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bak-Sneppen model: a ring of species each with a fitness; each step the least-fit species and its two neighbours are replaced with fresh random fitnesses, driving the system to self-organized criticality with avalanches of extinction - the minimum fitness rises in punctuated jumps.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Balafon}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Balafon}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Balafon: a struck wooden-bar xylophone over gourd resonators that add a characteristic membrane buzz - the bright, woody West-African mallet voice. Tune sets the pitch, Buzz the gourd rattle, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BandedWG}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BandedWG}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Strike, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Banded waveguide resonator (Essl/Cook/Serafin): a strike on in0 excites a bank of four high-Q modal resonators tuned to inharmonic ratios, so it rings like a struck bar, bowl or glass rather than a plucked string - the model behind physically-voiced marimbas, glass harmonicas and Tibetan singing bowls. Tune sets the fundamental (in1 = Pitch CV), Material morphs the ratios from wooden bar toward glass bowl, Decay the ring time, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BandNoiseTuned}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BandNoiseTuned}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tuned noise band: white noise driven through a steep resonant band-pass at the set pitch, so the noise rings into a breathy, whistling pitched tone - wind, flute-edge and surf-like timbres that a clean oscillator cannot make; raise Reso toward self-oscillation for a pure whistle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BanjoOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BanjoOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Banjo: a bright, lightly-damped Karplus-Strong string whose output is coupled into a quick drum-head resonance, giving the twangy, plinky, fast-decaying attack of a banjo; re-plucked at the set rate for rolls.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bansuri}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bansuri}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bansuri: the Indian bamboo transverse flute - an open-bore air-jet waveguide voiced warmer and breathier than a metal Western flute, with a soft woody tone and heavy breath noise, the meditative voice of Hindustani classical music. Breath pushes it overblown, Air sets the breathiness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BaoMemristor}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BaoMemristor}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bao memristive system: a Lorenz-like flow with a flux-controlled memristor whose state-dependent conductance adds a fourth dimension of memory, producing memristor-flavoured chaos with extreme multistability.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BarModeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BarModeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bar-mode oscillator: sines at the bending-mode ratios of a free-free bar (1, 2.76, 5.40, 8.93, 13.34) - the sharply-inharmonic, fast-thinning overtones of a xylophone or marimba bar (which tuners flatten by undercutting); a struck-metal pitched tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BarnsleyFern}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BarnsleyFern}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Barnsley fern: the chaos game over four affine contraction maps (stem, successively smaller leaflets) whose probabilistically-chosen iteration converges to the famous self-similar fern; the wandering point's coordinate is read out as a fractal-distributed CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BartlettHannOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BartlettHannOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bartlett-Hann oscillator: each cycle is the Bartlett-Hann window 0.62 - 0.48|p-0.5| - 0.38cos(2*pi*p), a hybrid of the triangular and Hann windows - a rounded bump with slightly straighter flanks than a pure cosine window, a mellow tone with its own harmonic balance.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Basimilus}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Basimilus}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Harmonic percussion synth (Noise Engineering Basimilus Iteritas): a trigger on in0 fires a stack of six sine partials whose spacing morphs from harmonic toward inharmonic, run through a wavefolder and shaped by a fast percussive decay - the digital, metallic counterpart to the analog drum voices, good for kicks, snares, zaps and bells. Tune sets the fundamental, Harmonics the partial spread, Fold the wavefolder crunch, Decay the length, and Level the output. Gate it from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BassGuitarPluckOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BassGuitarPluckOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bass guitar: a low, thick Karplus-Strong string with heavier damping for a round finger-style tone, plus an optional sharp attack transient for slap-and-pop; re-plucked at the set rate for basslines.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bassoon}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bassoon}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Breath, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bassoon: the woody, reedy low double-reed - a sawtooth through a low formant and a warm lowpass with reed buzz, played by holding in0 as breath. Reed sets the buzz, Breath the air, Level the output. in0 = Breath gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BeadHoop}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BeadHoop}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bead on a rotating hoop: a bead sliding on a vertical hoop spun about its axis; centrifugal force fights gravity in a pitchfork bifurcation, and a wobble in the spin rate drives the bead's angle chaotically.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BeddingtonMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BeddingtonMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Beddington map: a host-parasitoid model that adds host density-dependence to Nicholson-Bailey, taming the divergence into bounded cycles and chaos - a more realistic ecological two-species oscillator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BedheadMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BedheadMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bedhead attractor: an iterated map mixing a product term with a sine, tracing wispy, hair-like fractal strands (the 'bedhead' pattern), a fine, scratchy chaotic texture.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BeeBuzzOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BeeBuzzOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bee: the wingbeat buzz of a honeybee, a low ~200 Hz fundamental rich in harmonics, gently amplitude- and pitch-fluttered as the insect hovers and turns; the wingbeat knob sets the species pitch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BeetleScuttleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BeetleScuttleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Beetle scuttle: the dry skittering of a beetle's legs scrambling across a hard surface, a sparse irregular spatter of tiny high noise micro-bursts, fast and stop-start; the speed knob sets how frantically it scurries.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BellFM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BellFM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two-operator FM bell: a sine carrier is phase-modulated by a sine modulator running at a non-integer multiple of the carrier frequency, producing inharmonic metallic partials. Freq sets the carrier pitch, Ratio sets the modulator-to-carrier frequency ratio (typically fractional for bell tones), Index sets the modulation depth (brightness and partial spread), and Level scales the output. Higher Index and non-integer Ratio yield clangorous, bell-like timbres.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BellLead}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BellLead}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

FM bell lead: a bright, ringing two-operator FM bell tone playable as a lead - metallic, glassy and clear. Ratio sets the modulator interval, Bright the FM index, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BellPartialOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BellPartialOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bell-partial oscillator: the named partials of a tuned church bell - hum (0.5), prime (1), the minor-third tierce (1.2), quint (1.5), nominal (2) and beyond - whose deliberate minor-third tierce gives a bell its characteristic plaintive, slightly-dissonant ring.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BeltSanderOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BeltSanderOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Belt sander: a belt sander running flat on wood, a steady mid motor drone overlaid with the continuous grit-hiss of the abrasive belt looping and a periodic seam thump where the belt joins, the workshop-sanding sound; the belt rate sets the loop.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bendir}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bendir}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bendir: a North-African frame drum with gut snares stretched across the skin that add a buzzing rattle on every hit - a membrane tone under a snare-like buzz. Tune sets the pitch, Buzz the snare rattle, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BeniniOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BeniniOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Benini oscillator: each cycle is the Benini PDF x^(-Alpha)*e^(-Beta*ln(x)^2)*(Alpha+2*Beta*ln x)/x over x>=1, a heavy-tailed income distribution blending Pareto power-law and log-normal curvature - Alpha sets the power tail and Beta the log-normal bending. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Benjolin}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Benjolin}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Benjolin (Rob Hordijk): a self-patched chaos voice - two cross-modulating triangle oscillators feeding a rungler shift register that feeds their pitch back, producing wild, generative, never-repeating drones and sequences from no input. Rate sets the base speed, Chaos the rungler feedback depth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Berimbau}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Berimbau}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal berimbau: a trigger on in0 strikes the steel string of the Brazilian musical bow - a bright metallic plucked tone (near-harmonic partials) with a gourd-resonated body and the buzz of the caxixi rattle, the hypnotic pulse of capoeira. Tune sets the string pitch (open/pressed via Pitch CV), Decay the ring, Buzz the rattle amount, Level the output. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BernoulliPolyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BernoulliPolyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bernoulli-polynomial oscillator: each cycle is the nth Bernoulli polynomial B_n(x) on [0,1] (the polynomials behind the Euler-Maclaurin formula and sums of powers) - an oscillating polynomial with characteristic end symmetry, distinct from the orthogonal-polynomial families. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BernoulliShift}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BernoulliShift}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bernoulli shift: the doubling map x -> 2x mod 1 is the dynamical-systems picture of shifting a number's bits left, producing a sharp, deterministic noise stream - chaos as a bit-shift register.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BernsteinOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BernsteinOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bernstein oscillator: each cycle is a Bernstein basis polynomial x^K(1-x)^(N-K) on [0,1] (the building block of Bezier curves), a single compact bump whose position is set by K/N and whose width narrows as N grows - a placeable, scalable pulse. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BertramBurst}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BertramBurst}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bertram burster: a phenomenological polynomial model of bursting electrical activity with two slow variables on different timescales, capable of square-wave, parabolic and chaotic bursting - a flexible burster for rhythmic clusters.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BesselPolyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BesselPolyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bessel-polynomial oscillator: each cycle is a Bessel polynomial y_n(x) (1+x, 1+3x+3x^2, 1+6x+15x^2+15x^3) - a monotonically accelerating rise whose curvature steepens with Order, a polynomial ramp distinct from the orthogonal Chebyshev/Legendre families. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BetaNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BetaNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Beta noise: samples on [0,1] formed as X/(X+Y) from two gamma variables, whose two shape knobs sculpt the density from U-shaped (peaks at the edges) through uniform to a central bell - a bounded, fully-shapeable random source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BetaPdfOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BetaPdfOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Beta-PDF oscillator: each cycle is the Beta distribution shape x^(Alpha-1)(1-x)^(Beta-1) on [0,1], normalized to its mode - two knobs slide the bump from left-skewed to symmetric to right-skewed and from narrow to broad, a continuously morphable compact waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BetaPrimeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BetaPrimeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Beta-prime oscillator: each cycle is the beta-prime PDF x^(a-1)(1+x)^(-a-b) on x>=0, a right-skewed bump with a polynomial tail (the distribution of a ratio of gammas) - Alpha sets the rise, Beta the tail decay; peak-normalized and bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BezierOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BezierOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bezier-curve oscillator: one cycle is a cubic Bezier whose two middle control points (Shape1/Shape2) bend the waveform between the fixed endpoints, sweeping from a soft pulse to a skewed bell to a near-saw - an arbitrary smooth waveshape from two knobs.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BhalekarGejji}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BhalekarGejji}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bhalekar-Gejji attractor: a three-dimensional chaotic system (introduced in the fractional-calculus literature) whose single product nonlinearity and constant drive fold a compact scroll, here integrated as an integer-order flow.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BhaskaraOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BhaskaraOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bhaskara oscillator: generates its cycle from Bhaskara I's 7th-century rational sine approximation 16x(pi-x)/(5*pi^2-4x(pi-x)), mirrored for the negative half - a near-perfect sine whose tiny approximation error contributes a faint, fixed upper-harmonic warmth. A 'soft sine' variant distinct from a pure lookup sine.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BicornOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BicornOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bicorn oscillator: the y-coordinate of the bicorn (cocked-hat) curve, cos^2 t (Shape + cos t)/(3 + sin^2 t), a smooth two-humped waveform whose symmetry and harmonic content shift with Shape - a rounded twin-peak tone distinct from any sine or pulse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BifoliumOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BifoliumOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bifolium oscillator: the radial curve sin(theta)*cos^2(theta), which expands to an equal-weight in-phase blend of the fundamental and third harmonic - a hollow, double-petalled waveform distinct from the nephroid's 3:1 weighting, mellow and woody.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BinaryEntropyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BinaryEntropyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Binary-entropy oscillator: each cycle is the Shannon binary entropy H(p) = -p*log2(p)-(1-p)*log2(1-p), a symmetric hump that is zero at both ends and peaks at the centre with steep, near-vertical shoulders - a flat-topped-yet-pointed bump distinct from the polynomial and cosine windows.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BinaryNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BinaryNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Binary noise: a one-bit random telegraph signal that flips between +1 and -1 at a decimated rate, the harsh, hollow hiss of single-bit quantized noise - Rate sets the sample-and-hold step (lower = chunkier, more tonal) and Bias skews the duty between the two levels.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BinaryStarOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BinaryStarOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Binary-star oscillator: the radial-velocity curve of a star orbited in an eccentric orbit (true anomaly through Kepler's equation), the skewed, asymmetric wobble astronomers measure to find companions and exoplanets - sine-like when circular, sharply lopsided when eccentric.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BinauralBeat}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BinauralBeat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Binaural beat source: two sine tones a few Hz apart summed to one channel, so they alternately reinforce and cancel at the difference frequency - the slow amplitude throb used for entrainment tones (true binaural needs one tone per ear).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Birds}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Birds}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bird ambience: a bed of randomly-timed bird tweets - each a fast frequency-swept sine whistle (the chirp glide) at a scattered pitch - the dawn-chorus / forest / garden ambience of film sound design. Distinct from the Insects stridulation bed: birds are gliding whistles. Self-running. Density sets how often tweets fire, Pitch the whistle base frequency, Sweep the chirp glide depth, Variety the random pitch range between calls, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Birdsong}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Birdsong}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Birdsong: random whistled chirps - each a short FM sweep that rises or falls at a random pitch and rate, the way a songbird trills. Sparse and pretty as an ambience layer, dense and frantic as a dawn chorus. Rate sets the chirp frequency, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BirdsongOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BirdsongOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Birdsong oscillator: a high whistle that rapidly glides up and down in pitch and is grouped into trilled chirp phrases with gaps, the bright frequency-swept warble of a songbird; the chirp rate and glide depth shape the species' call.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BirnbaumSaundersOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BirnbaumSaundersOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Birnbaum-Saunders oscillator: each cycle is the fatigue-life PDF (a reliability distribution), a sharply-rising right-skewed bump - Beta sets the scale/peak position and Gamma the spread, peak-normalized to a bounded waveform with a distinctive crack-propagation skew.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BirthDeathProcess}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BirthDeathProcess}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Birth-death process: a population whose per-capita birth and death rates make it grow or shrink by one at random, fluctuating and occasionally going extinct then reseeding (the basic stochastic population/queue model) - a jittery boom-and-bust integer CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BitGlitchOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BitGlitchOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bit glitch: a corrupted digital signal, a tone that randomly drops into harsh bit-mangled aliased bursts (bit-flips and sample-rate errors) between clean moments, the broken-machine sound of datamosh; the error rate sets the corruption.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BiweightOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BiweightOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Biweight oscillator: each cycle is the biweight (quartic) kernel (1-x^2)^2, a compact bump flatter on top and steeper at the edges than the parabolic Welch - a smooth pulse with a rounder shoulder and faster-decaying harmonics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlackbodyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlackbodyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Blackbody oscillator: each cycle is Planck's law spectral curve x^3/(e^x-1), the universal blackbody radiation shape - a smooth bump that rises as a cubic and falls exponentially with the characteristic Planck skew; Spread sets how much of the spectrum the cycle traverses. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlackHole}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlackHole}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Black hole - a crushing sub-bass gravitational drone slowly bending downward into the abyss, with a faint event-horizon shimmer. Pull sets the intensity, Tone the shimmer, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlackmanHarrisOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlackmanHarrisOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Blackman-Harris oscillator: each cycle is the 4-term Blackman-Harris window, a cosine-sum bump with extremely low sidelobes - a very smooth, rounded, near-Gaussian pulse whose steep symmetric taper gives a mellow tone with fast-decaying even harmonics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Blancmange}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Blancmange}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Blancmange (Takagi) oscillator (a first as a synth block): synthesises the blancmange curve - a fractal made by stacking ever-smaller TRIANGLE waves, each half the height and twice the rate of the last. Like the Weierstrass function it is continuous everywhere but jagged at every scale, yet its triangle (rather than cosine) building blocks give a softer, more granular roughness - a self-similar timbre that thickens as Detail adds layers. Detail sets how many triangle octaves to sum. A triangle-based fractal waveform distinct from the cosine-based Weierstrass. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlancmangeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlancmangeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Blancmange (Takagi) curve: a sum of triangle waves at doubling frequencies and halving amplitudes, the pudding-shaped fractal that is continuous everywhere but has a corner at every scale - a soft, self-similar, octave-stacked waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Blaster}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Blaster}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sci-fi blaster: a noisy, metallic energy-weapon shot - a fast pitch-swept tone mixed with ring-modulated noise and a short ringing tail. Tune sets the pitch, Grit the noise, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bleep}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bleep}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bleep-techno tone (Warp / Sheffield 'Sweet Exorcist' sound): a simple root-plus-fifth motif from sine-to-square waves with a hard on/off organ-style amp gate and an octave-down sub - the ultra-deep, dub-system bleep of early Warp techno. Tune sets the pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Wave morphs sine toward square, Fifth sets the level of the +7-semitone partial (the signature root/5th bleep), Sub the octave-down sub-bass weight, and Level the output. A gate on in0 switches it on and off with organ snap - no slow attack or release.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlenderOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlenderOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Blender: a kitchen blender grinding, a buzzy motor tone that surges and bogs as the blades catch and free, with a rattly chopping modulation, the chunky roar of crushing ice; the load knob sets how much it labours.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlinkingVCO}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlinkingVCO}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Blinking oscillator: two oscillators whose coupling link switches on and off at a fast clock (a 'blinking network'); the on-average coupling still synchronizes them, but the switching injects chaotic jitter - stochastic-coupling dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlitOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlitOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

BLIT sawtooth: a band-limited impulse train (the closed-form sinc-sum) leaky-integrated into a sawtooth, giving the bright edge of a saw with the high harmonics correctly band-limited - alias-free even up high.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BLITSaw}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BLITSaw}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Band-limited sawtooth built additively (summed harmonics up to Nyquist) so it stays alias-free even at high pitch - the clean, classic analog saw. Bright sets the harmonic count from mellow to razor-sharp.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlitSquare}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlitSquare}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

BLIT square: a bipolar band-limited impulse train (two opposite-sign sinc sums a half-cycle apart) leaky-integrated into a square wave, keeping the high harmonics correctly band-limited - an alias-free square even up high.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Blizzard}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Blizzard}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Howling blizzard - storm-wind noise driven through a sweeping resonant peak that wails and gusts. Howl sets the wail depth, Tone the iciness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlizzardWindOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlizzardWindOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Blizzard: a howling winter gale, a resonant band of wind noise whose pitch and loudness surge in long gusts, over a fine high hiss of driven snow; the gust rate sets how violently the storm heaves.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlochDriven}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlochDriven}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Driven Bloch equations: the magnetization (Mx, My, Mz) of a spin ensemble under an oscillating RF field with T1/T2 relaxation; strong driving makes the nutation chaotic - the model behind NMR and maser instabilities.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlochOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlochOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bloch oscillation: an electron in a periodic lattice under a constant force does not accelerate away but oscillates in real space (the Wannier-Stark effect), tracing a sawtooth-like real-space position from the integral of a cosine band - solid-state physics as a waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlockingOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlockingOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Blocking oscillator: a transformer-coupled circuit that fires a brief, sharp pulse then blocks itself off for a long recovery while a capacitor recharges - the spiky, low-duty waveform of flyback supplies, fence chargers and early sweep generators.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlowflyNicholson}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlowflyNicholson}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nicholson's blowflies: a delay-differential population model (recruitment depends on the egg-laying population one maturation-time ago: x' = P x(t-tau) e^{-x(t-tau)} - d x) that famously matches lab fly cultures crashing into chaotic cycles.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Blown}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Blown}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Waveguide wind instrument (STK-clarinet): a bore delay line with a nonlinear reed reflection, driven by breath from the gate. The blown counterpart to Karplus-Strong.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlownBottle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlownBottle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Breath, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Blown bottle (Helmholtz resonator): a breathy hoot from white-noise breath excited through a single sharp resonant peak at the pitch - the hollow, woody tone of blowing across a bottle. Tune sets the pitch, Breath the air drive, Level the output. in0 = Breath gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlueNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlueNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Blue noise: white noise passed through a first difference, tilting the spectrum up at +3 dB/octave so high frequencies dominate; the airy hiss used for dithering and high-frequency texture (deterministic RNG).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlumBlumShub}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlumBlumShub}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Blum-Blum-Shub: the cryptographically-secure generator x -> x^2 mod N (N a product of two primes), whose least-significant bit is provably hard to predict; its bit-stream is a high-quality deterministic white-noise source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bodhran}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bodhran}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bodhran: the Irish frame drum played with a tipper - a tunable low-mid membrane with a dry, woody skin tone and a short decay. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BogdanovMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BogdanovMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bogdanov map: the discrete normal form of the Bogdanov-Takens bifurcation, whose orbit spirals and folds through an annular chaotic ring - a 2-D map capturing the birth of chaos from a double-zero eigenvalue.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BohmanOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BohmanOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bohman oscillator: each cycle is the Bohman window (1-|x|)cos(pi|x|) + sin(pi|x|)/pi, a cosine-product bump with a distinctive gently-shouldered shape - smoother-edged than Welch but brighter than Blackman-Harris, a mid-character rounded pulse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BoidsFlock1D}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BoidsFlock1D}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boids (1-D): a dozen agents on a line steer by Reynolds' three rules - move toward the flock centre, match neighbours' velocity, and avoid crowding - producing emergent swarming, splitting and merging; the flock centroid drifts as a smooth organic CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BoilingOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BoilingOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boiling oscillator: random bubble events, each a short rising-pitch chirp (a collapsing cavity's note glides up as it shrinks), at a rate that climbs from a slow simmer to a rolling boil - granular bubble synthesis of boiling water.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BoingOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BoingOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cartoon boing: the springy 'boing' of a bouncing object, a tone whose pitch wobbles rapidly up and down with a decaying amplitude (a vibrating spring), the classic comedy bounce; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bonang}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bonang}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gamelan bonang: a bossed bronze kettle-gong with a clear, bright pitch over inharmonic partials and a medium shimmering decay - the melodic voice of the Indonesian gamelan. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bongo}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bongo}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal bongo: a trigger on in0 strikes a small tuned hand drum - a tight high-pitched membrane whose inharmonic modes (about 1 : 1.6 : 2.3) ring briefly with a quick downward pitch drop on the hit and a touch of finger-slap noise. Tune sets the head pitch (high or low bongo), Decay the short ring, Slap the attack-noise amount, Level the output. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BonkOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BonkOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bonk: a comedic hollow head-bonk, a woodblock-like resonant knock with a fast downward pitch drop and a quick decay, the cartoon clonk-on-the-noggin; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BooOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BooOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boo: a crowd voicing its displeasure, a low 'oooo' vowel drone of many detuned voices with a rough beating edge, swelling and wavering, the disapproving rumble of a booing audience.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BoostConverter}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BoostConverter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boost converter chaos: a step-up switching regulator whose discontinuous-conduction switching gives border-collision bifurcations straight into chaos, a hallmark of piecewise-smooth power-electronic systems - read as the inductor ripple.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BoualiOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BoualiOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bouali attractor: a chaotic flow modelling economic/predator-prey-style dynamics, tracing a folded ribbon orbit - a distinctive strange attractor for slow, evolving modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BoubakerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BoubakerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boubaker oscillator: each cycle is the nth Boubaker polynomial (B_1=x, B_2=x^2+2, B_n=x*B_{n-1}-B_{n-2}; the +2 in B_2 breaks the pure-Chebyshev pattern) - a physics polynomial family giving lobed waveforms with a distinct low-order offset. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BounceOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BounceOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bounce oscillator: each cycle is the easeOutBounce animation curve - a series of decreasing parabolic bounces rising to the top - so the waveform climbs in three settling hops, a rhythmic, percussive contour unlike any smooth or ramp wave.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BouncingBall}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BouncingBall}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}
