Bouncing ball: a ball in free fall repeatedly striking a vibrating table, where the table's phase at each impact randomizes the next flight; strong shaking gives chaotic, period-doubled bounce heights - the classic impact-map chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BouncyBallOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BouncyBallOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bouncing ball: a rubber ball dropped and left to bounce, a series of short pitched impacts whose gaps shrink and pitch drops as the ball settles, then the cycle restarts, the playful Galileo-bounce; the rate sets how often it is dropped.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bowed}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bowed}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bowed-string waveguide (STK-bowed): two delay lines for the string halves with a stick-slip bow-table at the bow point, driven by the gate. Violin/cello family.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BowedBar}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BowedBar}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Bow, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bowed metal bar / singing bowl: a sustained, shimmering inharmonic metal tone with a slow swell, played by holding in0 as bow pressure - the eerie, ringing bowed-vibraphone or Tibetan-bowl voice. Tune sets the pitch, Shimmer the beating, Level the output. in0 = Bow gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BowedBarVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BowedBarVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bowed bar: three inharmonic bar modes are continuously re-energized by a stick-slip friction drive, so they sustain instead of decaying - the eerie sustained tone of a bowed vibraphone bar or musical saw.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BowedString}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BowedString}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Bow, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bowed string (violin / cello): a sawtooth Helmholtz motion with a slow bow attack, bow-noise scrape and a body resonance, expressively controlled by holding in0 as bow pressure. Tune sets the pitch, Bow the scrape amount, Level the output. in0 = Bow gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BowedStringChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BowedStringChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Over-bowed string: a bowed-string waveguide pressed past the Helmholtz regime, where the stick-slip friction and string feedback lock into the raucous subharmonic and chaotic multiphonics players call 'surface sound' or wolf-tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BowFriction}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BowFriction}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bowed-string model: a delay-line string driven by a velocity-dependent stick-slip friction curve self-oscillates into a sustained bowed tone whose bite depends on bow force - physical bowed synthesis.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BoxingBellOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BoxingBellOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ring bell: the bright clang of a boxing-ring bell signalling the round, a struck metal bell with a strong fundamental and inharmonic shimmer ringing for a long time, fired at the set rate; the call to fight.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Braaam}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Braaam}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Inception 'BRAAAM': the massive low brass blast that defined trailer scoring - a stack of detuned saws in octaves and a fifth (the brass cluster) over a deep sub sine, swelling in over a slow attack and driven through saturation for the menacing low-end roar. Tune sets the fundamental (in1 = Pitch CV), Swell the attack fade-in, Detune the cluster beating (the dread), Sub the deep sub-sine weight, Drive the saturation, and Level the output. A gate on in0 fires the blast; hold for the long swell.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrakeJudder}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrakeJudder}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brake judder: a disc brake where normal and tangential pad motions couple through friction, so above a friction threshold the two modes lock unstably and the brake grabs and judders - the mode-coupling mechanism behind brake squeal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Brass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Brass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Synth brass (OB-X / Jupiter brass): two detuned saws through a resonant low-pass driven by a filter envelope - the cutoff snaps bright on the attack then drops to a mellower sustain, the timbral movement that makes a saw sound brassy. The eurodance / trance / funky-house brass stab and swell. Tune sets the pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Detune the saw spread (thickness), Cutoff the base brightness, FiltEnv how far the attack opens the filter, Decay how fast the bright attack drops to the sustain, and Level the output. A gate on in0 plays the note (hold for the sustain).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrassEnsemble}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrassEnsemble}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Analog brass ensemble: five detuned sawtooths with a slow filter-swept attack and ensemble chorus, the bold, swelling ARP/Solina brass-section pad. Cutoff sets the brightness, Attack the swell time, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrassLip}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrassLip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brass lip-reed model: a delay-line bore closed by a pressure-controlled lip valve nonlinearity self-oscillates into a buzzing brass tone whose brightness blooms with input drive - physical brass synthesis.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrassWGOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrassWGOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brass waveguide: a bore driven by a lip-reed (a pressure-controlled valve with its own resonance) and shaped by a flared-bell brightness, producing the buzzy, harmonically-rich tone of a trumpet or trombone that blares harder as you blow.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrayLiebhafsky}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrayLiebhafsky}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bray-Liebhafsky reaction: the first discovered homogeneous chemical oscillator (hydrogen peroxide decomposing via iodate/iodine), reduced to its autocatalytic core, ticking out relaxation oscillations and bursting.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Breathing}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Breathing}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slow human breathing - filtered noise swelling brighter on the inhale and darker on the exhale. Rate sets the breath pace, Tone the airiness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BreathingOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BreathingOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Breathing: the slow rhythm of human breath, a band of airy noise swelling on the inhale and falling on the exhale once per breath, the soft hiss of lungs filling and emptying; the rate sets the breaths per minute.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BreathyVoiceOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BreathyVoiceOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Breathy voice: a soft glottal pulse train mixed with continuous aspiration noise (the glottis never fully closes), the intimate, airy vocal quality of a whispered-sung tone; the breath knob crossfades from clear voice to pure air.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BreitWignerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BreitWignerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Breit-Wigner oscillator: each cycle is the relativistic Breit-Wigner resonance 1/((x^2-M^2)^2+M^2*W^2), a sharp resonance peak at Mass M with a quartic (steeper-than-Lorentzian) roll-off - Width sets the linewidth; peak-normalized to a bounded, spiky resonance waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BriansBrain}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BriansBrain}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brian's Brain: a three-state automaton (off, on, dying) where an off cell turns on only with exactly two on neighbours, every on cell next becomes dying, and dying cells go off; almost every pattern explodes into a sky of drifting gliders. On-cell density is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BriggsRauscher}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BriggsRauscher}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Briggs-Rauscher reaction: the iodine-clock oscillator that flips amber-clear-blue, reduced to its activator-inhibitor-iodide core, firing sharp relaxation pulses with a long quiescent interval - a chemical-clock spike source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrockHommes}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrockHommes}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brock-Hommes market: fundamentalist and trend-chasing traders switch strategies by past profit, so the price deviation from fundamentals oscillates and chaotically bubbles-and-crashes - behavioural-finance dynamics as a control source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrookOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrookOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brook: a babbling stream, a continuous bed of band-passed flow noise sprinkled with random little rising bubble tones, the gentle chatter of water running over rocks; density sets how lively the brook.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrownianBridgeWalk}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrownianBridgeWalk}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brownian bridge: a random walk constrained to start and end at zero over each period (the walk minus its linear drift to the endpoint), giving wandering shapes that always return home - the stochastic process used to interpolate between fixed points.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrownianNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrownianNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brown noise generator: white noise integrated into a bounded random walk rolls off at 6 dB/octave, the deep, rumbly low-weighted noise of distant surf or thunder.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrownNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrownNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brown/red noise - a deep -6 dB/oct rumble (integrated white noise), the heaviest of the noise colours for thunder, wind and sub texture. Tone opens the top, Level sets the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrusselatorDiffusion}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrusselatorDiffusion}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coupled Brusselators: two Brusselator reaction cells joined by diffusion, the minimal Turing system where unequal diffusion of activator and inhibitor breaks symmetry into out-of-phase chemical oscillation and spatiotemporal chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrusselatorOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrusselatorOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brusselator: the textbook autocatalytic chemical-reaction oscillator (x'=a-(b+1)x+x^2 y, y'=bx-x^2 y); a periodic forcing of its feed rate pushes its limit cycle into chaos - reaction-kinetics rhythms as sound.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bubble}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bubble}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bubbles: a sparse scatter of underwater bubbles, each a short sine that RISES in pitch as it shrinks and pops - the inverse of a falling rain drop. Sprinkle it for an aquatic ambience or a playful blip layer. Density sets the bubble rate, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BubbleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BubbleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bubble: the iconic underwater 'bloop' of a rising air bubble, a short sine burst whose pitch sweeps upward as the bubble shrinks toward the surface (the physically-modelled bubble acoustic), fired at the set rate with random size variation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BubbleOscillator}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BubbleOscillator}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Acoustic bubble: a gas bubble in a sound field expanding and violently collapsing per the Rayleigh-Plesset equation; strong driving gives the period-doubling, chaotic radial pulsation behind single-bubble sonoluminescence.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bubbles}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bubbles}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Underwater bubbles - random rising-pitch blips for a fish-tank or undersea texture. Rate sets the bubble density, Pitch the blip range, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Buchla259}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Buchla259}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Complex oscillator (Buchla 259 / West Coast): a principal sine is frequency-modulated by a second oscillator and then run through a wavefolder, so one voice spans pure tones, metallic FM and bright folded harmonics with no subtractive filter. Ratio sets the modulator interval, FM the modulation depth, Fold the wavefold amount. in0 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BuckConverter}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BuckConverter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Buck converter chaos: a switching DC-DC buck regulator under current-mode control, where the switching ramp interacts with the inductor current to period-double into chaos - a real power-supply instability, read as the ripple waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BudykoSellers}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BudykoSellers}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Budyko-Sellers model: a global energy-balance climate model whose ice-albedo feedback creates a bistable warm/snowball Earth; a periodic solar forcing makes the temperature flicker between the two states - tipping-point climate dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bullroarer}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bullroarer}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bullroarer: the ancient whirled aerophone - a slat on a cord spun overhead roars as it rotates, the tone swelling in pitch and amplitude on each pass and fluttering as the slat spins on its own axis; the ritual sound of many archaic cultures. Whirl sets the rotation speed, Roar the tonal-vs-noise balance.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BumpWaveletOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BumpWaveletOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bump function: the classic compactly-supported smooth function exp(-1/(1-x^2)), which is infinitely differentiable yet exactly zero outside one period (a true mollifier), modulating a tone into a grain that starts and ends in perfect silence with no discontinuity at any derivative.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BurgersMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BurgersMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Burgers map: a 2-D discretization of the Burgers turbulence equation whose coupled quadratic terms give a shearing, eddy-like chaotic orbit - turbulence-flavoured chaos as a sound source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BurgersShock}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BurgersShock}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Burgers shock oscillator: solves the Burgers wave equation's simple-wave relation u = sin(phase - Steepen*u) each sample, so the wave shears itself - the crests outrun the troughs and a smooth sine steepens into a near-vertical SHOCK front as Steepen climbs, the physics behind a brass instrument turning 'brassy' when blown hard. The wave is warped by its own amplitude (not a fixed transfer curve), brightening into a sawtooth-like shock. in0 is the Pitch CV; Steepen sets how close to shock.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BurkeShawOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BurkeShawOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Burke-Shaw attractor: a tightly-wound spiral chaotic flow that fills a thin shell, producing a fast, hissy, almost-tonal chaos useful for noisy oscillator textures.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BurningShipOrbit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BurningShipOrbit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Burning Ship: the Mandelbrot iteration but with the real and imaginary parts made absolute before squaring, whose folding produces the famous ship-and-antenna fractal; iterating the orbit and slowly scanning the parameter, the real part (reseeded on escape) is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BurrQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BurrQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Burr-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the Burr-XII inverse-CDF ((1-p)^(-1/K)-1)^(1/C), a doubly-parametrized heavy-tailed ramp - C shapes the early rise and K the tail weight, a more flexible blowup than the single-parameter Lomax. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BurrXOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BurrXOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Burr-X oscillator: each cycle is the Burr type-X PDF 2*alpha*x*e^(-x^2)*(1-e^(-x^2))^(alpha-1), a Rayleigh-based exponentiated bump whose skew and rise sharpen with Alpha - a generalized-Rayleigh shape distinct from the Weibull-based families. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BusySignalOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BusySignalOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Busy-signal oscillator: the engaged tone, a 480 + 620 Hz sine pair gated in the steady half-second-on, half-second-off cadence of a busy line (or the faster reorder/fast-busy when Rate is raised).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ButterflyCurveOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ButterflyCurveOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Butterfly-curve oscillator: the x-coordinate of Fay's transcendental butterfly curve, sin(t)*(e^cos t - Wings*cos 4t), giving a harmonically rich, asymmetric waveform with sharp wing-beats whose spikiness the Wings knob controls - a complex timbre from one closed-form curve.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ButterworthOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ButterworthOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Butterworth oscillator: each cycle is the Butterworth lowpass magnitude response 1/sqrt(1+x^(2*Order)) - a maximally-flat plateau that drops to a steep cliff whose sharpness Order sets - a distinct flat-topped-then-falling waveform borrowed from filter design.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BuzzerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BuzzerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Buzzer: the harsh low buzz of a game-show wrong-answer or quiz-show timeout, a gritty saw-and-square tone with a mains-frequency rasp, blaring in long held blasts; the rate sets the buzz cadence.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ByteBeat}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ByteBeat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bytebeat oscillator (a first as a synth block): the famous 'one-line music' - feed a running sample counter through a short expression of bit-shifts, ORs, ANDs and multiplies, keep the low 8 bits, and a whole chiptune-like texture falls out of pure integer arithmetic. The bitwise interference of the counter against its own shifted copies produces self-similar, glitchy, rhythmic patterns for free. Formula selects among classic bytebeat expressions and Speed sets the counter rate (its pitch and tempo). A bit-arithmetic waveform unlike any oscillator or sample. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BytebeatOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BytebeatOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bytebeat: the famous one-line music genre where an 8-bit sample is a bitwise/arithmetic formula of a sample counter t, producing complex self-similar fractal rhythms and timbres from a single expression; several classic formulas are selectable.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cabasa}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cabasa}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cabasa: a trigger on in0 fires the steel-bead cabasa - a brighter, raspier, grittier rattle than the Shaker, with a sharp metallic-bead attack and a fast scrape-like decay. Tone shifts the bead brightness, Decay the rasp length, Level the output. Needs only a trigger.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CaiOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CaiOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cai attractor: a chaotic system with a squared term in its third equation that broadens the Lorenz butterfly into a fuller, rounder twin-wing orbit, another distinct Lorenz-family flow.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cajon}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cajon}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal cajon: a trigger on in0 plays the box drum - a low wooden front-plate resonance (the bass 'thump' when struck in the centre) layered with a bright buzzing rattle of internal snare wires that rings on after the tone. Tune sets the box pitch, Decay the body, Snare the wire-buzz amount and brightness, Level the output. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CalciumSpike}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CalciumSpike}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Calcium oscillator: Goldbeter's two-pool model of calcium-induced calcium release, where cytosolic and store calcium pump and leak between each other to fire the spiky Ca2+ oscillations that carry signals inside living cells.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CalogeroOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CalogeroOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Calogero-Moser oscillator: two particles in a harmonic trap repelling through an inverse-cube force (the integrable Calogero model) plus a symmetry-breaking perturbation that turns the regular motion chaotic - many-body-physics chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CameraShutterOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CameraShutterOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Camera shutter: the snap of an SLR taking a photo, a two-part mechanical click-clack (mirror up, then the shutter curtain) with a tiny motor whirr of the film advance, fired at the set rate; the iconic ka-chunk.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Campfire}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Campfire}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crackling campfire - a warm noise bed punctuated by random spitting crackles and pops. Crackle sets the pop activity, Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CantorOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CantorOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cantor-spectrum oscillator (a first as a synth block): an additive tone that keeps harmonic k only when k written in base 3 contains NO digit 1 - the membership rule of the Cantor set, which leaves overtones at 2, 6, 8, 18, 20, 24... and removes the fundamental entirely. The result is a lacunary (gap-riddled) fractal spectrum, the audible cousin of the Cantor middle-thirds set, giving a pitched-but-rootless, metallic-hollow timbre with self-similar holes. Partials sets the highest harmonic considered. A fractal-set spectrum distinct from the other number-theoretic oscillators.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CantorStaircase}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CantorStaircase}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Devil's staircase: the Cantor function, which climbs only on the Cantor set and is flat almost everywhere; sampled around a pitch period it makes a self-similar stepped ramp with plateaus at every scale - fractal staircase modulation/timbre.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CapacitorWhineOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CapacitorWhineOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Capacitor whine: the piercing high-pitched squeal of a stressed switching power supply or ceramic capacitor (coil whine), a near-sine tone with a thin edge that wavers slightly with load, the maddening whine of cheap electronics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CardiacAlternans}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CardiacAlternans}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cardiac alternans: the action-potential-duration restitution map, where each beat's duration depends on the preceding rest interval; pacing faster period-doubles into long-short-long alternans, the arrhythmia precursor - heartbeat-timing chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CardiacPurkinje}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CardiacPurkinje}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Purkinje fiber: a reduced model of the heart's Purkinje conduction-fiber action potential with a slow pacemaker current, firing the long-plateau cardiac spikes and, when over-driven, early-afterdepolarization chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CardioidOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CardioidOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cardioid oscillator: the heart-shaped curve r = 1 - cos(theta) (an epicycloid with one cusp, and the shape of a directional-microphone pattern) projected to one axis, giving an asymmetric single-lobe waveform with a soft cusp.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CarHornOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CarHornOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Car horn: two tones a small interval apart (typically a major third around A and C-sharp) blown together with a buzzy edge, the brash, beating honk of an automobile horn; the interval knob tunes the dyad.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Carillon}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Carillon}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Carillon bell: a large struck tower bell with rich inharmonic partials and a long, humming decay - the deep, resonant cast-bronze voice. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring (several seconds), Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CashRegisterOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CashRegisterOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cash register: the cheerful ka-ching of a till ringing up a sale, a bright two-note bell ding followed by the wooden thunk of the drawer popping open, fired at the set rate; the sound of a prize won.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CasioCZ}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CasioCZ}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Casio CZ-style phase-distortion oscillator: a sine whose phase is warped toward a resonant window, producing a sweepable formant peak. Mod sets the distortion depth and Res the resonant-peak harmonic - classic 80s digital reso without a filter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CassiniOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CassiniOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cassini-oval oscillator: traces the x-coordinate of a Cassini oval as the angle sweeps a cycle; the Pinch knob squeezes the oval toward its lemniscate limit, narrowing the waveform's lobes and adding harmonics - a smooth two-lobed shape that sharpens into a pinched waist.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Castanets}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Castanets}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Castanets: a sharp pair of wooden clicks - two fast highpassed noise bursts a few milliseconds apart, the flamenco hand-percussion snap. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the click length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CatalanOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CatalanOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Catalan-number oscillator (a first as a synth block): an additive tone whose overtones land on the Catalan numbers - the 1st, 2nd, 5th, 14th, 42nd... harmonics. Because the Catalan numbers explode almost as fast as 4^n, only a few partials fit below Nyquist and they are spaced ever further apart, so the tone is nearly a pure sine with two or three widely-separated, ringing upper partials - a strikingly sparse, bell-clear spectrum. Partials sets how many Catalan overtones to attempt, alias-free up to Nyquist. A combinatorial spectrum distinct from the other number-theoretic oscillators. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CatenaryOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CatenaryOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Catenary oscillator: each cycle is one arc of the catenary curve (the shape of a hanging chain, cosh x), normalized to a bipolar waveform - a smooth U with curvature set by Width, giving a soft, rounded timbre between a triangle and a parabola but with the catenary's distinct droop.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CatmullRomOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CatmullRomOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Catmull-Rom oscillator: each cycle is the Catmull-Rom cubic interpolation kernel - a central lobe flanked by small negative side-lobes (the kernel behind smooth spline interpolation) - giving a rounded pulse with a touch of ringing, distinct from the strictly-positive smoothing windows.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CatPurrOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CatPurrOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cat-purr oscillator: a low rumbling tone amplitude-modulated at the ~25 Hz purr frequency with a gentle inhale/exhale swell, the contented buzzing of a purring cat produced by rhythmic laryngeal muscle twitches.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CauchyNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CauchyNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cauchy noise: each sample is drawn from the Cauchy (Lorentzian) distribution via tan(pi(u-1/2)), whose undefined mean and variance produce frequent large outliers among small values - a spiky, heavy-tailed alternative to Gaussian white noise (clamped).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CayleyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CayleyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cayley-sextic oscillator: the projection cos^3(theta/3)*cos(theta) of Cayley's sextic, where the slow cos^3(theta/3) term shapes a lobed amplitude contour over the fast cosine - a sixth-order algebraic curve giving a distinctive multi-lobed, partially-rectified tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CeilingFanOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CeilingFanOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ceiling fan: the soft rhythmic whoosh of a ceiling fan, a band of airflow noise pulsed once per blade pass with a faint motor hum and a slow wobble from a bent blade; the speed knob sets the blade-pass rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CelestaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CelestaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Celesta: a small steel plate struck by a felt hammer over a wooden resonator box, a soft, sweet, bell-like tone with a gentle attack and a pure near-harmonic spectrum (the celesta of the Sugar-Plum Fairy); re-struck at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CelloBowOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CelloBowOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cello: the bowed-string waveguide tuned to the cello's low range with a darker bow filter and heavier string, giving the deep, woody, resonant sustain of a bowed cello; bow force sets the grip from gentle to gritty.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CellularNeuralNet}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CellularNeuralNet}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cellular neural network (Chua-Yang): a ring of analog cells coupled to their neighbours through a fixed feedback template and a piecewise-linear output, the architecture that performs image processing in hardware; with an active template it self-organizes into striped or oscillating patterns.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cepheid}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cepheid}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cepheid pulsating star: the kappa-mechanism that makes Cepheid and RR-Lyrae stars pulse - when the star contracts, an ionization layer becomes MORE opaque and dams up the outflowing heat, which then drives the layers back outward; expanding, the layer clears and the heat escapes, so the star falls back and the cycle repeats. A self-sustaining thermal-valve oscillation with the stars' lopsided light curve (brightening faster than it dims). in0 is the Pitch CV; Kappa sets the valve strength, Drive the heat-to-motion coupling.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CesOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CesOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

CES oscillator: each cycle is the constant-elasticity-of-substitution form (0.5*x^Rho + 0.5*(1-x)^Rho)^(1/Rho) over [0,1], whose Rho morphs the curve from a U (negative Rho, min-like) through geometric to an arithmetic blend - a continuously-bending symmetric shape from economics. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChainRattleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChainRattleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rattling chains: the metallic clanking of dragged or shaken chains, a dense scatter of random short bright metal clinks at varying pitches, the ghost-dragging-his-chains sound of a haunting; density sets how violently they rattle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChainsawOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChainsawOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chainsaw: a two-stroke chainsaw revving, a harsh high buzzing engine with a bright sawtooth snarl and a chain rattle, the lumberjack roar; the rev knob raises the pitch as the throttle opens.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChantOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChantOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chant: a crowd chanting in unison, a rough voiced-vowel tone gated into a steady rhythmic shout pattern (the two-syllable terrace chant of a football crowd); the chant rate sets the tempo of the slogan.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChaosOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChaosOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Audio-rate chaotic oscillator built on the logistic map blended with a sine carrier: Chaos pushes it from a clean tone into noisy, glitchy, semi-pitched textures, and Tone tames the top end. A digital-noise sound source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChaoticFiberLaser}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChaoticFiberLaser}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaotic fiber laser: an erbium-doped fiber laser whose long upper-state lifetime gives slow relaxation oscillations that, under pump modulation or feedback, period-double into the spiking intensity chaos used in chaos-communication links.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChaoticReed}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChaoticReed}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaotic reed: a clarinet-style reed-and-bore waveguide blown past its stable regime, where the reed's nonlinear flow valve and the bore feedback lock into the squealing multiphonic and chaotic oscillations real players fight to avoid.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChapmanRichardsOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChapmanRichardsOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chapman-Richards oscillator: each cycle is the forestry growth curve (1-e^(-K*x))^P, a flexible S/ramp where K sets the rise rate and P the shape - from a saturating exponential (P=1) to a sigmoid with a slow start (P>1), a tunable growth-shaped ramp.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Charango}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Charango}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Charango: the tiny high-pitched Andean lute (traditionally an armadillo-shell body) - five courses of paired strings give a bright, shimmering, chorused pluck with the courses' slight detune beating against each other; the sparkling voice of Andean folk. Shimmer sets the course detune.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChayNeuron}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChayNeuron}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chay neuron: a three-variable model of a pancreatic-beta-cell membrane whose calcium dynamics gate bursts of spikes, producing complex chaotic bursting patterns - rhythmic clusters of activity.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cheby}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cheby}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chebyshev waveshaping oscillator: a sine is shaped by a Chebyshev polynomial so Order directly selects which harmonic dominates, and Drive blends from pure sine to the full harmonic. Precise additive-style timbres with one knob.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChebyMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChebyMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chebyshev map: iterates x -> cos(n * arccos(x)), the degree-n Chebyshev polynomial, which is exactly, provably chaotic on [-1,1] for n>=2 and uniformly fills the interval - mathematically pure deterministic noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChebyshevRippleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChebyshevRippleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chebyshev-ripple oscillator: each cycle is the Chebyshev type-I lowpass magnitude response 1/sqrt(1+Ripple^2*T_n(x)^2), a passband that ripples up and down Order times then drops in a steep cliff - a wavy flat-top with a sharp cutoff, distinct from the smooth Butterworth response.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChebyshevUOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChebyshevUOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chebyshev U wave: the second-kind Chebyshev polynomial U_n, which equals sin((n+1)theta)/sin(theta) - a comb of n+1 evenly-weighted partials whose order sets the brightness; the second-kind sibling of the harmonic-stacking first kind.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChebyshevVOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChebyshevVOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chebyshev-V oscillator: each cycle is a third-kind Chebyshev polynomial V_n(x) (2x-1, 4x^2-2x-1, 8x^3-4x^2-4x+1) over [-1,1] - an asymmetric orthogonal-polynomial waveform distinct from the first-kind (T) and second-kind (U) Chebyshev shapes. Order picks the degree.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChebyshevWOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChebyshevWOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chebyshev-W oscillator: each cycle is a fourth-kind Chebyshev polynomial W_n(x) (2x+1, 4x^2+2x-1, 8x^3+4x^2-4x-1) over [-1,1] - the mirror partner of the third-kind V, an asymmetric orthogonal-polynomial waveform with the opposite endpoint behaviour. Order picks the degree.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChemostatChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChemostatChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Forced chemostat: nutrient feeding a microbe feeding a predator in a flow reactor; a periodic dilution rate pushes the three-trophic chain into chaos - a lab-realizable ecological chaos with Monod-type uptake kinetics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChenCelikovsky}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChenCelikovsky}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chen-Celikovsky attractor: a Lorenz-family system at the opposite end of the Vanecek-Celikovsky classification, with the cross-term moved to a different equation, producing a distinct twin-scroll orbit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChenDistOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChenDistOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chen oscillator: each cycle is the Chen PDF Beta*x^(Beta-1)*e^(x^Beta)*e^(-Lambda(e^(x^Beta)-1)), a lifetime distribution with a bathtub-shaped hazard - it produces a sharply-rising, hard-cut-off bump whose abruptness Beta and Lambda set. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChenLeeHyper}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChenLeeHyper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic Chen-Lee: the rigid-body Chen-Lee attractor extended with a fourth state and feedback into hyperchaos, combining the symmetric multi-lobe orbit with two-directional chaotic expansion.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChenLeeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChenLeeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chen-Lee attractor: a model of rigid-body rotation with feedback that is also studied in anti-control of chaos, tracing a smooth, symmetric multi-lobed orbit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChenMomeni}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChenMomeni}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chen-Momeni attractor: a chaotic system engineered to produce a four-wing orbit through balanced quadratic couplings, switching between four lobes for jumpy, multi-state modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChenOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChenOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chen attractor: a double-scroll chaotic flow (a cousin of Lorenz with a different fold), producing a denser, more energetic chaotic signal for harsh tones and wild modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChialvoMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChialvoMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chialvo neuron map: a two-variable iterated model of an excitable neuron that spikes and bursts via an exponential recovery term, clocked at a chosen rate for sharp neural firing patterns.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Chip}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Chip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chiptune / 8-bit lead (NES-style): a pulse wave at a selectable duty cycle, quantized down to the lo-fi console DAC crunch, with the classic fast 'arpeggio' that machine-guns the pitch between chord tones to fake a chord on a single channel - the happy-hardcore / 8-bit-rave lead. Tune sets the pitch (in0 = Pitch CV), Duty the pulse width (0 = thin 12.5 percent .. 1 = 50 percent square), Arp the arpeggio speed in Hz (0 = off; when on it fakes a major chord), Vibrato the pitch wobble, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChipOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChipOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chiptune pulse oscillator (NES / Game Boy voice): a square with four selectable duty cycles - 12.5%, 25%, 50%, 75% - for the classic 8-bit pulse timbres, from thin and reedy to full and hollow. Duty steps through the four widths, Level scales the output. in0 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChirikovStd}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChirikovStd}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Standard map: the Chirikov kicked-rotor, the textbook gateway from regular to chaotic motion; raising the kick strength K dissolves smooth orbits into global chaos - tunable order-to-chaos as a sound source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Chirp}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Chirp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chirp / sweep oscillator: a trigger on in0 sweeps the pitch from the base Freq up (or down) by Range octaves over Time, then holds - the rising 'pew' zap and falling laser drop. in1 transposes the base pitch. A one-shot frequency sweep, not the continuous Generative walk.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChiSquaredNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChiSquaredNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chi-squared noise: the sum of the squares of k Gaussian samples (degrees of freedom k), the distribution behind variance estimates and goodness-of-fit tests; right-skewed for small k and approaching Gaussian as k grows - a positive, skew-tunable source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChlorineArsenite}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChlorineArsenite}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Iodate-arsenite reaction: an autocatalytic bistable chemistry in a stirred-flow reactor whose feed-vs-reaction competition flips it between low and high states; a slow inflow modulation gives relaxation switching between the two branches.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChoirPad}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChoirPad}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Choir 'aah' pad: a lush vocal ensemble - several detuned voices through an open vowel formant with a gentle vibrato, the synth-choir pad. Vowel morphs the vowel, Width the detune, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChoppingOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChoppingOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chopping: a knife chopping vegetables on a wooden board, a series of short woody thwack impacts at a steady rhythm each a damped low-mid resonance, the rhythmic prep-cook sound; the chop rate sets the tempo.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChorusUnisonOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChorusUnisonOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chorus-unison oscillator: seven sine voices detuned around the pitch and each slowly wandering in tuning, the rich, swirling 'super-unison' of a thick analog stack or a choir singing in not-quite-unison; the spread sets how lush the swarm.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChossatGolubitsky}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChossatGolubitsky}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chossat-Golubitsky icon: an iterated complex map built to commute with a dihedral symmetry group, so its chaotic orbit fills a perfectly symmetric mandala-like attractor - 'symmetry in chaos' as a structured source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChuaArray}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChuaArray}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coupled Chua circuits: two Chua double-scroll oscillators linked through their voltages, which synchronize, phase-lock or desynchronize into spatiotemporal chaos as the coupling changes - a minimal cell of a chaotic-circuit array.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChuaCircuit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChuaCircuit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chua's circuit: the canonical analog chaos generator, three states coupled through a piecewise-linear (Chua diode) nonlinearity that produces the famous double-scroll attractor - the most-studied chaotic oscillator in electronics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChuaCubic}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChuaCubic}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cubic Chua: Chua's circuit with the piecewise-linear diode replaced by a smooth cubic characteristic, which removes the corners and gives a rounder, smoother double-scroll than the classic piecewise Chua.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChuaInductorless}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChuaInductorless}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Inductor-free Chua: an op-amp/gyrator realization of Chua's circuit that replaces the bulky inductor with a synthetic-impedance block, giving the same double-scroll chaos in a fully-active circuit - read as the capacitor voltage.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChuaMemristor}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChuaMemristor}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Memristive Chua: Chua's circuit with the Chua diode replaced by a flux-controlled memristor, adding a fourth (flux) state and extreme multistability to the classic double-scroll - a canonical memristive chaos circuit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChuaMultiScroll}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChuaMultiScroll}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Multi-scroll Chua: Chua's circuit with a staircase (multi-breakpoint) nonlinearity instead of the single Chua diode, so the attractor grows several stacked scrolls the orbit wanders between - richer chaos than the classic double-scroll.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChuaSine}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChuaSine}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sine Chua: Chua's circuit with the diode replaced by a sinusoidal characteristic, so the single double-scroll becomes a long row of scrolls the orbit can hop along - a grid-multi-scroll generalization of Chua.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChuaTorus}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChuaTorus}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Torus-breakdown Chua: Chua's circuit parameterized in its two-frequency quasiperiodic regime, where an invariant torus wrinkles and breaks into chaos as the coupling shifts - the Ruelle-Takens route inside the canonical chaos circuit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
