\phantomsection\label{blk:CicadaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CicadaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cicada oscillator: a piercing high drone built from a very fast train of tymbal clicks (the buckling ribs of the cicada's sound organ) under a slow intensity swell, the deafening summer wall of sound a chorus of cicadas makes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cicadas}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cicadas}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cicada drone - the dense, buzzing, amplitude-pulsing wall of a summer afternoon. Buzz sets the rasp rate, Pitch the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cimbalom}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cimbalom}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cimbalom: the Hungarian hammered dulcimer - metal strings STRUCK by soft mallets (a sharp struck excitation, not a pluck) ringing into a long, bright, shimmering metallic sustain; the ringing, reverberant voice of Romani and Eastern-European music; re-struck at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CircadianGoodwin}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CircadianGoodwin}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Goodwin circadian oscillator: the original three-variable enzyme model (mRNA -> protein -> repressor, the repressor inhibiting the gene through a steep Hill term) that gives the ~24-hour molecular oscillation at the core of circadian clocks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CircadianOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CircadianOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Circadian oscillator: a smooth, asymmetric daily-rhythm curve (a fundamental plus a second harmonic to model the steep morning rise and gentle evening decline) of the body's internal clock, rendered as a slow, organic, sun-like swell.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CircEaseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CircEaseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Circular-ease oscillator: each cycle is the easeOutCirc curve sqrt(1-(t-1)^2), a quarter-circle arc that rises steeply at first then flattens to a horizontal tangent at the top - a geometric ramp with a distinctly rounded shoulder unlike a parabola or cosine.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CIRProcess}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CIRProcess}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cox-Ingersoll-Ross process: dr = a(b-r)dt + sigma*sqrt(r)*dW, a mean-reverting random walk whose square-root diffusion keeps it non-negative (used for interest rates and as Heston's variance) - colored noise that hugs a floor and reverts to a level.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Clap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Clap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Analog hand-clap: a trigger on in0 fires band-passed noise shaped by a multi-burst envelope - three quick retriggers then a diffuse tail - the characteristic 909 clap. Tone sets the band-pass colour, Spread the gap between the bursts, Decay the tail length, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Clap707}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Clap707}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-707 hand clap: three rapid noise bursts plus a short bright tail through a highpass - tighter and crisper than the 808 clap, the 707's house clap. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the tail, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Clap808}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Clap808}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-808 hand clap: a trigger fires three rapid bursts of bandpassed noise followed by a longer diffuse tail - the stacked-hands attack and room ring of the iconic 808 clap. Tone sets the noise colour, Decay the tail length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Clap909}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Clap909}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Handclap: a trigger on in0 fires three quick bursts of band-passed noise (the multiple hands) followed by a longer diffuse tail - the classic drum-machine clap. Spread sets the gap between bursts, Decay the tail length, Tone the noise colour, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClappOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClappOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaotic Clapp oscillator: the Clapp RF oscillator (a Colpitts with an extra series capacitor) driven by its transistor nonlinearity into chaos, a three-state circuit with a tighter frequency-defining loop than the Colpitts.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClapSynth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClapSynth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Synth hand clap: a gate fires a cluster of fast high-passed noise bursts with a reverb-like tail, recreating the multi-slap transient of several hands clapping - a drum-machine clap voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClapSynthOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClapSynthOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hand-clap voice: three quick stuttered noise bursts followed by a diffuse decaying tail, the 808 clap trick that mimics many hands not quite clapping together; re-triggered at the set rate, the spread knob sets the burst spacing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Clarinet}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Clarinet}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Breath, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clarinet: a closed-pipe reed voice with only odd harmonics (a softened square through a woody lowpass) plus breath noise, played by holding in0 as breath. The hollow, reedy clarinet timbre. Tune sets the pitch, Breath the air noise, Level the output. in0 = Breath gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClarinetWGOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClarinetWGOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clarinet waveguide: a delay-line bore with a phase-inverting reflection (giving the odd-harmonic, hollow timbre of a closed-open cylinder) driven by a clipped single-reed nonlinearity; the breath knob blows it from a pure tone into a squawk.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClausenOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClausenOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clausen oscillator: sums sin(k*theta)/k^Order over the harmonics (the Clausen function for Order=2) - the sine-series counterpart to the polylog's cosine series, giving a smooth odd-symmetric waveform whose harmonic roll-off Order sets, band-limited to Nyquist.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Clav}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Clav}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clavinet (Hohner D6): a Karplus-Strong plucked string struck by a gate, run through a pickup comb filter that notches the spectrum - the bright, percussive, slap-funk voice of funk, disco, French house and funky house. A rubber-tip excitation burst fills the string, the feedback loop rings at the pitch, and the comb pickup gives the signature hollow bite. Tune sets the pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Decay the string sustain, Bright the string damping and attack brightness, Pickup the comb depth (the clav twang), and Level the output. A gate on in0 plucks it; play it staccato.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Clave}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Clave}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal clave / woodblock: a trigger on in0 strikes a solid piece of wood - two tight high resonant modes (about 1 : 1.5) that ring for only a few milliseconds, the bright dry 'tok' that cuts through any mix. Tune sets the pitch (clave up high, woodblock lower), Decay the very short ring, Level the output. No pitch bend, no body - just wood. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Clave808}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Clave808}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-808 clave / rimshot click: a single high resonant sine ping with a very fast decay - the dry, sharp woodblock 'tick' that cuts through a mix. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the click length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClaveHit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClaveHit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clave: a single very-high-Q resonant mode rings briefly when struck, the sharp, bright, dry crack of a clave or rimshot - a one-mode percussion voice excited by the input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClaveSynthOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClaveSynthOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clave voice: a single high, dry, quickly-decaying resonant tone, the crisp 'tok' of two claves or a woodblock struck together, re-triggered at the set rate; the classic Latin-percussion click.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Clavinet}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Clavinet}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clavinet: a hammered string struck against a metal anvil - a bright, percussive Karplus-Strong pluck with a sharp funky attack, the Hohner D6 wah-bait. Bright sets the string brilliance, Decay the damping, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClavinetVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClavinetVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clavinet voice: a bright, tightly-damped Karplus string struck by a gate and read through a pickup comb filter, the funky, percussive, wah-friendly tone of a Hohner Clavinet.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Clavioline}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Clavioline}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clavioline: the 1947 valve monosynth (the riff on the Tornados' 'Telstar') - a buzzy reed-organ square with octave-thick harmonics and a slight vibrato. Buzz sets the harmonic edge, Vibrato the wobble, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClickConsonantOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClickConsonantOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Click oscillator: very brief, sharply-band-passed transients produced by a sudden release of an oral vacuum, the click consonants of Khoisan and Bantu languages; the tone knob shifts the click from a deep 'pop' to a high 'tsk'.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CliffordAttr}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CliffordAttr}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clifford attractor: a 2-D trigonometric iterated map (sin/cos of cross terms) that traces delicate, swirling fractal curves - a distinctive strange-attractor texture, sampled at a chosen rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClockEscapementOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClockEscapementOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clock escapement: the tick-tock of a mechanical clock, two slightly-different short resonant clicks alternating at the pendulum rate (the escapement releasing one tooth per swing); the rate sets the pendulum length.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClockTick}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClockTick}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clock - a steady tick-tock with two alternating click pitches, the metronome/grandfather-clock pulse. Rate sets the tempo, Tone the click character, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CO2Laser}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CO2Laser}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modulated CO2 laser: a single-mode laser whose cavity loss is periodically modulated drives the intensity, population inversion and polarization into chaotic spiking - the experimentally-classic route to laser chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CobwebModel}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CobwebModel}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cobweb market map: prices set from last period's expectation against a nonlinear supply curve; when supply is steep enough the price iterates period-double into chaotic boom-bust swings - the cobweb theorem gone wild.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Coin}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Coin}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Arcade coin pickup - the classic two-note 'bling' reward chime, auto-repeating. Rate sets the repeat interval, Pitch the note, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoinPickupOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoinPickupOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coin pickup: the iconic platformer coin-grab, a square-wave tone that snaps up a major sixth partway through (the cheerful two-note 'bling') and decays, fired at the set rate; the reward sound of arcade games.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CollatzAudio}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CollatzAudio}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Collatz sequence: the famous 3n+1 iteration (halve if even, else 3n+1) restarted from a growing seed, whose wildly-varying hailstone trajectories are clocked out as an erratic stepped control voltage - number theory you can hear.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CollatzFractal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CollatzFractal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Collatz fractal: the 3n+1 rule extended to the complex plane by its smooth interpolation z -> (2+7z-(2+5z)cos(pi z))/4, whose iteration draws the fractal boundary of the Collatz basins; the reseeded orbit's real part makes a spiky CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ColorNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ColorNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Noise generator producing white, pink or brown noise from an xorshift source filtered to taste. Color selects the spectrum: flat white, a one-pole pink-tilted blend, or a heavily integrated brown noise with a steeper low-frequency emphasis. Level sets the output amplitude, making it a self-running source for textures, test signals or modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ColpittsHyper}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ColpittsHyper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic Colpitts: the chaotic Colpitts LC oscillator augmented with an extra LC tank and coupling, lifting it to four dimensions with two positive Lyapunov exponents - a broadband RF hyperchaos generator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ColpittsOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ColpittsOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaotic Colpitts: the classic Colpitts LC oscillator pushed into chaos by its transistor's exponential nonlinearity, a real radio-frequency circuit whose three state variables (two capacitor voltages, one inductor current) tumble chaotically.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CombFmOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CombFmOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Comb-resonated FM: a 2-operator FM tone is run through a feedback comb tuned to the same fundamental, so the comb reinforces the FM's harmonic series into a sharp, resonant, almost-physical timbre that neither FM nor a comb makes alone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CombHarmonicOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CombHarmonicOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Comb-harmonic oscillator: a full harmonic series whose partial amplitudes are scalloped by a raised-cosine comb, carving evenly-spaced spectral notches like a comb/flanger frozen onto an oscillator; the spacing knob slides the teeth through the spectrum.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CombResonanceOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CombResonanceOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Comb-resonance oscillator: a periodic impulse fed into a tuned feedback comb filter, whose evenly-spaced resonant peaks ring out a hollow, metallic, pitched timbre - the sound of plucked-string and flanger resonance frozen into a tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CombustionInstability}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CombustionInstability}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Combustion instability: an acoustic chamber mode coupled to a time-lagged, saturating flame heat-release (the Crocco n-tau model), which self-amplifies into the screeching limit-cycle and chaotic howl that plagues rockets and gas turbines.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ComplexOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ComplexOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Complex oscillator (Make Noise DPO / Buchla 259): a modulation oscillator thru-zero-FMs a primary oscillator, whose output is then run through a sine wavefolder for rich, evolving harmonics. Freq sets the primary pitch, Ratio the modulator's frequency relative to it, FM the thru-zero modulation depth (inharmonic clang at high settings), and Fold the wavefolder drive (adds bright folded harmonics). One voice spanning clean tones to metallic, animated timbres. Distinct from the separate ThruZero and Wavefolder blocks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CompoundPoisson}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CompoundPoisson}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Compound Poisson process: a running total that jumps by a random amount each time a Poisson event fires and holds flat in between, the staircase model of accumulated insurance claims or queue arrivals - a piecewise-constant random ramp, reset on overflow.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ComputerBootOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ComputerBootOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Computer boot: the cheerful ascending run of data tones a retro computer plays on power-up, a square-wave stepping upward through a scale then looping, the bleeping startup chime of a vintage machine; the rate sets the boot speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ConfirmBlipOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ConfirmBlipOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Confirm blip: the short positive blip of a confirmed action or accepted input, a single clean tone with a quick upward pitch chirp, the OK / accepted feedback; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Conga}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Conga}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal conga: a trigger on in0 strikes a tall hand drum - a deeper, woodier membrane than the Bongo, with modes near 1 : 1.6 : 2.2, a longer open ring and a pronounced pitch drop. Tune sets the head pitch, Decay the ring length, Tone moves from an open tone toward a tighter heel-slap, Level the output. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Conga808}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Conga808}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-808 conga: a tuned, resonant sine drum with a short pitch drop and medium decay - the round, woody 808 conga/tom voice used for melodic fills. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CongaHit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CongaHit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Conga/bongo: two pitched membrane modes with a short decay ring when struck by the input, the round, hand-drum tone of a tuned skin - feed an impulse or gate to play.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CongaSynthOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CongaSynthOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Conga voice: a clear pitched membrane tone with a slight upward pitch snap and a short skin-like decay, the warm open-hand-drum hit of a conga or bongo, re-triggered at the set rate; tune from a low tumba to a high quinto.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ConservativeJerk}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ConservativeJerk}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Conservative jerk: a third-order jerk system with zero net damping (the divergence vanishes), so it preserves phase-space volume and its chaos is conservative - dense, non-attracting, sticky orbits rather than a thin attractor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ContopoulosOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ContopoulosOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Contopoulos system: a Hamiltonian model of a star orbiting in a galactic potential with two coupled anharmonic oscillators, whose ordered orbits dissolve into chaos as the coupling grows - conservative galactic-dynamics chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ConveyorRumbleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ConveyorRumbleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Conveyor rumble: the low continuous rumble of a running conveyor belt over its rollers, with a periodic bump each time the belt seam passes a roller, the industrial drone of a moving belt; the bump rate sets the belt speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CorkPopOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CorkPopOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cork pop: the hollow 'pop' of a cork leaving a bottle (or a finger-in-cheek pop), a short resonant blip with a fast upward pitch chirp and a soft thump, fired at the set rate; the celebratory uncorking.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoronaDischargeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoronaDischargeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Corona discharge: the faint hiss of ionised air leaking off a high-voltage line, a band of high-frequency noise with a continuous fizz of tiny micro-discharges, the eerie crackle around power transmission gear in damp weather.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoronaTrichel}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoronaTrichel}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Trichel-pulse corona: the self-quenching current pulses of a negative corona discharge, where each avalanche builds space charge that chokes itself off until it clears; raising the voltage drives the pulse train from regular into chaotic.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Corrugaphone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Corrugaphone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Corrugaphone (whirly tube): the singing corrugated tube you spin overhead - airflow past the ridges sheds vortices that lock to the nearest tube harmonic, so raising Flow makes the pitch JUMP up the harmonic series (2nd, 3rd, 4th...) rather than glide, and it can never sound the fundamental. A breathy, stepped, otherworldly voice no flute here makes. in0 is the Pitch CV (tube fundamental); Flow selects the harmonic, Breath the airy noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CosineMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CosineMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cosine map: iterates x -> a cos(x), whose single smooth fold yields period-doubling and chaos as the amplitude rises - a transcendental cousin of the logistic map with a rounder, symmetric hump.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CosmicNoiseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CosmicNoiseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cosmic noise: the steady background hiss of the radio sky - galactic synchrotron emission and the cosmic microwave background - modelled as a tunable power-law colored noise from white toward deep red; the color knob sweeps the spectral tilt of the cosmos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoughOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoughOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cough: a sudden glottal burst - a short voiced thump opening into a rasp of turbulent throat noise that quickly fades, the explosive clearing of a cough, fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoulletOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoulletOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coullet attractor: a jerk-equation chaotic system with a cubic restoring term, producing a smooth single-scroll orbit - a clean, low-cost chaos source related to the Genesio-Tesi family.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CountdownBeepOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CountdownBeepOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Countdown beep: a timer ticking down, a short clean beep repeated at a steady rate then a long final tone, the tense countdown of a game show or bomb timer; the rate sets the beep interval.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoupledFitzHugh}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoupledFitzHugh}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coupled FitzHugh-Nagumo: two excitable cells linked by diffusion that synchronize in-phase, lock anti-phase or fire chaotically out of step as the coupling and excitability change - a minimal model of neural-pair rhythm coordination.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoupledFormOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoupledFormOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coupled-form oscillator: two state variables rotate around a circle via the magic-circle recursion s1+=eps*s2; s2-=eps*s1 with eps=2*sin(pi*f/fs), producing a sine by recursive feedback rather than a phase accumulator. Magnitude-stable, self-starting, and naturally in quadrature - the resonant-oscillator structure used in Chamberlin SVFs.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoupledLogistic}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoupledLogistic}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coupled logistic maps: two logistic-map oscillators that exchange a fraction of their state each step (a coupled-map-lattice cell), which can synchronize, beat or desynchronize into spatiotemporal chaos as coupling changes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoupledLorenz}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoupledLorenz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coupled Lorenz: two Lorenz oscillators linked through their x-variables, sweeping from independent chaos through on-off intermittent synchronization to full identical sync as coupling rises - the textbook chaos-synchronization demonstration.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoupledMapLattice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoupledMapLattice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coupled map lattice (Kaneko): a ring of logistic maps each diffusively coupled to its two neighbours, producing spatiotemporal chaos, traveling waves and frozen-random domains as the coupling and nonlinearity vary - local coupling, unlike the mean-field global version.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoupledPendulumPair}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoupledPendulumPair}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coupled pendulum pair: two pendula linked by a torsion spring trade energy back and forth (the classic beating-modes demo); driven hard, their angles fall out of step into chaotic, never-repeating swings.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoupledRossler}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoupledRossler}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coupled Rossler: two Rossler oscillators linked through their x-variables, which transition between phase synchronization, lag synchronization and high-dimensional hyperchaos as the coupling and detuning change - a classic chaos-synchronization testbed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CournotDuopoly}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CournotDuopoly}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cournot duopoly: two firms adjust output toward their best response against the other's last move; with nonlinear costs and fast adjustment their outputs period-double into chaotic quantity wars - economic game-theory chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cowbell}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cowbell}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Analog cowbell (808-style): a trigger on in0 fires two detuned pulse oscillators a fixed ratio apart, band-passed and shaped by a short percussive decay for the classic metallic clank. Tune sets the lower oscillator, Detune the ratio of the upper one (the cowbell interval), Decay the ring length, and Level the output. Patch a Clock, Euclid or sequencer gate into in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cowbell808}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cowbell808}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-808 cowbell: a trigger on in0 fires the two-square-oscillator 'clonk' (640 Hz + 800 Hz) through a bandpass with a medium decay - the unmistakable 808 cowbell. Tune scales the pitch, Decay the length, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CowbellSynth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CowbellSynth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Synth cowbell: two square oscillators a dissonant interval apart, band-passed and shaped by a sharp percussive envelope on each gate - the unmistakable 808 cowbell voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CowbellSynthOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CowbellSynthOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cowbell voice: two square oscillators a non-harmonic interval apart (the famous 808 cowbell ratio near 845/540 Hz) gated by a sharp decay, the clanky percussion that demands more cowbell; re-triggered at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CowMooOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CowMooOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cow moo: the long low lowing of a cow, a deep voiced 'moo' vowel that swells and falls with a slight pitch droop and a breathy edge, fired at the set rate; the placid sound of the pasture.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CPGNetwork}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CPGNetwork}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Central pattern generator: three mutually-inhibiting neural oscillators that settle into rotating phase patterns (the circuits that produce walking, swimming and breathing gaits); detuned, they wander chaotically between gait patterns.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CR78Hat}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CR78Hat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Roland CR-78 hi-hat: the lo-fi metallic hat of the CompuRhythm - a thin cluster of detuned squares mixed with noise through a sharp highpass, with a fast decay. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Crackle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Crackle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vinyl/dust crackle generator - random filtered pops and ticks for lo-fi, vinyl and analog-warmth textures. Density sets how busy the crackle is, Tone the pop brightness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CraneSway}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CraneSway}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crane sway: a payload hanging from a trolley that is driven back and forth; the pendulum's nonlinear response to the trolley acceleration swings, beats and chaotically slews - the load-sway problem crane controllers fight.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Crash808}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Crash808}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-808 crash cymbal: a long, splashy wash of six inharmonic squares plus noise through a highpass, decaying over a second - the 808's big accent cymbal. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the wash length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Crash909}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Crash909}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crash cymbal: a long, bright wash of inharmonic squares plus white noise through a highpass, decaying over a second or more - the splashy crash for accents and builds. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the wash length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CreakyFloorOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CreakyFloorOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Creaky floor: an old wooden floorboard groaning under a footstep, a low woody resonance that bends in pitch as the weight shifts with a stick-slip creak, fired at the set rate; the giveaway of sneaking through an old house.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CreakyVoiceOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CreakyVoiceOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Creaky voice (vocal fry): very low, irregularly-spaced glottal pulses with jittered timing, the popping, rattling register at the bottom of the voice; the jitter knob sets how erratic and gravelly the creak becomes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Creature}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Creature}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Monster / creature roar: a vocal-cord source (saw plus a sub-octave for size) roughened by a vocal-fry flutter, shaped by a sweeping formant band-pass (the vowel of the roar) and driven through distortion - the layered beast vocalization of film and game sound design. Tune sets the pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Growl the vocal-fry roughness (the guttural rasp), Formant the vowel centre, Size the sub-octave weight and how big the creature reads, Drive the aggression, and Level the output. A gate on in0 sounds the roar with a pitch-up attack. Distinct from the musical Growl bass - this is an organic vocalization.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CreepyLaughOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CreepyLaughOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Creepy laugh: a distant unsettling cackle, a low voiced tone chopped into a 'heh-heh-heh' laugh pattern with a hollow distorted edge, the disembodied villain laugh of horror; the laugh rate sets the cackle speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CricketChirpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CricketChirpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cricket chirp: a high carrier tone gated into short pulses grouped into chirps with silent gaps, the stridulation of a cricket whose chirp rate famously tracks temperature (Dolbear's law); a pulsed bioacoustic insect tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Crickets}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Crickets}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Night crickets - rhythmic high chirps over a faint air bed for an evening/forest ambience. Rate sets the chirp tempo, Pitch the chirp frequency, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CristalBaschet}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CristalBaschet}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cristal Baschet: the Baschet brothers' glass-rod organ - wetted fingers stroke glass rods coupled to metal resonators, a friction-driven, extremely high-Q glassy tone with a slow shimmer and long ring, the ethereal sound of Forbidden Planet / experimental film scores.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrookedEggOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrookedEggOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crooked-egg oscillator: each cycle is cos^3(theta)+sin^3(theta), the radial form of the crooked-egg curve - a lopsided wave that factors into (cos+sin)(1-cos*sin), giving an asymmetric fundamental-plus-third-harmonic shape with a distinct skew. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrotaleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrotaleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crotale: a small thick tuned bronze disc struck with a mallet, ringing a high, bright, slowly-decaying tone with shimmering inharmonic upper partials (the antique-cymbal sound of orchestral crotales); re-struck at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Crotales}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Crotales}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crotales (antique cymbals): small tuned bronze discs with a bright, high, shimmering inharmonic ring and a very long decay - the sparkling orchestral bell-cymbal. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the shimmer, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Crowd}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Crowd}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crowd murmur - band-passed noise with a slow random swell for the ambient hubbub of a room or stadium. Energy sets the activity, Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrowdGaspOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrowdGaspOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crowd gasp: a sudden collective intake of breath as an audience reacts in shock, a swelling band of airy breath noise that rushes in and tensely holds before fading, fired at the set rate; the held-breath of a stunned crowd.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrowdLaughOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrowdLaughOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crowd laugh: an audience erupting in laughter, many overlapping voiced 'ha-ha' bursts at scattered pitches swelling to a peak then dying down, the sitcom laugh-track wash; the rate sets the laughter's pace.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrowdMurmurOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrowdMurmurOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crowd murmur: the indistinct babble of a room full of talking people, a band of voiced-formant noise that swells and ebbs with the ebb and flow of conversation, the hum of a busy hall before a show.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrowdWhistleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrowdWhistleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crowd whistle: the piercing scattered whistles of an excited or jeering crowd, randomly-timed high near-sine chirps at varying pitches over a faint murmur, the catcalls of a stadium; the density sets how many are whistling.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrtMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrtMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chua cubic map: a discrete two-dimensional map with the cubic characteristic of a Chua circuit, folding the plane through an S-shaped nonlinearity into a double-scroll-flavoured fractal attractor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cubic1DMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cubic1DMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cubic map: the odd-symmetric iterated map x -> a x - x^3, whose two humps give a period-doubling cascade with symmetry-breaking and coexisting attractors - a bipolar cousin of the logistic map.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cuica}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cuica}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal cuica: a trigger on in0 plays the Brazilian friction drum - a resonant tone whose pitch swoops up or down as a wet thumb drags the internal stick, the squeaking, talking, almost human voice of a samba bateria. Tune sets the base pitch, Decay the ring, Glide the direction and depth of the pitch swoop (bipolar), Level the output. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer; modulate Glide for talking lines.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CurseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CurseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Curse: a dark malediction, a low cluster of dissonant detuned partials sliding slowly downward with a menacing beating growl, the ominous swell of a hex or debuff; the slide rate sets how fast the dread descends.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CuspMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CuspMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cusp map: an iterated map with a square-root nonlinearity (x -> 1 - a*sqrt(|x|)) that folds the line through a cusp, giving a spiky chaotic stream distinct from the polynomial maps.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CutleryClinkOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CutleryClinkOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cutlery clink: metal forks, knives and spoons clinking as they are sorted or dropped in a drawer, a random scatter of short bright high-pitched metallic clinks, the table-setting jingle; density sets how busy.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CyclicCARing}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CyclicCARing}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cyclic CA: a ring of cells with N colours where a cell advances to its next colour if a neighbour already holds that colour; this 'eat-the-next-colour' rule spontaneously forms rotating waves and spirals that lock into a periodic pattern - read out as a mean-colour CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CycloidOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CycloidOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cycloid oscillator: the arch traced by a point on the rim of a rolling wheel (the brachistochrone and tautochrone curve), here the height profile per revolution - a series of smooth arches with cusps where the point momentarily touches the ground.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cymbal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cymbal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Analog cymbal (808-style metal): a trigger on in0 fires six square oscillators tuned to a fixed inharmonic ratio set, summed and high-passed, with a fast onset plus a long metallic tail. Distinct from the Hat (which filters noise) - this is the additive square-oscillator method for ride/crash timbres. Tune sets the base frequency of the cluster, Decay the tail length, Tone the high-pass brightness, and Level the output. Patch a Clock, Euclid or sequencer gate into in0; patch a Note source into in1 to keytrack the cluster.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Cymbal808}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Cymbal808}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-808-style cymbal/crash: like the 808 hat but with six inharmonic square oscillators ringing through a long, shimmering, slowly-decaying highpassed tail. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the (long) ring length, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CymbalSynth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CymbalSynth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Synth cymbal: six square oscillators at inharmonic ratios are summed, band-passed bright and mixed with high-passed noise, then struck by a gate with a long decay - the metallic 808-style cymbal/ride voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CymbalSynthOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CymbalSynthOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cymbal voice: a dense bank of inharmonic square oscillators high-passed into a shimmering metallic wash with a long decay (the 909 crash recipe), re-triggered at the set rate; the decay knob sets ride versus crash.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CzPulseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CzPulseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

CZ phase-distortion pulse: the cosine phase ramps to the half point, holds flat, then ramps on - the held section pins the carrier at its trough, producing the hollow, square/pulse-like Casio CZ tone. Width sets the ramp portion (narrow = squarer/brighter).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CzSawOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CzSawOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

CZ phase-distortion saw: a cosine carrier is read through a two-segment phase ramp with an inflection at Bend, speeding the phase early then slowing it - the classic Casio CZ sawtooth, morphing from a pure sine (Bend 0.5) to a bright buzzy saw (Bend near 0) with no aliasing-prone hard edges.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DadrasOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DadrasOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dadras attractor: a four-parameter chaotic flow that sweeps out a wide, multi-scroll orbit, a high-energy strange attractor useful for broad, unpredictable modulation and rough tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DagumOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DagumOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dagum oscillator: each cycle is the Dagum (Burr-III) PDF a*p*x^(a*p-1)/(1+x^a)^(p+1), a flexible skewed income-distribution bump - A controls the rise sharpness and P the tail, peak-normalized to a bounded asymmetric waveform distinct from the Beta/Wald skews.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DagumQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DagumQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dagum-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the Dagum inverse-CDF (p^(-1/P)-1)^(-1/A), an income-distribution quantile that stays low then accelerates into a heavy upper tail - A and P jointly set the rise and the tail steepness. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DaisyWorld}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DaisyWorld}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Daisyworld: Lovelock and Watson's model of black and white daisies regulating a planet's temperature through albedo feedback; under a rising forcing it gives slow self-stabilizing population oscillations - Gaia-hypothesis dynamics as a modulator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DansgaardOeschger}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DansgaardOeschger}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dansgaard-Oeschger events: the abrupt millennial warm/cold flips recorded in ice cores, modelled as a bistable climate potential with a slow ramp that triggers stochastic-resonance-like jumps between glacial states - very-slow climate flicker.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Darbuka}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Darbuka}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Darbuka / goblet drum: the sharp Middle-Eastern hand drum with a deep 'dum' at the centre and a crisp 'tek' at the rim - Rim morphs from the low boom to the bright edge slap. Tune sets the pitch, Rim the dum-to-tek balance, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Daxophone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Daxophone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Daxophone: Hans Reichel's bowed wooden tongue - a strip of wood bowed at one end, its pitch set by where it is clamped, producing uncannily vocal, animal-like cries and barks; a friction-driven waveguide through a movable formant resonance that gives it the talking, throaty character. Voice sweeps the formant (the clamp position).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DayGrowth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DayGrowth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Day's growth map: Richard Day's classical model where capital grows by saving but is dragged down nonlinearly by pollution/diminishing returns, giving a hump-shaped map that period-doubles into chaotic boom-bust growth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DblPendulum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DblPendulum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Double pendulum: simulates two coupled pendulums whose motion is famously chaotic and exquisitely sensitive to initial conditions, reading the bob angles as the output - swinging, organic chaos with sudden flips.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DCMotorChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DCMotorChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

DC motor chaos: a brushed DC motor under PWM supply whose Stribeck (velocity-weakening) friction and back-EMF coupling make the shaft speed stick-slip and chaotically ripple - a real drive-system instability.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DeathwatchBeetleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DeathwatchBeetleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Deathwatch beetle: the eerie tapping of a deathwatch beetle knocking its head against old timber to attract a mate, a short series of dry resonant ticks repeated after a pause, the ominous tick of quiet old houses.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DeffuantOpinion}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DeffuantOpinion}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Deffuant model: agents holding continuous opinions meet in random pairs and move toward each other only if they already agree within a confidence threshold; wide thresholds collapse everyone to consensus, narrow ones freeze into separate opinion clusters - the spread tracks that.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DegaussOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DegaussOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Degauss: the deep wobbling thunk of a CRT monitor degaussing, a low mains-frequency tone that surges loud as the coil energises then rings down and decays to nothing over a couple of seconds, looping at the trigger rate; the classic booiiing of an old screen.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
