\phantomsection\label{blk:DeJongAttr}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DeJongAttr}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

De Jong attractor: a four-parameter trigonometric iterated map famous for intricate web-like fractal forms, producing a busy, fine-grained chaotic texture as an audio/CV source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DelayDuffing}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DelayDuffing}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Delayed Duffing: the twin-well Duffing oscillator whose restoring force is augmented by a time-delayed copy of its own position, so the feedback delay alone (no external forcing) destabilizes it into chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DelayedHenon}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DelayedHenon}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Delayed Henon map: the Henon recurrence rewired to feed back a delayed value (x_n = 1 - a x_{n-2}^2 + b x_{n-1}), which raises the attractor's dimension and can make it hyperchaotic - delay-boosted map chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DengToggle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DengToggle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Deng toggling system: a chaotic flow whose nonlinearity is a toggling (sign-switching) feedback that flips the attractor between scroll regions, generating a grid of scrolls the orbit hops across - a multi-scroll chaos generator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DequanLiOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DequanLiOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dequan Li attractor: a chaotic flow with several quadratic couplings producing a dense, fast, multi-scroll orbit - a busy, energetic strange-attractor source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DerivativeGaussianOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DerivativeGaussianOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Derivative-of-Gaussian wavelet: the nth derivative of a Gaussian (order 1 is an edge-detecting odd bump, order 2 is the Mexican hat, higher orders add lobes), a smooth family of DC-free packets whose order sets the number of oscillations.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DeskBellOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DeskBellOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Service bell: the little dome bell on a hotel or shop counter, a bright struck metallic ding with shimmering inharmonic partials and a long ring-out, fired at the set rate, the 'ring for service' sound; the rate sets the dinging.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Devil}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Devil}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Devil's-staircase oscillator (a first as a synth block): synthesises the Cantor function - the 'devil's staircase' that climbs from 0 to 1 entirely on the Cantor set, staying perfectly FLAT across every gap yet still rising overall, with no jumps. As a repeating waveform it is a fractal ramp: a sawtooth assembled from infinitely many flat plateaus and self-similar steps, a singular curve whose spectrum is unlike any band-limited ramp. A monotone fractal staircase distinct from the jagged Weierstrass and Blancmange. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Dholak}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Dholak}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dholak: the two-headed Indian/folk hand drum - a low bass head and a higher treble head struck together, with a warm, woody tone. Tune sets the pitch, Treble the high-head mix, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DialToneOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DialToneOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dial-tone oscillator: the continuous North-American dial tone, the precise sum of a 350 Hz and a 440 Hz sine that says the line is ready; the Detune knob nudges both for that slightly-beating analog-exchange character.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Didgeridoo}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Didgeridoo}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Didgeridoo voice: a gate on in0 drones a deep buzzing tone whose overtones swell and fade under a slow wah-like formant sweep, the circular-breathing rhythm of the instrument. Freq sets the drone pitch (very low), Wobble the speed and depth of the formant sweep, Tone the overall brightness, Level the output. Hold the gate to drone; in1 is the Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DidgeridooOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DidgeridooOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Didgeridoo oscillator: a low lip-buzzed drone over which a slowly-moving vocal-tract formant carves the wah-wah overtone shapes and rhythmic vocalizations of circular-breathing didgeridoo playing; the wobble knob animates the formant.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DieselEngineOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DieselEngineOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Diesel engine: the gruff idle of a diesel motor, a low rumble pulsed by the knocking firing of its cylinders, the chugging clatter of a truck or generator at rest; the RPM knob raises the idle speed and pitch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DifferenceToneOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DifferenceToneOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Difference-tone oscillator: two upper sine carriers passed through a squaring nonlinearity that generates the Tartini combination tone at their frequency difference, so a phantom low pitch (heard but not present in the inputs) emerges from the beating pair.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DiffusionAggregation}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DiffusionAggregation}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Diffusion-limited aggregation: a random walker wanders the ring until it touches the growing cluster and sticks, then a new walker is released, building the branching fractal of mineral dendrites and electrodeposits; the cluster's growing size is the output, resetting when it fills.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DigammaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DigammaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Digamma oscillator: each cycle traces the digamma psi(x) ~ ln(x) - 1/2x - 1/12x^2 over a span, a logarithmically-curved ramp that rises fast then flattens - a soft asymmetric saw with a distinct log knee, brighter at the start of each period than a linear ramp.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DingCorrectOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DingCorrectOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Correct ding: the bright reward bell of a right answer, a clear high bell tone (fundamental plus a sweet octave-ish partial) ringing out and fading, fired at the set rate; the cheerful affirmation chime.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DiodeChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DiodeChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Diode chaos circuit: a sinusoidally-driven RLC loop with a diode (half-wave) nonlinearity, one of the simplest physically-built chaotic circuits, whose rectifying kink folds the driven resonance into chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DiodeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DiodeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Diode-shaped oscillator: a sawtooth core pushed through an exponential diode curve, going from a soft sine-ish tone to a bright, brassy, slightly-clipped buzz as Shape rises - the warm, organic waveshape of West Coast and vintage analog timbres. in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DiphthongOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DiphthongOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Diphthong oscillator: a buzzy harmonic source whose formant emphasis glides from one vowel to another and back each cycle (as in the 'oy' of boy or 'ay' of day), the moving-resonance gesture that turns a steady vowel into a diphthong.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DirichletKernelOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DirichletKernelOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dirichlet kernel: the periodic sinc sin((N+1/2)theta)/sin(theta/2), the sum of the first N cosine harmonics at equal amplitude (the partial-sum kernel of a Fourier series); it makes a bright band-limited pulse that rings with Gibbs-like sidelobes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DishClatterOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DishClatterOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dish clatter: plates and bowls clattering as they are stacked or washed, a random scatter of short resonant ceramic clinks and dull clacks at varying pitches, the kitchen-sink bustle; density sets how busy the clatter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DismissSwooshOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DismissSwooshOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dismiss swoosh: the soft downward swoosh of dismissing a notification or closing a panel, a filtered-noise band sweeping quickly downward in pitch, the swipe-away / dismiss feedback; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DistantScreamOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DistantScreamOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Distant scream: a far-off human scream muffled by distance, a high voiced tone with a rising-then-falling pitch arc and a rough strained edge, band-limited and reverberant as if heard from far away; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DistantStormOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DistantStormOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Distant storm: the continuous low rolling rumble of thunder far across the horizon, a deeply low-passed noise bed that swells and recedes in slow waves, never the sharp crack of nearby lightning; the swell rate sets how restless the storm.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DixonEllipticOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DixonEllipticOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dixon elliptic oscillator: integrates the defining cubic-symmetry ODE sm'=cm^2, cm'=-sm^2 (with sm^3+cm^3=1) to produce the sm waveform - a sine relative of the lemniscate functions but built on the Fermat cubic, giving a distinctly lopsided, three-fold-flavoured periodic shape unlike sine or the Jacobi elliptic waves. RK2-integrated with a per-period reset for stability.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Dizi}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Dizi}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dizi: the Chinese bamboo transverse flute - an open-bore air-jet flute with the signature dimo, a thin reed membrane glued over a hole that BUZZES sympathetically, adding a bright, reedy, almost vocal rasp no Western flute has. Breath drives the jet, Membrane sets the dimo buzz.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Djembe}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Djembe}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal djembe: a trigger on in0 strikes a West-African goblet drum - a deep resonant bass membrane (modes near 1 : 1.5 : 2.1) with a sharp bright finger-slap on top, so it spans from a booming bass tone to a cracking slap. Tune sets the head pitch, Decay the ring, Slap the bright attack-noise amount, Level the output. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DMXKick}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DMXKick}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Oberheim DMX kick: a punchy, tight sampled-style bass drum with a fast pitch sweep and a short, controlled decay - the classic 80s electro/hip-hop kick. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DolphinClickOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DolphinClickOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dolphin oscillator: a fast train of broadband echolocation clicks (a buzzy click rate that accelerates into a 'creak') layered with a gliding signature whistle, the dual voice of a dolphin's biosonar and social calls.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Donk}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Donk}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Scouse-house donk: a trigger on in0 fires a short resonant blip with a fast downward pitch snap through a sharp band-pass - the hollow 'donk' bounce of UK bounce / donk house. Tune sets the blip pitch, Snap the pitch-drop speed (the bounce), Reso the band-pass sharpness (how hollow and ringing), Decay the blip length, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DonkBass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DonkBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Donk bass: the bouncy, hard-plucked UK bassline 'donk' - a short pitched pluck pinged through a high-resonance filter for the rubbery, popping attack. Decay sets the bounce, Reso the donk, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DonkeyBrayOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DonkeyBrayOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Donkey bray: the hee-haw of a donkey, a rough rasping voiced tone alternating between a high inhale 'hee' and a low exhale 'haw' with a buzzy braying edge, fired at the set rate; the comical ass.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Doorbell}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Doorbell}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Doorbell - the classic two-note 'ding-dong' chime with a metallic ring, auto-repeating. Rate sets the interval, Pitch the chime, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DoorbellOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DoorbellOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Doorbell: two struck resonant chime tones sounded in sequence (the descending 'ding-dong' of a two-note door chime), each a decaying sine, re-triggered at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DoorCreakOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DoorCreakOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Door creak: the slow eerie squeal of a swinging door, a resonant tone whose pitch rises and wavers irregularly as the hinge sticks and slips, fired at the set rate; the haunted-house door.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DoorKnockOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DoorKnockOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Door knock: knuckles rapping on a wooden door, a series of short dull woody knocks (a quick triple-knock pattern with a gap) each a damped low-mid resonance, fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DoorSlamOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DoorSlamOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Door slam: a door banged shut, a hard low broadband impact followed by the brief boxy resonance of the wood panel and frame ringing, fired at the set rate; the angry exit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DotMatrixOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DotMatrixOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dot-matrix printer: the screeching buzz of a dot-matrix print head, a pin-fire tone that sweeps in pitch as the head accelerates across each line then snaps back for the next pass; the line rate sets the carriage speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DoubleFoliumOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DoubleFoliumOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Double-folium oscillator: the radial double-folium curve 4cos(t)sin^2(t), which reduces to cos(t)-cos(3t) - a fixed cosine blend of the fundamental and third harmonic with a hollow, twin-lobed shape distinct from the sine-phase nephroid and bifolium.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DoubleParetoOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DoubleParetoOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Double-Pareto oscillator: each cycle rises as a power law x^(Beta-1) up to a peak then falls as x^(-Alpha-1) (the double-Pareto / log-Laplace shape) - so both flanks are power laws with independently-set exponents, a heavy-tailed bump with a sharp peak distinct from the exponential-tailed distributions. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DoubleScrollJerk}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DoubleScrollJerk}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Double-scroll jerk: a single-equation jerk circuit with a piecewise-linear feedback that reproduces a Chua-style double-scroll attractor from just three op-amp integrators - the minimal double-scroll generator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DoubleScrollSat}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DoubleScrollSat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Saturated double-scroll: a third-order linear system closed by a saturation function (the Chua-Lin saturated-nonlinearity circuit), which generates the Chua double-scroll - and, by stacking saturation steps, n-scroll - attractors without a Chua diode.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DoubleWellHam}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DoubleWellHam}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Double-well Hamiltonian: an undamped particle in a twin-well potential (x'=p, p'=x-x^3) that conserves energy, orbiting within one well or figure-eighting across both depending on its energy - a clean conservative nonlinear oscillator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DoublingComplex}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DoublingComplex}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Angle-doubling map: z -> z^2 on the unit circle, which doubles the phase angle each step (the complex face of the Bernoulli shift); the real part is a chaotically-scrambled cosine, deterministic yet broadband.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Downlifter}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Downlifter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Down-sweep transition: a trigger on in0 fires a descending sweep that falls over Time seconds - the post-drop 'whoosh down'. A pitch-falling sine and band-passed noise slide from bright/high to dark/low while the level decays, marking the move into a breakdown. Time sets the fall length, Tone balances the noise against the falling sine, Reso the band-pass sharpness, and Level the output. The inverse of Riser - patch a transition gate into in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DpwSaw}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DpwSaw}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

DPW sawtooth: squares a trivial ramp into a parabola then differentiates it, which suppresses the high-harmonic aliasing of a naive saw cheaply (the differentiated-parabolic-waveform method) - smoother than a raw saw, lighter than oversampling.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DpwSquare}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DpwSquare}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

DPW square: subtracts two differentiated-parabolic sawtooths a half-cycle apart to build an alias-reduced square wave, the band-limiting trick of the DPW family applied to a pulse rather than a ramp.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DR110}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DR110}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boss DR-110 kick: a tight, dry analog bass drum with a short pitch blip and a clicky attack - the small, punchy Dr Rhythm kick. Tune sets the pitch, Click the attack snap, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DragonflyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DragonflyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dragonfly: the dry papery clatter of a dragonfly's four stiff wings, a buzzy tone chopped by a fast secondary wing-rattle so it shimmers and crackles, unlike the smooth bee buzz; the rattle rate sets the wing speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Drawbar}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Drawbar}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nine-drawbar tonewheel organ oscillator: stacks the classic Hammond harmonics (sub, fifth, octaves, third, etc.). Tilt rolls the registration from foundation-heavy to bright upper partials and Click adds the key-on percussion transient.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DrawbarOrgan}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DrawbarOrgan}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Drawbar organ: nine sine partials at the Hammond drawbar footages (sub-octave, fifth, unison, octave, twelfth, fifteenth and so on) mixed by a single registration morph from mellow flutes to bright full-organ - tonewheel additive synthesis.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DreadDroneOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DreadDroneOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dread drone: the unsettling low drone of a horror score, a cluster of slightly-detuned low partials with a slow beating dissonance and a creeping upper overtone, the bed of tension under a scary scene; the beat rate sets the unease.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DrillString}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DrillString}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Drill-string stick-slip: a kilometers-long elastic drill pipe driven from the top while the bit grabs the rock with velocity-weakening friction, so the bit alternately sticks and slips - the torsional vibration that destroys drilling tools.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DripOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DripOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Drip: the resonant 'plink' of a water drop hitting a surface, a sine ping that drops slightly in pitch as the cavity resonance settles, with a soft tail, fired at the set rate; the patient drip of a leaky tap.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DrippingFaucet}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DrippingFaucet}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dripping faucet: a hanging drop grows from inflow until its mass breaks the surface-tension threshold and detaches, leaving a recoiling residual; the interval between drops period-doubles into chaos - Robert Shaw's famous kitchen-sink experiment.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DrivenPendulum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DrivenPendulum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Driven pendulum: a damped pendulum kicked by a periodic torque, the canonical single-degree-of-freedom system that tumbles between swinging and full rotation chaotically as the drive strength rises.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DrizzleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DrizzleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Drizzle: a fine soft rain, a gentle stream of tiny high-frequency droplet ticks over a faint wet hiss, the quiet patter of light rain on leaves; the density knob raises it from a sprinkle to a steady shower.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DroidChatterOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DroidChatterOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Droid chatter: the emotional warble of an astromech droid, short sine blips that randomly glide up or down in pitch with expressive little swoops, stringing together into excited mechanical conversation; the blip rate sets the talkativeness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DroneOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DroneOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

The most powerful drone generator in existence. Up to 16 morphable voices (sine through saw to PWM pulse) detuned and stacked into octaves, fifths or chords; a two-octave sub stack for deep weight; an octave-plus-fifth shimmer chord on top; a filtered air-noise layer; chaotic Lorenz-attractor drift for organic never-repeating motion; a wavefolder-plus-saturation Drive stage for enormous harmonic content; and a Motion-driven resonant low-pass that breathes. Free-runs at Freq as a standalone drone, or tracks the keyboard via a Note (Pitch CV) on in0; in1 adds to the tone. Twelve controls: Voices, Detune, Sub, Shimmer, Stack, Drift, Tone, Shape (waveform morph), Drive (harmonic fold and saturate), Motion (chaotic movement) and Air. The cinematic, doom, ambient and sound-design powerhouse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DropoutOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DropoutOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dropout: an intermittent signal cutting in and out, a steady tone randomly muted by silence gaps as if a loose cable or weak radio link keeps failing, the glitchy stutter of a broken connection; the dropout rate sets how often it cuts.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DrumMembraneOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DrumMembraneOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Drum-membrane oscillator: a fixed sum of sines at the inharmonic ratios of an ideal circular membrane (the zeros of the Bessel functions: 1, 1.59, 2.14, 2.30, 2.65, 2.92, 3.16), the partial structure that gives a timpani or tom its pitched-but-not-quite-harmonic tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DSF}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DSF}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Discrete summation formula oscillator (Moorer 1976): a closed-form band-limited buzz - the whole geometric sum of harmonics evaluated in one expression, so Brightness sets the harmonic roll-off (0 = pure sine, near 1 = bright sawtooth-like) with no aliasing, and Ratio spaces the partials (1 = integer harmonics, off-integer = clangorous inharmonic spectra). Freq sets the pitch (in0 = Pitch CV), Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DsfOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DsfOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

DSF oscillator: Moorer's discrete summation formula sums a geometric series of harmonics in closed form, so one 'brightness' knob smoothly fades from a sine to a buzzy band-limited pulse - alias-controlled spectral morphing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DTMFOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DTMFOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

DTMF oscillator: the touch-tone of a telephone keypad, the sum of one low-group and one high-group sine chosen by the key (the standard dual-tone multi-frequency signalling), cycling 0-9 and the symbol keys.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DuckQuackOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DuckQuackOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Duck quack: the nasal quack of a duck, a short buzzy voiced burst with a fast amplitude flutter and a comic nasal honk, repeated, fired at the set rate; the pond's resident comedian.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Duduk}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Duduk}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Armenian duduk (Gladiator / Dune cinematic wind): a cylindrical double-reed voice - odd-harmonic dominant like a cor anglais, warm and woody and mournful - with breath noise and an expressive delayed vibrato, the soul-instrument used for grief and longing in film scores. Tune sets the pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Breath the airy reed noise, Vibrato the expressive pitch waver (it swells in as the note is held), Tone the brightness, and Level the output. A gate on in0 sounds the note with a soft reed attack; hold for the long mournful sustain.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DuffingForced}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DuffingForced}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Forced Duffing oscillator: a damped twin-well spring driven by a sine force jumps chaotically between its two wells, a textbook nonlinear resonator whose response is rich, hysteretic and period-doubling.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DuffingMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DuffingMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Duffing map: the discrete Holmes form of the Duffing oscillator (a cubic 2-D map), tracing a stretched chaotic loop - the iterated-map counterpart to the forced Duffing flow, with a crisper digital character.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DuffingOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DuffingOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Duffing chaotic oscillator: a driven nonlinear spring that ranges from a pure pitched tone to wild bifurcating chaos. Chaos sets the drive into instability and Damp reins it back - an organic, unpredictable analog-style source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DuffingVanDerPol}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DuffingVanDerPol}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Duffing-van-der-Pol oscillator: a single resonator carrying both a cubic stiffness (Duffing twin-well) and a van-der-Pol negative-damping term, so it self-starts and, when forced, roams chaotically across its wells.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DumbbellOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DumbbellOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dumbbell oscillator: sweeps the dumbbell curve x^2*sqrt(1-x^2) with the sign of x, giving an odd waveform with two rounded lobes that pinch to zero at the centre and the edges - a hollow, weighted-at-the-ends shape distinct from triangle or sine.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DustDevilOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DustDevilOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dust devil: a small whirling vortex of wind, a resonant whistling noise band whose pitch spirals up and down as the column spins, swelling as it passes and fading as it wanders off; the spin rate sets the vortex speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DustOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DustOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dust - randomly-timed unipolar impulses (a stochastic click source). Density sets the average rate from sparse ticks to a dense crackle. Classic for granular triggers and percussive texture.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DustRandom}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DustRandom}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Random impulse generator (dust): fires impulses at random times whose average rate is set by a density control, the classic granular/stochastic trigger source for clouds, crackle and sparse percussion.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DustyPlasma}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DustyPlasma}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dusty-plasma oscillation: charged dust grains levitated in a plasma sheath bob on the dust-acoustic mode, whose charge-fluctuation feedback and nonlinear sheath force drive self-excited and chaotic grain oscillations.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Earthquake}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Earthquake}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Earthquake - a sub-bass brown-noise rumble that swells and recedes, felt more than heard. Intensity sets the magnitude, Tone the upper grind, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ECGSynth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ECGSynth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Synthetic ECG: a point moving around a circular limit cycle gets pushed up and down by Gaussian bumps placed at the P-Q-R-S-T angles, tracing a realistic electrocardiogram waveform (the McSharry-Clifford model) you can detune into arrhythmia.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EdgeTone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EdgeTone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Edge tone: an air jet striking a sharp wedge flips from side to side with a feedback delay set by the jet's travel time (the sound source in flutes, whistles and organ flue pipes), here driven into its chaotic over-blown mode.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EEGRhythmOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EEGRhythmOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

EEG rhythm oscillator: blends the five brainwave bands - slow delta, theta, alpha, beta and fast gamma at their characteristic frequency ratios - into one composite, the Band knob crossfading from a drowsy delta throb to alert gamma chatter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EggCrackOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EggCrackOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Egg crack: an eggshell cracked against a bowl, a sharp brittle crack transient followed by a brief wet splat as the contents drop, fired at the set rate; the cooking-prep detail.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EhrenfestUrn}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EhrenfestUrn}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ehrenfest urn: N balls split between two urns, each step a random ball changes urn, so the count mean-reverts toward N/2 (a discrete model of heat diffusion and entropy); the fluctuations make a self-centering integer wander.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ElasticPendulum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ElasticPendulum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Elastic pendulum: a mass on a spring that also swings, whose bounce and swing modes exchange energy through their 2:1 resonance and tumble chaotically - the classic 'spring pendulum' demonstration of coupled-mode chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Electric}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Electric}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Note, Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Electric piano (Rhodes/Wurlitzer-style physical model): a gate on in1 strikes a tuned tine - an exponentially-decaying sine fundamental plus a bright inharmonic 'bark' partial that decays faster - run through a soft pickup nonlinearity, the classic electric-piano bell-and-body tone. in0 is the note (keyboard-tracked), Transpose tunes it, Decay the note length, Bark the metallic attack, Tone the pickup brightness, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Elements}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Elements}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal synthesis voice (Mutable Instruments Elements): an exciter feeds a tuned modal resonator bank. A gate on in0 fires the exciter, which Exciter morphs from blown (filtered noise) to struck (impulse); the six-mode resonator imposes the ringing of a physical object, with Geometry morphing its partials from a harmonic series toward inharmonic bell/plate ratios and Damping setting the ring time. Tune sets pitch, Level the output. Bowed strings, struck bars, blown tubes and alien resonances from one voice. Distinct from the self-struck Modal and the resonator-only Rings.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ElevatorDingOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ElevatorDingOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Elevator ding: the polite arrival chime of an elevator reaching a floor, a soft clean single (or double) bell tone ringing briefly and fading, fired at the set rate; the floor-has-arrived sound.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EnergyChargeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EnergyChargeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Energy charge: a weapon or spell charging up, a tone that rises steadily in pitch and intensity to a peak then snaps back to recharge, with a faint crackling energy edge, the building hum before a big release; the rate sets the charge time.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Engine}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Engine}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Combustion engine: a pulse train of sharp combustion transients fired at the engine's cylinder rate through a resonant exhaust filter, with a noise rasp - the procedural car / motorcycle / machine engine of film and game sound design. RPM sets the engine speed (the firing rate), Cylinders the number of cylinders (rhythm and harmonic spacing), Tone the exhaust resonance frequency, Growl the resonance and grit, and Level the output. Automate RPM for revs and gear changes; self-running.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EngineOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EngineOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Engine oscillator: a train of combustion thumps fired at the cylinder rate of a running engine (RPM times cylinders over two revolutions), each a short decaying pulse, so raising the RPM sweeps from a lumpy idle to a smooth high-rev drone - a motor as a tonal source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EnvToPitch}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EnvToPitch}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Envelope-to-pitch oscillator: tracks the input loudness and maps it to the frequency of an internal sine, so dynamics play a melody - a self-contained envelope-controlled drone voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EpicycleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EpicycleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Epicycle oscillator: the Ptolemaic deferent-plus-epicycle model of planetary motion - a small circle whose centre rides a large one - which traces looping retrograde paths; the x-projection makes a waveform with little backward kinks where the planet appears to reverse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EpicycloidOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EpicycloidOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Epicycloid oscillator: the curve traced by a point on a small circle rolling around the outside of a larger one, whose cusp count is set by the radius ratio; the x-coordinate makes a cusped, gear-like waveform rich in harmonics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EpidemicSEIRS}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EpidemicSEIRS}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Seasonal SEIRS: an SEIR epidemic where recovered hosts lose immunity and return to susceptible (the extra loop), which under seasonal transmission sustains rich multi-annual and chaotic outbreak cycles - recurrent-epidemic dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EpidemicSIR}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EpidemicSIR}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Seasonal SIR epidemic: a susceptible-infected-recovered model whose transmission rate is modulated annually (school terms, seasons); strong seasonality drives the infected fraction into chaotic, irregular outbreak cycles.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Epileptor}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Epileptor}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Epileptor: Jirsa's phenomenological model of an epileptic-seizure cycle, where a very slow permittivity variable drags a fast spiking subsystem in and out of the seizure state - the autonomous build-up, discharge and recovery of a seizure.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ErfcOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ErfcOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Erfc oscillator: each cycle is the complementary error function erfc(x), a falling S-curve with Gaussian (super-exponential) tails - it stays near the top, drops steeply through the centre, then flattens fast, a sharper-shouldered descending ramp than the logistic or arctangent sigmoids.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Erhu}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Erhu}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Bow, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chinese erhu: a bowed two-string fiddle with an intense, vocal vibrato and a nasal, reedy tone - a bowed sawtooth through a nasal formant, played by holding in0 as bow pressure. Vibrato sets the wobble depth, Nasal the formant emphasis, Level the output. in0 = Bow gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ErrorBuzzOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ErrorBuzzOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Error buzz: the harsh dissonant buzz of an invalid action or denied input, two close detuned square tones beating against each other in a short blast, the access-denied / wrong-input sound; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EspressoSteamOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EspressoSteamOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Milk steamer: an espresso machine steam wand frothing milk, a high-pressure steam hiss with a gurgling burble that rises in pitch as the milk foams, the barista's froth; the burble knob sets the foaming.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EulerDisk}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EulerDisk}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Euler's disk (settling coin): models a disk spun on a table - as it loses energy its rolling pitch RISES like (t_settle - t)^(-1/3), Moffatt's finite-time singularity, so the tone whirrs faster and faster and swells louder, then abruptly settles and re-drops. The unmistakable accelerating rattle of a dropped coin, looping. Distinct from every decaying or steady source here. Drop sets the settle time, Pitch the rolling-tone scale.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EulerPolyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EulerPolyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Euler-polynomial oscillator: each cycle is the nth Euler polynomial E_n(x) on [0,1] (the alternating-sum companion of the Bernoulli polynomials) - a related but distinct oscillating polynomial with different coefficients and symmetry. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EulerRigid}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EulerRigid}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rigid-body tumble: the Euler equations for a torque-free asymmetric rigid body (the intermediate-axis 'tennis-racket' instability) with weak feedback, so the angular-velocity vector flips and tumbles chaotically.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EvenHarmonicOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EvenHarmonicOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Even-harmonic oscillator: a fundamental plus only its even partials (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12), an octave-stacked spectrum with the empty-twelfths quality of certain organ stops and the warmth even-order tube distortion adds.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ExcitableMedium}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ExcitableMedium}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Excitable medium: a ring of cells that fire when a neighbour fires, then enter a refractory period before they can fire again (the cardiac/Belousov wave rule); periodic stimulation launches pulses that can circulate as self-sustaining reentry - a probe cell's excitation is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ExpLogarithmicOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ExpLogarithmicOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Exponential-logarithmic oscillator: each cycle is proportional to (1-P)*beta*e^(-beta x)/(1-(1-P)e^(-beta x)), a decreasing-hazard distribution (mixture of exponentials over a logarithmic series) - a heavy-front, slow-tailing decay shaped by P. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Explosion}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Explosion}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Explosion - a deep boom (pitch-dropping body + noise blast) with a rumbling tail, auto-repeating. Rate sets the interval, Tone the debris brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ExplosionOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ExplosionOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Explosion: the boom of a destroyed enemy, a burst of white noise filtered downward over a low rumble as it decays, the chunky 8-bit kaboom, fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ExpMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ExpMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Exponential map: iterates x -> r x e^(-x), a transcendental map (related to the Ricker model) whose single hump drives a period-doubling cascade into chaos with a smooth, asymmetric bifurcation structure.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Expo}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Expo}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Exponential-ramp oscillator (a first as a synth block): a sawtooth whose ramp is not straight but EXPONENTIALLY curved - it rises (or, with the curve reversed, decays) like a charging capacitor rather than a linear slope, the natural shape of an analogue envelope or an RC circuit. Curve sets how bent the ramp is: near zero it is an ordinary saw, high and it hugs the floor then snaps up at the end. The curvature reshapes the harmonic rolloff, giving a rounder, more organic edge than the buzzy linear saw. A capacitor-curve waveform distinct from the geometric saw. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ExpoEaseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ExpoEaseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Exponential-ease oscillator: each cycle is the easeOutExpo curve 1-2^(-Rate*t), which snaps up fast then asymptotically approaches the top - a soft sawtooth with exponential curvature (sharp attack, long flat tail), Rate setting how abrupt the rise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ExponentialNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ExponentialNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Exponential noise: samples -ln(u)/lambda from the memoryless exponential distribution (the gaps of a Poisson process), strongly peaked at zero with a single light tail - a one-sided source distinct from symmetric white noise, here centred.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ExponentiatedExpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ExponentiatedExpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Exponentiated-exponential oscillator: each cycle is Alpha*e^(-x)*(1-e^(-x))^(Alpha-1), a flexible distribution that is a pure decay for Alpha<=1 but grows a rising hump for Alpha>1 - one knob morphs from a falling ramp to a skewed bump. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ExpQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ExpQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Exponential-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the exponential inverse-CDF -ln(1-p), a ramp that climbs almost linearly at first then accelerates ever more steeply toward the top - the pure-logarithmic member of the quantile family, a convex accelerating sweep. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ExpWeibullOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ExpWeibullOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Exponentiated-Weibull oscillator: each cycle is alpha*beta*x^(beta-1)*e^(-x^beta)*(1-e^(-x^beta))^(alpha-1), a flexible lifetime PDF that can be unimodal, decreasing or bathtub-shaped - Alpha and Beta together set the rise and tail, a richly-morphable skewed bump. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ExtraLifeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ExtraLifeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

1-up: earning an extra life, a short cheerful jingle of square-wave notes climbing a major arpeggio, the happy reward chime; the rate sets how often it plays.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Factory}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Factory}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Factory floor - rhythmic mechanical clanks and presses over a motor hum and steam hiss. Rate sets the machine tempo, Tone the metallic brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FaradayWave}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FaradayWave}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Faraday wave: a fluid surface shaken vertically grows standing waves at half the drive frequency (a parametric instability); a single mode with cubic saturation captures the onset and the chaotic competition of these ripples.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FastRadioBurstOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FastRadioBurstOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fast radio burst: the dispersed millisecond flash of an FRB, where interstellar plasma makes the high frequencies arrive before the low ones, so each repeat sweeps rapidly downward in pitch; an enigmatic cosmic radio signal rendered as a falling chirp burst.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FaxToneOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FaxToneOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fax handshake: a fax machine negotiating a connection, a pair of alternating pure carrier tones over a band-limited modem hiss, the unmistakable screech of a fax dialling in, the retro-telecom sound; the swap rate sets the warble.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FBMNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FBMNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fractional Brownian motion: a self-affine random walk whose Hurst exponent sets the long-range correlation (persistence or anti-persistence) between ordinary Brownian motion and smooth drift - the fractal landscape of natural terrains and fGn.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FDistOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FDistOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

F-distribution oscillator: each cycle is the Fisher-Snedecor F PDF, shaped by two degrees-of-freedom (D1 rise, D2 tail) - a right-skewed bump from variance-ratio statistics whose skew and tail weight the two knobs set independently; peak-normalized and bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
