\phantomsection\label{blk:FeedbackFM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FeedbackFM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Single-operator FM with self-feedback (the DX7 feedback operator): a sine modulates its own phase, sweeping smoothly from pure tone toward a saw-like buzz as Feedback rises. Tone tames the top end.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FeedbackFMOp}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FeedbackFMOp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Feedback FM operator: a sine oscillator phase-modulated by its own previous output (the DX7 feedback operator), which morphs continuously from a pure sine through a sawtooth-like spectrum into noise as the feedback amount rises - one operator, a whole timbral range.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FejerKernelOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FejerKernelOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fejer kernel: the average of Dirichlet kernels (a squared sinc), always non-negative and with triangular harmonic weighting, giving a smooth single bump per cycle with far gentler sidelobes than the Dirichlet kernel - a clean unipolar pulse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FermatPolyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FermatPolyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fermat-polynomial oscillator: each cycle is the nth Fermat polynomial (F_n = 3x*F_{n-1} - 2*F_{n-2}, giving 3x, 9x^2-2, 27x^3-12x...) - a steeper recurrence than Fibonacci, so its lobes grow and sharpen faster, a distinct polynomial-sequence waveform. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FermatSpiralOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FermatSpiralOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fermat-spiral oscillator: the radial projection sqrt(theta)*cos(theta) of Fermat's parabolic spiral over several turns per cycle - a sine whose amplitude grows as a square root then resets, a gentler swell than the linear Involute or exponential LogSpiral.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FermiAccelMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FermiAccelMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fermi-Ulam model: a ball bounces between a fixed wall and an oscillating one, gaining or losing speed at each kick depending on the wall phase - Fermi's mechanism for cosmic-ray acceleration; the simplified velocity-phase map mixes accelerating chaos with trapping islands.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FermiMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FermiMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fermi-Ulam map: models a ball bouncing between a fixed and a vibrating wall (Fermi's cosmic-ray acceleration idea), whose velocity gains and loses energy chaotically - a stochastic-acceleration source with bursts of speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FerroResonance}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FerroResonance}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ferroresonance: a power transformer's saturable iron core resonating with line capacitance, whose cubic magnetization nonlinearity under AC drive jumps between low and high overvoltage modes and chaotically - a dangerous grid instability.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FibonacciHarmonicOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FibonacciHarmonicOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fibonacci-harmonic oscillator: partials placed at the Fibonacci harmonic numbers (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21), so the overtones spread ever wider in the golden cadence - a sparse, increasingly-inharmonic-feeling spectrum with a shimmering top.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FibonacciOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FibonacciOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fibonacci-harmonic oscillator (a first as a synth block): an additive tone whose overtones land only on the Fibonacci numbers - the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 5th, 8th, 13th, 21st... harmonics. The gaps between partials widen by the golden ratio as you climb, so the low end is dense and the top sparse, giving a warm body with a thin, glassy shimmer on top - a spectrum tuned to phi rather than the even comb of a saw. Partials sets how many Fibonacci overtones to stack, alias-free up to Nyquist. Distinct from the prime-harmonic and tilt-based oscillators.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FibonacciPolyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FibonacciPolyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fibonacci-polynomial oscillator: each cycle is the nth Fibonacci polynomial F_n(x) (F_n = x*F_{n-1}+F_{n-2}) - a polynomial whose oscillating lobes increase with Order, a recurrence-defined waveform distinct from the orthogonal Chebyshev/Legendre families. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FibreString}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FibreString}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two coupled Karplus-Strong plucked strings tuned a hair apart for a rich, chorused acoustic pluck/pad. Damp sets the decay (short pluck to long sustain) and Detune the beating between the two strings.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FifthLead}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FifthLead}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Power-fifth lead: a saw doubled a perfect fifth above for the bold, open parallel-fifths organ/rock lead. Mix sets the fifth level, Tone the brightness, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FinanceOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FinanceOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Finance chaos attractor: a three-state model of interest rate, investment demand and price index whose coupled nonlinear dynamics are chaotic, repurposed as a slow, organic strange-attractor modulation source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Fire}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Fire}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fire / combustion texture: three procedural layers - a brown-noise roar (the steady burn), an airy high-frequency hiss (combustion), and randomly-timed crackle pops (wood splitting) - the campfire / torch / burning-building ambience of film sound design. Self-running. Roar sets the low rumble level, Crackle the density of the popping, Hiss the airy combustion noise, Bright the crackle brightness, and Level the output. Layer two instances for a richer fire; automate Crackle for flare-ups.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FireballOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FireballOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fireball: a hurled ball of flame, a broadband noise roar swept by a fast-opening then closing lowpass (the whoosh of the fireball passing) with a crackling fiery edge, fired at the set rate; the magic-projectile staple.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FireCrackle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FireCrackle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Amount}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fire: a crackling campfire - a low filtered-noise roar speckled with random snaps and pops. Crackle sets the snap rate, Tone the brightness, Level the output. in0 = Amount gate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FireCrackleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FireCrackleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fire oscillator: a soft filtered-noise bed of flame hiss sprinkled with random sharp crackle pops, the comforting roar-and-snap of a campfire or hearth; the crackle knob sets how busy the pops are.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FireflySync}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FireflySync}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Firefly synchronization: a dozen integrate-and-fire oscillators each charge toward a flash threshold and, when one fires, nudge all the others forward (Mirollo-Strogatz pulse coupling), so an initially-random swarm spontaneously locks into unison flashing. The flash count is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FishCurveOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FishCurveOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fish-curve oscillator: the x-coordinate cos(t) - sin^2(t)/sqrt(2) of the fish curve, DC-removed, which is a fixed in-phase blend of the fundamental and second harmonic - an asymmetric waveform with a single bulged lobe and strong even-harmonic colour.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FitzHughForced}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FitzHughForced}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Forced FitzHugh-Nagumo: the excitable two-variable neuron driven by a periodic current, which mode-locks, skips beats and fires chaotic spike patterns (the devil's-staircase response of a forced excitable cell).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FitzNagumo}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FitzNagumo}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

FitzHugh-Nagumo neuron: a two-variable excitable model that fires voltage spikes when its current crosses threshold, giving clicky, pulsing, neuron-like rhythms a wavetable can't produce.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FizzOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FizzOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fizz: the effervescent hiss of a freshly-poured carbonated drink, a dense random crackle of countless tiny high-frequency bubble bursts, slowly thinning as the soda goes flat; density sets the carbonation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FKNReaction}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FKNReaction}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

FKN reaction: the Field-Koros-Noyes mechanism behind the Belousov-Zhabotinsky reaction, reduced to its activator-inhibitor-catalyst core, firing sharp relaxation pulses with a long refractory interval - the chemical-clock spike train.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FlagFlutter}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FlagFlutter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Flag flutter: a flexible panel in a flow whose bending and torsion modes lock together past a critical wind speed and flutter, then chaotically flap; aeroelastic mode-coupling instability, the flapping-flag and bridge-deck mechanism.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FlameOscillator}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FlameOscillator}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Flame instability: a premixed flame front whose heat-release responds to its own curvature and a downstream acoustic feedback, pulsating and chaotically flickering - the thermo-diffusive instability behind combustion roar and flame chatter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FlexibleWeibullOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FlexibleWeibullOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Flexible-Weibull oscillator: each cycle is (Alpha+Beta/x^2)*e^(Alpha*x-Beta/x)*e^(-e^(Alpha*x-Beta/x)), the flexible-Weibull extension whose Beta/x term gives a steeper left rise than a plain Weibull - a sharply front-loaded bump with an exponential cut-off. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FluorescentBuzzOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FluorescentBuzzOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fluorescent buzz: the gritty 100/120 Hz buzz of a magnetic-ballast fluorescent tube (twice the line frequency, since the lamp fires on both half-cycles) overlaid with random flicker crackle from an aging starter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Flute}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Flute}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Breathy flute voice: a gate on in0 sounds a soft nearly-pure tone (fundamental with a whisper of upper harmonics) wrapped in breath noise and a gentle vibrato, with a slow soft attack. Freq sets the note, Breath the airy-noise amount, Vibrato the pitch waver, Level the output. Hold the gate to sustain; in1 is the Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FluteWGOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FluteWGOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Flute waveguide: an open bore excited by an air jet modelled as a cubic nonlinearity plus breath noise, giving the breathy, pure, even-and-odd-harmonic tone of a flute or whistle; the breath knob adds air hiss and pushes it overblown.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two-operator FM voice (the DX7 building block): a sine carrier is phase-modulated by a sine modulator at Ratio times the frequency, with Index setting depth and modulator self-Feedback adding harmonic edge toward noise. Integer ratios give harmonic, organ/bass timbres; non-integer ratios give bells and metallic tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FMBass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FMBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two-operator FM bass: a sine carrier frequency-modulated by a second sine at an integer ratio, the punchy, metallic DX-style bass with a bright attack-to-body movement. Ratio sets the modulator interval, Index the FM brightness, Decay the index falloff. in0 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FMDrum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FMDrum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

FM percussion voice: a trigger on in0 fires a 2-operator FM tone with a fast decay - metallic toms, bells, blips and zaps depending on Ratio. Tune sets the carrier pitch, Ratio the modulator ratio (inharmonic at non-integers), Decay the length, Level the output. in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FOF}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FOF}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Formant-wave-function synthesis (CHANT): each fundamental period retriggers a bank of decaying formant grains, generating vocal/formant tones. Vowel morphs A-E-I-O-U.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FofGrain}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FofGrain}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

FOF formant synthesis: each pitch period triggers a grain that is an exponentially-decaying sine at a formant frequency (the IRCAM Chant technique), building voiced, formant-rich tones one excitation grain at a time.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FofSynth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FofSynth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

FOF synthesis (IRCAM CHANT): each fundamental period triggers a formant wave function - a sine at the formant frequency wrapped in a sharp-attack, exponential-decay grain; summing several gives the ringing vowel formants of the singing voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FogHorn}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FogHorn}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fog horn: a low two-tone horn (a fundamental plus a fifth above) that swells in and out on a slow breath envelope - the mournful blast of a lighthouse or a ship in fog, ducking the dry signal under it. Pitch sets the horn fundamental, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FoldedNormalOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FoldedNormalOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Folded-normal oscillator: each cycle is the folded-normal PDF phi(x-Mu)+phi(x+Mu) (the distribution of |N(mu,1)|), which is a half-Gaussian at Mu=0 and develops a shifted, shouldered bump as Mu grows - peak-normalized, a distinct one-sided shape from the standard Gaussian.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FoldedTowel}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FoldedTowel}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Folded-towel map: Rossler's three-dimensional 'folded towel' map, an iconic hyperchaotic system whose attractor looks like a folded towel and expands chaotically in two directions at once.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FoldFM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FoldFM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Folded FM: a 2-operator FM carrier is driven into a sine wavefolder, stacking FM sidebands and fold harmonics into a screaming, complex-oscillator timbre that neither FM nor folding makes alone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FoliumDescartesOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FoliumDescartesOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Folium-of-Descartes oscillator: each cycle is the y-coordinate 3t^2/(1+t^3) of the folium of Descartes, a fast-rising loop that peaks early then trails off down its asymptotic tail - an asymmetric bump from the classic cubic curve. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Footstep}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Footstep}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Footstep foley: a trigger on in0 fires a layered foot-floor impact - a damped low resonance (the body of the step) plus a band-passed noise contact - with a Surface control that morphs from a tight solid hit (wood / asphalt) to a scattered cluster of stochastic micro-pops (gravel / aggregate). The walk, run and stomp foley of film and game sound design. Weight sets the step body pitch, Surface the solid-to-gravel morph, Tone the contact brightness, Hardness the impact sharpness, and Level the output. Trigger per step; vary Weight / Surface per footfall.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Footsteps}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Footsteps}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Footsteps: a steady tread of soft low thumps - filtered noise bursts with a quick body-and-decay, paced like walking. Useful as a rhythmic foley/percussion bed or a sub-pulse generator. Rate sets the steps per minute, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Forcefield}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Forcefield}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Energy force-field - a detuned electric hum pulsing with a slow tremolo and a faint crackle, the protective barrier. Power sets the intensity, Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ForceFieldOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ForceFieldOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Force field: the angry buzzing crackle of an energy barrier, a harsh saw-and-square drone amplitude-modulated by a fast ring buzz and sprinkled with random electric crackle, the sound of a shield you should not touch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ForestFireSOC}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ForestFireSOC}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Forest-fire model: cells are empty, growing trees, or burning; fire spreads to neighbouring trees, burnt cells clear, empty cells regrow, and rare lightning ignites new fires - the Drossel-Schwabl self-organized-criticality model whose fire sizes are scale-free; fire density is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FormantBass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FormantBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Formant (talking) bass: a sawtooth shaped by a two-formant vowel filter that morphs through vowels, for a bass that says 'wow' and 'yoy'. Vowel morphs the vowel, Sub the sub-octave weight, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FormantBuzz}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FormantBuzz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Formant buzz voice: a harmonic-rich sawtooth excites two resonant band-pass formants whose centre frequencies morph with the Vowel knob (ah -> ee -> oo), giving a buzzy talking/vocal timbre from a single oscillator - a poor-man's vocal synth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FormantGrainOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FormantGrainOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Formant grain: FOF synthesis (fonction d'onde formantique), where each pitch-period grain is a windowed sine burst at a formant frequency, the technique behind CHANT vocal synthesis; the fundamental sets pitch while the formant and decay shape the vowel.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FormantStackOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FormantStackOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Formant-stack oscillator: a full harmonic series whose partials are weighted by a resonant Gaussian peak at a fixed formant frequency, so the timbre keeps a vowel-like resonance at one absolute pitch regardless of the note played - source-and-formant synthesis in one block.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FourModeNS}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FourModeNS}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Truncated Navier-Stokes: a four-mode spectral truncation of the two-dimensional incompressible flow equations (Franceschini-Tebaldi), whose energy cascades chaotically among Fourier modes - a minimal model of fluid turbulence.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FourOpFM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FourOpFM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Four-operator FM: a stack of four sine operators (4 modulates 3 modulates 2 modulates the carrier), giving deep, evolving FM timbres from bells to brass to clangour. Ratio sets the operator interval, Index the FM depth, Decay the index falloff. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FourOpFMVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FourOpFMVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Four-operator FM: a serial stack of four FM operators (op4 modulates op3 modulates op2 modulates op1 carrier) at independent ratios, the deep-FM algorithm behind DX-style metallic and evolving timbres.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FourWingOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FourWingOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Four-wing attractor: a chaotic flow whose orbit jumps between four lobes ('wings'), giving abrupt, multi-state switching dynamics - chaos with a distinct sense of discrete regions.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FractalDreamMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FractalDreamMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fractal-dream map: a sine-based 2-D iterated map (Pickover) that traces dreamy, swirling, doubly-symmetric fractal curves, a distinctive flowing chaotic texture as an audio/CV source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FractalFlame}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FractalFlame}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fractal flame (Scott Draves): an iterated function system whose affine maps are post-composed with nonlinear 'variations' (sinusoidal, spherical, swirl), the algorithm behind Electric Sheep; the warped chaos-game orbit traces a flame-like attractor read out as a CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FractalNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FractalNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fractal noise: a bank of octave-spaced filtered noises summed with a slope control, sweeping the spectrum continuously from white (slope 0) through pink (1) to brown/red (2) - any 1/f^beta colour from one knob.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FrameDrum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FrameDrum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Frame drum: a shallow hand drum with a warm mid-pitched membrane tone and a soft skin slap - the open, resonant world-percussion drum. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FrechetOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FrechetOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Frechet oscillator: each cycle is the Frechet extreme-value PDF x^(-1-a) e^(-x^-a), which slams up from zero to a sharp peak then trails off in a heavy power-law tail - a spiky, front-loaded waveform with a long decay, Alpha setting the sharpness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FrechetQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FrechetQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Frechet-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the Frechet inverse-CDF (-ln p)^(-1/Alpha), a ramp that lingers near zero then accelerates sharply toward the top as the extreme-value tail dominates - Alpha sets the violence of the late rise. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FreeReed}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FreeReed}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Free-reed model: a mass-on-spring reed driven past an asymmetric airflow valve self-oscillates with the buzzy, harmonically-rich, slightly-detuned tone of a harmonica or accordion reed under air pressure.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FreitasMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FreitasMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Freitas map: a two-dimensional piecewise map studied for its robust chaos (chaotic over a whole parameter window with no periodic windows), giving a reliably noise-like deterministic stream.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FrenchHorn}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FrenchHorn}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Breath, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

French horn: the warm, round, mellow brass - a sawtooth through a softer formant and a gentle lowpass with a slow swell, played by holding in0. Brass sets the formant bite, Breath the air, Level the output. in0 = Breath gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FricativeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FricativeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fricative oscillator: turbulent noise high-passed and shaped by a single broad resonant peak, the hiss of an unvoiced fricative like sh, s or f; the peak frequency sweeps the sound from a dark 'sh' to a bright 's'.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Fridge}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Fridge}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Refrigerator hum - a low compressor drone with a faint mechanical buzz and the occasional thermostat click, the quintessential room-tone. Tone sets the brightness, Buzz the rattle, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FrogCroakOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FrogCroakOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Frog-croak oscillator: a low buzzy tone chopped into a fast pulse train and gated into croak bursts, the ribbiting call of a frog or toad whose pulse rate and croak rate set the species; a comb-buzzy amphibian voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Frogs}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Frogs}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Frog pond - random low croaks with a watery resonance for a swamp/marsh night ambience. Activity sets how busy the pond is, Pitch the croak depth, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FrostCrackleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FrostCrackleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Frost crackle: the delicate snapping of ice crystals forming and contracting in the cold, a sparse field of very short, very high ticks with a faint glassy ring, the near-silent sound of a deep freeze; the density knob sets how fast the frost spreads.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FroudePendulum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FroudePendulum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Froude pendulum: a pendulum mounted on a steadily-rotating shaft whose velocity-dependent friction pumps energy in, self-oscillating and going chaotic - the mechanism behind machinery shudder and stick-slip vibration.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FutureBass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FutureBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Future-bass lead-bass: bright detuned supersaws with a pitch-glide attack and a lowpass, the gliding, vocal-ish drop bass of future bass. Glide sets the attack slide, Detune the width, Cutoff the brightness. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Gabber}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Gabber}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gabber / hardcore kick: a 909-style kick - a sine body with a downward pitch sweep - overdriven hard into clipping until it becomes a distorted, square-ish melodic tone, the way the classic gabber kick was made by feeding a 909 through an overdriven mixer channel. The pitch tail falls off slower than a clean kick so the body stays melodic. Tune sets the body pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), PSweep the pitch-tail length, Decay the body length, Distort the overdrive amount (the gabber character), Tone the final clip hardness (soft -> square), and Level the output. A trigger on in0 strikes it.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GaborAtomOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GaborAtomOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gabor atom: a sinusoid at a chosen carrier ratio windowed by a Gaussian, the minimum-uncertainty grain at the heart of granular synthesis and the Gabor transform; the carrier and window-width knobs set the grain's pitch and its time-frequency spread.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GallopingLine}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GallopingLine}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Conductor galloping: an ice-coated transmission line whose asymmetric cross-section gives negative aerodynamic damping (the Den-Hartog criterion), self-exciting large slow vertical oscillations that can clash the phases - power-grid aeroelastic chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GaltonBoard}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GaltonBoard}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Galton board: a ball bouncing left/right down N rows of pegs lands in a binomial bin, and by the central limit theorem the heap of many balls approaches a bell curve; summing N Bernoulli bounces makes a quantized, near-Gaussian source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GameBoyWaveOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GameBoyWaveOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Game Boy wave channel: a 32-sample, 4-bit wavetable (the DMG's user-definable voice), here morphing between a few classic LSDj-style wave shapes; its coarse quantization gives the gritty, characterful timbre of handheld chiptune.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GameOfLife1D}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GameOfLife1D}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

1-D life: a 32-cell ring where a cell lives next step only if exactly one of its two neighbours is alive (a one-dimensional birth/survival rule), breeding little gliders and blinkers; the live-cell density is read out as a fluttering CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GameOver}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GameOver}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Game-over jingle - a descending square-wave arpeggio that tumbles down, auto-repeating. Rate sets the replay interval, Pitch the key, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GameOverOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GameOverOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Game over: the deflating defeat sound, a square-wave tone sliding mournfully downward in pitch (often with a final low note), the dreaded end-of-game cue; the rate sets how often it repeats.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GammaFuncOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GammaFuncOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gamma-function oscillator: each cycle traces Gamma(x) (the continuous factorial, via Stirling) over a range spanning its minimum near x=1.46 - so the waveform dips to a soft trough then rises steeply, a convex U-with-a-steep-side shape distinct from the polynomial and bell curves. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GammaNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GammaNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gamma noise: the sum of k independent exponential variables (the Erlang distribution), the waiting time for k Poisson events; increasing the integer shape k pulls the distribution from a sharp exponential toward a symmetric bell - a tunable-skew positive source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GammaPdfOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GammaPdfOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gamma-PDF oscillator: each cycle is the Gamma distribution shape x^(K-1) e^(-x/Theta), a skewed bump whose rise sharpness (K) and decay length (Theta) are independent - peak-normalized to a bounded asymmetric waveform spanning near-exponential (low K) to near-Gaussian (high K).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GammatoneOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GammatoneOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gammatone: the impulse response of the auditory filters in the cochlea - a gamma-distribution envelope times a tone - widely used to model the ear's frequency analysis; looped per period it gives a soft attack-and-ring grain shaped like a hair-cell response.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GargleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GargleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gargle: a low throat tone whose saturation drive and amplitude burble on a smoothed random signal, so the timbre gurgles and bubbles wetly the way a gargle or a mud-pot does - Tone sets the pitch, Burble how violently it churns.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GarimaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GarimaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Garima oscillator: each cycle is the Garima PDF proportional to (1+Theta+Theta*x)e^(-Theta*x), a lifetime distribution whose linear lift is itself scaled by Theta - so the rise and the decay rate move together, a tightly-coupled skewed bump. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GaussCFMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GaussCFMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gauss map: the continued-fraction shift x -> {1/x} (fractional part of the reciprocal), whose iterates are the continued-fraction digits of the seed; ergodic with the Gauss-Kuzmin measure, it is a chaotic source biased toward small values.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GaussianPulseTrain}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GaussianPulseTrain}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gaussian pulse train: one Gaussian bump per period whose Width sets the spread (narrow gives a bright impulse-like click, wide gives a near-sine), the maximally time-frequency-compact pulse - a clean band-shaping source for grains and clicks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GaussMixtureOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GaussMixtureOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gaussian-mixture oscillator: each cycle sums two Gaussians placed symmetrically at +/-Sep, so Sep morphs the waveform from a single centred bump (Sep~0) into a clearly bimodal twin-peak shape - a two-hump waveform unlike the single-peaked bells. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GaussMouse}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GaussMouse}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gauss map: an exponential 'mouse' map whose bifurcation diagram folds back on itself, producing period-doubling and chaotic regimes from a bell-shaped iterator - smooth 1-D chaos with a distinctive flavor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GBMProcess}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GBMProcess}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Geometric Brownian motion: the multiplicative random walk dS = mu*S dt + sigma*S dW underlying the Black-Scholes model, where proportional drift and volatility make a positive, exponentially-trending, never-negative path - a stock-price-style slow CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GearRattle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GearRattle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gear rattle: a driven gear pair with backlash, where the teeth lose and regain contact across a dead-zone, producing the impacting, chattering chaos of an unloaded gearbox - piecewise-stiffness vibro-impact dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GearWhineOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GearWhineOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gear whine: the pitched whine of meshing gear teeth (the tooth-pass frequency and its harmonics) with a touch of irregular clatter from backlash, the sound of a gearbox under load; the mesh frequency sets the gear speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GegenbauerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GegenbauerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gegenbauer wave: the ultraspherical polynomial C_n^alpha on cos(theta), the family that interpolates between Chebyshev (alpha small) and Legendre (alpha = 1/2) and beyond, so alpha continuously reshapes the partial weighting of an n-lobe orthogonal-polynomial tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Geiger}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Geiger}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sparse-impulse noise: each sample has a probability set by Rate of firing a random-amplitude impulse, which then decays exponentially, all scaled by Level. Rate sets the average clicks per second and Decay sets how long each impulse rings before fading. Low Rate with short Decay gives sparse ticks like a Geiger counter, while higher Rate and longer Decay thicken it toward a crackling texture.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GeigerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GeigerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Geiger oscillator: sharp clicks arriving as a memoryless Poisson process, the random spacing of radioactive-decay events on a Geiger counter; the count-rate knob sweeps from a slow tick to the menacing buzz of a hot source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Gendy}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Gendy}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dynamic-stochastic (GENDYN) oscillator (a first as a synth block): Xenakis's radical idea of synthesising sound directly from probability - the waveform is a handful of breakpoints joined by straight lines, and after every cycle each breakpoint takes a small RANDOM WALK in height. So the wave keeps its rough shape but never repeats exactly, slowly mutating from one cycle to the next - the engine behind his GENDY pieces, sound built bottom-up from chance rather than from notes. Points sets the number of breakpoints and Step how far they wander each cycle (0 = frozen, high = noisy). A stochastic waveform distinct from every fixed-shape oscillator. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GeneralizedExtremeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GeneralizedExtremeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Generalized-extreme-value oscillator: each cycle is the GEV PDF, the single family unifying the Gumbel, Frechet and reversed-Weibull extreme-value laws - the shape parameter Xi morphs the tail from light (Xi<0, bounded) through Gumbel (Xi~0) to heavy (Xi>0), one knob across all three. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GeneralizedGammaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GeneralizedGammaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Generalized-gamma oscillator: each cycle is x^(D-1)*e^(-(x/1.5)^P), a three-way family that becomes exponential, Weibull or gamma depending on D and P - so two knobs span a wide range of skewed bump shapes from sharp spike to broad hump. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GenesioHyper}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GenesioHyper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic Genesio: the Genesio-Tesi jerk system extended to a fourth state with feedback into hyperchaos, layering a second positive Lyapunov exponent onto its smooth single-scroll jerk dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GenesioTesi}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GenesioTesi}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Genesio-Tesi attractor: a third-order jerk system (one variable, its first three derivatives) with a single quadratic term, the minimal chaotic 'jerk' circuit - smooth, single-scroll chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GeneToggle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GeneToggle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Genetic toggle switch: two genes each repressing the other form a bistable memory (Gardner-Collins synthetic biology); a slow forcing of one promoter flips it back and forth, giving a relaxation square-wave between cell states.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GenGaussianOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GenGaussianOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Generalized-normal oscillator: each cycle is exp(-|x|^Beta), so Beta morphs the symmetric bump from a sharp Laplacian cusp (Beta<1) through a pointed exponential (1) to a rounded Gaussian (2) - the cusp/peaked regime that complements the flat-top SuperGauss, rich in odd-order spectral character.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GenLogisticOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GenLogisticOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Generalized-logistic oscillator: each cycle is the type-I skew-logistic PDF a*e^-x/(1+e^-x)^(a+1), a logistic bell that Alpha skews to one side (a=1 is symmetric logistic) - a continuously-lopsided bump with logistic tails, peak-normalized and bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Geyser}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Geyser}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Geyser - a cyclic eruption that builds from a low gurgle into a hissing high-pressure blast, then subsides. Cycle sets the eruption period, Tone the spray brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GeyserOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GeyserOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Geyser: a periodic eruption, a low rumble that swells and then bursts into a roaring jet of pressurised steam and water before subsiding, looping at the eruption rate; the violent breath of a hot spring.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GFSRNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GFSRNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

GFSR noise: a generalized feedback shift register XORs the word with shifted copies of itself at two tap positions (the family behind the Mersenne Twister's recurrence), producing fast maximal-length noise with a distinct broadband spectrum.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GhostWailOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GhostWailOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ghost wail: the eerie wailing moan of a ghost, a pure tone slowly gliding up and down in pitch with a wide tremulous vibrato and a hollow breathy edge, the woo-oo-oo of a haunting; the glide rate sets the moan.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GingerMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GingerMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gingerbreadman map: a piecewise-linear 2-D map (x' = 1 - y + |x|) whose orbit fills a six-pointed chaotic region - angular, glitchy chaos with hard corners, unlike the smooth attractors.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GinzburgLandau}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GinzburgLandau}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Complex Ginzburg-Landau: the universal amplitude equation near a Hopf bifurcation, here a single mode whose amplitude saturates while its phase winds - the building block of pattern-forming and spatiotemporal-chaos models.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GlacialPaillard}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GlacialPaillard}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Paillard ice-age model: ice volume grows slowly then collapses when insolation crosses a threshold, switching between glacial regimes - a multiple-state relaxation oscillator that reproduces the sawtooth of the Pleistocene ice ages.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GlassFM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GlassFM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two-operator FM with bright, inharmonic ratios for bell, glass and electric-piano tones. Ratio sets the modulator-to-carrier frequency ratio and Index the modulation depth (brightness and clang).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GlassHarmonicOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GlassHarmonicOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Glass-harmonic oscillator: the widely-spaced ringing modes of a rubbed glass shell (ratios near 1, 2.3, 4.2, 6.6, 9.4), the pure, slowly-decaying, almost-sine-with-a-halo tone of a wine glass or Franklin glass armonica.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GlassHarp}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GlassHarp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pressure, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rubbed glass (glass harmonica / wine glass): a sustained, almost-pure tone with a slow swell and a faint shimmering second partial, played by holding in0 as the 'finger pressure'. The ethereal, breathy crystal-glass voice. Tune sets the pitch, Shimmer the vibrato depth, Level the output. in0 = Pressure gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GlassMode}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GlassMode}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Glass resonator: three high, sparsely-spaced inharmonic modes with a very long decay ring like a wet finger on a wineglass or a singing bowl when excited by the input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GlissonOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GlissonOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Glisson: a granular stream of glissons - grains that each sweep their own pitch from start to end (Roads's gliding grain) - producing a streaky, smeared texture full of tiny internal portamenti; the glide knob sets how far each grain slides.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GlitchStutterOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GlitchStutterOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Glitch stutter: a digital stutter effect, a tone hard-gated into a fast rhythmic on/off pattern that occasionally jumps pitch, the buffer-repeat glitch of IDM and breakcore; the stutter rate sets the chop speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GlobalCoupledMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GlobalCoupledMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Globally-coupled maps: three logistic maps each pulled toward the population mean (Kaneko's GCM), which spontaneously form and dissolve synchronized clusters - the simplest model of collective chaos and chimera-like clustering.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Glockenspiel}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Glockenspiel}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal glockenspiel: a trigger on in0 strikes a metal bar modelled as bright, long-ringing inharmonic partials (1 : 2.7 : 5.4 : 8.9), the clear bell-like tone of a glock/celesta. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the (long) ring, Mallet the brightness, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GlottalPulse}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GlottalPulse}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Glottal pulse source: a Rosenberg-shaped glottal flow pulse (asymmetric open phase + sharp closure) locked to the input pitch - the raw vocal-fold excitation you feed into a formant filter to synthesize voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GlottalPulseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GlottalPulseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Glottal pulse: the Rosenberg model of airflow through the vibrating vocal folds - a smooth rising open phase and a sharper closing return - which is the raw source signal that vocal-tract formants then shape into speech; the open-quotient knob sets vocal effort from breathy to pressed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GlowDischarge}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GlowDischarge}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Glow-discharge oscillator: the ionization-and-recombination dynamics of a neon glow tube biased near breakdown, whose negative-resistance plasma self-oscillates and, under a series capacitance, chaotically flickers.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GlucoseInsulin}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GlucoseInsulin}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Glucose-insulin loop: a model of the body's blood-sugar control where insulin suppresses glucose and glucose stimulates insulin with a delay, producing the ~2-hour ultradian oscillations (and, when forced, irregular swings) of metabolism.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GlycolyticHopf}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GlycolyticHopf}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Higgins glycolytic oscillator: the substrate-product feedback of the phosphofructokinase reaction whose autocatalysis crosses a Hopf bifurcation into the sustained glycolytic oscillations measured in yeast extracts - metabolic limit-cycle rhythm.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
