\phantomsection\label{blk:GoatBleatOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GoatBleatOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Goat bleat: the maaa of a goat, a voiced vowel with the famously fast, deep, almost-comic vibrato (faster and wider than a sheep's) that gives the screaming-goat character, fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GoldenDragon}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GoldenDragon}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Golden dragon: a dragon-curve variant whose two contraction ratios are set by the golden ratio (r and r^2 with r the plastic-like root), giving an asymmetric self-similar fractal subtly different from the classic Heighway dragon - a uniquely-textured coordinate CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GoldenOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GoldenOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Golden-ratio oscillator (a first as a synth block): an INHARMONIC tone whose partials are spaced not by whole numbers but by powers of the golden ratio (1, 1.62, 2.62, 4.24, 6.85...). Because no partial is a simple multiple of another, nothing fuses into a clear pitch the way a harmonic tone does - the result is a shimmering, metallic, bell-like timbre that sits between a pitch and a noise, the most irrationally-spaced spectrum there is. Partials sets how many golden overtones to stack, alias-free up to Nyquist. A phi-spaced inharmonic spectrum distinct from the integer-harmonic oscillators. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GoldenStretchOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GoldenStretchOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Golden-stretch oscillator: partials placed at powers of the golden ratio (1, 1.618, 2.618, 4.236 ...) instead of integers, the maximally-irrational spacing that minimizes any sense of a shared fundamental - a smooth, perfectly-inharmonic, bell-without-beating tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GompertzMakehamOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GompertzMakehamOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gompertz-Makeham oscillator: each cycle is the mortality hazard Alpha*e^(Beta*x)+lambda - a flat constant-risk floor that bends upward into an exponential climb - giving a waveform that hugs a baseline then accelerates skyward, the shape behind actuarial life tables. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GompertzOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GompertzOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gompertz oscillator: each cycle is the asymmetric Gompertz double-exponential sigmoid e^(-3 e^(-c x)), which rises slowly then snaps - a lopsided soft sawtooth rich in even harmonics, distinct from the symmetric Gudermannian/tanh ramps; Steep sets the asymmetry.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GompertzPdfOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GompertzPdfOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gompertz-PDF oscillator: each cycle is the Gompertz density b*eta*e^(b*x)*e^(-eta(e^(b*x)-1)), an increasing-hazard distribution that ramps up then is cut off sharply by the double-exponential - a right-leaning bump with a soft rise and an abrupt fall, distinct from the Gompertz sigmoid. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GompertzQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GompertzQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gompertz-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the Gompertz inverse-CDF (1/B)*ln(1 - ln(1-p)/Eta), a log-of-log ramp with a gentle start and a hard-cut-off finish (the inverse of the increasing-hazard Gompertz law) - B and Eta jointly shape the curvature. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Gong}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Gong}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal gong: a trigger on in0 strikes a large gong - a dense cluster of low inharmonic metal partials that wash together into a shimmering, slowly-decaying roar with a long sustain. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the long tail, Shimmer the upper-partial brightness and clangour, Level the output. Distinct from the noise-based Cymbal - this is tuned, blooming metal. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GongHit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GongHit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gong: three densely-spaced low inharmonic modes plus a noisy strike transient ring with a long shimmering decay, the crashing, slowly-blooming wash of a struck gong or tam-tam.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GongModeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GongModeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gong oscillator: a dense cluster of low inharmonic modes (ratios near 1, 1.46, 1.97, 2.44, 3.16, 4.0) with a slow detuning shimmer that mimics the nonlinear mode-coupling 'build-up' of a struck gong or tam-tam - a roaring, evolving metallic wash.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GongShowOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GongShowOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Big gong: the dramatic crash of a large struck gong (the game-show 'you're done' hit), a dense cluster of low inharmonic partials that shimmer up after the strike then slowly fade, fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GoodwinCycle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GoodwinCycle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Goodwin growth cycle: an economic predator-prey model where employment and the wage share chase each other (workers as predator, profit as prey), giving the self-sustaining boom-and-slump business cycle - econophysics as a slow modulator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GooseHonkOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GooseHonkOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Goose honk: the loud aggressive honk of a goose, a strident harmonic-rich voiced blast with a hard nasal edge, blaring in repeated honks, fired at the set rate; the warning of the gander.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GopyAttractor}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GopyAttractor}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Strange nonchaotic attractor (Grebogi-Ott-Pelikan-Yorke): a map quasiperiodically forced at the golden-ratio frequency, x' = 2*sigma*tanh(x)*cos(2 pi theta), whose attractor is geometrically fractal yet has no positive Lyapunov exponent - structured but not chaotic, a rare dynamical regime.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GrainCloudOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GrainCloudOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Grain cloud: an asynchronous granular texture where short windowed sine grains are sprayed at random times around a center pitch (Curtis Roads's grain cloud), building a shimmering, breathy cloud whose thickness rises with grain density.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GrainletOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GrainletOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Grainlet: a granular stream where each grain's active window shortens as its frequency rises (Roads's grainlet, after wavelet logic) so high grains are brief and low grains stretch, yielding a self-scaling texture; the link knob sets how tightly duration tracks pitch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GrainOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GrainOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Granular formant oscillator: emits one Hann-windowed sine grain per cycle of the fundamental, with the grain's internal frequency set by Formant - so pitch follows Freq while a fixed formant peak stays put, giving vocal, buzzy, FOF-style timbres. Freq sets the pitch, Formant the grain harmonic, Level the output. in0 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Granular}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Granular}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Granular oscillator/texture: continuously captures the input into a ~2 s ring buffer and sprays overlapping Hann-windowed grains read back from it. Size sets grain length in ms, Density the spawn rate in grains/sec, Pitch transposes each grain in semitones, and Jitter randomizes the read origin for a diffuse, smeared texture. Mix blends the grain cloud against the dry input -- low density gives a sparse stutter, high density a thick wash.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GranularGas}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GranularGas}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Granular gas: a shaken collection of inelastically-colliding grains whose energy input fights collisional cooling; the balance oscillates and, between clustered and gaseous states, jumps chaotically - non-equilibrium granular dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GrasshopperOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GrasshopperOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Grasshopper: stridulation, where the insect rasps a leg across a wing to make a fast train of dry noisy pulses grouped into short buzzing phrases with gaps; the pulse rate sets the rasp tempo.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GravWaveChirpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GravWaveChirpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gravitational-wave chirp: the audio analogue of a black-hole/neutron-star inspiral, where frequency and amplitude both sweep upward ever faster to a peak (merger) then cut to a brief ringdown, the LIGO 'chirp' that swept up and was heard in 2015; the rate sets the inspiral speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GrayScott}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GrayScott}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gray-Scott reaction: the two-species reaction kinetics behind Turing-pattern formation, run as a point oscillator that pulses and oscillates between chemical concentrations - organic, cell-like rhythmic modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GreenbergHastings}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GreenbergHastings}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Greenberg-Hastings model: the canonical excitable cellular automaton with rest, excited and graded refractory states, where a resting cell fires only if a neighbour is excited and then cycles through refractoriness before it can fire again - producing clean traveling fronts and reentrant waves.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GreyNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GreyNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Grey noise: white noise shaped by an inverted equal-loudness curve (boosted lows and highs, scooped mids) so it sounds equally loud at every frequency - the perceptually-flat noise used in hearing tests.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GrindCoffeeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GrindCoffeeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coffee grinder: beans crunching through a burr grinder, a harsh low motor whir overlaid with a gritty crackling crunch as beans shatter, the morning-ritual grind; the grit knob sets how many beans crunch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GrossPitaevskii}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GrossPitaevskii}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gross-Pitaevskii mode: a single mode of a Bose-Einstein condensate whose self-interaction (cubic nonlinearity) and a modulated trap make its amplitude and phase oscillate and, under strong driving, go chaotic - matter-wave nonlinear dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Growl}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Growl}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dubstep / brostep growl bass: an FM voice whose modulation index and a midrange formant band-pass are swept together by one LFO, then pushed through heavy distortion - moving the FM amount and the formant at once is what gives the Skrillex-style 'talking' growl, distinct from a plain filter wobble. Tune sets the pitch (in0 = Pitch CV), Rate the growl LFO speed (tempo-sync it), Ratio the FM modulator ratio, Index the FM depth, Formant the band-pass centre the LFO sweeps, Drive the distortion, and Level the output. Layer a clean Sub808 under it for the low end.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GrowlBass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GrowlBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Growl bass: a sawtooth through a sharp formant bandpass swept by a built-in LFO, giving a vocal, growling, talking-bass character. Growl sets the LFO rate, Formant the centre, Drive the grit. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GudermannianOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GudermannianOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gudermannian oscillator: each cycle is the Gudermannian gd(x)=2*atan(e^x)-pi/2 over a symmetric span, a transcendental S-curve that links the circular and hyperbolic functions - a soft sawtooth with a gentler-than-tanh knee, smoothly sweeping from near-sine to near-saw with Width.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Guiro}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Guiro}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Guiro scraper: a trigger on in0 plays one stroke across the ridges of a guiro - a 'rrrip' of band-passed noise rhythmically chopped by the teeth at Rate, the rasping scrape of Latin and cumbia percussion. Rate sets how fast the ridges pass, Length the stroke duration, Tone the brightness, Level the output. Re-trigger for the up-down stroke pattern.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GuitarPluckOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GuitarPluckOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Guitar pluck: a Karplus-Strong delay line excited by a noise burst and damped by a lowpass in the feedback, the classic plucked-string algorithm; re-plucked at the set rate, the damping knob takes it from a bright nylon ping to a dull muted thunk.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GulpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GulpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gulp: the sound of swallowing, a short downward liquid glug ending in a soft throat click, the cartoonish 'gulp' of a hard swallow, fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GumbelCdfOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GumbelCdfOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gumbel-CDF oscillator: each cycle is the Gumbel (extreme-value) cumulative e^(-e^(-x)), a strongly asymmetric S-curve that rises sharply on the left then crawls toward the top - a lopsided ramp distinct from the symmetric logistic/erf sigmoids. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GumbelNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GumbelNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gumbel noise: samples -ln(-ln u) from the type-I extreme-value distribution, the limiting law of the maximum of many samples (flood levels, record temperatures); right-skewed with a long upper tail - a maxima-flavoured asymmetric noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GumbelQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GumbelQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gumbel-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the Gumbel (extreme-value) inverse-CDF -ln(-ln(p)), a doubly-logarithmic ramp that crawls up slowly then shoots toward the top - a strongly right-skewed monotone curve distinct from the symmetric quantiles. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GumowskiMiraMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GumowskiMiraMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gumowski-Mira map: a 2-D map from particle-accelerator physics whose rational nonlinearity produces ornate, organic, almost-biological fractal orbits - a richly-textured chaotic source unlike the polynomial maps.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GunnDiode}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GunnDiode}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gunn diode: a bulk-semiconductor device whose electron transfer between energy valleys gives a negative-differential-resistance (N-shaped) velocity curve, self-oscillating into the relaxation/chaotic regime of a Gunn microwave source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Gunshot}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Gunshot}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gunshot SFX: a trigger on in0 fires the layered crack of a weapon - a bright transient snap, a band-passed noise body for the weight, a sub LFE thump with a fast pitch drop, and a noise tail for the room reflection. The film / game firearm report. Tone sets the body and transient brightness, Body the low-mid weight, Sub the LFE thump level, Tail the room-decay length, and Level the output. Trigger from a sequencer or one-shot.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Guqin}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Guqin}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Guqin: the ancient Chinese 7-string fretless zither of the scholar tradition - a soft silk-string pluck with a long, quiet, intimate sustain and the famous sliding portamento (no frets) plus flageolet harmonics; the meditative voice of Chinese literati music. Slide sets the attack glide, Harmonic the flageolet shimmer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GurgleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GurgleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gurgle: water draining down a plughole, a low resonant bubbling tone whose pitch and loudness lurch irregularly as air glugs back through the vortex, the throaty gurgle of an emptying sink.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Guzheng}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Guzheng}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Guzheng: the Chinese plucked zither - a bright, ringing metallic Karplus string with the signature attack pitch-bend (the player's press behind the bridge), giving the shimmering, pentatonic, glissando-rich voice of Chinese classical music; re-plucked at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GyroNutation}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GyroNutation}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gyroscope nutation: a fast-spinning heavy top whose axis precesses while nodding (nutating); weak spin and a forcing torque make the nodding angle wander chaotically between precession and tumbling - rotational rigid-body dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GyrostatOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GyrostatOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gyrostat: a rigid body carrying a spinning internal rotor whose Euler equations, with a gyrostatic momentum bias, tumble chaotically - the attitude dynamics of a spinning spacecraft losing control.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HaarWaveletOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HaarWaveletOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Haar wavelet: the first and simplest wavelet, +1 on the first half of the period and -1 on the second (a single-cycle square step), the orthonormal basis behind the fastest wavelet transform - a hard, hollow, square-edged tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HadleyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HadleyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hadley attractor: a low-order model of a Hadley convection cell in the atmosphere, whose chaotic orbit drifts on a slow, weather-like timescale - organic long-form modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Hail}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Hail}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hailstorm - a dense barrage of sharp icy ticks over a rain bed. Intensity sets the hail density, Tone the hardness of each strike, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HailOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HailOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hail: a dense random scatter of hard icy impacts hammering a surface, each a short bright high-passed click, building from occasional taps to a deafening clatter; the density knob sets the storm's intensity.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HalfCauchyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HalfCauchyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Half-Cauchy oscillator: each cycle is the half-Cauchy PDF 2/(pi(1+x^2)) over x>=0, a one-sided Lorentzian that starts at its peak and decays with a very heavy (power-law) tail - a front-loaded falling waveform with far more tail energy than a half-normal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HalfLogisticOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HalfLogisticOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Half-logistic oscillator: each cycle is the half-logistic PDF 2e^-x/(1+e^-x)^2 over x>=0, a one-sided bump that starts at its peak and decays with logistic (heavier-than-Gaussian) tails - a front-loaded falling waveform distinct from the half-normal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HalleyFractal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HalleyFractal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Halley fractal: the cubically-convergent Halley root-finding iteration applied to z^3-1 in the complex plane, whose basins of attraction interlock in a fractal pattern with thinner boundaries than Newton's; the reseeded orbit's real part is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HalvorsenOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HalvorsenOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Halvorsen attractor: a symmetric three-equation chaotic flow with quadratic cross-terms that traces a tight three-lobed spiral - a denser, faster-folding chaos source than the Lorenz family.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HammerDulcimerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HammerDulcimerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hammered dulcimer: a bright metal string struck by a light mallet, modelled as a quick-attack Karplus pluck with a faint detuned sympathetic shimmer (the neighbouring courses ringing), the silvery sustain of a hammered dulcimer or cimbalom.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Hammond}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Hammond}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tonewheel organ (Hammond): nine sine drawbars summed into one rich sustained voice, with a percussive key-click on attack and a Leslie-style vibrato - the gospel/rock B3 sound. Bright mixes in the upper drawbars, Leslie the rotary depth, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Handbell}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Handbell}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Handbell: a clear, ringing tuned handbell - a strong fundamental with an octave and a twelfth, a bright strike and a medium shimmering decay. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Handpan}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Handpan}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Struck handpan / hang drum: mellow near-harmonic partials (1 : 2 : 3 : 4) with a soft attack and medium-long sustain - the warm, meditative hang voice. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HandpanHit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HandpanHit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Handpan voice: three modes tuned to a fundamental, its octave and its twelfth (the near-harmonic ratios of a hang/handpan dimple) ring with a warm, bell-meets-marimba tone when struck by the input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HandpanOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HandpanOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Handpan: a struck note-dimple of a hang/handpan, voiced as a soft fundamental locked with its octave and compound-fifth overtones (the deliberate handpan tuning), giving a warm, meditative, bell-like ding; re-struck at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HannPoissonOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HannPoissonOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hann-Poisson oscillator: each cycle is a Hann window multiplied by a two-sided exponential (Poisson) taper, so Alpha sharpens the bump's decay - a cosine bell with exponentially-steepened skirts, between a Hann and a sech pulse in character.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HannSquaredOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HannSquaredOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hann-squared oscillator: each cycle is sin^4(pi*p), the Hann window squared - a narrower, more sharply-peaked bump than the Hann with even softer edges, giving a focused pulse with a fast-decaying harmonic series.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HardLead}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HardLead}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hardstyle lead: detuned sawtooths slammed through drive into a bright filter for the screaming, euphoric rawstyle lead. Detune sets the width, Drive the distortion, Tone the brightness. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HardSync}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HardSync}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hard-sync oscillator: a slave saw is reset every cycle of the master pitch, and Sync sweeps the slave frequency up for the classic tearing, vowel-like sync lead. Shape blends saw toward square.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HardSyncBlep}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HardSyncBlep}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Band-limited hard sync: a slave saw is reset by a master oscillator and the discontinuity at each sync reset is smoothed with a PolyBLEP, giving the screaming sync-sweep timbre without the heavy aliasing of a naive reset.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HardSyncOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HardSyncOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hard sync: a bright slave sawtooth is force-reset to zero every time the master oscillator completes a cycle, so sweeping the slave-to-master ratio sweeps a formant peak through the spectrum - the screaming sync-lead timbre.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HardSyncSaw}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HardSyncSaw}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hard-sync sawtooth: a free-running internal saw is reset to phase zero on each input zero crossing, so its (independent) Freq sweeps the classic hard-sync formant tearing while staying locked to the input pitch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Harmonica}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Harmonica}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Harmonica voice: a gate on in0 sounds two slightly-detuned free reeds beating together into the bright wheezing tone of a blues harp, with vibrato. Freq sets the note, Detune the reed beating (thin to thick), Vibrato the waver, Level the output. Hold the gate to sustain; in1 is the Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HarmonicaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HarmonicaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Harmonica oscillator: a free reed whose buzzy, slightly-detuned tone (a sawtooth-rich source through the reed's own resonance) plus a wavering airflow models the reedy, bluesy timbre of a blues harp; the bend knob droops the pitch like a draw-bend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HarmSweepOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HarmSweepOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sweeping-formant additive: eight harmonics of the pitch are summed under a Gaussian emphasis window that an LFO slides up and down the harmonic series, so a bright resonant peak travels through the spectrum - a vowel-like wah that is built into the oscillator, not a filter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HarperMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HarperMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kicked Harper map: an area-preserving map x' = x + K sin(p), p' = p - L sin(x') derived from the Harper (almost-Mathieu) Hamiltonian behind the Hofstadter butterfly; it mixes regular tori and chaotic seas as the kick strengths vary - a quantum-chaos-flavoured source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HarpPluckOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HarpPluckOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Harp pluck: a Karplus-Strong string with a softened (lowpassed) excitation and very light damping, giving the gentle attack and long, shimmering decay of a concert-harp string; re-plucked at the set rate for arpeggiated runs.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Harpsichord}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Harpsichord}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Harpsichord: a plectrum-plucked string pair (8' + 4' octave) with a bright, thin, buzzy attack and a quick decay - the baroque keyboard voice. Bright sets the upper-octave mix, Decay the ring, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HarpsichordOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HarpsichordOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Harpsichord: a metal string plucked by a quill plectrum - a very bright, lightly-damped Karplus string preceded by a sharp pluck click - giving the crisp, jangly, uniform-dynamic tone of a harpsichord; re-plucked at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HarpsichordVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HarpsichordVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Harpsichord voice: a bright, lightly-damped Karplus string plucked sharply by a gate (the quill jack) with a touch of body resonance, the crisp, metallic, even-decaying tone of a harpsichord.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HartleyChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HartleyChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaotic Hartley oscillator: the tapped-inductor Hartley RF oscillator pushed past its limit cycle by the transistor's exponential transfer, a three-state circuit that tumbles into chaos - companion to the chaotic Colpitts.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HassellMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HassellMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hassell map: the population model x -> r x/(1+x)^b, whose tunable competition exponent moves it between stable, cyclic and chaotic regimes - a rational-feedback population chaos with its own bifurcation route.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HastingsPowell}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HastingsPowell}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hastings-Powell food chain: resource, consumer and top predator linked by saturating (Holling type-II) feeding, whose three-level interaction produces the famous 'tea-cup' chaotic attractor of population ecology.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Hat}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Hat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Analog hi-hat: a trigger on in0 fires bright high-passed metallic noise with a short percussive decay. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the length (short = closed, long = open hat), Metal blends in inharmonic ringing for a more cymbal-like tone, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Hat606}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Hat606}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-606 hi-hat: a bright metallic hat built from four detuned squares through a sharp highpass with a fast decay - thinner and brasher than the 808 hat, the sizzling Drumatix top end. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the length (short = closed, long = open), Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Hat808}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Hat808}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-808 hi-hat: a trigger on in0 fires six detuned square-wave oscillators (the 808's metal-on-metal ring) through a bright highpass with a fast decay. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the length (short = closed, long = open), Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Hat909}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Hat909}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-909 hi-hat: six detuned squares blended with a burst of white noise through a sharp highpass - noisier and more sizzling than the 808 hat, the bright house/techno cymbal top. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the length (short = closed, long = open), Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HatSynth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HatSynth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Synth hi-hat: a gate fires a bright high-passed noise burst with a fast decay (open vs closed set by Decay), the sizzle on top of a drum-machine kit - a self-contained hat voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HealOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HealOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Healing glow: a restorative spell, a warm major chord of soft sine partials that swells in gently with a slow shimmering tremolo and a faint rising sparkle, the comforting glow of a heal/buff; the rate sets the pulse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Heartbeat}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Heartbeat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Heartbeat: a self-clocking 'lub-dub' double sub-thump - two soft low pulses per beat (the first louder), each a sine with a fast pitch drop and a muffled decay - the tension pulse of film and game sound design. Rate sets the beats per second (resting ~1, racing ~2.5), Tune the thump pitch, Decay the thump length, Tone the muffled softness (low-pass), and Level the output. Self-running; automate Rate to race the pulse for suspense.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HeartbeatOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HeartbeatOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Heartbeat: the lub-dub of a beating heart, two soft low thumps per cycle (the first louder, the second quieter and close behind) repeating at the pulse rate, the muffled cardiac pulse heard through a stethoscope; the BPM knob sets the heart rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HeartCurveOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HeartCurveOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Heart-curve oscillator: the y-coordinate 13cos t - 5cos2t - 2cos3t - cos4t of the classic heart curve, a fixed-phase blend of the first four harmonics with decreasing weights - a warm, slightly hollow tone with a built-in spectral tilt, no filtering needed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HedgeTrimmerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HedgeTrimmerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hedge trimmer: an electric hedge trimmer cutting, a buzzing motor under a fast reciprocating blade clatter that rattles as the teeth chatter back and forth, the garden-hedge sound; the stroke rate sets the blade speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HeighwayDragon}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HeighwayDragon}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Heighway dragon: the chaos game over the two rotate-and-scale maps that generate the dragon curve (the paper-folding fractal), whose 45-degree contractions tile the plane; the iterate's coordinate is a self-similar, square-cornered fractal CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Helicopter}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Helicopter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Helicopter - a deep rotor thump (blade-rate amplitude chop) under an engine whine. Rotor sets the blade rate, Tone the engine brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HelicopterOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HelicopterOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Helicopter oscillator: periodic blade-slap thumps at the blade-passage frequency layered with a steady rotor-whine tone, the unmistakable chop-chop-whine of a helicopter; blade count and RPM set the rhythm of the slaps.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Helmholtz}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Helmholtz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Helmholtz resonator: a single high-Q resonance (the air-spring of a bottle or cavity) excited by breath noise whistles into a pure, breathy tone, the physics of blowing across a bottle top.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HelmholtzDuffing}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HelmholtzDuffing}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Helmholtz-Duffing oscillator: a forced oscillator with both quadratic and cubic stiffness (an asymmetric potential with a finite escape barrier), used to model capsize and snap-through; near the escape it is chaotic and intermittent.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Henon3D}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Henon3D}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

3-D Henon map: a three-dimensional generalization of the Henon map (x'=a-y^2-b z, y'=x, z'=y) whose extra delayed coordinate gives a thicker, more folded fractal attractor than the planar Henon.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HenonArea}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HenonArea}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Area-preserving Henon map: the conservative quadratic twist map (a rotation followed by a parabolic shear) whose phase plane is a fractal sea of chaos riddled with stable islands - Hamiltonian map chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HenonHeiles}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HenonHeiles}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Henon-Heiles system: the energy-conserving Hamiltonian model of a star orbiting in a galactic potential, whose coupled quadratic potential makes bounded orbits go chaotic - conservative (non-decaying) chaos with rich quasi-periodic structure.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HenonHeilesSection}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HenonHeilesSection}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Henon-Heiles system: the two-degree-of-freedom Hamiltonian devised for stellar orbits in a galactic potential, whose cubic coupling turns regular orbits chaotic above a critical energy; integrated here as a conservative oscillator, one position coordinate is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HenonMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HenonMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Henon map: the canonical 2-D quadratic chaotic map (x'=1-a x^2+y, y'=b x) whose attractor is the textbook stretched-and-folded fractal, iterated at a chosen rate as a crisp digital chaos source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HenonOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HenonOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Henon attractor source: the two-variable Henon map (x' = 1 - a x^2 + y, y' = b x) produces a folded, banded chaotic signal - a 2-D strange attractor turned into sound, distinct from 1-D chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HermiteGaussOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HermiteGaussOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hermite-Gaussian wave: the energy eigenstates of the quantum harmonic oscillator - a Hermite polynomial times a Gaussian - swept across the phase; the Gaussian taper makes the cycle ends meet smoothly while the order sets the lobe count, a physics-derived band-limited pulse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HestonVol}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HestonVol}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Heston model: a price driven by a volatility that is itself a randomly-varying (CIR) process, so calm and turbulent regimes alternate - the two coupled SDEs that give the volatility clustering and fat tails real markets show.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HiccupOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HiccupOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hiccup: the abrupt 'hic' of a diaphragm spasm snapping the glottis shut, a very short voiced chirp cut off by silence, repeating at the slow irregular hiccup rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HicksCycle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HicksCycle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hicks trade cycle: a multiplier-accelerator economy whose investment is clipped between a full-employment ceiling and a scrappage floor; those piecewise limits turn an explosive oscillation into a bounded, irregular business cycle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HiddenLineEq}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HiddenLineEq}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Line-equilibrium flow: a three-dimensional chaotic system whose equilibria form an entire line rather than isolated points; its attractor is hidden because that line is unstable everywhere, a modern class of exotic chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HiHatSynthOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HiHatSynthOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hi-hat voice: six inharmonically-tuned square oscillators summed and high-passed into a metallic hiss, gated by a fast decay (the 808 hi-hat recipe), re-triggered at the set rate; the decay knob switches between closed and open hats.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HillEquationOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HillEquationOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hill-equation oscillator: each cycle is the Hill dose-response sigmoid x^N/(K^N+x^N) (cooperative-binding pharmacology) - the Hill coefficient N sets the steepness of the switch and K its midpoint, a sigmoid ramp whose sharpness and threshold are independently controllable.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HindmarshNetwork}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HindmarshNetwork}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coupled HR neurons: two Hindmarsh-Rose bursting cells joined by a fast chemical synapse, which synchronize, fire in anti-phase or burst chaotically depending on coupling - a minimal model of neural-network rhythm generation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HindmarshOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HindmarshOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hindmarsh-Rose neuron: a three-variable model that fires bursts of spikes separated by quiet intervals (bursting), giving rhythmic clusters of clicks - neural burst patterns no LFO produces.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HindmarshRose2D}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HindmarshRose2D}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hindmarsh-Rose fast subsystem: the two-variable core of the HR neuron (voltage + recovery, without the slow adaptation), a compact relaxation oscillator that fires fast spikes or sits bistable depending on the current.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HingeSqueakOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HingeSqueakOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hinge squeak: a sharp rusty metal-on-metal squeal, a high resonant tone modulated by a fast irregular stick-slip chatter, the piercing squeak of an unoiled hinge; the squeal pitch sets the metal size.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HippopedeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HippopedeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hippopede oscillator: the x-coordinate of Booth's hippopede r^2 = 4b(1 - b*sin^2(theta)); as B approaches 1 the curve tends to a figure-eight and the waveform sharpens toward a rectified-cosine shape with strong even harmonics - a smoothly morphable lobed tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HitzlZele}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HitzlZele}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hitzl-Zele map: a three-dimensional quadratic iterated map (one of the early computer-search-discovered 3-D chaotic maps) whose coupled squared terms fold a compact strange attractor - crisp, high-rate map chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HIVModel}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HIVModel}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

HIV dynamics: healthy and infected T-cells and free virus in the classic Perelson-style model, whose infection-and-clearance feedback can oscillate and (with immune-response delay) go chaotic - viral-load dynamics as a modulator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HodgepodgeMachine}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HodgepodgeMachine}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hodgepodge machine: a chemical cellular automaton where cells carry an infection level - healthy cells catch it from infected neighbours, infected cells intensify then suddenly recover - reproducing the rotating spiral waves of the Belousov-Zhabotinsky reaction. Mean infection is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HodgkinHuxley}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HodgkinHuxley}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hodgkin-Huxley neuron: the Nobel-winning four-variable conductance model (membrane voltage plus sodium m/h and potassium n gating) fires biophysically-accurate action-potential spike trains under injected current - the gold-standard spiking-neuron source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HodgkinHuxleyBurst}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HodgkinHuxleyBurst}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bursting Hodgkin-Huxley: the full HH spiking neuron augmented with a slow calcium-activated potassium current that periodically silences the fast spikes, producing biophysically-detailed bursting and, when forced, chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HopalongB}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HopalongB}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hopalong (variant B): a three-parameter form of Barry Martin's hopalong iterated map, whose sign-and-sqrt fold lays down a denser, more lattice-like fractal than the basic hopalong - jumpy, granular chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HopalongMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HopalongMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hopalong attractor: Barry Martin's iterated map (using sign and square-root of the running coordinate) that hops around building a layered fractal, a distinctive jumpy chaotic texture popularised in computer-recreations columns.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HopfieldChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HopfieldChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaotic Hopfield network: four continuous-time neurons connected by an asymmetric weight matrix (which breaks the usual convergence guarantee) wander chaotically through state space instead of settling - an artificial-neural-network chaos source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HopfNetwork3}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HopfNetwork3}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Three-neuron Hopfield net: three sigmoidal neurons wired with an asymmetric weight matrix that defeats the usual convergence, so the network wanders chaotically through state space instead of settling on a memory - a compact neural-network chaos source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HopfOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HopfOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hopf oscillator: a normal-form Hopf limit cycle whose amplitude self-stabilizes to sqrt(mu) - a self-sustaining sine that grows from silence and is amplitude-locked, the model behind cochlear and active-resonator synthesis.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HorseNeighOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HorseNeighOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Horse neigh: the whinny of a horse, a bright voiced tone that starts high, breaks into a fast fluttering warble and descends, fired at the set rate; the spirited call of a horse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HouseflyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HouseflyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Housefly: the irritating erratic buzz of a fly, a mid-frequency wingbeat tone that randomly jumps in pitch as the insect darts and changes direction, never settling; the jumpiness knob sets how frantic the flight.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HulthenOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HulthenOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hulthen oscillator: each cycle is the Hulthen screened-potential e^(-Theta*r)/(1-e^(-Theta*r)), which diverges to a sharp spike near the origin (like a Coulomb tail) then decays exponentially - a very front-loaded, spiky-then-falling waveform. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Hum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Hum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Closed-mouth hum: the soft, nasal 'mmm' of humming - a mellow sine with a low nasal formant and a gentle second harmonic. Nasal sets the formant emphasis, Vibrato the wobble, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.
