\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HurtOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HurtOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hurt: taking a hit, a short harsh buzz of square wave mixed with noise and a quick downward pitch drop, the unpleasant damage zap, fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Hyper4Wing}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Hyper4Wing}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic four-wing: a four-dimensional system whose orbit jumps between four wings while expanding chaotically in two directions, combining multi-lobe switching with hyperchaos for especially wild modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperbolicChirpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperbolicChirpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperbolic-chirp oscillator: within each cycle the phase warps hyperbolically (via ln(1-Bend*t)), so the frequency sweep accelerates even more sharply toward the cycle end than a log chirp - a dramatic rising glide that snaps back. Bend sets how extreme the late acceleration is.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperbolicDistOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperbolicDistOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperbolic-distribution oscillator: each cycle is exp(-Alpha*sqrt(1+x^2)+Beta*x), the hyperbolic PDF whose log-density is a hyperbola - so it has a sharper peak and heavier, exponentially-decaying tails than a Gaussian, with Beta skewing it; peak-normalized and bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperbolicSpiralOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperbolicSpiralOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperbolic-spiral oscillator: the projection (1/theta)*cos(theta) of the spiral r=1/theta, a cosine whose amplitude decays as the cycle progresses then snaps back - a reverse-ramp swell (loud attack, fading body) opposite to the growing Fermat/Involute spirals.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperChen}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperChen}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic Chen: the Chen attractor augmented with a fourth controller state and feedback into hyperchaos (two positive Lyapunov exponents), an especially turbulent broadband chaotic source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperChen5D}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperChen5D}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

5-D hyperchaotic Chen: the Chen attractor lifted to five dimensions with two controller states, yielding multiple positive Lyapunov exponents and a dense, maximally-unpredictable chaotic signal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperexpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperexpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperexponential oscillator: each cycle is a mixture of two exponential decays at different rates, Mix blending a fast and a slow falloff - a front-loaded waveform with a compound (knee-then-tail) decay distinct from a single exponential; peak-normalized and bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperJerk}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperJerk}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperjerk (snap) system: a single fourth-order differential equation in one variable and its first three derivatives (a 'snap' circuit), the minimal scalar route to hyperchaos with two positive Lyapunov exponents.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperJerk5D}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperJerk5D}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

5-D hyperjerk: a single fifth-order scalar equation (x and its first four derivatives - 'snap' and 'crackle') with a tanh nonlinearity, the minimal one-variable route to multi-exponent hyperchaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperJha}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperJha}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic Jha: a four-state Lorenz-derived hyperchaotic system with an added linear feedback loop, producing dense high-dimensional chaos for the most unpredictable modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperLorenz}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperLorenz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic Lorenz: the Lorenz system augmented with a fourth feedback state so it has two positive Lyapunov exponents, expanding chaotically in two directions for an especially unpredictable, broadband signal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperLorenz5D}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperLorenz5D}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

5-D hyperchaotic Lorenz: the Lorenz system extended with two extra feedback states, supporting up to three positive Lyapunov exponents - an extremely high-dimensional, broadband chaotic source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperLu}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperLu}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic Lu: the Lu attractor extended with a fourth state and feedback gain into hyperchaos, a dense, high-dimensional chaotic source for the most turbulent modulation and tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperPang}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperPang}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic Pang: a Lorenz-derived four-dimensional system with an extra state coupled into the first two equations, yielding hyperchaos with a broad, twin-scroll-plus-drift orbit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperRabinovich}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperRabinovich}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic Rabinovich: the plasma-wave Rabinovich system extended with a fourth state and feedback into hyperchaos, combining the spiky cubic dynamics of Rabinovich-Fabrikant with two-directional chaotic expansion.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperRikitake}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperRikitake}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic Rikitake: the two-disk geodynamo model extended with a fourth feedback state into hyperchaos, intensifying the chaotic field reversals of the Rikitake dynamo - turbulent geomagnetic-style switching.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperRossler}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperRossler}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rossler hyperchaos: the four-variable Rossler system with two positive Lyapunov exponents, so it expands chaotically in two directions at once - a more violently unpredictable signal than 3-D chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperSaw}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperSaw}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Seven detuned saw voices in a tight unison (a classic supersaw): Detune spreads the voices and Blend balances the centre voice against the spread sides for anything from a subtle thickening to a screaming trance lead.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperSprott5D}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperSprott5D}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

5-D hyperchaotic Sprott flow: a minimal five-variable chain with a single quadratic nonlinearity and cyclic linear coupling tuned for two positive Lyapunov exponents - an elegant, high-dimensional hyperchaos generator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperWang}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperWang}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic Wang: a four-state system with two quadratic couplings and a linear controller that produces two positive Lyapunov exponents, a dense broadband hyperchaotic source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HyperYu}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HyperYu}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic Yu: a four-state Lorenz-derived system with a fourth feedback variable producing two positive Lyapunov exponents, a dense, high-dimensional chaotic source for broadband turbulence.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HypocycloidOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HypocycloidOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hypocycloid oscillator: the curve from a point on a circle rolling inside a larger one (the family of the astroid and deltoid), whose ratio sets the number of inward cusps; the x-coordinate gives a pinched, star-like waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:IceCrackOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{IceCrackOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cracking ice: a frozen lake or glacier under stress, sharp snapping cracks each followed by a low eerie groan as the fracture propagates and the sheet resonates, fired at the set rate; the haunting voice of ice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:IceSpellOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{IceSpellOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ice spell: a freezing frost magic, a glassy cluster of high inharmonic partials with a crystalline shimmer and scattered shattering tinkles, the cold crackle of an ice attack; the rate sets the cast frequency.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:IkedaDelay}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{IkedaDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ikeda delay system: a delay-differential model of light in a nonlinear ring cavity (x' = -x + mu sin(x(t-tau))), whose feedback through a sine of its delayed self generates broadband delay-induced chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:IkedaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{IkedaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ikeda map: the discrete laser-ring-cavity map whose rotation angle depends on the current radius, iterated at a chosen rate - a tightly-folded 2-D chaos with a very different texture from the flows.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Impact}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Impact}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cinematic drop impact: a trigger on in0 fires a deep sub-sine whose pitch plunges from a high boom down to Tune, layered with a short noise crack on the transient and a long decaying sub tail - the 'boom' that lands on a drop or downbeat. Tune sets the final sub pitch, Boom the pitch-plunge depth and time, Decay the sub-tail length, Noise the transient crack level, and Level the output. Distinct from Kick: a deep cinematic hit, not a tuned drum.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ImpactBoomOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ImpactBoomOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Impact boom: a cinematic trailer hit, a deep sub-frequency thump with a downward pitch drop layered with a bright noise crack at the transient, the chest-thumping BRAAAM-impact of film sound design; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ImpactOscillator}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ImpactOscillator}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Impact oscillator: a sinusoidally-driven spring-mass that bounces off a rigid wall with partial restitution; near-grazing impacts trigger the discontinuity-induced chaos studied in rattling machinery and vibro-impact systems.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Impulse}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Impulse}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Band-limited impulse/pulse train (a BLIT): a stream of clean clicks at the pitch rate, the raw material for subtractive synthesis and excitation. Width morphs the impulse toward a narrow pulse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:InharmBellOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{InharmBellOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Inharmonic bell: five sine partials tuned to non-integer ratios (the clangorous spectrum of a struck bell or metal bar), with a Stretch knob that pulls the upper partials further from harmonicity - from chime to dissonant gong, sustained rather than struck.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Insects}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Insects}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Insect night ambience: a bed of randomly-timed cricket chirps - a high tone (the stridulation pitch) pulsed by a fast wing-stroke amplitude modulation and gated into short chirps - the field, jungle and night-time critter ambience of film sound design. Self-running. Density sets how often the chirps fire, Pitch the stridulation tone, Rate the wing-stroke pulse speed inside each chirp, Spread the random pitch variation between insects, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:InsectSwarmOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{InsectSwarmOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Insect-swarm oscillator: a cluster of detuned buzzing tones (a cloud of flying insects), each randomly fluttering in level as individuals weave nearer and farther, summing into the restless drone of a swarm; spread sets how wide the detune.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:IntegrateFireChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{IntegrateFireChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Driven integrate-and-fire: a leaky integrate-and-fire neuron with a sinusoidal drive whose firing times become chaotic (devil's-staircase / mode-locking structure), emitting an irregular pulse train locked loosely to the drive.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:IntercomBuzzOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{IntercomBuzzOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Intercom buzzer: the harsh rasping buzz of an apartment door-entry intercom releasing the lock, a gritty square-wave tone gated in short blasts with a mains-frequency rasp; the let-me-in buzz.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:IntermodOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{IntermodOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Intermodulation oscillator: two tones at a non-integer ratio passed through a cubic nonlinearity, which breeds sum-and-difference intermodulation product tones (2f1-f2, 2f2-f1, ...) - the clangorous sideband cluster of overdriven mixers and amps.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:InverseExponentialOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{InverseExponentialOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Inverse-exponential oscillator: each cycle is the PDF of 1/X for an exponential X, (1/x^2)e^(-1/x), which is zero at the origin, rises to a sharp off-centre peak near x=0.5, then decays with a heavy tail - a hollow-then-spiked shape distinct from the centre-peaked bells. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:InverseGammaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{InverseGammaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Inverse-gamma oscillator: each cycle is the inverse-gamma PDF x^(-a-1) e^(-b/x), a right-skewed bump with a hard zero approach at the origin and a heavy polynomial tail - Alpha sets the tail weight, Beta the peak position; peak-normalized and bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:InverseRayleighOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{InverseRayleighOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Inverse-Rayleigh oscillator: each cycle is the inverse-Rayleigh PDF (1/x^3)e^(-1/(2*sigma^2*x^2)), which is zero at the origin, rises to a sharp off-centre peak, then decays with a heavy power-law tail - Sigma sets the peak position, a distinctly front-skewed spike. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:InversiveCongruential}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{InversiveCongruential}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Inversive congruential noise: a nonlinear PRNG that uses the modular multiplicative inverse (x = a*x^-1 + c mod p) instead of a plain multiply, breaking the lattice structure of linear generators - a noise free of the planes that plague LCGs.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:InvoluteOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{InvoluteOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Involute oscillator: the x-coordinate of the involute of a circle, cos(t)+t*sin(t), swept over several turns per cycle - a sine whose amplitude grows linearly across the period then resets, giving a built-in ramp-shaped swell distinct from the exponential LogSpiral.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:IsingMeanField}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{IsingMeanField}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mean-field Ising magnet: the average magnetization of a spin system relaxing toward its self-consistent (tanh) value under an oscillating applied field; near the Curie point the dynamic hysteresis loop pulses and chaotically flips - phase-transition dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ISLMModel}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ISLMModel}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

IS-LM cycle: the textbook macroeconomic model of goods and money markets, where a nonlinear investment response coupled to interest-rate dynamics self-oscillates and (with capital accumulation) goes chaotic - aggregate-economy cycles.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Izhikevich}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Izhikevich}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Izhikevich spiking-neuron oscillator (2003): integrates the two-variable model whose membrane voltage fires spikes and bursts, taken straight as a sound/control source - the Regular, Bursting, Chattering and Fast firing modes give clicky, gurgling and rhythmic textures no wavetable makes. A biological neuron model repurposed for synthesis. Rate scales the firing speed (in0 = Pitch CV), Mode picks the pattern, Current the drive (more = faster, denser firing), Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:IzhikevichNet}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{IzhikevichNet}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Izhikevich network: a dozen of the efficient two-variable spiking neurons wired in a ring with excitatory coupling, so spikes propagate and the population settles into synchronous bursts or asynchronous chatter depending on drive - the summed firing makes a lively neural CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:IzhikevichNeuron}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{IzhikevichNeuron}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Izhikevich neuron: a two-variable model with a reset rule that reproduces a huge range of cortical firing patterns (regular, fast-spiking, chattering) from two knobs - efficient biological spiking dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JacobiCnOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JacobiCnOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jacobi elliptic cn: the elliptic cousin of the cosine from its q-series, which sharpens from a smooth cosine toward a narrowing pulse as the nome increases - the 90-degree partner of sn with a distinct, more peaked harmonic spectrum.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JacobiDnOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JacobiDnOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jacobi elliptic dn: the strictly-positive elliptic function (the delta amplitude) from its q-series, which rises from a flat DC level toward a train of sharp positive humps as the nome grows - a unipolar, formant-like elliptic source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JacobiSnOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JacobiSnOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jacobi elliptic sn: the doubly-periodic generalization of the sine, built from its q-series (nome) so that as the nome rises the smooth sine continuously hardens toward a square wave - one knob morphs sine to square through every elliptic shape between.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JacobsLadderOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JacobsLadderOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jacob's ladder: the classic climbing spark between two diverging rods, a buzzing arc tone that rises in pitch and thins as it travels up the widening gap, then snaps back to the bottom to climb again; the climb rate sets the ascent speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JafariNE1}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JafariNE1}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

No-equilibrium flow I: a minimal three-state chaotic system from the Jafari-Sprott search that has no fixed points at all, so its attractor is 'hidden' - the orbit stays chaotic with nowhere to converge.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JafariNE17}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JafariNE17}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

No-equilibrium flow XVII: a later entry in the Jafari-Sprott catalogue of fixed-point-free chaotic systems, with a quadratic-plus-constant third equation yielding a distinct hidden attractor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JafariNE2}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JafariNE2}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

No-equilibrium flow II: a second Jafari-Sprott system with no fixed points, whose product-and-constant structure hides a chaotic attractor that exists without any equilibrium to organize it.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JafariNE5}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JafariNE5}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

No-equilibrium flow V: another fixed-point-free Jafari-Sprott system whose absolute-value nonlinearity sustains a hidden chaotic attractor, found only by scanning initial conditions rather than locating equilibria.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JafariNE9}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JafariNE9}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

No-equilibrium flow IX: another of the Jafari-Sprott no-equilibrium systems, with a different nonlinear coupling that yields a distinct hidden chaotic attractor (no fixed points, found only by initial-condition search).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JanoschekOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JanoschekOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Janoschek oscillator: each cycle is the Janoschek growth curve 1-(1-Beta)e^(-x^Gamma), a sigmoid rise that starts from a raised floor Beta (not zero) and whose inflection sharpness Gamma is independent - a growth ramp with a built-in lower asymptote. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JansenRit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JansenRit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jansen-Rit neural mass: a cortical-column model of coupled excitatory and inhibitory populations whose sigmoid feedback generates alpha-band EEG-like oscillations and, with strong input, chaotic and epileptiform rhythms.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JawHarp}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JawHarp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jaw harp: a fixed-pitch metal-tongue twang whose bright resonant formant is swept by an envelope on each pluck, the boing-wah 'mouth harp' sound where the drone stays put and the formant moves.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JawHarpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JawHarpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jaw-harp oscillator: a fixed-pitch buzzing lamella (a decaying-then-replucked twang) filtered by a sweepable mouth-cavity formant that selects which overtones ring, the 'boing-wow' twang of a jew's-harp shaped by the player's mouth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JeffcottRotor}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JeffcottRotor}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jeffcott rotor: a spinning unbalanced shaft whose whirl orbit intermittently rubs the stator, the nonlinear contact stiffness folding the synchronous whirl into chaotic, rattling motion - a rotating-machinery fault model, read as the shaft displacement.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JerkAbs}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JerkAbs}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Absolute-value jerk: the elegant chaotic jerk system x'''=-a x'' - x' - |x| + 1, one of the simplest electronic circuits that produces chaos, with a sharp piecewise-linear fold.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JerkCosine}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JerkCosine}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cosine jerk: a jerk system whose nonlinearity is a cosine of the velocity (x''), an unusual feedback that bends the flow into a distinctive folded chaotic attractor unlike the polynomial jerks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JerkExp}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JerkExp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Exponential jerk: a jerk system x'''=-a x'' - x' - (e^x - 1) whose one-sided exponential restoring force breaks the symmetry, giving an asymmetric, spiky chaotic flow.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JerkMemristive}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JerkMemristive}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Memristive jerk: a third-order jerk circuit whose nonlinearity is a flux-controlled memristor (cubic memductance), so the chaotic flow carries the pinched-hysteresis character of a memristor element.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JerkMult}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JerkMult}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Multiplicative jerk: a jerk system x'''=-a x'' + x x' - x whose product-of-state nonlinearity (a multiplier element) drives a smooth single-scroll chaotic flow distinct from the polynomial-in-x jerks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JerkPiecewise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JerkPiecewise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Piecewise jerk: a jerk system whose nonlinearity is a hard min/max clamp of x, the easiest chaotic circuit to build from op-amp comparators - producing a crisp, angular chaotic flow.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JerkQuad}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JerkQuad}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Quadratic jerk: the Sprott jerk system x'''=-a x'' + (x')^2 - x, whose squared velocity term folds a smooth single-scroll chaotic attractor from a minimal third-order equation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JerkSign}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JerkSign}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Signum jerk: a jerk system x'''=-a x'' - x' + x - 2 sgn(x) whose hard relay nonlinearity switches the restoring force, giving an abruptly-switching, square-edged chaotic flow.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JerkSine}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JerkSine}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sine jerk: a jerk system x'''=-a x'' - x' - sin(x), the third-order pendulum-like flow whose bounded sinusoidal restoring force folds a smooth chaotic attractor - the autonomous cousin of the driven pendulum.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JerkTanh}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JerkTanh}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tanh jerk: a jerk system x'''=-a x'' - x' - 2 tanh(x) whose smooth saturating nonlinearity yields a gently-folding chaotic flow - the soft-clip cousin of the signum jerk.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Jet}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Jet}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jet engine - a massive filtered-noise roar topped by a screaming turbine whine. Thrust sets the power, Tone the whine pitch, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JetEngineOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JetEngineOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jet engine: the scream of a turbofan, a high turbine whine and its harmonics riding on a massive broadband combustion roar, building with throttle; the throttle knob spools the engine up in pitch and intensity.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JetFlute}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JetFlute}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Air-jet flute model: a delay-line waveguide closed by a cubic jet nonlinearity and excited by breath noise self-oscillates into a breathy flute tone - physical wind synthesis from a delay and a polynomial.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JetMidlatitude}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JetMidlatitude}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Charney-DeVore jet: a low-order model of the mid-latitude atmospheric jet that flips between a fast 'zonal' flow and a stuck 'blocking' pattern; topographic and thermal forcing make the regime transitions irregular - weather-regime chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JewsHarp}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JewsHarp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jew's harp / mouth harp: a fixed low twang reed whose harmonics are shaped by a sweeping mouth-cavity formant, retriggered on in0 - the wobbling 'boing-wow' of a plucked lamellophone. Tune sets the twang pitch, Sweep the formant motion, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JohnsonSuOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JohnsonSuOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Johnson-SU oscillator: each cycle is the Johnson SU PDF, a Gaussian warped through an inverse-hyperbolic-sine transform so Gamma skews it and Delta sets its tail weight independently - an extremely flexible bell spanning sharp-and-heavy-tailed to broad, peak-normalized and bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JonesPewseyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JonesPewseyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jones-Pewsey oscillator: each cycle is the Jones-Pewsey circular family (cosh(k*psi)+sinh(k*psi)cos(theta))^(1/psi), a single shape that contains the von Mises, cardioid and wrapped-Cauchy as special cases - Kappa sets concentration, Psi morphs the peak character continuously. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JosephsonArray}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JosephsonArray}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coupled Josephson junctions: two driven superconducting junctions sharing a load resistor, whose phases compete between synchronization and chaos - the building block of Josephson-junction arrays used as voltage standards and chaotic emitters.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JosephsonJunction}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JosephsonJunction}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Josephson junction: a superconducting junction whose phase obeys a damped, driven pendulum equation (phi'' + beta phi' + sin phi = I + A sin wt); the tilted washboard potential makes the voltage chaotic - quantum-electronics chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JosephsonStack}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JosephsonStack}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stacked Josephson junctions: three series junctions sharing a bias current and coupled through their phases (as in intrinsic-junction THz emitters), whose competition between synchronization and chaos shapes the emitted spectrum.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JovianBurstOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JovianBurstOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jovian S-burst: the fast staccato 'popcorn' of Jupiter's decametric radio storms (driven by the Io flux tube), short noisy crackle bursts at a rapid random rate, the sound radio astronomers hear from Jupiter on a shortwave receiver.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JuliaOrbit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JuliaOrbit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Julia orbit: iterates the complex map z -> z^2 + c (for a fixed c on a Julia-set boundary) and reseeds whenever the orbit escapes, so the real part wanders chaotically through the fractal's filigree - complex-plane dynamics as sound.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JuliaQuartic}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JuliaQuartic}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Quartic Julia orbit: iterates the complex map z -> z^4 + c, whose four-fold symmetry folds the orbit through a busier, higher-symmetry fractal boundary than the quadratic Julia set - denser complex-plane chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JumpDiffusion}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JumpDiffusion}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Merton jump-diffusion: a Brownian-motion price path with occasional sudden Poisson-timed jumps of random size, capturing the crashes and gaps that pure diffusion misses - a smooth wander punctuated by abrupt discontinuous leaps.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JumpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JumpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jump: the springy hop of a platform hero, a square-wave tone that sweeps quickly upward in pitch as the character leaps, fired at the set rate; the boing of arcade jumping.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JunoVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JunoVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Juno-style voice: a single sawtooth with a square sub-oscillator an octave down and PWM movement, run through a lowpass and lightly detuned for the lush Roland Juno-106 character. Cutoff sets the filter, Sub the sub-oscillator level, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KaiserPulseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KaiserPulseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kaiser pulse: one cycle shaped by the Kaiser window I0(beta*sqrt(1-x^2))/I0(beta), the near-optimal adjustable window built from the modified Bessel function; beta trades main-lobe width against sidelobe level, morphing the pulse from rectangular toward Gaussian.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KaldorModel}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KaldorModel}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kaldor business cycle: a macroeconomic model where the sigmoidal investment response to income, coupled to capital accumulation, self-oscillates and (when forced) goes chaotic - cyclical economic dynamics as a control source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KaleckiCycle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KaleckiCycle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kalecki cycle: the first delay-based business-cycle model, where investment decisions take a gestation time to become capital; that delay alone destabilizes the economy into sustained (and, when nonlinear, chaotic) cycles.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Kalimba}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Kalimba}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal kalimba (thumb-piano tine): a trigger on in0 plucks a metal tine modelled as a strong fundamental plus a couple of mellow inharmonic partials (1 : 2.0 : 3.9) with a soft buzzy onset. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring length, Buzz the attack-noise transient, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KalimbaModeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KalimbaModeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kalimba oscillator: a clamped cantilever tine whose few stretched bending modes (ratios near 1, 6.27, 17.5) give the mbira/kalimba its plucky, slightly-buzzy, fast-decaying ping dominated by the fundamental with sparse high overtones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KapitzaPendulum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KapitzaPendulum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kapitza pendulum: a pendulum whose pivot vibrates so fast that the normally-unstable inverted (upright) position becomes stable; near the stability boundary it wobbles chaotically about the top - the counterintuitive dynamic-stabilization effect.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KaplanYorke}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KaplanYorke}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kaplan-Yorke map: a two-dimensional map coupling a modular doubling of one coordinate with an exponentially-damped cosine drive of the other, producing a banded fractal chaos used to study chaotic dimension.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KappaDistOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KappaDistOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kappa oscillator: each cycle is the kappa distribution (1+x^2/Kappa)^(-Kappa-1), a symmetric bell (from space-plasma physics) whose tails are heavy for small Kappa and approach a Gaussian as Kappa grows - one knob continuously sweeping tail weight between Lorentzian-like and Gaussian-like. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KarplusStretch}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KarplusStretch}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stretched Karplus-Strong: a gate plucks a tuned delay loop whose damping filter is blended toward all-pass by a Stretch knob, extending sustain from a dull thud to an endless drone - extended string synthesis.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KatydidOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KatydidOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Katydid: the raspy 'katy-did' night call, a pair of short high broadband rasps repeated steadily with a gap, the relentless summer-night chorus of the bush cricket; the call rate sets the species and temperature.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KauffmanNK}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KauffmanNK}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kauffman NK network: sixteen Boolean nodes each updated by a fixed random logic function of two fixed inputs (wiring and rules derived deterministically from node index); the network settles onto one of its attractor cycles - a model of gene-regulatory order at the edge of chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KazooOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KazooOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kazoo: humming through a kazoo's membrane, a buzzy nasal tone where a tuned drone is broken up by a fast membrane rattle into the classic comb-and-paper raspberry, the party-favour buzz; the pitch sets the hum.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KeenModel}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KeenModel}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Keen model: Steve Keen's extension of Goodwin's growth cycle with private debt, whose nonlinear investment-and-interest feedback produces Minsky-style booms followed by debt-driven crashes - financial-instability dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Kenong}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Kenong}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gamelan kenong: a large bronze kettle-gong with a deep, round, resonant tone and a long humming decay - the time-keeping voice of the gamelan. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KeplerOrbitOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KeplerOrbitOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kepler-orbit oscillator: solves Kepler's equation each cycle for the eccentric anomaly of an orbit and reads out the radial position, so a circular orbit (eccentricity 0) gives a pure sine and a comet-like ellipse gives a slow drift with a sharp swing at perihelion.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Kettle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Kettle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boiling kettle - a bubbling hiss that rises into a building steam whistle, then resets. Cycle sets the boil time, Pitch the whistle height, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KettleWhistleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KettleWhistleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kettle whistle: a stovetop kettle coming to the boil, a high near-sine whistle that rises in pitch as the steam builds then holds steady, with a breathy noise edge and a faint warble; the boil knob sets how close to full boil.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KeyboardTypeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KeyboardTypeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mechanical keyboard: fingers clattering across a keyboard, a stream of short clicky key-press impacts fired at the typing rate, each a brief filtered tick with a hard onset, the office-desk typing sound; the rate sets the words-per-minute.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KeyClickOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KeyClickOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Key click: the short mechanical tick of a keypress (a keyboard tap or UI button), a very brief filtered-noise click with a faint resonant body, fired at the set rate; the tactile feedback blip.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KeyJingleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KeyJingleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jingling keys: a keyring being shaken or picked up, a random scatter of short bright metallic clinks at varying high pitches as the keys knock together, the pocket-jangle of keys; density sets how vigorously they shake.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Kick}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Kick}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Analog kick drum: a trigger on in0 fires a sine whose pitch sweeps from a high click down to the body Tune, with a percussive amp Decay. Tune sets the fundamental, Punch the pitch-drop time (snap), Decay the body length, Drive adds saturation for harder kicks, and Level the output - the synthesized 808/909-style kick. Patch a Clock, Euclid or sequencer gate into in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Kick606}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Kick606}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-606 kick drum: a short, punchy bass drum with a fast pitch sweep and a tight decay - brighter and snappier than the 808's long boom, the driving Drumatix kick. Tune sets the base pitch, Decay the length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Kick707}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Kick707}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-707 kick drum: a tight, clicky digital-flavoured bass drum - a short pitch-swept sine with an added transient click at the attack, the dry 707 thump used across early house. Tune sets the pitch, Click the attack snap, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Kick808}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Kick808}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-808-style kick drum: a trigger on in0 fires a long, deep sine boom with a downward pitch sweep and a soft saturated tail - the classic 808 sub-kick. Tune sets the body pitch, Decay the (long) length, Punch the attack pitch-sweep amount, Level the output. in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Kick909}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Kick909}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-909-style kick drum: punchier and clickier than the 808 - a trigger on in0 fires a sine body with a fast, snappy pitch envelope plus a sharp transient click for cut. Tune sets the body pitch, Decay the length, Click the attack-click amount, Level the output. in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KickedTop}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KickedTop}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kicked top: an angular-momentum vector that precesses freely then receives a periodic nonlinear twist (the classical limit of a quantum-chaos paradigm), tumbling chaotically over the sphere - read as one spin component.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KickSynth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KickSynth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Synth kick drum: a gate triggers a sine whose pitch drops from a punchy transient down to the tuned fundamental while the amplitude decays - a self-contained 808/909-style kick voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KickSynthOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KickSynthOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kick-drum voice: a sine whose pitch drops sharply from a click down to a low boom, re-triggered at the set rate, the classic 808/909 analog bass-drum synthesis; the drop and decay knobs shape it from a tight thud to a long sub.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KingsDream}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KingsDream}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

King's Dream attractor: a sine-and-power iterated map from Pickover's collection that traces sweeping, dreamlike fractal arcs, a flowing chaotic texture with broad, smooth orbits.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Knock}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Knock}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Door knock - a periodic double or triple rap on wood with a hollow resonance. Rate sets how often, Tone the wood resonance, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KochWave}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KochWave}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Koch waveform: the height profile of a Koch snowflake edge, built by recursively replacing each segment with a bumped four-segment zig-zag, gives a crinkled fractal waveform whose jaggedness deepens with the iteration depth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KohonenSOM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KohonenSOM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Self-organizing map (Kohonen): a chain of nodes with adjustable weights is shown random inputs; the closest-matching node and its neighbours are nudged toward each input, so the initially-jumbled weights untangle into a smooth ordered map - a probe node's weight settles as it self-organizes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KolmogorovFlow}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KolmogorovFlow}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kolmogorov flow: a three-mode Galerkin truncation of two-dimensional shear-driven fluid turbulence, whose energy sloshes chaotically between large- and small-scale modes - a toy model of the route to turbulence.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Kora}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Kora}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kora: the West African 21-string harp-lute - a warm nylon-string Karplus pluck rung through a calabash-gourd body resonance, the soft, rolling, harp-like tone of griot music; re-plucked at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Koto}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Koto}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Japanese koto: a plucked zither string with a bright woody attack and the characteristic upward pitch bend as the note settles (the ato-oshi press), over a Karplus-Strong string. Decay sets the ring, Bend the attack pitch rise, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Krell}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Krell}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Self-generating Krell patch: a self-running voice that, at random intervals, jumps to a new random pitch and glides its amplitude toward a new random level - the endlessly-evolving generative tone (after the Forbidden Planet patch). Rate sets the event speed, Range the pitch spread in octaves, Level the output. No input needed; patch it and walk away.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KumaraswamyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KumaraswamyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kumaraswamy oscillator: the closed-form Beta-like PDF a*b*x^(a-1)(1-x^a)^(b-1) on [0,1], normalized to its mode - similar to the Beta bump but with distinctly different edge tails, giving a related-yet-separate family of skewable compact waveforms.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KumaraswamyQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KumaraswamyQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kumaraswamy-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the closed-form Kumaraswamy inverse-CDF (1-(1-p)^(1/B))^(1/A), a bounded [0,1] S whose two exponents bend the early and late curvature independently - a fully-bounded, doubly-skewable ramp distinct from the unbounded heavy-tail quantiles.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
