\phantomsection\label{blk:KuramotoChain}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KuramotoChain}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kuramoto chain: four phase oscillators at spread frequencies coupled all-to-all; as coupling rises they pass through chimera-like partial synchronization into full lock, the mean field tracing a rich evolving waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KuramotoChainNN}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KuramotoChainNN}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kuramoto chain: a line of phase oscillators with spread-out natural frequencies coupled only to their immediate neighbours (not all-to-all), so synchronization spreads locally and can form travelling phase waves and partially-locked clusters - the order parameter tracks the local sync.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KuramotoSivashinsky}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KuramotoSivashinsky}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Kuramoto-Sivashinsky modes: a low-mode truncation of the equation governing unstable flame fronts and falling liquid films, whose competing growth and damping of two Fourier modes produce spatiotemporal-chaos-flavoured signals.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LacOperon}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LacOperon}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lac operon: the classic bacterial gene switch where lactose induces the enzymes that import it, a positive feedback giving bistability and, with degradation delays, oscillatory induction - molecular-biology dynamics as a control source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LaggedFib}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LaggedFib}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lagged-Fibonacci generator: a PRNG that combines two earlier outputs from a ring of past values (x_n = x_{n-j} + x_{n-k} mod 1), a long-period additive generator whose noise has a flatter spectrum than a simple linear-congruential source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LaguerreFuncOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LaguerreFuncOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Laguerre-function oscillator: each cycle is the nth Laguerre function L_n(x)*e^(-x/2) (the radial hydrogen-atom / quantum form), which crosses zero n times then decays - so Order sets how many oscillating lobes appear before the exponential tail, a damped multi-lobe waveform. Magnitude-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LaguerreGaussOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LaguerreGaussOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Laguerre-Gaussian wave: a Laguerre polynomial multiplied by a decaying exponential (the radial profile of optical Laguerre-Gaussian beams and hydrogen orbitals), swept over the phase as an asymmetric, one-sided-decaying pulse whose order sets the ripple count.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LaiSystem}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LaiSystem}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lai system: a three-dimensional autonomous chaotic flow with two quadratic terms producing a compact multi-scroll attractor, one of the systematically-constructed systems used in chaos-based secure communication.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LanczosSincOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LanczosSincOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lanczos-sinc oscillator: each cycle is a sinc multiplied by a wider sinc window, sinc(x)*sinc(x/Lobes), the Lanczos resampling kernel - a central lobe with a controlled number of decaying side-lobes that taper smoothly to zero at the edges, distinct from the infinite sinc and the sinc-squared. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LandauLifshitz}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LandauLifshitz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Landau-Lifshitz spin: a magnetization vector precessing about an effective field with Gilbert damping; an oscillating drive field tips it into chaotic precession on the unit sphere - the physics of spin-torque oscillators.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LangKobayashi}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LangKobayashi}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lang-Kobayashi laser: a semiconductor laser whose field re-enters after a delay (optical feedback) destabilizes into coherence-collapse chaos; here a delayed self-feedback of the intensity drives broadband chaotic fluctuation - the physics of a laser pointed at a mirror.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LaplaceNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LaplaceNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Laplace noise: samples from the double-exponential distribution (a sharp peak at zero with exponential tails on both sides), drawn via the inverse-CDF -sign(u)*ln(1-2|u|); spikier than Gaussian but lighter-tailed than Cauchy.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LaplaceQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LaplaceQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Laplace-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the Laplace inverse-CDF (ln(2p) below the midpoint, -ln(2(1-p)) above), a two-sided logarithmic ramp with a sharp cusp at the centre and steep ends - a distinctly kinked S unlike the smooth quantiles. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LaplaceResonanceOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LaplaceResonanceOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Laplace-resonance oscillator: three voices locked in the 1:2:4 orbital resonance of Jupiter's moons Io, Europa and Ganymede (whose periods never let all three align), summed into a perfectly-periodic but intricately-phased composite - celestial mechanics as an additive tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LarsenHowl}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LarsenHowl}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Larsen feedback howl: a delayed feedback loop with a resonant peak and amplitude limiting models a microphone pointed at a speaker, building up the squealing PA-feedback tone that locks to the loop's strongest resonance.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Laser}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Laser}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sci-fi laser blaster: a trigger on in0 fires a square tone with a steep attack and a fast descending pitch drop, ring-modulated by a carrier for the metallic 'pew', through a resonant low-pass that tracks the pitch, with a noise-grit layer. The blaster bolt and beam of sci-fi sound design. Tune sets the start pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Drop the pitch-envelope descent depth, Ring the ring-mod carrier (metallic colour), Length the decay (short = bolt, long = beam), Grit the noise layer, and Level the output. Distinct from Zap: ring-modulated and metallic, with a beam length.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LaserShootOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LaserShootOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Laser shot: the pew of a space-shooter blaster, a square-wave tone diving rapidly in pitch with a touch of buzz, fired at the set rate; the 8-bit weapon staple.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Latoocarfian}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Latoocarfian}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Latoocarfian attractor: Clifford Pickover's four-sine iterated map (x'=sin(by)+c sin(bx), y'=sin(ax)+d sin(ay)), which folds into delicate, organic, often bird- or leaf-like fractal forms - a flowing chaotic texture.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LatticeBoltzmann1D}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LatticeBoltzmann1D}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lattice-Boltzmann (D1Q3): a mesoscopic fluid model where left, rest and right particle populations stream to neighbours and relax toward local equilibrium each step (the BGK collision), recovering wave-and-diffusion behaviour; the cell density sloshes as a fluid pulse circulates.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LaueOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LaueOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Laue oscillator: each cycle is the N-slit diffraction intensity (sin(N*theta)/sin(theta))^2 normalized - a train of sharp principal maxima separated by Slits-1 tiny subsidiary peaks - so raising Slits concentrates the energy into ever-narrower spikes, a grating-interference waveform distinct from any single bump.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LaughOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LaughOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Laughter: a string of voiced 'ha-ha-ha' bursts, a vowel tone chopped into rhythmic syllables that drift slightly in pitch, the contagious rhythm of a laugh; the syllable rate sets how fast the laughter tumbles.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LawnmowerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LawnmowerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lawnmower: a petrol push-mower running, a low four-stroke engine putt-putt with a steady blade whir and exhaust chuff, the summer-yard drone; the RPM sets the engine speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LeafBlowerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LeafBlowerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Leaf blower: a handheld leaf blower at full throttle, a loud high-pressure air whoosh of broadband noise over a whining impeller motor, the autumn-yard roar; the throttle shapes the air rush.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LegendreOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LegendreOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Legendre wave: the Legendre polynomial P_n evaluated on cos(theta) as the phase sweeps, an orthogonal-polynomial waveform whose order sets the number of lobes per cycle - the angular harmonics of a sphere, rendered as a band-limited tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LemniscateOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LemniscateOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lemniscate sine: the elliptic sine sl at the lemniscatic modulus (k^2 = 1/2, the case Gauss studied that connects pi to the lemniscate constant), giving a fixed, slightly-squared sine with the characteristic lemniscatic overtone balance.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LengyelEpstein}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LengyelEpstein}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lengyel-Epstein model: the two-variable kinetics of the chlorite-iodide-malonic-acid reaction (the first lab demonstration of Turing patterns), an activator-inhibitor oscillator with sharp relaxation pulses.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LennardJonesOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LennardJonesOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lennard-Jones oscillator: each cycle traces the 12-6 intermolecular potential 4((1/r)^12-(1/r)^6) - a steep repulsive wall, a sharp attractive well, then a flat tail - giving a strongly asymmetric waveform with a hard spike and a rounded dip, the shape of a diatomic bond. Magnitude-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LevelClearOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LevelClearOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Level clear: completing a stage, a triumphant rising fanfare of square-wave notes ending on a held high chord-tone, the victorious end-of-level flourish; the rate sets how often it plays.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LevyDistOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LevyDistOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Levy oscillator: each cycle is the Levy stable PDF sqrt(c/2pi)*e^(-c/2x)/x^1.5, which stays near zero, spikes up sharply, then decays in an extremely heavy power-law tail - the most front-loaded, long-tailed of the distribution waveforms; C sets where the spike sits.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LevyFlightWalk}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LevyFlightWalk}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Levy flight: a random walk whose step sizes are drawn from a heavy-tailed (power-law) distribution, so long quiet drifts are punctuated by sudden large jumps - the super-diffusive motion of foraging animals and turbulent tracers, here a bounded slow CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LevyNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LevyNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Levy-flight noise: a leaky random walk driven by Cauchy-distributed steps, so the signal mostly drifts slowly but occasionally takes a sudden large jump - a heavy-tailed, intermittent control/noise source quite unlike smooth Gaussian or flat white noise. Jump sets the step scale.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LFGlottal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LFGlottal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Liljencrants-Fant glottal source: synthesizes the glottal-flow derivative directly - an exponentially-growing sinusoid through the open phase that snaps to a sharp negative spike at glottal closure, then an exponential return to zero. The most widely-used physiological voice-source model, richer and more controllable than the Rosenberg pulse: Open sets the open quotient (breathy to pressed), Return the closure abruptness (which sets the spectral tilt / brightness). Feed it through a formant filter for vowels.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LibrationOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LibrationOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Libration oscillator: the slow pendulum-like rocking of a body trapped in an orbital resonance (like the Trojan asteroids swinging about Jupiter's Lagrange points, or the Moon's face nodding), modeled as a resonant pendulum whose amplitude sets a gentle sway or a wide tumble.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LightSwitchOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LightSwitchOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Light switch: the crisp snap of a wall light switch toggling, a single short mechanical click with a tiny resonant tick, fired at the set rate; the small everyday clunk of on and off.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LimaconOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LimaconOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Limacon oscillator: the limacon of Pascal r = b + a*cos(theta), which morphs from a convex oval through a dimpled curve to a self-intersecting inner loop as the a/b ratio grows (the cardioid is the boundary case); the x-projection gives a tunably-asymmetric waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LindleyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LindleyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lindley oscillator: each cycle is the Lindley PDF proportional to (1+x)e^(-Theta*x), a right-skewed lifetime-distribution bump - a near-exponential decay lifted by a linear factor so it rises briefly before falling; Theta sets the decay rate. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LinearFailureOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LinearFailureOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Linear-failure-rate oscillator: each cycle is (A+B*x)*e^(-(A*x+B*x^2/2)), the distribution whose hazard rises linearly (constant A plus slope B) - blending an exponential (B=0) and a Rayleigh (A=0) into one shape, a smoothly-tunable front-loaded decay. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LinnSnare}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LinnSnare}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

LinnDrum snare: a tight, punchy sampled-style snare - a short tonal body under a crisp, highpassed noise crack, the early-80s drum-machine backbeat. Tone sets the body-vs-noise balance, Decay the length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LissajousOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LissajousOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lissajous oscillator: traces the classic two-frequency figure x=sin(theta), y=sin(ratio*theta + phase) and reads out the y-axis, so integer ratios give harmonically-related multi-lobe waves and the phase knob rotates the figure - the oscilloscope-art curve as a tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LiSystem}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LiSystem}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Li system: a three-dimensional autonomous chaotic flow (Li, 2009) with three quadratic cross-terms that produces a distinctive multi-wing attractor, studied for its rich coexisting-attractor structure.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LituusOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LituusOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lituus oscillator: the projection (1/sqrt(theta))*cos(theta) of the lituus spiral r=1/sqrt(theta), a cosine whose amplitude falls off as an inverse square root - a gentler decay than the hyperbolic spiral, giving a long-tailed fading swell per cycle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LiuChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LiuChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Liu attractor: a chaotic system with a single quadratic and a squared term that traces a compact double-wing orbit, a tidy strange attractor for smooth chaotic modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LiuChenLu}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LiuChenLu}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Liu-Chen-Lu system: a Lorenz-family chaotic flow with two quadratic cross-terms positioned to give a wide, flat twin-scroll, one of the systematically-generated members of the generalized Lorenz canonical family.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LiuSuOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LiuSuOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Liu-Su attractor: a chaotic flow whose third equation uses an exponential of a product term, giving a sharply asymmetric, rapidly-folding orbit distinct from the purely-polynomial Lorenz family.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LoadingPulseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LoadingPulseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Loading pulse: the gentle breathing pulse of a loading or processing indicator, a soft tone whose amplitude swells in and out at a steady rate, the please-wait heartbeat of a UI spinner; the pulse rate sets the tempo.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LockClickOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LockClickOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lock click: a deadbolt or key lock turning, a short two-stage mechanical clunk (the bolt sliding then seating) with a metallic tick, fired at the set rate; the reassuring sound of locking up.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LocustSwarmOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LocustSwarmOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Locust swarm: the ominous wall of sound of a locust plague, many overlapping wingbeat buzzes at slightly different pitches blurring into a dense seething drone with a slow swell; density sets how thick the swarm.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogCauchyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogCauchyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Log-Cauchy oscillator: each cycle is the log-Cauchy PDF (1/(x*pi))*sigma/((ln x)^2+sigma^2), a right-skewed bump with an even heavier (log-Cauchy) tail than the log-normal - Sigma sets the width; peak-normalized and bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogChirpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogChirpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Log-chirp oscillator: within each cycle the instantaneous frequency rises exponentially (the phase follows (e^(Rate*t)-1)), so the pitch glides up faster and faster then resets - the constant-Q sweep used in acoustic measurement, repeated at the fundamental. Rate sets the sweep curvature.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogisticDelay}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogisticDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Delayed logistic map: a logistic recurrence that also feeds back its previous value (a delay-coupled population model), whose extra memory produces invariant-circle and torus-breakdown routes to chaos distinct from the plain logistic map.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogisticHenon}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogisticHenon}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Logistic-Henon hybrid: a two-dimensional map that drives a Henon-style delayed feedback with a logistic nonlinearity, combining the period-doubling of the logistic map with the folding of Henon - a denser, more tunable map chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogisticKernelOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogisticKernelOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Logistic-kernel oscillator: each cycle is the logistic density kernel 1/(2+2cosh(x)) = sech^2(x/2)/4, a smooth symmetric bump with a flatter peak and heavier-than-Gaussian exponential tails - the derivative-of-a-sigmoid shape, distinct from the plain sech pulse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogisticNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogisticNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Logistic noise: samples ln(u/(1-u)) from the logistic distribution, bell-shaped like the Gaussian but with slightly heavier tails (the law behind logistic regression and the sigmoid) - a symmetric noise with gentle outliers.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogisticOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogisticOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Logistic-map chaos source: iterates x = r*x*(1-x) at a chosen step rate, sweeping from periodic to fully chaotic as r climbs past 3.57 - structured noise / a strange-attractor oscillator, not a wavetable.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogitNormalOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogitNormalOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Logit-normal oscillator: each cycle is the logit-normal PDF on (0,1) - a Gaussian in logit space mapped back - so Sigma morphs it from a single centred bump (small Sigma) to a U-shaped bimodal curve with peaks near both edges (large Sigma), a bounded shape no plain distribution offers. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogLogisticOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogLogisticOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Log-logistic oscillator: each cycle is the Fisk (log-logistic) PDF (b/a)(x/a)^(b-1)/(1+(x/a)^b)^2, a skewed bump with a heavier tail than the gamma - Beta sets the peakedness and Alpha the scale, peak-normalized to a bounded asymmetric waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogLogisticQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogLogisticQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Log-logistic-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the Fisk inverse-CDF (p/(1-p))^(1/Beta), an odds-ratio ramp that is gentle in the middle and blows up toward both ends (heaviest at p->1) - Beta sets the steepness. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogNormalCdfOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogNormalCdfOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Log-normal-CDF oscillator: each cycle is the log-normal cumulative Phi(ln(x)/Sigma), an S-curve in the log domain - so it rises gently, steepens, then tails off asymmetrically, Sigma setting the spread. A skewed ramp distinct from the linear-domain sigmoids. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogNormalNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogNormalNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Log-normal noise: the exponential of a Gaussian, a right-skewed positive distribution arising wherever many small multiplicative effects combine (particle sizes, incomes, biological growth) - clustered low values with occasional large excursions.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LogSpiralOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LogSpiralOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Log-spiral oscillator: the radial projection of a logarithmic spiral - a sine of several turns whose amplitude grows exponentially across each cycle then resets - giving a built-in sawtooth-shaped tremolo on the tone, a rhythmic swell that repeats at the pitch period.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LomaxOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LomaxOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lomax oscillator: each cycle is the Lomax (Pareto-II) PDF (a/lambda)(1+x/lambda)^(-a-1), a monotonically decaying heavy-tailed shape that starts high and trails off slowly - a front-loaded sawtooth-like waveform whose tail weight Alpha controls, peak-normalized and bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LomaxQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LomaxQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lomax-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the Lomax (Pareto-II) inverse-CDF (1-p)^(-1/Alpha)-1, a ramp that starts at zero and accelerates into a heavy power-law tail toward the top - Alpha sets the tail violence. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Lorentzian}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Lorentzian}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lorentzian-pulse oscillator (a first as a synth block): emits the Lorentzian (Cauchy) bell - 1 / (1 + (t/Width)^2) - once per cycle, a peak with a tall sharp tip and long, slowly-fading shoulders, the lineshape of a resonance in physics. Its fatter tails (compared with a Gaussian or cosine pulse) give a brighter, buzzier spectrum that rolls off exponentially, while Width sets how narrow and piercing the spike is. A resonance-shaped pulse train distinct from the Theta and window pulses. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Lorenz84Osc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Lorenz84Osc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lorenz-84 attractor: a low-order model of the atmosphere's global circulation whose chaotic orbit drifts on a slow, weather-like timescale - organic, long-form modulation that never quite repeats.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Lorenz96}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Lorenz96}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lorenz-96 model: a ring of five atmospheric sites each advected by its neighbours and pushed by a uniform forcing, the standard testbed for weather-prediction and data-assimilation research - spatiotemporal weather-like chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LorenzChemical}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LorenzChemical}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chemical Lorenz: a mass-action reaction network engineered to realize Lorenz-type dynamics in concentration space (all variables non-negative), showing that the Lorenz butterfly can in principle be built from chemistry.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LorenzCurveOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LorenzCurveOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lorenz-curve oscillator: each cycle is the exponential-distribution Lorenz curve p + (1-p)*ln(1-p), a convex ramp that hugs zero at the start and accelerates to one at the end (the economics inequality curve) - a distinctly bowed ramp sitting below the diagonal. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LorenzMod1}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LorenzMod1}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modified Lorenz I: a reformulation of the Lorenz equations with a cubic cross-term that opens the butterfly into a broader, smoother two-lobed attractor - a distinct flavour of the classic chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LorenzMod2}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LorenzMod2}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modified Lorenz II: another quadratic reformulation of Lorenz with symmetric squared terms, tracing a four-lobed attractor that the orbit threads in a figure-eight - a higher-symmetry chaotic flow.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LorenzStenflo}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LorenzStenflo}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lorenz-Stenflo attractor: a four-variable extension of Lorenz from atmospheric acoustic-gravity waves, with an extra rotational mode that gives a richer, hyperchaotic orbit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LorenzWaterwheel}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LorenzWaterwheel}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Malkus waterwheel: a tilted wheel with leaky water cups that speeds up, stalls and chaotically reverses; its equations of motion reduce exactly to the Lorenz system - chaos you can build out of plumbing, read as the wheel's spin rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LotkaChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LotkaChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lotka-Volterra chaos: three competing populations whose nonlinear competition becomes chaotic at Vano's parameters, producing slow, organic, boom-and-bust oscillations - ecosystem dynamics as a modulation source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LotkaCompetition}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LotkaCompetition}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Competitive Lotka-Volterra: three species competing asymmetrically for a shared resource (May-Leonard dynamics), which cycle heteroclinically around the single-species states and, at the right asymmetry, become chaotic - competition-driven population chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Lozi3D}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Lozi3D}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

3-D Lozi map: a three-dimensional piecewise-linear map (Lozi's absolute-value fold extended with a third delayed coordinate), giving sharp-edged, angular fractal chaos with more depth than the 2-D Lozi.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LoziMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LoziMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lozi map: a piecewise-linear 2-D map (Henon with |x| in place of x^2) whose chaotic attractor has sharp, straight-edged structure - angular, glitchy chaos with hard corners, iterated at a chosen rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LucasOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LucasOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lucas-number oscillator (a first as a synth block): an additive tone whose overtones land on the Lucas numbers - 1, 3, 4, 7, 11, 18, 29... - the Fibonacci sequence's companion, built on the same golden-ratio recurrence but from a different start. Its partials share Fibonacci's phi-spaced widening yet sit on different harmonics, so it has a related warm-but-glassy character with its own distinct colour - a sibling timbre to the FibonacciOsc. Partials sets how many Lucas overtones to sound, alias-free up to Nyquist. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LucasPolyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LucasPolyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lucas-polynomial oscillator: each cycle is the nth Lucas polynomial L_n(x) (L_0=2, L_1=x, L_n = x*L_{n-1}+L_{n-2}) - the Fibonacci-polynomial companion with different seed values, giving a related but distinct family of recurrence-defined polynomial waveforms. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LuChenCheng}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LuChenCheng}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lu-Chen-Cheng system: a chaotic flow whose nonlinear cross-coupling and constant term build a generalized multi-scroll attractor, the orbit threading several stacked lobes for rich switching dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LuChenOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LuChenOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lu attractor: the bridge system between the Lorenz and Chen attractors, a double-scroll chaotic flow whose fold sits between the two - a distinct chaotic source for tones and modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LugiatoLefever}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LugiatoLefever}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lugiato-Lefever cavity: a driven, detuned Kerr-nonlinear optical resonator (the equation behind microresonator frequency combs), whose intracavity field is bistable and breaks into chaotic modulation instability under strong pumping.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LyapunovFractal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LyapunovFractal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lyapunov (Markus) fractal: drives the logistic map with a periodic A/B sequence of two growth rates and measures the Lyapunov exponent; scanning the two rates paints the Zircon-Zity fractal of stable (negative) and chaotic (positive) regions - the running exponent is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MachiningChatter}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MachiningChatter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Machining chatter: a turning tool cuts a surface left wavy by its previous pass, so the cutting force depends on the tool position one revolution ago; that delayed feedback self-excites the screeching chatter vibration that ruins finishes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MackeyGlass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MackeyGlass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mackey-Glass: a delay-differential equation from blood-cell production whose feedback acts on a delayed copy of itself (x' = beta x(t-tau)/(1+x(t-tau)^10) - gamma x); long delays push it from periodic to high-dimensional chaos - the classic time-delay chaos source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MagicSparkleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MagicSparkleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Magic sparkle: the glittering twinkle of pixie dust, random short high bell-like blips scattered across a bright pitch band each with a quick decay, the fairy-dust shimmer of fantasy games; density sets how busy the sparkle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MagnetarFlareOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MagnetarFlareOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Magnetar flare: a magnetar giant flare, a sudden hard spike of energy followed by a decaying tail that rings at the star's quasi-periodic seismic oscillation frequencies (QPOs detected after the 2004 SGR event); the burst rate sets how often the neutron star quakes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MagnetFractal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MagnetFractal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Magnet fractal: an escape-time iteration of the rational map ((z^2+c-1)/(2z+c-2))^2 that arose from renormalizing the Ising model of magnetism, whose fixed point at z=1 gives it a distinctive lobed shape; the reseeded orbit's real part is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MagneticDipole}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MagneticDipole}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Coupled magnetic dipoles: two pivoted compass needles that both attract each other and feel an oscillating external field; their nonlinear magnetic torques tangle into chaotic flipping - a desktop magnetic-pendulum chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MagnetronHumOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MagnetronHumOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Magnetron hum: the gritty droning buzz of a microwave oven's magnetron, a low mains-derived tone thick with harmonics and a faint high transformer whine, the angry hum of cooking; the harmonics knob sets the grit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MagPendulum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MagPendulum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Magnetic pendulum: a damped pendulum bob swinging over three attracting magnets, whose decision of which magnet to settle on is famously fractal and chaotically sensitive, reading the bob's x/y path as the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MainsHumOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MainsHumOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mains hum: the ground-loop drone of AC power, a line-frequency fundamental (50 or 60 Hz) stacked with the odd harmonics that transformer saturation adds, the ever-present buzz behind unbalanced audio gear.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MajorityRule}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MajorityRule}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Majority rule: each cell on the ring synchronously becomes the majority opinion of itself and its two neighbours; this deterministic coarsening rapidly grows aligned domains until it freezes into stripes - a self-organizing magnetization that snaps to a fixed pattern.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MandelOrbit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MandelOrbit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mandelbrot orbit tour: iterates z -> z^2 + c from zero while slowly sweeping c along the real axis, so the orbit cycles through the period-doubling and chaotic regimes mapped by the Mandelbrot set - a self-scanning bifurcation source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Mandolin}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Mandolin}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mandolin: the 8-string (4 double-course) mandolin - bright steel strings in slightly-detuned pairs picked with a sharp plectrum and sustained by the fast tremolo roll; the chiming, shimmering courses give it a brilliance distinct from a single-string lute. Tremolo sets the roll rate, Course the pair detune.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Maracas}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Maracas}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Maracas / shaker: a trigger on in0 fires a short burst of bright high-passed noise with a fast decay - the crisp shaker tick. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the length, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MarbleRollOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MarbleRollOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Marbles: glass marbles rolling and knocking on a hard floor, a random scatter of short bright glassy clicks at varying pitches, the marble-game clatter; density sets how busy the rolling.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MarchingFeetOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MarchingFeetOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Marching feet: a column of soldiers marching in step, a low broadband thud on each beat with a slight scatter (not perfectly synchronised) and a dusty scuff, the ominous tramp of boots; the step rate sets the march tempo.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MargolusGas}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MargolusGas}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lattice gas: a one-dimensional HPP-style automaton where cells hold left- and right-moving particles that stream and bounce on head-on collision, conserving particle number - the discrete microworld behind fluid simulation. The net momentum flux is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Marimba}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Marimba}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal marimba: a trigger on in0 strikes a wooden bar modelled as inharmonic decaying sine partials (the characteristic 1 : 3.9 : 9.2 bar modes), warm with a soft mallet onset. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring length, Mallet the brightness (how long the upper modes sustain), and Level the output. A melodic mallet voice to pair with the drum kit; gate it from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MarimbaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MarimbaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Marimba: a struck rosewood bar voiced from its characteristic stretched partials near the 1:4:10 ratio (the deep-cut tuning of a marimba) plus a tuned resonator-tube hum under the fundamental, re-struck at the set rate; warm, woody and round.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MarshallOlkinOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MarshallOlkinOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Marshall-Olkin oscillator: each cycle is the Marshall-Olkin exponential PDF Alpha*lambda*e^(-lambda x)/(1-(1-Alpha)e^(-lambda x))^2, a tilt-stretched exponential whose Alpha reshapes the early decay (a stability/proportional-odds add-on to the exponential). Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MathieuChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MathieuChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Parametric Mathieu oscillator: a resonator whose stiffness is modulated periodically (the Mathieu equation), so parametric pumping at the right depth pushes it through instability tongues into chaotic motion - the physics of a child pumping a swing, taken to extremes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MathieuOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MathieuOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mathieu oscillator: integrates the Mathieu equation y'' + (a - 2q cos 2t) y = 0, the parametric oscillator behind vibrating elliptical drumheads and ion-trap stability; near the stability-band edges the amplitude swells into rich beating modes before the limiter holds it bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MaxwellOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MaxwellOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Maxwell oscillator: each cycle is the Maxwell-Boltzmann speed PDF x^2 e^(-x^2/2a^2), a skewed bump that rises as a parabola and falls as a Gaussian - an asymmetric pulse with a soft front and a long tail, Spread setting the skew and harmonic content.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MaynardSmith}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MaynardSmith}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Maynard-Smith map: the population recurrence x -> r x/(1 + (x/k)^b), whose tunable density-dependence exponent moves it from a stable point through cycles into chaos - a flexible ecology-derived bifurcation source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Mbira}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Mbira}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mbira (thumb piano): a plucked metal tine with the characteristic buzzing rattle of its bottle-cap resonators - the warm, percussive African lamellophone. Tune sets the pitch, Buzz the rattle, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MegastableSine}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MegastableSine}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Megastable sine oscillator: a linear oscillator whose damping is a sine of position, creating a countable infinity of nested coexisting limit cycles, so which orbit (and thus which timbre) it settles into depends on the starting energy.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Mellotron}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Mellotron}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mellotron: the tape-replay keyboard's wobbly string/choir ensemble - lightly-detuned sawtooth voices with tape wow and a breath of hiss, the lo-fi orchestral pad of prog and pop. Wobble sets the tape flutter, Tone the brightness, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Melodica}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Melodica}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Breath, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Melodica: the buzzy, sustained free-reed of the breath-blown keyboard harmonica - lightly-detuned reed sawtooths through a soft lowpass, played by holding in0. Reed sets the detune buzz, Tone the brightness, Level the output. in0 = Breath gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MemristorChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MemristorChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Memristor oscillator: a minimal circuit built around a memristor whose state-dependent conductance (z-squared term) provides negative damping, self-oscillating into chaos - the simplest chaotic circuit with a memristor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MemristorHindmarsh}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MemristorHindmarsh}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Memristive Hindmarsh-Rose: the bursting HR neuron coupled to a memristor that models the magnetic-flux feedback across the membrane, enriching the burst patterns and adding hidden, flux-dependent chaotic firing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MemristorTwin}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MemristorTwin}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Twin-memristor oscillator: an LC loop loaded by two flux-coupled memristors whose state-dependent conductances interact, producing the coexisting-attractor 'extreme multistability' that makes memristive circuits prized chaos sources.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MenuBlipOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MenuBlipOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Menu blip: the tiny beep of moving the cursor in a game menu, a very short clean square-wave tick, fired at the set rate; the crisp UI navigation sound.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MetalFM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MetalFM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Three-operator FM tuned to inharmonic ratios for metallic, gong and bell percussion. Ratio sets the (deliberately non-integer) modulator spacing and Index the clang/brightness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MeteorEchoOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MeteorEchoOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Meteor echo: the brief Doppler-shifted ping of a radar/forward-scatter reflection off the ionized trail of a meteor (the 'pings' of meteor-scatter radio), a short tone that drops in pitch as the meteor recedes, scattered randomly across the sky at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MeyerWaveletOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MeyerWaveletOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Meyer wavelet: a smooth, band-limited wavelet defined by a raised-cosine roll-off in the frequency domain (infinitely differentiable, unlike the abrupt Shannon), giving a gently-ringing packet with no harsh sidelobes - approximated here from its smooth spectral window.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MichaelisMentenOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MichaelisMentenOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Michaelis-Menten oscillator: each cycle is the enzyme-kinetics saturation curve x/(K+x), a hyperbolic rise that climbs fast then asymptotes - K sets the half-saturation point and thus the curvature, a smooth saturating ramp distinct from exponential or power approaches.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Microwave}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Microwave}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Microwave oven - a steady transformer hum and turntable whir, ending each cycle with the finished ding. Cycle sets the run length, Tone the hum brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MicrowaveHumOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MicrowaveHumOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Microwave hum: a running microwave oven, a steady mains-frequency magnetron drone with harmonics over the faint rumble of the rotating turntable motor, the kitchen-appliance hum; the pitch sets the mains base.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MiddleSquarePRNG}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MiddleSquarePRNG}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Middle-square PRNG: von Neumann's 1949 method - square the seed and keep the middle digits as the next seed; famously prone to collapsing into short cycles or zero, its quirky, sometimes-stalling output is a vintage deterministic noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MidpointFractal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MidpointFractal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Midpoint displacement: the diamond-square algorithm that builds fractal mountain terrain by recursively offsetting segment midpoints by shrinking random amounts; precomputed into one cycle, it makes a jagged self-affine waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Minkowski}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Minkowski}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Minkowski question-mark oscillator (a first as a synth block): synthesises Minkowski's ?(x) function - the 'question-mark' curve that warps the number line by walking the Stern-Brocot tree, sending the quadratic irrationals to the rationals and flattening the golden ratio's run of 1s into a plain binary fraction. As a waveform it is a singular staircase like the devil's staircase but woven from continued fractions instead of base 3, giving a different self-similar ramp with its own fractal spectrum. A continued-fraction singular function distinct from the Cantor (Devil) and the smooth ramps. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MinkowskiQ}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MinkowskiQ}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}
