Minkowski question-mark function: the singular, strictly-increasing map that sends quadratic irrationals to rationals and flattens on every rational, built from the continued-fraction expansion - a fractal staircase-like waveform with structure at all scales.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MinorityGame}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MinorityGame}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Minority game (El Farol bar problem): many agents each decide whether to 'attend' based on recent winning history, and the minority side wins; their competing adaptive strategies make the attendance fluctuate around the half mark with self-organized, never-settling deviations.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MinskyCircle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MinskyCircle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Minsky circle: the famous PDP-1 rotation hack x -= e*y; y += e*x (updating x before y), which draws an almost-circle - actually a slightly-eccentric ellipse that never quite closes; it makes a clean, slowly-precessing quadrature sine pair.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MinstdNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MinstdNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

MINSTD noise: the Park-Miller 'minimal standard' multiplicative generator (x = 16807*x mod 2^31-1), the reference LCG that fixed RANDU's flaws and became the textbook default; a clean, well-tested deterministic white noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MiraMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MiraMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mira map: a 2-D iterated map with a rational nonlinearity (the basis of the Gumowski-Mira family) tuned to its embroidery regime, where the orbit stitches ornate, lace-like fractal loops - a richly-patterned chaotic texture.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MirandaStone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MirandaStone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Miranda-Stone attractor: a refined parameterization of the Lorenz-84 atmospheric model that exposes a delicate, symmetric chaotic flow with slow recurrences - organic, weather-like long-form modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MissingFundamentalOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MissingFundamentalOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Missing-fundamental oscillator: stacks only the 2nd through 6th harmonics of a pitch while omitting the fundamental itself, yet the ear still hears that absent fundamental (the residue-pitch phenomenon that lets tiny speakers seem to produce bass).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MitchellNetravaliOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MitchellNetravaliOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mitchell-Netravali oscillator: each cycle is the Mitchell-Netravali cubic reconstruction kernel (B=C=1/3, the imaging-standard balance of sharpness and ringing) - a rounded central lobe with gentler side-lobes than Catmull-Rom, a softer ringing pulse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MLCircuit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MLCircuit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Murali-Lakshmanan-Chua circuit: a sinusoidally-driven series circuit with a single Chua-diode (piecewise-linear) element, the simplest non-autonomous chaotic circuit - just one nonlinearity plus a forcing term.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MobiusOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MobiusOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mobius oscillator (a first as a synth block): an additive tone where each harmonic k is weighted by the Mobius function mu(k) - so harmonics on a repeated-prime multiple (4, 8, 9, 12...) are silenced entirely, while the squarefree ones are added with a PLUS or MINUS sign depending on whether they have an even or odd number of prime factors. The mix of dropped partials and phase-flipped (inverted) partials carves a spectrum found nowhere in ordinary synthesis - a signed, squarefree-only comb. Partials sets the highest harmonic considered. Distinct from the other number-theoretic oscillators.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Modal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Modal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal synthesis: a bank of 6 tuned decaying resonators struck by a gate (bells, mallets, struck metal). Structure morphs harmonic to inharmonic-bar partials.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ModalBar}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ModalBar}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal bar resonator: three tuned two-pole resonators at the inharmonic ratios of a struck bar ring when excited by the input (feed it an impulse, gate or noise) - mallet-percussion bodies by modal synthesis.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ModeBeatLaser}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ModeBeatLaser}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mode-beating laser: two longitudinal cavity modes drawing on one gain medium, whose cross-gain saturation makes their intensities flicker antiphase and chaotically as the pump rises - the multimode intensity dynamics of a free-running laser.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ModemOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ModemOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modem oscillator: frequency-shift keying that hops between a mark and a space tone driven by a pseudo-random bit stream, the screeching data warble of a dial-up modem handshake; the baud rate sets how frantically it chatters.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ModFM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ModFM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modified-FM oscillator (Lazzarini & Timoney 2009): a closed-form formant generator - a carrier sine is shaped by exp(Index*(cos(modulator)-1)), placing one resonant formant peak at the carrier whose bandwidth widens with Index, while Ratio (modulator/carrier) sets the harmonics under it. The FOF/CHANT formant sound from a single modified-FM expression - vocal 'aah/ooh', brass and resonant-sweep timbres. Freq sets the pitch (in0 = Pitch CV), Ratio the formant spacing, Index the bandwidth, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MoireOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MoireOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Moire oscillator: two harmonic series whose spacings differ slightly, so their partials drift through each other and beat in a shifting interference pattern - the auditory equivalent of the moire pattern seen through two overlaid grids.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Monsoon}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Monsoon}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Torrential downpour - dense high rain hiss over a deep rumble, with random thunder swells. Downpour sets the rain density, Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MonsoonOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MonsoonOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Monsoon: a torrential tropical downpour, a thick roaring wall of dense rain noise filling the mid band with countless overlapping droplet impacts and a low water-sheet rumble underneath, the relentless drum of heavy rain on a roof.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MoogVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MoogVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Minimoog-style voice: three slightly-detuned sawtooths stacked and pushed through a self-resonant four-pole Moog ladder filter for the fat, creamy mono-synth lead/bass. Cutoff sets the filter, Reso the ladder resonance, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MooreSpiegelOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MooreSpiegelOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Moore-Spiegel oscillator: a model of an ionized gas parcel oscillating in a star's atmosphere, a third-order system whose amplitude swells and collapses chaotically - astrophysical chaos as sound.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MorganMercerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MorganMercerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Morgan-Mercer-Flodin oscillator: each cycle is the MMF growth sigmoid (floor*Beta + x^Delta)/(Beta + x^Delta), a rational S-curve rising from a nonzero floor to a ceiling - Delta sets the steepness and Beta the half-point, a rational alternative to the exponential sigmoids. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MorganVoyceOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MorganVoyceOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Morgan-Voyce oscillator: each cycle is the nth Morgan-Voyce polynomial B_n (B_n = (x+2)*B_{n-1} - B_{n-2}, the polynomials describing resistor-ladder networks) - a recurrence with a +2 offset that produces lobed waveforms distinct from the Chebyshev family. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Morinkhuur}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Morinkhuur}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Morin khuur: the Mongolian horsehead fiddle - two horsehair string bundles bowed together (not pressed to a fingerboard) give a wide, breathy, harmonic-rich bowed tone with a characteristic flat, overtone-laden voice that pairs with khoomei throat-singing. BowForce sets the grip, Overtones the harmonic richness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MorletOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MorletOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Morlet wavelet: a sinusoid of a chosen number of cycles wrapped in a Gaussian envelope (the workhorse of continuous wavelet analysis), here looped per period as a wave packet whose Cycles knob trades a near-sine for a tight burst - a time-frequency-balanced grain.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MorphOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MorphOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

A single oscillator that morphs continuously through the four classic waveforms - sine, triangle, saw, square - as Morph sweeps 0 to 1, with a Warp control that bends the phase for extra harmonic motion.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MorrisLecar}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MorrisLecar}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Morris-Lecar neuron: a two-variable conductance-based membrane model (calcium + potassium gates) that fires smooth, rounded action potentials - a biophysically-grounded spiking oscillator distinct from the cubic neuron models.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MorrisLecarForced}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MorrisLecarForced}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Forced Morris-Lecar: the calcium-potassium membrane oscillator driven by a periodic current, whose interplay of intrinsic and forcing frequencies produces Arnold-tongue mode-locking and chaotic firing - excitable-cell forced dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MorseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MorseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Morse oscillator: a steady tone keyed on and off in dots and dashes, here endlessly transmitting 'SOS' (the dot-dot-dot dash-dash-dash dot-dot-dot distress call); the WPM knob sets the keying speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MorsePotentialOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MorsePotentialOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Morse-potential oscillator: each cycle traces the Morse anharmonic molecular potential (1-e^(-A*x))^2 - a steep repulsive wall on one side flattening to a dissociation plateau on the other - giving a strongly asymmetric waveform with a hard edge and a soft tail, A setting the wall steepness. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MosquitoOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MosquitoOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mosquito oscillator: a thin, high, slightly-detuned whine that wavers in pitch with the insect's wingbeat, the maddening drone of a mosquito near your ear; the vibrato depth and rate make it dart and waver.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MothFlutterOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MothFlutterOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Moth: the soft papery flutter of a moth batting against a lampshade, a low breathy buzz that swells and fades irregularly with random soft thumps as it bumps the surface; the flutter rate sets the wing speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MotorcycleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MotorcycleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Motorcycle: the throaty exhaust of a revving motorbike, a buzzy firing tone whose pitch surges up and falls back as the rider blips the throttle, with a hard distorted edge; the rev rate sets how often it revs.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MouseClickOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MouseClickOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mouse click: a computer mouse button pressed, a short two-stage tick (a bright down-click immediately followed by a softer release tick), fired at the set rate, the point-and-click office sound; the rate sets the clicking.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MoyalOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MoyalOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Moyal oscillator: each cycle is the Moyal PDF exp(-(x+e^-x)/2), a strongly right-skewed bump (the classic analytic approximation to the Landau energy-loss distribution) with a sharp leading edge and a long exponential tail - a distinctly lopsided waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MridangamHit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MridangamHit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tuned hand-drum: a pitched membrane with a fast downward pitch glide and harmonically-loaded overtones gives the singing, bending 'tun/dhin' strokes of a mridangam or tabla when struck.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MultifractalNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MultifractalNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Multifractal noise: white noise multiplied by a slowly-wandering log-normal envelope (a multiplicative cascade), so the loudness clusters into bursts and lulls with heavy-tailed statistics - the intermittent texture of turbulence and rainfall.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MultiplyWithCarry}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MultiplyWithCarry}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Multiply-with-carry noise: Marsaglia's MWC generator packs a 16-bit value and its carry into one word and updates x = a*(x low) + (x high), a fast generator with an enormous period and better equidistribution than a plain LCG.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Multisampler}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Multisampler}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Note, Gate, Velocity}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Multisampler: on each note-on (from the note, gate, and velocity inputs) it strikes every region whose key and velocity zones match, resampling each by note-vs-root plus fine tune so it plays at the right pitch. Slot selects which loaded .sfz region set to use (0 to 7), Loop forces looping of the regions' loop or play windows, and Level scales output; velocity crossfades and round-robin sequencing come from the region data. Use it for realistic multisampled instruments where timbre tracks key and velocity.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MultiScrollJerk}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MultiScrollJerk}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Multi-scroll jerk: a jerk system whose nonlinearity is a sine of x, so its restoring force has infinitely many zero crossings and the orbit can wander across a row of stacked scrolls - a grid-of-scrolls chaotic flow.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MultivibratorOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MultivibratorOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Astable multivibrator: the two-transistor square-wave circuit whose output is not an ideal square but shows the exponential RC capacitor-charge curve on each transition, giving a softer, rounded-edged square with a characteristic analog slump.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MunmuangsaenSri}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MunmuangsaenSri}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Munmuangsaen-Srisuchinwong system: an unusually simple chaotic flow with only one nonlinear term and no equilibrium point, designed as a minimal hidden-attractor chaos generator for hardware random-number use.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MusicalSaw}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MusicalSaw}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Musical saw: a hand saw bowed on its flexible edge - a bowed-string waveguide filtered very dark so the tone collapses toward a pure, eerie, voice-like sine (the bending blade sets the pitch); the ghostly, theremin-adjacent timbre, but produced by stick-slip bow friction rather than a heterodyne oscillator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MusicalSawOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MusicalSawOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Musical-saw oscillator: the bowed flexible saw blade produces an almost-pure sine (the flat sweet spot of the bend vibrates cleanly) with a wide, slow, expressive vibrato and a faint metallic shimmer - the ghostly, vocal wail of the singing saw.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MusicBox}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MusicBox}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal music box: a trigger on in0 plucks a steel comb tine - a bright bell-like tone from a strong fundamental plus high inharmonic partials (1 : 5.4 : 13) with a fast tinkling attack and medium decay, the unmistakable music-box timbre. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Tone the brightness of the upper partials, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MusicBoxOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MusicBoxOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Music box: the fragile haunting tinkle of a wind-up music box, a thin bell-like tone with a slightly-detuned shimmer and a quick decay, the eerie nursery melody of horror films; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NadarajahHaghighiOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NadarajahHaghighiOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nadarajah-Haghighi oscillator: each cycle is alpha*lambda*(1+lambda*x)^(alpha-1)*e^(1-(1+lambda*x)^alpha), a generalized-exponential lifetime PDF built on (1+lambda*x) rather than x^beta - giving a distinct rise/cut-off balance with a true zero density at the origin for alpha>1. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NagelSchreckenberg}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NagelSchreckenberg}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nagel-Schreckenberg model: cars on a ring accelerate, brake to avoid the car ahead, randomly dawdle, then advance by their speed - the minimal traffic automaton that spontaneously breeds phantom stop-and-go jams out of free flow. The average speed (traffic flow) is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NailGunOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NailGunOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nail gun: a pneumatic framing nailer firing, a sharp percussive punch immediately followed by a short blast of escaping compressed air, fired at the set rate, the construction-site sound; the rate sets the firing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NakagamiOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NakagamiOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nakagami oscillator: each cycle is the Nakagami-m PDF x^(2m-1) e^(-m x^2), the fading-channel distribution; the shape M morphs from a sharply-rising one-sided spike (low m) to a more symmetric Gaussian-like bump (high m) - a smoothly tunable asymmetric pulse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NasalOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NasalOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nasal oscillator: a glottal buzz routed through the heavily-damped, low resonance of the nasal cavity with an antiresonance notch above it, the muffled hum of m, n and ng where sound radiates through the nose rather than the mouth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Nebula}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Nebula}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nebula - a slowly shifting cloud of detuned high sines over a soft noise wash, an icy interstellar shimmer. Drift sets the movement, Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NeimarkSackerMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NeimarkSackerMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Neimark-Sacker map: the discrete analogue of a Hopf bifurcation, where a fixed point sheds an invariant circle (quasiperiodicity) that wrinkles and breaks into chaos as the gain rises - the map route to torus-breakdown chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NeonRelaxation}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NeonRelaxation}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Neon relaxation network: capacitors charging through resistors until neon bulbs strike and dump them, two such relaxation oscillators coupled so their firing times interfere into chaotic, irregular blinking - vintage relaxation-oscillator chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NeonRelaxOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NeonRelaxOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Neon-lamp relaxation oscillator: a capacitor charges until it reaches the lamp's strike voltage, then the lamp fires and dumps it down to the lower extinguish voltage before going dark again - a two-threshold hysteresis sawtooth, the simplest possible oscillator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NephroidOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NephroidOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nephroid oscillator: the coordinate 3*sin(t) - Shape*sin(3t) traced from the kidney-shaped nephroid, a clean blend of the fundamental and its third harmonic in fixed phase - Shape dials the third-harmonic weight from a pure sine to a hollow, clarinet-like tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NESPulseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NESPulseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

NES pulse: the Nintendo 2A03's pulse channel with its four hard-coded duty cycles (12.5%, 25%, 50%, 75%), the thin, nasal square-wave voice behind countless NES melodies and basslines.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NESTriangleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NESTriangleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

NES triangle: the 2A03's triangle channel quantized to its 16 discrete steps and locked at full volume (the chip gives it no amplitude control), the slightly-staircased bass-and-lead triangle that anchored NES soundtracks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NeuralAvalanche}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NeuralAvalanche}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Neuronal avalanches: a self-organizing branching network where each firing neuron triggers, on average, a critical number of others; tuned near criticality it emits scale-free, power-law-distributed bursts of activity (deterministic RNG) - cortical SOC.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NeuralSpikeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NeuralSpikeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Neural spike train: a leaky integrate-and-fire neuron whose membrane ramps toward threshold, fires a sharp spike and then sits in a refractory dip before recharging - the all-or-nothing pulse train of a real neuron, its rate set by the input current.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NeuroBass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NeuroBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Neurofunk bass: a detuned-saw sub pushed through cascaded distortion and a resonant lowpass for the screaming, metallic, multi-layered DnB bass. Detune sets the beating, Drive the distortion, Cutoff the filter. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NewtonLeipnikHyper}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NewtonLeipnikHyper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hyperchaotic Newton-Leipnik: the rigid-body-with-torque Newton-Leipnik attractor extended with a fourth feedback state into hyperchaos, intensifying its twin-lobed orbit into a denser, higher-dimensional tumble.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NewtonLeipOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NewtonLeipOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Newton-Leipnik attractor: a rigid-body rotation model with feedback torque that supports two coexisting strange attractors, tracing a complex twin-lobed chaotic orbit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NewtonMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NewtonMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Newton fractal map: iterates Newton's root-finding step for z^3-1 in the complex plane and reseeds at a slowly-rotating start each time it converges, so the output hops chaotically between the three roots' basins - the Newton-fractal boundary as a stepped source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Ney}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Ney}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ney: the end-blown reed flute of Persian/Turkish/Arab music - blown across the rim so a large fraction of the tone is turbulent breath, giving an intensely airy, hollow, human voice with a husky attack; Breath drives the tone, Air the prominent breath component.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NicholsonBailey}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NicholsonBailey}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nicholson-Bailey model: the classic host-parasitoid map whose coupled exponential search terms produce diverging, oscillating boom-crash cycles - ecological instability iterated into a jagged chaotic stream.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NishioOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NishioOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nishio oscillator: a chaotic circuit built from two LC stages coupled through a diode bridge, whose piecewise conduction folds the coupled resonances into chaos - a classic coupled-oscillator chaos demonstrator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Noise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Noise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Colored-noise generator: Color morphs from white (flat spectrum) toward a low-passed pink-ish tone, scaled by Level. Use it as a percussion/wind source, a sample-and-hold input, or to add air and dither. It takes no signal input - it is a pure generator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoiseBlendOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoiseBlendOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tone-to-breath oscillator: blends a pure sine at the pitch with a low-passed noise whose colour tracks the same frequency, sweeping from a clean tone to a breathy, airy, pitched hiss - flute-attack, vocal-breath and wind textures from one knob.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoiseColor}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoiseColor}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tunable coloured noise: one knob sweeps the spectral tilt from deep brown (-6 dB/oct, rumbling) through pink (-3 dB/oct), white (flat), blue (+3 dB/oct) to violet (+6 dB/oct, hissy). A single source for every noise colour, where the other noise blocks are fixed. Level scales the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoiseFM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoiseFM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Noise-FM oscillator: a sine carrier is phase-modulated by band-limited noise, scattering its energy into a gritty, breathy, partly-pitched tone - between an oscillator and colored noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoseHooverMod}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoseHooverMod}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modified Nose-Hoover: the time-reversible Nose-Hoover oscillator with an added cubic restoring term that breaks its near-conservative symmetry into a dissipative attractor - a bridge between conservative and dissipative chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoseHooverOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoseHooverOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nose-Hoover oscillator: a time-reversible chaotic system from molecular-dynamics thermostats, producing a smooth, breathing chaotic tone with long quasi-periodic stretches.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NotificationPingOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NotificationPingOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Notification ping: the soft pleasant two-tone chime of a phone or app notification, a clean bell tone followed by a higher partner tone, both ringing briefly and fading, fired at the set rate; the you-have-a-message alert.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Novachord}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Novachord}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Novachord: the 1939 vacuum-tube polyphonic - a soft, divide-down organ tone with a slow vibrato and a gentle, hollow voice, the first commercial synthesizer. Vibrato sets the wobble, Tone the brightness, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NovaFractal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NovaFractal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Nova fractal: Newton's method for z^3-1 with a relaxation factor and an added constant (z -> z - R*(z^3-1)/(3z^2) + c), blending Newton basins with Mandelbrot-like structure into glowing nova filaments; the orbit's real part is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NTSCSyncOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NTSCSyncOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

NTSC oscillator: the high-pitched whine of an analog television, the 15.734 kHz horizontal-line-scan frequency shaped as the sawtooth-with-sync-pulse of the flyback, plus the audible field-rate flicker - the sound of an old CRT left on.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Oboe}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Oboe}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Breath, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Oboe: a bright, nasal, penetrating double-reed - a narrow pulse driven through a strong resonant formant with reed buzz, played by holding in0 as breath. Reed sets the buzz, Breath the air, Level the output. in0 = Breath gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Ocarina}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Ocarina}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Breath, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ocarina: a vessel flute with a nearly pure tone plus a soft second harmonic and a touch of breath, played by holding in0. The round, whistle-like clay-flute timbre. Tune sets the pitch, Breath the air noise, Level the output. in0 = Breath gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Ocean}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Ocean}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ocean surf - filtered noise swelling in and out like waves rolling onto a beach. Rate sets the wave period, Tone the brightness (spray vs deep roll), Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OceanSwellOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OceanSwellOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ocean swell: filtered noise shaped to the Pierson-Moskowitz spectrum of a fully-developed sea, giving the slow, narrow-banded heave of ocean swell whose peak period the wind-speed knob sets - a rolling, randomized low-frequency surge.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OceanWave}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OceanWave}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Amount}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ocean waves: slow swells of surf - filtered noise that rises and breaks in a long, repeating crash-and-recede cycle. Rate sets the swell period, Tone the spray brightness, Level the output. in0 = Amount gate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OctaveOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OctaveOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Octave-stack oscillator (a first as a synth block): an additive tone whose overtones are only the powers of two - the 1st, 2nd, 4th, 8th, 16th... harmonics, i.e. the fundamental plus a stack of pure octaves above it. Because every partial is an octave of the one below, they fuse into a single, enormously reinforced pitch with the bright, hollow body of a full organ stop or a Shepard-tone layer, no in-between harmonics to muddy it. Partials sets how many octaves to stack, alias-free up to Nyquist. A pure-octave spectrum distinct from the dense harmonic saw. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OddHarmonicOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OddHarmonicOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Odd-harmonic oscillator: sums only the odd partials (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13) with an adjustable rolloff, the spectrum of a square wave or a stopped pipe like the clarinet - hollow and woody, with no even overtones to brighten it.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OminousTickOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OminousTickOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ominous tick: the slow dread-laden tick of a clock counting down to something terrible, a hard dry resonant tick repeated at a steady slow rate over a faint low rumble, the time-is-running-out tension; the tick rate sets the tempo.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OndesMartenot}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OndesMartenot}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ondes Martenot: the haunting, vocal early-electronic voice - a sine with a few harmonics and a deep, expressive vibrato (the classic 'gliding' theremin-cousin), played by holding in0. Vibrato sets the wobble, Timbre the harmonic richness, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Ondioline}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Ondioline}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ondioline: the buzzy, intensely-vibratoed reed-organ keyboard (Jean-Jacques Perrey's voice) - a harmonically-rich pulse with a fast, deep vibrato and a percussive attack. Vibrato sets the wobble, Tone the brightness, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OpenHat909}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OpenHat909}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-909 open hi-hat: a long, sizzling wash of six detuned squares and white noise through a sharp highpass, the bright, ringing open hat of house and techno. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OPOChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OPOChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Optical parametric oscillator: a pumped chi-2 cavity converting pump into signal and idler photons, whose three-wave mixing with detuning produces self-pulsing and chaotic intensity dynamics above threshold - nonlinear-optics chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OpticalBistable}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OpticalBistable}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Optical bistability: a nonlinear optical cavity whose transmitted field depends on its own intensity-shifted phase, giving a hysteretic input-output map that period-doubles into chaos as the drive rises - the foundation of all-optical switching.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OrchHit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OrchHit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Orchestra hit (Fairlight ORCH5 / Stravinsky stab): a trigger on in0 fires a dense detuned cluster of saws stacked in octaves and fifths - strings and brass hitting one staccato chord - with a sharp attack, a short decay and 8-bit-style bit-crush for the lo-fi digital crunch that made the sound iconic in rave, hip-hop, hardcore and techno. Tune sets the pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Spread the detune width of the cluster, Decay the staccato length, Bits the lo-fi crunch (low = crunchier), Tone the brightness, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OregonatorOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OregonatorOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Oregonator: the three-variable model of the Belousov-Zhabotinsky oscillating chemical reaction, whose relaxation kinetics produce sharp, spiky, slowly-recurring pulses - the famous self-organizing chemical clock as a modulator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Organ}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Organ}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Additive drawbar organ oscillator that sums four sine partials at the first four harmonics of Freq. Bar1, Bar2 and Bar3 set the drawbar levels of the fundamental, second and third harmonics (the fourth harmonic tracks Bar2 at half level), and Level sets the overall output. A self-running tone generator with no audio input, voiced from sine-only partials for a clean drawbar timbre.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OrganBeatOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OrganBeatOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Beating tonewheel organ: six drawbar partials (sub, fundamental, octave, twelfth, super-octave, larigot) summed with a small per-partial detune so they beat against each other like a real electromechanical organ - the Beat knob sets the chorus/age of the instrument.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OrnsteinUhlenbeckProc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OrnsteinUhlenbeckProc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process: noise that is constantly pulled back toward a mean while being kicked randomly (dx = -theta*x dt + sigma dW), giving a smooth, band-limited wander - the canonical model of a damped particle in thermal noise and of mean-reverting signals.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Oscillator}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Oscillator}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Audio-rate tone generator with sine, saw, square and triangle waves (Wave) at Freq. Use it as a ring-mod or FM carrier, a drone, a sub layer, or a test tone. It takes no signal input - patch its output into RingMod, a filter, or straight to Out.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Oud}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Oud}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Arabic oud: a warm, fretless plucked lute with a round, fast-decaying tone and no buzz - a softly-damped Karplus string voiced for the mellow Middle-Eastern lute. Decay sets the ring, Tone the brightness, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OutboardMotorOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OutboardMotorOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Outboard motor: the sputtering buzz of a small two-stroke boat engine, an irregular high firing tone with a watery gurgle as the prop bites and cavitates, the puttering of a dinghy; the RPM knob revs it up.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OwlHootOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OwlHootOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Owl-hoot oscillator: soft, breathy low-pitched hoots (a near-sine tone with a touch of breath noise) grouped into the rhythmic hoo-hoo-hoo phrase of an owl; the hoot rate sets the call's cadence.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PadStack}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PadStack}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Lush pad oscillator: the fundamental plus detuned copies an octave and two octaves up, gently detuned for slow beating. Spread sets the detune width and Octaves the upper-layer level - an instant wide synth-pad bed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PadVoices}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PadVoices}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Detuned sine pad: seven sine partials micro-detuned around the fundamental beat slowly against each other into a warm, wide, chorused pad drone - the lush PadSynth-style stack from pure sines.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PafOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PafOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phase-aligned formant: a carrier centred on a formant frequency is windowed once per pitch period by a raised-cosine bell, placing a clean, movable formant peak on a harmonic series (the PAF technique) - voiced, vowel-like timbres.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PagerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PagerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pager oscillator: the two-tone sequential alert that fires a pager - one tone held, then a second different tone - followed by the alert beeps, the dispatch-radio sound that summons on-call crews; the tone pair selects the address.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Pandeiro}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Pandeiro}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pandeiro: the Brazilian frame drum with jingling platinelas - a tuned membrane hit wrapped in a bright metallic jingle. Tune sets the pitch, Jingle the platinela mix, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PanFlute}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PanFlute}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Breath, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pan flute / shakuhachi: a breathy edge-blown voice - a sine fundamental wrapped in tuned breath noise from a resonant bandpass at the pitch, played by holding in0 as breath. Airy and hollow. Tune sets the pitch, Breath the air balance, Level the output. in0 = Breath gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PanOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PanOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pan attractor: a three-dimensional autonomous chaotic system in the Lorenz family with a single quadratic cross-term in each of two equations, tracing a compact, fast-spinning butterfly.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PaperShredderOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PaperShredderOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Paper shredder: a document fed into a shredder, a steady low motor whir overlaid with a harsh ripping crunch as the blades chew the paper, the confidential-disposal sound; the feed knob sets how much paper is tearing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PaperTearOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PaperTearOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Paper tear: the ripping rasp of a sheet of paper being torn, a dense crackle of tiny fibre snaps whose rate and brightness rise with the tearing speed, the satisfying rrrip of paper; the speed knob sets how fast it tears.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ParabolaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ParabolaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Parabola wave: a periodic train of parabola segments (the integral of a triangle wave), whose harmonics roll off as 1/n^2 for a tone softer and rounder than a triangle - a gentle, hollow source with very weak upper partials.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ParabolicSineOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ParabolicSineOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Parabolic-sine oscillator: builds its cycle from the classic two-parabola sine approximation (4/pi)*t - (4/pi^2)*t*|t| over t in [-pi,pi] - exact at the zero crossings and peaks, with a characteristic small odd-harmonic error (~ -24 dB third) that gives it a slightly brighter, hollower tone than a true sine. The cheap-synth sine, as a distinct timbre.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ParametricPendulum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ParametricPendulum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Parametric pendulum: a pendulum whose pivot is shaken vertically, so the gravity term is modulated; at the right drive it swings, period-doubles, then tumbles into full chaotic rotation - the parametric route to rotational chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ParetoCdfOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ParetoCdfOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pareto-CDF oscillator: each cycle is the Pareto cumulative 1-(1/x)^Alpha for x>=1, a power-law saturation that shoots up off the floor then asymptotes - Alpha sets how abrupt the rise, a heavy-headed ramp distinct from exponential or hyperbolic saturation. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ParetoIVOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ParetoIVOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pareto-IV oscillator: each cycle is the Pareto type-IV PDF (A/Gamma)*x^(1/Gamma-1)*(1+x^(1/Gamma))^(-A-1), a flexible heavy-tailed bump that generalizes the log-logistic (A=1) with an extra tail-shape control - Gamma sets the rise, A the tail. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ParetoNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ParetoNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pareto noise: a power-law source u^(-1/alpha) where a few enormous values dominate a sea of small ones (the 80/20 rule, city sizes, wealth); lowering alpha fattens the tail toward rare extreme spikes - a self-similar heavy-tailed noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ParetoQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ParetoQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pareto-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the Pareto inverse-CDF (1-p)^(-1/Alpha), a ramp that stays low then blows up toward the top as a power law - Alpha sets how violent the late acceleration is, a heavy-tailed quantile curve. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ParticleSwarm}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ParticleSwarm}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Particle-swarm optimization: a swarm of candidate solutions each accelerate toward their own best-found spot and the swarm's global best while chasing a slowly-drifting optimum, so the global-best position homes in with overshoot and re-convergence - a self-organizing tracking CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PartyBlowerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PartyBlowerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Party blower: a party horn blown out, a reedy squawk that rises in pitch as the paper tongue unrolls and ends in a little flutter, fired at the set rate, the birthday-party toot; the rate sets the blows.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ParzenOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ParzenOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Parzen oscillator: each cycle is the Parzen window, a piecewise-cubic B-spline bump that is flatter on top and rolls off as a cubic at the edges - a smooth pulse between the parabolic Welch and the cosine windows, with a soft, woody harmonic balance.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PaulaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PaulaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Paula oscillator: the Amiga's Paula chip set pitch by an integer sample-period divider off a fixed clock, the quantization that gave MOD-tracker music its particular tuning; a band-limited-ish saw stepped onto Paula's period grid.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PaulWaveletOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PaulWaveletOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Paul wavelet: an analytic (one-sided-spectrum) complex wavelet of integer order, more time-localized and asymmetric than the Morlet; its real part read per cycle gives a sharp leading lobe with a decaying oscillatory tail.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PCGNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PCGNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

PCG noise: O'Neill's permuted congruential generator advances a linear-congruential state then applies an xorshift-and-rotate output permutation, giving statistically-excellent white noise (passes BigCrush) with a different texture than plain LCG.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PearsonIvOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PearsonIvOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pearson-IV oscillator: each cycle is the Pearson type-IV PDF (1+(x/a)^2)^(-M) * e^(-Nu*atan(x/a)), a bell that Nu skews to one side and M sets the tail weight - a flexible skewed heavy-tailed shape (used for asymmetric financial returns); peak-normalized and bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PehlivanOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PehlivanOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pehlivan-Uyaroglu attractor: a chaotic flow with a single quadratic term and simple linear couplings, designed for low circuit cost, tracing a smooth twin-lobed orbit - a hardware-friendly strange attractor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PencilSharpenerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PencilSharpenerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}
