Pencil sharpener: an electric pencil sharpener running, a high-pitched motor whine under a gritty grinding crunch as the blades shave the wood, the classroom-desk sound; the grit knob sets how hard it bites.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PercolationCluster}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PercolationCluster}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Percolation: each ring site is independently occupied with probability p, re-rolled each step; the largest connected run of occupied sites jumps sharply once p crosses the percolation threshold - a critical-transition order parameter that flickers between fragmented and spanning.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PernarowskiBurst}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PernarowskiBurst}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pernarowski burster: a minimal three-variable polynomial model that captures square-wave bursting through a cubic fast subsystem gated by a single slow variable - a clean, analyzable bursting oscillator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PersistentWalk}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PersistentWalk}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Persistent random walk: a walker that keeps its current direction with high probability and only occasionally reverses (a correlated walk with momentum), giving long smooth runs instead of the jagged jitter of an ordinary random walk - a drifting, inertia-laden CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Pew}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Pew}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Laser pew: a quick descending-pitch zap - a sine swept rapidly down with a short decay, the retro arcade/sci-fi laser shot. Tune sets the start pitch, Sweep the drop, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhamHidden}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhamHidden}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hidden-attractor flow: a three-dimensional system deliberately built with no equilibrium points (a Pham-type construction), so its chaotic attractor is 'hidden' - reachable only from special initial conditions, not by tracking any fixed point.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhaseDist}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhaseDist}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Casio CZ phase-distortion oscillator: a phase ramp drives a cosine through a warped read so DCW morphs a sine toward the chosen shape, then scales by Level. Shape selects Saw or Pulse (true phase distortion) or three resonant modes (ResoSaw, ResoTri, ResoTrap) that are windowed hard-sync, and DCW sets the distortion amount or, in resonant modes, the resonant multiple (1 to 16). Low DCW stays near a clean sine while high DCW opens into bright sawtooth, pulse, or formant-like resonant tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhaseDistort}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhaseDistort}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Casio CZ phase distortion: a sine read with a warped phase (fast then slow), synthesising a resonant, filter-swept sawtooth without a filter - the bright, vocal PD timbre. Amount sets the distortion (dark to resonant), Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhaseDistortCZ}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhaseDistortCZ}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phase distortion (Casio CZ): a linear phase ramp is bent through a movable knee before driving a cosine, so the read-out speeds up then slows within each cycle - warping a sine toward a resonant saw/pulse without any filter, the signature CZ sound.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhaseFbOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhaseFbOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phase-feedback sine: the oscillator's own output is fed back into its phase (the DX-style feedback operator), so as Feedback rises the sine warps smoothly toward a sawtooth, adding a controllable wash of harmonics from one operator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhaserBlastOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhaserBlastOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phaser blast: the pew of a sci-fi energy weapon, a tone that swoops sharply downward in pitch with a ringing metallic overtone and a quick decay, fired repeatedly at the set rate; the laser-gun staple.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhaseWarp}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhaseWarp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phase-distortion oscillator (Casio CZ style): a pitch-locked phase ramp is bent through a movable inflection before a cosine, sweeping from sine toward a resonant, formant-like spectrum as Warp increases.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhasingOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhasingOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phasing oscillator: two identical tones, one running fractionally faster, so they slide continuously in and out of phase (Steve Reich's 'Piano Phase' technique), creating ever-shifting interference patterns from a single repeated voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhiPartials}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhiPartials}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Golden-ratio additive oscillator: partials stacked at powers of the golden ratio (phi) are maximally inharmonic (the least rational spacing), producing shimmering, never-beating metallic clusters.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhoenixFractal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhoenixFractal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phoenix fractal: an escape-time iteration z' = z^2 + c + p*z_prev that feeds back the previous iterate (a memory term p), producing curling phoenix-feather structures absent from the plain Mandelbrot; the orbit's real part (reseeded on escape) is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhoenixJulia}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhoenixJulia}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phoenix fractal orbit: the complex map z' = z^2 + c + p*z_prev (which remembers its previous value), whose flame-shaped 'Phoenix' Julia set folds the orbit through a memoryful complex-plane chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhotocopierOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhotocopierOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Photocopier: an office copier running a page, a steady mechanical hum under a scan-lamp carriage that whooshes across and back on each copy cycle, the document-copy sound; the cycle rate sets how fast it copies.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Piano}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Piano}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Additive piano (house piano stab): sums inharmonic partials tuned by the real piano stretch formula f*n*sqrt(1+B*n^2) - so the overtones run slightly sharp the way struck strings do - with a bright hammer-noise attack and a low-pass that darkens as the note decays (higher partials die first). The acoustic-piano chord stab of piano house, deep house and progressive. Tune sets the pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Inharm the string-stretch detune, Decay the note length, Hammer the attack-noise knock, Tone the initial brightness, and Level the output. A gate on in0 strikes it.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PianoHammerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PianoHammerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Piano: a hammer-struck string modelled as a Karplus delay with a soft hammer-pulse excitation plus a slightly-sharp second partial for the inharmonic stretch of stiff piano wire; re-struck at the set rate, with a long ringing decay.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PickoverAttr}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PickoverAttr}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pickover attractor: a 3-D trigonometric iterated map that folds into smooth ribbon-like surfaces, giving a flowing, less-grainy chaotic texture than the 2-D maps.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PIDChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PIDChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

PID-loop chaos: a nonlinear (saturating) plant under a proportional-integral-derivative controller whose gains are pushed past the stability margin, so the closed loop oscillates and chaotically hunts around its setpoint - control-theory instability.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PierceDiode}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PierceDiode}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pierce diode: an electron beam injected across a gap whose space-charge forms an oscillating virtual cathode; past the Pierce instability threshold the current chaotically self-modulates - a plasma-physics chaos source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PigOinkOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PigOinkOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pig oink: the grunting snort of a pig, a low buzzy voiced burst with a nasal honk and a snuffly noise edge, repeated in quick grunts, fired at the set rate; the contented sound of the sty.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PinchersMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PinchersMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pinchers map: a Pickover iterated map combining a hyperbolic-tangent pinch with a sine, which squeezes the orbit into pinched, pincer-like fractal lobes - a distinctive folded chaotic texture.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PinkGen}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PinkGen}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pink noise generator: white noise shaped by the Paul Kellet 7-pole filter to roll off at 3 dB/octave, the equal-energy-per-octave noise used for room tuning and natural-sounding wind/rain textures.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PinkNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PinkNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pink (1/f) noise - equal energy per octave, the natural-sounding noise for wind, surf and analog hiss. Tone tilts it from full pink toward darker low-pass, Level sets the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Pipa}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Pipa}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pipa: the Chinese pear-shaped lute - a bright nylon/silk Karplus string driven by the rapid finger tremolo (lunzhi) that sustains a note as a shimmering roll; Tremolo sets the re-attack rate from single plucks to a dense buzzing roll.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PipeOrgan}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PipeOrgan}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Church pipe organ (Interstellar-style): additive ranks at the true organ-stop pitches - 8' fundamental, 4' octave, 2 2/3' twelfth, 2' fifteenth, 1 3/5' seventeenth and 1 1/3' nineteenth (harmonics 1-6) - so the upper mutation stops reinforce the fundamental with cathedral brilliance, plus a wind 'chiff' on the attack and an optional tremulant. The huge sustained organ of cinematic and gothic scoring, distinct from the Hammond drawbar Organ. Tune sets the pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Stops the registration (how many upper ranks are drawn = brightness), Chiff the attack wind-noise, Tremulant the slow amplitude vibrato, and Level the output. A gate on in0 holds the note.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PiriformOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PiriformOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Piriform oscillator: the pear-curve coordinate cos(t)(1 + Pinch*sin t), morphing from a pure cosine (Pinch 0) to an asymmetric pear-shaped wave (Pinch 1) that bulges on one side - a smoothly skewable timbre with shifting even-harmonic content.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PistonPumpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PistonPumpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Piston pump: the rhythmic chuff of a reciprocating air pump or old engine, a low thump on each compression stroke followed by a breathy intake hiss, the pulsing of a piston cycling; the rate sets the strokes per second.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Plaits}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Plaits}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Macro-oscillator (Mutable Instruments Plaits): one voice, six synthesis Models selected by the Model param - Analog (saw/square), FM (2-op), Grain (formant), Chord (stacked intervals), Wavetable (sine-saw-square morph) and Modal (struck bar) - each shaped by Timbre and Morph. Tune sets pitch; an internal decay envelope (Decay) fires when a gate hits in0, so it plays as a drone unpatched or a pinged percussive voice when triggered. The Swiss-army oscillator covering most synthesis techniques in a single block.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PlanckTaperOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PlanckTaperOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Planck-taper oscillator: each cycle is the Planck-taper window - a flat-topped pulse whose edges fall to zero through the infinitely-smooth Planck bump function - so Taper morphs from a near-rectangle (buzzy) to a gently rounded pulse with no ringing, the smoothest possible band-limited flat-top.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PlantNeuron}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PlantNeuron}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Plant neuron: a model of molluscan bursting neurons where a slow calcium variable periodically gates fast spiking, producing the parabolic-bursting and chaotic spike-cluster patterns seen in real pacemaker cells.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PlasmaBallOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PlasmaBallOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Plasma globe: the high crackling fizz of the dancing tendrils inside a novelty plasma ball, a high noise band modulated by a fast flicker as the filaments writhe and snap to the glass; the flicker rate sets how restless the plasma.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PlasmaLangmuirOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PlasmaLangmuirOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Langmuir oscillation: electrons in a plasma sloshing against the ion background at the plasma frequency; at large amplitude the sinusoid steepens and 'wave-breaks' into a sharply-peaked, cusped waveform - the cold-plasma electrostatic oscillation pushed nonlinear.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PlasmaSheath}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PlasmaSheath}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Plasma sheath instability: the thin charged boundary layer at a plasma-wall interface whose ion transit time and ionization feedback self-oscillate, relaxation-spiking and chaotically flickering near the instability threshold.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PlateModeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PlateModeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Plate-mode oscillator: sines at the dense two-dimensional bending modes of a rectangular plate (ratios near 1, 1.58, 1.94, 2.0, 2.44, 2.92, 3.16), the crowded inharmonic spectrum behind metallic plate-reverb shimmer and thunder-sheet clang.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PLLChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PLLChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phase-locked-loop chaos: a third-order PLL whose loop filter and sinusoidal phase detector, when the input frequency steps too far, lose lock and break into chaotic phase slipping - the cycle-slip instability of real PLLs.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PlosiveOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PlosiveOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Plosive oscillator: a train of brief release bursts - a sharp noise transient with a quick spectral fall - the popping articulation of stop consonants like p, t and k; the rate sets how fast the stops repeat.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Pluck}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Pluck}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[PHY]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Note (CV), Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Karplus-Strong plucked-string physical model: a gate edge fills a delay line with a white-noise burst, then each pass averages neighbouring samples and scales by Decay so the noise settles into a pitched, decaying tone. Tune shifts the note in semitones (sets the delay length from the in0 note), Damp blends in more of the lowpass average to dull the highs, Decay sets how slowly the string rings out, and Level scales the output. Plucks on the in1 gate; short Decay and high Damp give a soft muted pluck, long Decay and low Damp a bright sustained string.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PluckBass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PluckBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Plucked bass: a tight, finger-plucked electric-bass tone over a Karplus-Strong string - warm, punchy and dynamic. Decay sets the sustain, Tone the brightness, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PluckBurst}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PluckBurst}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gated Karplus-Strong pluck: a gate fires a one-period noise burst into a tuned, damped delay loop, so each trigger plucks a string at the set pitch - physical string synthesis you play with a gate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PluckedBass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PluckedBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Plucked bass string: a low-tuned Karplus-Strong delay loop excited by a short filtered noise burst gives a warm, rounded fingered-bass pluck with natural string decay - a self-contained bass voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PluckExcite}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PluckExcite}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Transient-plucked string: an envelope detector fires a one-period noise burst into a tuned, damped Karplus-Strong delay loop on every input transient, so drums or any percussive input replay as a plucked string at the set pitch - a resonant excitation effect, not a continuous resonator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PluckPickPos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PluckPickPos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pick-position pluck: the noise burst exciting a Karplus-Strong string is comb-filtered by a pick-position parameter (as on a real string, plucking near the bridge thins the lows), shaping the harmonic balance of each pluck.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PMOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PMOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phase-modulation oscillator: a sine modulator running at Ratio times Freq offsets the carrier phase by depth Index, and the warped phase is read through the selected carrier waveform, then scaled by Level. Wave selects the carrier shape (Sine, Tri, three bent/saturated triangle variants, narrowed sine), Ratio sets the modulator-to-carrier frequency, and Index sets modulation depth. Raising Index and Ratio builds increasingly bright, inharmonic FM-style spectra.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PoinsotSpiralOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PoinsotSpiralOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Poinsot-spiral oscillator: the projection (1/cosh(d*theta))*cos(theta) of Poinsot's spiral, a cosine under a smooth hyperbolic-secant amplitude decay across each cycle - a soft plucked-like swell that fades to silence, with Decay setting how fast it dies within the period.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PointillistGrainOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PointillistGrainOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pointillist grain: very sparse, isolated grains scattered in time, each a short tone at a randomly-chosen pitch from a band, producing the dotted, droplet-like texture of pointillist electronic music; density and pitch-range set how busy and how wide.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PoissonKernelOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PoissonKernelOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Poisson kernel: (1-r^2)/(1-2r cos theta + r^2), the harmonic-extension kernel of the disc whose harmonics roll off geometrically as r^n; the radius r continuously tunes from a flat sine (r small) to a sharp positive spike (r near 1) - a one-knob brightness pulse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:POKEY}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{POKEY}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Atari POKEY oscillator: a square wave fed through the chip's polynomial-counter noise, sweeping from a clean tone to the dirty, buzzing distortion-bass POKEY is famous for. Dirt sets the noise blend, Width the pulse duty, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PokeyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PokeyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

POKEY oscillator: the Atari 8-bit POKEY chip gated its square tone through long polynomial (LFSR) counters to make its trademark gritty, buzzy 'distortion' timbres - a square clocked by a pseudo-random divider rather than a clean divide.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PolarizationLaser}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PolarizationLaser}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Polarization laser: a laser whose two orthogonal polarization modes compete for the same gain, switching and antiphase-flickering chaotically as the pump and anisotropy change - the polarization dynamics of VCSELs.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PolyaUrnProc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PolyaUrnProc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Polya urn: draw a ball, return it plus another of the same colour, repeat - so the colour proportion reinforces itself and converges to a random limit (a martingale, the model of cumulative advantage); a self-reinforcing drift that locks somewhere new each run.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PolyBlepSaw}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PolyBlepSaw}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

PolyBLEP sawtooth: a naive ramp with a polynomial band-limited step correction applied at the wrap, cancelling the worst aliasing of a digital saw at low CPU cost - the standard modern virtual-analog saw.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PolyBlepSquare}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PolyBlepSquare}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

PolyBLEP square: a naive variable-width pulse with polynomial band-limited step corrections at both edges, cancelling the dominant aliasing - the standard low-cost virtual-analog square/PWM oscillator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PolyBlepTri}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PolyBlepTri}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

PolyBLEP triangle: a band-limited square (naive square plus polynomial edge corrections) is leaky-integrated into a triangle, giving a clean alias-free triangle with the soft, hollow spectrum a naive ramp triangle aliases away.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PolylogOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PolylogOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Polylogarithm oscillator: sums cos(k*theta)/k^s over the harmonics - the real part of the polylog Li_s(e^{i theta}) - so the Order knob (s) sets the harmonic roll-off continuously, from a bright sawtooth-like spectrum (low s) to a near-pure cosine (high s), band-limited to Nyquist.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PomeauManneville}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PomeauManneville}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Intermittency map: the Pomeau-Manneville map sits near-fixed for long 'laminar' stretches then erupts into a chaotic burst before relaminarizing - quasi-periodic calm interrupted by sudden chaos, a route-to-turbulence texture.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PopcornFractal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PopcornFractal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Popcorn fractal (Pickover): the coupled map x' = x - h*sin(y + tan(3y)), y' = y - h*sin(x + tan(3x)), whose tangent terms scatter the orbit into popping clusters that outline a delicate fractal; the x-coordinate is read as a jittery CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PopGunOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PopGunOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pop gun: a toy cork gun fired, a short hollow resonant pop with a woody body thump, fired at the set rate, the playful cork-pop; the rate sets the firing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PopulationGenetics}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PopulationGenetics}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Population-genetics map: an allele frequency iterated by frequency-dependent selection, where over-strong selection against the common type drives the gene pool into period-doubling and chaotic generation-to-generation swings.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PortaLead}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PortaLead}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Portamento lead: a smooth, gliding monophonic saw lead that slides between notes - the expressive, singing solo voice. Glide sets the slide time, Tone the brightness, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PortalOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PortalOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Portal: an open dimensional rift, a deep hum overlaid with a swirling spectral vortex (a band of noise whose pitch spirals continuously) and a faint otherworldly ring, the gateway-to-another-realm drone; the swirl rate sets the vortex speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PotionBrewOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PotionBrewOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Potion brew: a bubbling cauldron of magical brew, a cluster of rising bubble pops over a watery flow bed sprinkled with tiny glittering sparkle blips, the cute alchemy of mixing a potion; the density sets how vigorously it bubbles.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PourLiquidOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PourLiquidOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pouring liquid: liquid glugging from a bottle or jug, a watery flow bed punctuated by rhythmic resonant glugs whose pitch rises as the vessel empties, the satisfying pour; the glug rate sets the flow.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PourOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PourOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pour: the glugging of liquid pouring from a bottle, a dense random cluster of small rising bubble pops over a soft turbulent flow-noise bed, the sound of filling a glass; density sets how fast it pours.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PowerDistOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PowerDistOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Power-distribution oscillator: each cycle is the power-function PDF shape x^(Alpha-1) on [0,1] (the inverse of the Pareto), a rising ramp whose curvature Alpha controls - linear at Alpha=2, concave below, convex above - a continuously-bendable ramp distinct from a straight saw.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PowerDrillOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PowerDrillOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Power drill: the whine of an electric hand drill, a buzzy motor tone whose pitch sags and steadies as the bit bites and the load fluctuates, the rising-and-falling drone of drilling; Load sets how much the motor bogs down.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PowerLomaxOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PowerLomaxOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Power-Lomax oscillator: each cycle is x^(Beta-1)*(1+x^Beta)^(-Alpha-1), a power transform of the Lomax that combines an adjustable rise (Beta) with a heavy power-law tail (Alpha) - a flexible front-loaded heavy-tailed waveform. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Powerup}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Powerup}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sci-fi power-up / energy charge: a trigger on in0 starts a tone that rises over Time - the pitch sweeps up Range octaves while bright harmonics fade in and a fast shimmer tremolo intensifies toward the peak, the 'charging up' of a weapon, shield or pickup. Tune sets the start pitch, Time the charge duration, Range the octaves it climbs, Shimmer the high-harmonic sparkle and tremolo, and Level the output. Trigger to charge; the energy peaks and holds at the top of the sweep. Distinct from the noise-based Riser - this is a tonal ascending charge.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PowerUpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PowerUpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Power-up: grabbing an upgrade, a fast ascending run of square-wave blips climbing a scale to a bright peak, the rewarding rising flourish of collecting a power-up; the rate sets how often it fires.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PrecessionOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PrecessionOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Precession oscillator: a radial oscillation whose elliptical orbit slowly rotates (apsidal precession, as Mercury's orbit does), giving a rosette-tracing two-frequency wave whose slow precession rate continuously reshapes the waveform cycle to cycle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PredatorPreyCA}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PredatorPreyCA}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spatial predator-prey: a ring of cells that are empty, prey or predator; prey spreads into empty space, predators eat adjacent prey and reproduce, and predators starve where no prey remains - giving the chasing population waves of a spatial Lotka-Volterra system.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PredatorPreyDelay}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PredatorPreyDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Delayed predator-prey: a Lotka-Volterra system whose prey self-limitation acts with a maturation delay; the delay destabilizes the coexistence equilibrium into large-amplitude, irregular boom-bust population cycles.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PredatorPreyMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PredatorPreyMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Predator-prey map: a discrete-generation predator-prey system whose coupled growth and predation terms cycle and, at high growth rates, go chaotic - boom-bust ecology iterated into a jagged stepped source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PredatorRickerMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PredatorRickerMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ricker predator-prey map: a discrete-generation predator-prey system with Ricker (exponential) prey growth and saturating predation, whose coupled over-compensation drives quasi-periodic and chaotic population cycles.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PressureWasherOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PressureWasherOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pressure washer: a power washer blasting water, a high-pressure jet hiss pulsed by the throb of the pump piston, the driveway-cleaning roar; the pump rate sets the pulsation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PrimeAdd}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PrimeAdd}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Prime additive oscillator: sums sine partials only at prime-numbered harmonics (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13), a sparse harmonic series that sounds hollow and faintly inharmonic, distinct from a full saw/square.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PrimeAdditive}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PrimeAdditive}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Additive oscillator that sums only the prime-numbered harmonics (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, ...) with a tunable rolloff - a hollow, woody, clarinet-like spectrum you can't get from saw/square. Partials sets how many primes and Rolloff their decay.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PrimeHarmonicOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PrimeHarmonicOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Prime-harmonic oscillator: stacks sine partials only at the prime harmonic numbers (1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13), skipping every composite overtone, for a hollow, slightly-dissonant spectrum heard nowhere in ordinary harmonic tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PrimeOrgan}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PrimeOrgan}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Prime-harmonic oscillator (a first as a synth block): an additive tone built from the fundamental plus only the PRIME harmonics - the 2nd, 3rd, 5th, 7th, 11th... partials, with the composite ones (4th, 6th, 8th, 9th...) left out entirely. Because every overtone is at a prime multiple, the usual octave and fifth reinforcements are missing, giving a hollow, slightly bell-like organ timbre that no harmonic or subtractive source produces. Partials sets how many prime overtones to stack, alias-free up to Nyquist. A number-theoretic spectrum, distinct from the tilt-shaped Additive oscillator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PrinterOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PrinterOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dot-matrix printer: an old dot-matrix printer at work, a buzzy mid-frequency carriage drone interrupted by rhythmic print-head zips that sweep across each line, the retro-office print sound; the line rate sets the carriage speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ProlateSlepianOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ProlateSlepianOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Prolate-spheroidal (Slepian) pulse: an approximation of the discrete prolate spheroidal sequence, the window that packs the most energy into the smallest bandwidth (the optimal time-frequency-concentration window); the concentration knob sets how tightly the single lobe is focused.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Propeller}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Propeller}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Propeller plane - a chopping blade thrum over a droning piston engine. Rpm sets the blade rate, Tone the engine brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PropellerPlaneOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PropellerPlaneOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Propeller plane: the droning of a piston prop aircraft, a blade-pass tone with strong harmonics beating against the engine firing buzz, the steady drone of a small Cessna; the RPM knob sets the engine speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ProphetVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ProphetVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Prophet-style voice: a sawtooth and a pulse oscillator detuned together through a two-pole filter, the punchy, slightly-gritty Sequential Prophet-5 poly character. Cutoff sets the filter, Reso the resonance, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PseudoVoigtOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PseudoVoigtOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pseudo-Voigt oscillator: each cycle is a linear blend Eta*Lorentzian + (1-Eta)*Gaussian (the spectroscopy line-shape approximation), so Eta morphs continuously between a Gaussian core (soft, fast tails) and a Lorentzian (sharp peak, heavy tails) - one knob across the whole Voigt family. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PsyBass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PsyBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Psytrance rolling bassline: a self-clocking 16th-note sub that retriggers a fast-attack / short-decay amp envelope and a downward filter sweep on every step, the hypnotic driving roll under a psytrance kick. A saw runs through a resonant low-pass whose cutoff starts bright and decays toward the sub each step. Tune sets the pitch (in0 = Pitch CV), Rate the step-clock frequency (tempo-sync it so the steps land on 16ths), Cutoff the filter floor, Reso the squelch, Decay the per-step length, and Level the output. Sidechain it under the kick (Pump / Ducker) for the classic off-kick pump.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PTSymmetric}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PTSymmetric}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

PT-symmetric oscillator: two coupled modes - one with gain, one with matched loss - the parity-time-symmetric system from non-Hermitian physics. Its pitch follows sqrt(coupling^2 - gain^2), so turning Gamma up drives the system toward its 'exceptional point' and the pitch droops smoothly toward zero as the two modes coalesce - a square-root pitch law and a peculiar amplitude fade unlike any detune or glide here. in0 is the Pitch CV (coupling); Gamma sets how close to the exceptional point.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Pulsar}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Pulsar}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pulsar synthesis (Roads): a pulsaret (windowed sine) is emitted once per fundamental period, then silence. Freq sets pitch, Formant the pulsaret cycles, Width the duty.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Pulsar2}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Pulsar2}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pulsar-synthesis oscillator: a single-cycle pulsaret is emitted once per fundamental period then followed by silence, so Width sets a movable formant region independent of pitch - the granular/pulsar tone behind Curtis Roads' synthesis and a range of harsh-to-vocal digital timbres. Freq sets the fundamental, Width the duty (formant), Level the output. in0 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PulsarBeamOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PulsarBeamOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pulsar-beam oscillator: a rotating neutron star sweeps its narrow radio beam past us once per spin, giving a clocklike train of very sharp pulses against silence (the lighthouse model); the duty knob sets how narrow the beam, from a click to a fat blip.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Pulsaret}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Pulsaret}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pulsar synthesis oscillator: at each fundamental period a Hann-windowed sinusoidal grain (the pulsaret) is retriggered, with silence filling the rest of the cycle. Freq sets the fundamental (grain repetition rate), Formant sets the sine frequency inside the grain (formant pitch), Width sets the fraction of the period the grain occupies, and Level scales the output. Narrow widths and high formants give buzzy, formant-rich tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PulsaretOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PulsaretOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pulsaret: pulsar synthesis, where each period holds one short pulsaret waveform followed by a silent gap (Roads), so the period sets the audible fundamental and the pulsaret-to-gap duty sweeps a formant-like resonance through the spectrum.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PulsarSynth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PulsarSynth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pulsar synthesis (Curtis Roads): a short pulsaret waveform of fixed duration is emitted once per (longer) pulsar period followed by silence; the period sets the pitch and the pulsaret duration sets an independent formant, bridging granular and classic synthesis.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PulseGen}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PulseGen}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zero-cross pulse generator: fires a short decaying spike on each rising zero crossing of the input, so it emits a click train locked to the input's pitch - a trigger / excitation source for resonators.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PulseStackOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PulseStackOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Super-pulse: seven pulse waves spread symmetrically around the pitch beat against each other into a thick, hollow, detuned wall - the square-wave counterpart to a supersaw, with PW setting the duty of every layer for anything from reedy to nasal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PulseWidthOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PulseWidthOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pulse-width modulation: a rectangular oscillator whose duty cycle is set (and can be modulated) from a thin spike through a square to its inverse, sweeping the hollow-to-bright timbres of classic PWM string and lead patches.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PupilReflex}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PupilReflex}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pupil light reflex: the pupil area set by retinal light flux through a delayed neural loop (Longtin-Milton model); enough loop gain and delay destabilize it into the spontaneous pupil-size oscillations clinicians can induce with edge-lighting.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PuuBusinessCycle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PuuBusinessCycle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Puu business cycle: Tonu Puu's continuous multiplier-accelerator model where the accelerator is a cubic (van-der-Pol-like) function of income change, self-exciting a bounded economic limit cycle that goes chaotic when forced.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PWM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PWM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pulse-width-modulation oscillator (Juno-style): a single pulse wave whose duty cycle is swept by a slow LFO, so one oscillator breathes and thickens like a chorused string / pad - the classic Juno / Jupiter PWM movement behind hollow synth strings and reedy basses. Tune sets the pitch (in0 = Pitch CV), Rate the PWM LFO speed, Depth how far the width is swept, Sub an octave-down square for weight, and Level the output. Distinct from SuperPWM, which stacks many detuned pulses - this is the single classic PWM voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PWMLead}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PWMLead}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

PWM lead: a pulse-width-modulated square lead with continuous PWM movement for a thick, animated, hollow solo voice. Width sets the duty, Move the PWM depth, Tone the brightness. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PWMOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PWMOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM, Pitch Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pulse-width-modulation oscillator: a variable-width square wave, from a thin nasal pulse to a full square. Width sets the duty cycle and in1 modulates it (patch an LFO for the classic fattening PWM sweep). in2 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PWMSquare}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PWMSquare}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pitch-locked PWM square: an internal square locks to the input pitch and its pulse width is set by a knob, sweeping the hollow-to-nasal PWM timbre while tracking whatever you play.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PWMStrings}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PWMStrings}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

String-machine pad: a stack of pulse-width-modulated squares detuned into a wide ensemble with a gentle highpass, the shimmering Solina/Logan string-synth wash. Cutoff sets the body, Width the PWM motion, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PythagorasTree}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PythagorasTree}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pythagoras tree: the chaos game over the two scaled-and-rotated maps that recursively sprout left and right square branches, building the classic fractal tree; the iterate's height is read out as a branching, self-similar CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:QexponentialOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{QexponentialOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

q-exponential oscillator: each cycle is the Tsallis q-exponential (2-q)*lambda*(1-(1-q)*lambda*x)^(1/(1-q)), which is a pure exponential at q=1, has compact support for q<1, and a heavy power-law tail for q>1 - one knob spanning bounded-decay to fat-tailed decay. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:QGaussOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{QGaussOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

q-Gaussian oscillator: each cycle is a Tsallis q-Gaussian bump (1-(1-q)x^2)^(1/(1-q)) with compact support, so Q morphs the pulse from a parabola-like dome to a sharp Gaussian-ish spike with hard zero edges - a tunable bell-shaped waveform that a plain Gaussian cannot reach.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:QiOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{QiOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Qi attractor: a chaotic flow with strong quadratic cross-coupling that sweeps a wide, fast, multi-lobed orbit, a high-energy strange attractor for aggressive tones and broad modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:QuadMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{QuadMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Quadratic map: the iterated map x -> a - x^2, the additive-form sibling of the logistic map with the same period-doubling cascade into chaos but a symmetric, bipolar output range.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:QuadraticChirpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{QuadraticChirpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Quadratic-chirp oscillator: within each cycle the phase follows t^Power, so the frequency sweeps up as a polynomial rate - gentler at the start than a log chirp and tunable from near-linear (Power~1) to a steep late ramp (Power high). A smooth accelerating glide, repeated at the fundamental.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:QuadrupTwoOrbit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{QuadrupTwoOrbit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Quadruptwo attractor: a Pickover iterated map built from signs, sines and logs that lays out a four-fold-symmetric tracery, a distinctive crystalline chaotic texture from his strange-attractor collection.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Quasar}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Quasar}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Quasar - a pulsing stream of radio-telescope beeps and bursts over cosmic static. Pulse sets the beep rate, Tone the pitch, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:QuasarFlickerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{QuasarFlickerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Quasar flicker: the aperiodic red-noise variability of an accreting supermassive black hole, modelled as heavily-integrated (Brownian) noise so low frequencies dominate, the slow random brightening and dimming of a quasar's accretion disk; the cutoff sets how fast it wanders.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RabinovichOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RabinovichOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rabinovich-Fabrikant attractor: a chaotic flow from plasma-wave physics with cubic nonlinearities, tracing an intricate, spiky orbit very sensitive to its parameters - delicate, edge-of-chaos textures.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RabiOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RabiOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rabi oscillation: the population of a driven two-level quantum system cycling between its states as sin^2(Omega t/2); adding detuning raises the floor and speeds the generalized Rabi frequency, morphing a full-depth raised-cosine into a shallow, faster flutter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RadarChirpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RadarChirpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Radar oscillator: a repeating short linear-FM 'chirp' pulse (the pulse-compression waveform of modern radar that sweeps frequency within each ping) followed by a listening gap; the pulse rate and sweep width set the radar's character.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RademacherOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RademacherOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rademacher function: the sign of sin(2^k * theta), an exact square wave at the chosen power-of-two harmonic and the orthonormal building block of the Walsh-function system; stacking several gives the binary-rate timbres of digital and Walsh synthesis.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RailHunting}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RailHunting}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wheelset hunting: a coned railway wheelset's lateral-and-yaw oscillation driven by creep forces and flange contact; above a critical speed it self-excites into the snaking 'hunting' limit cycle and, with flange impacts, chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rain}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rain}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rain texture: a steady band-passed noise wash (the hiss of falling rain) under randomly-timed resonant droplet 'plinks' - short decaying pings at scattered pitches, the patter of drops on surfaces. Distinct from Fire's broadband crackle: rain is a wet high wash plus pitched droplets. Self-running. Wash sets the steady rain level, Drops the droplet density, Pitch the droplet resonance, Tone the wash brightness, and Level the output. Layer instances for heavier rain; add Thunder for a storm.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
