\phantomsection\label{blk:RainGen}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RainGen}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rain: a Poisson scatter of little resonant water droplets, each a short pitched 'plip' at a random pitch, the density rising with the input level (silence = light drizzle, signal = downpour). A rain-bed generator played by dynamics. Density sets the drop rate, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RainOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RainOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rain oscillator: a dense Poisson shower of tiny filtered-noise droplet impacts whose mean rate the Intensity knob sets, from a few scattered drops to a steady downpour hiss - granular rainfall synthesis with no loops.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RainPad}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RainPad}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Amount}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rain: a steady downpour - a wash of highpassed noise speckled with random droplet ticks. Density sets the droplet rate, Tone the brightness, Level the output. in0 = Amount gate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RaisedCosineOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RaisedCosineOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Raised-cosine pulse: the smooth flat-topped pulse used as the inter-symbol-interference-free shaping filter in digital communications, with a roll-off knob setting how much of the period is flat versus cosine-tapered - from a wide plateau to a single cosine lobe.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RaisedCosOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RaisedCosOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Raised-cosine pulse: a flat-topped pulse with cosine-tapered edges (the Tukey window / comms pulse shape), where Width sets the duty and Taper morphs the edges from a hard rectangle (buzzy) to a soft raised-cosine bump (mellow) - band-limited-feeling shaping from one waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RamaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RamaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rama oscillator: each cycle is the Rama PDF proportional to (1+x^3)e^(-Theta*x), a lifetime distribution whose cubic factor produces the most delayed, pronounced hump of the Lindley family before the exponential decay. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RampCoreOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RampCoreOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ramp-core morph oscillator: one phase ramp derives a saw, a triangle and a pulse, and the Shape knob crossfades continuously across all three (saw -> tri -> pulse) so a single core sweeps the classic analog waveshapes without switching; PW sets the pulse duty.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RandomFibonacci}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RandomFibonacci}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Random Fibonacci: the recurrence x = +/-x_prev +/- x_prev2 with random signs, whose magnitude grows like Viswanath's constant 1.1319... almost surely (a surprising result given the chaotic sign-flipping); tracked as a log-scaled, periodically-renormalized wander.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RanduNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RanduNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

RANDU noise: IBM's infamous 1960s linear-congruential generator (x = 65539*x mod 2^31), notorious because its triples fall on just 15 planes in 3-D; its conspicuous low-order-bit structure makes a deliberately-flawed, lattice-correlated noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RanunculoidOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RanunculoidOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ranunculoid oscillator: the coordinate 6*sin(t) - Shape*sin(6t) from the five-cusped ranunculoid epicycloid, a clean blend of the fundamental and its sixth harmonic - Shape adds a bright, bell-like upper partial without the lower odd harmonics a square or saw would bring.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RatchetOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RatchetOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ratchet: the rapid clicking of a spring-loaded pawl skipping over the teeth of a turning gear (a socket-wrench or noisemaker), a fast train of short broadband clicks whose rate is the tooth-pass frequency.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rattleback}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rattleback}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rattleback: the curved 'celt' top whose skew-symmetric mass distribution converts spin into rocking and back, spontaneously reversing its rotation direction over and over - a real mechanical curiosity, read as the spin axis.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RattlesnakeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RattlesnakeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rattlesnake oscillator: a dry, dense buzz of noise pulses at the rattle's shake rate, the warning sound of interlocking keratin segments knocking together; the shake rate and swell set how agitated the snake.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RayleighDuffing}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RayleighDuffing}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rayleigh-Duffing oscillator: a resonator with both a cubic stiffness (Duffing twin-well) and a velocity-cubed self-pumping (Rayleigh) term under sinusoidal forcing, combining two nonlinear-oscillator routes to chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RayleighNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RayleighNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rayleigh noise: the magnitude of a two-component Gaussian vector (sqrt(-2 ln u)), the distribution of the envelope of narrowband noise and of wind speeds - a strictly-positive, right-skewed source, here centred for audio.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RayleighOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RayleighOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rayleigh self-oscillator: a nonlinear damping term pumps energy into a resonator until it settles on a stable limit cycle, the physics behind self-sustaining reed and bowed tones - a sound that starts itself.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RayleighQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RayleighQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rayleigh-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the Rayleigh inverse-CDF sqrt(-2*ln(1-p)), a concave ramp that rises briskly then bends toward a steep finish - the quantile counterpart of the Rayleigh distribution. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RCLadderOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RCLadderOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

RC phase-shift oscillator: three cascaded RC stages give the 180-degree feedback for a sine oscillator, but a saturating amplifier and extra gain tip the three-pole loop into chaos - a textbook audio oscillator over the edge.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ReciprocalDistOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ReciprocalDistOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Reciprocal oscillator: each cycle is the reciprocal (log-uniform) distribution PDF proportional to 1/x, a smooth hyperbolic decay from a high start to a low tail - a logarithmic-falling ramp distinct from linear, exponential or power-law decays.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ReciprocalNormalOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ReciprocalNormalOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Reciprocal-normal oscillator: each cycle is the PDF of 1/X for a standard normal X, e^(-1/2x^2)/x^2, which is zero at the origin, rises to an off-centre peak, then decays with a heavy tail - a distinctly hollow-then-spiked shape unlike the centre-peaked Gaussian. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Recorder}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Recorder}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Breath, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Recorder: the simple, pure, slightly-breathy whistle of the wooden recorder - a sine with a soft second harmonic and a touch of air, played by holding in0. Breath sets the air noise, Tone the second-harmonic mix, Level the output. in0 = Breath gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RecordScratchOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RecordScratchOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Record scratch: a DJ scratching a record, a tone whose pitch swings rapidly up and down (forward-and-back hand motion) over a faint vinyl rumble, the turntablist 'wikka-wikka', fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ReeseBass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ReeseBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Reese bass: two heavily-detuned sawtooths beating against each other through a resonant lowpass, the growling, metallic drum-and-bass / neurofunk sub-bass. Detune sets the beating rate, Cutoff the filter brightness, Reso the growl. in0 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ReeseSynth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ReeseSynth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Reese bass: two saws detuned just enough to beat against each other, summed and rolled off with a low-pass, the growling, phasing drum-and-bass/neurofunk sub-bass timbre from one block.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RefrigeratorHumOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RefrigeratorHumOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Refrigerator hum: the low background drone of a fridge compressor, a soft mains-derived hum with a couple of harmonics and a faint intermittent rattle, the sound you only notice when it stops; the rattle knob adds mechanical buzz.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RefWhistleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RefWhistleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Referee whistle: the shrill blast of a sports whistle, a high near-sine tone with the fast fluttering warble of the pea rattling inside, blown in sharp bursts; the rate sets the blast pattern.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RelaxationSawOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RelaxationSawOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Relaxation oscillator: a capacitor charges toward a supply through a resistor, then dumps when it hits a threshold - so the ramp is the curved exponential-charge segment of real analog ramp generators, not the straight line of an ideal sawtooth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RelayChatterOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RelayChatterOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Relay chatter: an electromechanical relay rapidly pulling in and dropping out, a buzzing train of hard mechanical clicks at the chatter rate with a faint coil hum, the angry buzz of a relay fed marginal voltage; the rate sets the chatter speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RenyiMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RenyiMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Renyi beta-map: the expanding map x -> beta*x mod 1 for any real beta > 1 (the doubling/tripling maps are the integer cases), reading off the base-beta expansion of the seed - a continuously-tunable deterministic noise source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RepressilatorGene}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RepressilatorGene}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Repressilator: the engineered three-gene loop where each gene's protein represses the next in a ring (Elowitz and Leibler's synthetic clock), producing sustained genetic-circuit oscillations from Hill-function feedback.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ReservoirEcho}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ReservoirEcho}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Echo-state reservoir: a pool of neurons wired with fixed random recurrent weights (derived deterministically from index) and driven by a small input, holding a fading memory of its history; a weighted readout of the reservoir gives a rich, recurrent-but-stable evolving CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ResoComb}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ResoComb}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Noise-excited resonant comb: white noise fed through a tuned feedback comb rings at the pitch, giving a metallic, pitched-noise tone between a flute and a struck pipe. Reso sets the ring time, Tone the brightness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ResonateFire}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ResonateFire}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Resonate-and-fire neuron: a damped sub-threshold oscillator that fires and resets when its rotation crosses threshold, so it responds selectively to inputs near its resonant frequency - a frequency-preferring spiking model.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ResonatorBank}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ResonatorBank}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bank of three tuned resonators excited by the input - strike it with a click or noise and it rings like a struck object, feed it audio and it adds metallic, bell-like resonant body. Freq sets the fundamental, Spread detunes the upper resonators (harmonic to inharmonic), Decay the ring time, Mix the wet amount. A compact modal/Rings-style resonator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ResoSawOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ResoSawOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Resonant-saw oscillator: the Casio CZ 'fake resonance' technique - a sine hard-synced to the fundamental at Reso times its frequency, windowed each cycle by a falling sawtooth ramp, so a sweepable formant peak rides on a sawtooth body. Reso sets the formant frequency in harmonics of the fundamental.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ResoTrapOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ResoTrapOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Resonant-trapezoid oscillator: a CZ-style resonant waveform whose formant sine is held at full level for the first half of each cycle then ramped down - a flat-topped resonance window giving a brighter, more sustained formant than the saw or triangle variants. Reso sets the formant in harmonics of the fundamental.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ResoTriOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ResoTriOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Resonant-triangle oscillator: a CZ-style resonant waveform where the synced formant sine is windowed by a triangle that rises then falls across each fundamental cycle, so the formant swells in the middle of the period - a softer, more vocal resonance than the resonant-saw. Reso sets the formant in harmonics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RespiratoryRhythm}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RespiratoryRhythm}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Respiratory rhythm: a model of the pre-Botzinger complex (the brainstem pacemaker for breathing), where a persistent-sodium current and slow inactivation generate the inspiratory burst-and-pause rhythm, irregular when stressed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RestrictedThreeBody}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RestrictedThreeBody}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Restricted three-body: a massless test particle moving in the gravity of two orbiting masses (Sun-Earth-spacecraft), whose path through the rotating potential is chaotic and threads the Lagrange-point gateways - celestial-mechanics chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ReverseBass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ReverseBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hardstyle / jumpstyle reverse bass: a trigger on in0 (the offbeat gate) restarts a sub that swells up from silence along an accelerating exponential - the 'reversed reverb tail' shape the genre is built on - then is hard-cut by the next trigger so it sits in the gap between kicks. A sine sub blended with a saw is driven through tanh for weight. Tune sets the pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Swell the rise time, Curve the swell shape (higher = slower start, sharper rush at the end), Drive the distortion, and Level the output. Gate it on the off-beats against a kick on the downbeat.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ReverseBeepOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ReverseBeepOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Reverse beeper: the steady, slow square-wave beep of a reversing truck or forklift (a fixed-pitch tone gated on and off about once a second), the ubiquitous back-up warning tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rhodes}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rhodes}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

FM electric piano (DX-style tine EP): a sine carrier phase-modulated by a sine at Ratio, where the modulator index decays fast to give the metallic 'tine' bark on the attack before settling to a mellow near-sine body - the warm electric piano of deep, soulful and lo-fi house. Tune sets the pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Ratio the tine frequency ratio (higher = more bell/bark), Bark the attack FM depth (brightness / velocity), Tine how fast the bark fades, Decay the note length, and Level the output. A gate on in0 strikes the note.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RhodesEP}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RhodesEP}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

FM electric piano: a 1:1 carrier/modulator FM pair with a fast-decaying modulation index produces the bell-like attack and mellow body of a Rhodes/DX tine piano, struck by a gate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RichardsCurveOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RichardsCurveOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Richards-curve oscillator: each cycle is the Richards generalized-logistic growth S, 1/(1+e^(-B(t-0.5)))^(1/Nu), whose asymmetry the Nu parameter controls - the inflection slides off-centre (Nu<1 leans early, Nu>1 late), giving a lopsided S-ramp no symmetric sigmoid produces.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RickerMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RickerMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ricker map: the fisheries population model x -> x e^(r(1-x)), whose over-compensation drives period-doubling into chaos as the growth rate rises - an exponential-feedback route to chaos distinct from the logistic map.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RickerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RickerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ricker / Mexican-hat wavelet: the negative second derivative of a Gaussian, a single positive bump flanked by two symmetric dips, widely used in seismic and edge-detection work; per cycle it makes a soft, DC-free thump with a controllable width.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Ride808}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Ride808}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-808 ride cymbal: a sustained metallic ping - inharmonic squares with a tonal centre and a steady, bell-like decay, more pitched and controlled than the crash. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the ring, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RiemannOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RiemannOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Riemann function: the sum of sin(pi n^2 x)/n^2, Riemann's candidate for a continuous-but-nowhere-differentiable function (differentiable only at special rationals), here pitch-locked as a softly-spiky fractal waveform with quadratic-harmonic content.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RifeVincentOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RifeVincentOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rife-Vincent oscillator: each cycle is the order-2 Rife-Vincent window 1 - (4/3)cos(2*pi*p) + (1/3)cos(4*pi*p), a cosine-sum bell with a wider, flatter main lobe than Hann - a mellow rounded pulse with its own low-harmonic spectral balance.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RijkeTube}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RijkeTube}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rijke tube: a heated gauze in a pipe feeds energy into the air column with a time lag, so the acoustic mode self-amplifies into the loud thermoacoustic howl of a singing flame - here driven through its limit cycle into chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RikitakeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RikitakeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rikitake dynamo: a coupled two-disk dynamo model of the Earth's magnetic field that chaotically reverses polarity, giving a signal that wanders then abruptly flips sign - chaos with dramatic sign reversals.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rim909}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rim909}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-909 rimshot: a short, sharp, slightly-tonal click - a fast high-frequency ping under a bright noise transient, the 909's dry rim. Tune sets the ping pitch, Decay the click length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rimshot}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rimshot}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Analog rimshot (808-style): a trigger on in0 fires a very short burst of two high resonant tones (a bright tap plus a lower ring) with an extremely fast decay for the sharp click of a rim hit. Tune sets the bright tone (the lower ring tracks it), Decay the click length (very short), and Level the output. Patch a Clock, Euclid or sequencer gate into in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rimshot808}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rimshot808}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-808 rimshot / claves: a trigger on in0 fires a very short, sharp pitched click with a tiny resonant ring - the tight 808 rim hit. Tune sets the pitch, Level the output. in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RimshotSynthOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RimshotSynthOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rimshot voice: a very short, sharply-decaying resonant tone with a click attack (the sound of a stick striking the rim and head together), re-triggered at the set rate; bright, dry and percussive.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RingFM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RingFM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ring-modulated FM: a 2-operator FM voice is multiplied by an independent third oscillator, stacking FM sidebands with ring-mod sum/difference tones into a dense, clangorous, metallic spectrum tuned by two ratio knobs - bell and gong timbres a plain FM pair can't reach.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RingInverter}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RingInverter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ring oscillator: three logic inverters in a loop, each a slew-limited tanh stage; the odd-stage feedback self-oscillates and, with an extra cross-coupling and asymmetry, the node voltages tumble chaotically - on-chip oscillator dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RingPair}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RingPair}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two oscillators ring-modulated together: Ratio detunes the second against the first and Mix blends the ring product against the carrier for metallic, clangorous, bell-like inharmonics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RingStackOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RingStackOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ring stack: a carrier sine ring-modulated by a second sine at a tunable ratio, producing the sum-and-difference sidebands of classic ring modulation; non-integer ratios give the clangorous, bell-like inharmonic spectra heard in gongs and sci-fi tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RinzelNeuron}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RinzelNeuron}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rinzel burster: Rinzel's three-variable reduction of a bursting neuron, where a slow recovery current periodically lifts a fast spiking subsystem across its threshold - producing clean square-wave bursts of spikes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Riser}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Riser}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Build-up riser: a trigger on in0 restarts a sweep that rises over Time seconds. White noise is driven through a resonant band-pass whose cutoff climbs while a pitch-rising sine fades in underneath and the whole thing swells in level - the classic pre-drop tension build. Time sets the sweep length, Tone balances the noise against the rising sine, Reso sets the band-pass sharpness, and Level the output. Patch a bar clock or downbeat gate into in0; the swell accelerates toward the drop.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RiserSweepOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RiserSweepOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Riser: the build-up tension sweep before a drop, a noise band and tone that both climb steadily in pitch and intensity to a peak and then reset, the staple transition of EDM; the rate sets the build length.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RissetBell}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RissetBell}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Risset bell: five inharmonic sine partials at Risset's classic frequency ratios, each with its own exponential decay, struck by a gate - additive bell synthesis where the higher partials die first, just like a real bell.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RissetBellOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RissetBellOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Risset bell: the additive bell from Jean-Claude Risset's Introductory Catalogue of Computer-Synthesized Sounds, eleven sustained partials at his published inharmonic ratios (0.56, 0.92, 1.19, 1.7, 2, 2.74, 3, 3.76, 4.07...) - a foundational electronic-music timbre.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RissetDrumOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RissetDrumOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Risset drum: the drum timbre from Risset's catalogue - a cluster of low inharmonic partials mixed with a filtered-noise bed - which fuses into a pitched-yet-percussive membrane tone; the noise knob sets how dry or rattly the skin.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RissetGliss}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RissetGliss}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shepard-Risset glissando: the continuous-frequency cousin of the Shepard tone, where octave-spaced partials slide smoothly and ceaselessly under a fixed Gaussian spectral envelope, giving the illusion of pitch gliding up (or down) forever.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RLDiodeDriven}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RLDiodeDriven}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Driven RL-diode: a resistor-inductor-diode loop driven by a sine source, the circuit in which period-doubling to chaos was first measured in the lab; its junction-capacitance nonlinearity folds the driven current chaotically.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Robot}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Robot}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Robot / computer voice (sci-fi): a pulse source ring-modulated by a low metallic carrier, shaped by a formant band-pass and stepped to a new pitch at Rate (the flat, phoneme-by-phoneme 'robot talk'), then bit-crushed for digital grain - the 1980s computer-voice and droid chatter of film and game sound design. Tune sets the base pitch (in0 = Pitch CV), Rate the speech-step speed (how fast the pitch jumps), Ring the ring-mod carrier (metallic clang), Formant the vowel centre, Crush the bit-grain, and Level the output. Distinct from the Vocoder/RingMod effects - this is a self-contained robotic voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RobotBeepOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RobotBeepOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Robot beeps: the chirpy data-blip language of a friendly robot, a square-wave tone whose pitch hops to a new random step on each fast beep, the expressive bleep-bloop chatter of an astromech droid; the beep rate sets the talking speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RocheBeatOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RocheBeatOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ellipsoidal-variation oscillator: the light curve of a close binary whose mutual tides stretch each star into an egg shape, so we see more surface twice per orbit - a double-humped brightness wave (at twice the orbital rate) with unequal minima, plus the eclipse dips.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RocketLaunchOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RocketLaunchOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rocket launch: the earth-shaking roar of a rocket lifting off, a swelling wall of low broadband noise overlaid with the random crackle of supersonic combustion instabilities, building to full thrust; the build knob sets the ignition swell.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RockPaperScissors}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RockPaperScissors}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Replicator RPS: the evolutionary-game dynamics of a rock-paper-scissors population, which spirals around the equal-mix point in a heteroclinic cycle - the strategy fractions chase each other in slow, ever-lengthening rounds.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RollingPolygon}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RollingPolygon}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rolling-polygon oscillator: the height of the centre of a regular N-sided polygon as it rolls along a flat floor - it pivots on each corner, swinging the centre up along a cosine arc to a peak when a vertex points straight down, then dropping to a sharp CUSP as the next edge slaps flat. The waveform is a string of arcs with hard corners: a triangle (N=3) scallops deeply, while many sides smooth toward a rolling wheel. A cusped geometric wave unlike any sine or triangle here. in0 is the Pitch CV; Sides sets the scallop depth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RoosterCrowOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RoosterCrowOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rooster crow: the cock-a-doodle-doo of a cockerel at dawn, a strident voiced tone stepping through the call's rising-then-held syllables with a buzzy edge, fired at the set rate; the farmyard alarm clock.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RootRaisedCosineOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RootRaisedCosineOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Root-raised-cosine oscillator: each cycle is the RRC pulse (the matched-filter pulse shape of digital comms), a central lobe with symmetric ringing side-lobes whose extent the roll-off Beta controls - a band-limited pulse with controllable ringing distinct from the smoothing windows. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RoseCurveOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RoseCurveOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rose-curve oscillator: the rhodonea r = cos(k*theta) swept around the circle and projected to one axis, whose petal count (k) sets a comb of lobes per cycle - the flower-petal curve rendered as a band-limited waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RosenMorseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RosenMorseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rosen-Morse oscillator: each cycle is the Rosen-Morse potential -Depth*sech^2(x) + Tilt*tanh(x), combining a central well with an asymmetric step so one side ends higher than the other - a well-with-a-bias shape distinct from the symmetric Morse and sech pulses.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RosenzweigMac}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RosenzweigMac}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rosenzweig-MacArthur model: logistic prey with a saturating (Holling type-II) predator; a seasonal forcing of the prey's carrying capacity tips the predator-prey limit cycle into chaos - boom-bust population dynamics as a slow source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RosslerMod}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RosslerMod}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modified Rossler: the Rossler flow with an added cubic damping on the z equation that tames the spike and reshapes the spiral-and-fold into a smoother, rounder attractor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RosslerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RosslerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rossler attractor: integrates the three-equation Rossler flow, which spirals out then folds back, giving a smooth quasi-periodic-to-chaotic signal - a continuous strange attractor as a tone or slow mod source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RosslerPrototype4}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RosslerPrototype4}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rossler prototype-4: one of Rossler's lesser-known prototype systems that produces a screw-type / toroidal chaotic attractor rather than the familiar funnel, giving a smoother, more rotational chaotic orbit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RPSDynamics}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RPSDynamics}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rock-paper-scissors dynamics: three strategies each beating the next in a cycle, evolving by replicator equations so the populations chase each other round and round in a heteroclinic cycle that slows near each corner - the canonical model of cyclic ecological coexistence.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RPSSpatial}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RPSSpatial}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spatial rock-paper-scissors: a ring of cells each holding one of three species where a cell is invaded by an adjacent species that dominates it (rock crushes scissors, etc.); cyclic dominance keeps any species from winning and spins up endless rotating spiral waves of coexistence.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RubberStampOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RubberStampOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rubber stamp: a hand stamp pressed onto a document, a soft dull thunk with a brief inky paper slap, fired at the set rate, the bureaucratic 'approved' sound; the rate sets the stamping.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RucklidgeDouble}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RucklidgeDouble}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Double-diffusive convection: a Rucklidge-type model of fluid driven by two competing density gradients (heat and salt), whose interplay gives slow-fast oscillations and chaos distinct from single-diffusion Lorenz convection.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RucklidgeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RucklidgeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rucklidge attractor: a model of double-diffusive convection whose orbit loops with a slow, rolling motion - a smooth, low-frequency-flavoured chaotic source good for organic modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rule110Osc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rule110Osc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rule 110: the elementary cellular automaton proven Turing-complete, whose 32-cell row evolves complex, glider-laden patterns at the edge of chaos; the centre cell and density read out as an intricate, computation-rich control source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rule150Osc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rule150Osc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rule 150: the additive (left XOR centre XOR right) elementary cellular automaton, an additive rule whose nested triangular patterns differ from Rule 90's - another deterministic, self-similar fractal bit-stream generator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rule184Osc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rule184Osc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rule 184: the traffic-flow cellular automaton where 1s (cars) advance into empty cells, modelling the jam-to-free-flow transition; its conserved density gives drifting platoons of bits - a structured, density-dependent pattern.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rule30Osc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rule30Osc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rule 30: Wolfram's chaotic elementary cellular automaton (used as the random generator inside Mathematica), whose central column passes randomness tests; the 32-cell row evolves each step into a deterministic white-noise-like bit-stream.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rule54Osc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rule54Osc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rule 54: a class-IV elementary cellular automaton on the edge of chaos, whose background of blinkers hosts gliders that collide and interact like particles; the row evolution yields a structured, almost-rhythmic complex bit-stream.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Rule90Osc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Rule90Osc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rule 90: the XOR-of-neighbours elementary cellular automaton that draws the Sierpinski triangle; its 32-cell row evolves each clock step and the cells read out as a self-similar, additive-XOR bit pattern - deterministic fractal noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RulkovMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RulkovMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rulkov map neuron: a discrete fast-slow iterative model that produces spiking and bursting with very few operations, iterated at a chosen rate - cheap, sharp neural dynamics from a 2-D map.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SaddleNodeFlow}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SaddleNodeFlow}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

SNIC oscillator: a system poised at a saddle-node-on-invariant-circle bifurcation, where a slow variable repeatedly drags it just across threshold, firing spikes with an arbitrarily-long, current-tunable interval - the Type-I neural-excitability rhythm.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SaitoHysteresis}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SaitoHysteresis}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Saito hysteresis circuit: a piecewise-linear oscillator whose state is steered by a hysteretic relay (a Schmitt-trigger switching element), producing chaos from a simple linear system plus memory - hysteresis-driven chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SakaryaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SakaryaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sakarya attractor: a symmetric chaotic flow with product cross-terms tracing a twin-lobed butterfly orbit, a Lorenz-cousin strange attractor with its own folding character.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SaltzmanClimate}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SaltzmanClimate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Saltzman ice-age model: three slow climate variables (ice, CO2, ocean) whose nonlinear feedbacks self-sustain the ~100,000-year glacial cycles and can go chaotic - geologic-timescale oscillation as a very slow modulator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SambasFlow}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SambasFlow}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sambas flow: a Sambas-type three-dimensional system engineered with no equilibrium point, so its chaotic attractor is hidden; its single quadratic term and constant offset trace a folded scroll reachable only from special initial states.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sampler}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sampler}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Reads one of the 8 shared SampleStore slots with linear interpolation, advancing a playback position scaled by pitch and the slot/host sample-rate ratio, then scaled by Level. Slot picks the source, Pitch transposes -24 to +24 semitones, Start sets the entry point (0 to 0.99 of the buffer), and Loop chooses whether playback wraps back to Start or stops at the end. Useful for looped wavetable-style sources or one-shot sample playback depending on Loop.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SamuelsonOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SamuelsonOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Samuelson model: the original linear multiplier-accelerator income oscillator made nonlinear by a saturating investment response, so it self-sustains a bounded business-cycle rhythm instead of exploding or decaying.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SandpileSOC}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SandpileSOC}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Self-organized-criticality sandpile: grains drop onto a small lattice that topples whenever a site exceeds its threshold, spilling to neighbours in scale-free avalanches - the Bak-Tang-Wiesenfeld source of 1/f, power-law-distributed bursts.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sandstorm}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sandstorm}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Desert sandstorm - a gritty mid-band noise rush with grains whipping past. Force sets the gust strength, Grit the abrasiveness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sarangi}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sarangi}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sarangi: the bowed short-necked Indian fiddle - gut playing strings bowed in stick-slip motion over ~35 sympathetic strings (a second resonator bank) that ring in sympathy, giving the most vocal, human, halo-rich of all bowed tones. BowForce sets the grip, Sympathetic the resonant shimmer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sarod}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sarod}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sarod: the fretless Indian plucked lute - steel strings stopped against a polished metal fingerboard give a bright, glissando-rich pluck (no frets, so no fret buzz; notes slide) with a long, ringing metallic sustain and a high sympathetic-string shimmer; the deep, resonant counterpart to the sitar. Sympathetic sets the resonant shimmer tap.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Saron}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Saron}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gamelan saron: a struck bronze-bar metallophone with a bright, clangy attack and a short metallic decay - the driving melodic bar of the gamelan. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SaturableLaser}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SaturableLaser}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Passively Q-switched laser: a laser with a saturable absorber that bleaches under high intensity then recovers, dumping the cavity in giant repetitive pulses; coupling the gain, intensity and absorber gives chaotic pulse trains.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SawSwarm}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SawSwarm}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Detuned saw swarm: seven sawtooths spread around a centre frequency by a detune amount sum into the huge, shimmering unison of a supersaw - a self-contained trance lead/pad voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SawSync}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SawSync}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hard-sync sawtooth: a slave saw is reset every time the master cycle completes, so raising Ratio above 1 forces the bright, vocal, formant-rich sweep of oscillator hard-sync. Freq sets the master pitch, Ratio the slave-to-master frequency. in0 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SawtoothMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SawtoothMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sawtooth map: an area-preserving twist map whose kick is a linear sawtooth rather than a sine (the linearized standard map), giving uniformly-hyperbolic, fully-chaotic mixing across the whole phase plane.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sax}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sax}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Saxophone voice: a gate on in0 sounds a reedy harmonically-rich tone (a soft sawtooth) with vibrato - honking low, singing high. Freq sets the note, Tone the brightness and reediness (number of harmonics), Vibrato the waver, Level the output. Hold the gate to sustain; in1 is the Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SaxMultiphonic}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SaxMultiphonic}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Saxophone multiphonic: a single reed feeding two simultaneously-resonant bore modes (cross-fingered) whose nonlinear flow valve makes the two pitches beat, lock and split into the gritty multiphonic chords saxophonists growl.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SaxWGOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SaxWGOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Saxophone waveguide: a single-reed bore whose conical shape gives a non-inverting reflection and therefore the full harmonic series (brighter and reedier than the odd-only clarinet); breath pressure drives it from a soft tone to a honking growl.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Saz}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Saz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Saz (baglama): the Turkish/Anatolian long-neck lute - thin metal courses over a buzzing bridge give a bright, nasal, buzzing pluck, and the low drone strings ring under the melody; the driving voice of Anatolian and Alevi music. Buzz sets the bridge rattle, Drone the open-string ring.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Scanned}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Scanned}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Scanned synthesis (Mathews/Verplank): a 16-mass spring ring evolves slowly after a gate hammer-strike; a scan reads the mass positions at audio rate as the waveform. Tension/Damping shape the timbre's evolution.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ScannedString}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ScannedString}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Scanned synthesis: a ring of masses connected by springs vibrates at sub-audio rates while a scan pointer reads its evolving shape as the waveform, so the timbre morphs continuously with the string's slow dynamics (Verplank/Mathews).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ScannedSynth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ScannedSynth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Scanned synthesis (Verplank-Mathews-Shaw): a ring of masses joined by springs vibrates in slow, hand-shapeable low modes while a pointer scans around the ring at audio rate to read the waveform - the wave shape itself evolves dynamically as the string sloshes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ScatterGrainOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ScatterGrainOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Scatter grain: a grain stream whose onsets are randomly jittered around the nominal grain rate, smearing a steady pulse into a loose, organic spatter of grains; the jitter knob takes it from a tight stream to a chaotic scatter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SchellingSeg}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SchellingSeg}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Schelling segregation: two-type agents on a ring relocate when too few of their neighbours match them, so even a mild same-type preference cascades into fully-segregated blocks (the classic emergent-segregation result); the fraction of content agents climbs as domains coalesce.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SchmittOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SchmittOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Relaxation oscillator: an integrator charges toward a rail until a Schmitt trigger flips it, ramping the other way - the asymmetric ramp/square waveform of an RC relaxation oscillator core.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Schnakenberg}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Schnakenberg}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Schnakenberg reaction: a minimal two-variable trimolecular autocatalysis model (a standard Turing-pattern kinetics) whose activator-substrate feedback gives smooth chemical limit-cycle oscillations.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SchoolBellOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SchoolBellOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

School bell: a resonant metal bell tone rapidly re-struck by a buzzing clapper (an electric bell's solenoid hammer), giving the loud, continuous, slightly-rattly ring that signals the end of class; the clapper rate sets the buzz.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SchumannResonanceOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SchumannResonanceOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Schumann resonance: the standing electromagnetic waves in the cavity between the Earth's surface and the ionosphere, whose harmonic series (7.83, 14.3, 20.8, 27.3, 33.8 Hz ratios) is summed here as the planet's natural 'heartbeat' rendered as an audio tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
