\phantomsection\label{blk:SchusterMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SchusterMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Intermittency map: a quadratic-tangent map sitting just past a saddle-node bifurcation, so the orbit creeps slowly through the reinjection channel (laminar phase) then bursts chaotically - Schuster's textbook type-I intermittency.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ScreamLead}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ScreamLead}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Screaming lead: a heavily-driven saw forced through a sweeping resonant formant for a vocal, howling, distorted solo lead. Scream sets the formant sweep, Drive the distortion, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ScrollTickOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ScrollTickOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Scroll tick: the tiny tick of a scroll-wheel detent or picker increment, a very short high resonant blip repeated at the set rate, the subtle UI feedback of moving through a list.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Seagulls}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Seagulls}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Seagulls - periodic descending squawk-cries over a faint shore wash for a coastal ambience. Rate sets the call frequency, Pitch the cry register, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SecantFractal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SecantFractal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Secant fractal: the derivative-free secant root-finding iteration on z^3-1, which uses the previous two iterates instead of a tangent; its irrational (golden-ratio) convergence order paints fractal basins distinct from Newton's, read out as a CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SechCubedOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SechCubedOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sech-cubed oscillator: each cycle is sech^3(x), a smooth bump narrower and more sharply-peaked than plain sech but with the same soft exponential tails - a focused, click-free pulse with a distinct harmonic balance between the sech and Gaussian shapes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SechPulseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SechPulseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sech pulse: each cycle is a single smooth hyperbolic-secant bump (the soliton / 1-over-cosh shape), giving a rounded, click-free pulse whose width sets the harmonic richness - from a near-sine swell to a sharp bipolar spike, all without discontinuities.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SeesawOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SeesawOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Seesaw: a playground seesaw tipping up and down, a dry pivot squeak that rises and falls in pitch as the weight shifts across the fulcrum, the see-saw rhythm; the rate sets the tipping speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SEIRSeasonal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SEIRSeasonal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Seasonal SEIR epidemic: a susceptible-exposed-infected-recovered model (adding an incubation stage to SIR) under seasonal transmission, whose extra delay deepens the chaotic, multi-peaked outbreak dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SeismicChirpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SeismicChirpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Seismic chirp: a repeating glide that sweeps from a low rumble up through a faster shake, mimicking the arrival of fast compressional P-waves followed by the larger, lower shear S-waves of an earthquake on a seismogram - a quake rendered as a looping chirp.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SelkovOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SelkovOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Selkov oscillator: the two-variable model of glycolytic oscillations in living cells (x'=-x+ay+x^2 y, y'=b-ay-x^2 y), a smooth biochemical limit cycle whose period and shape shift with the feed parameters.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SemiconductorLaser}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SemiconductorLaser}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Semiconductor laser: the carrier-photon rate equations with nonlinear gain saturation; under current modulation the relaxation-oscillation resonance period-doubles into the spiking chaos seen in directly-modulated laser diodes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SemicubicalOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SemicubicalOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Semicubical oscillator: each cycle is Neile's semicubical parabola sign(x)*|x|^1.5, an odd power-law curve sitting between a triangle (exponent 1) and a parabola (exponent 2) - a soft ramp with a gently flattened centre and a 1.5-power cusp, a distinct curvature no integer-power wave has.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SerpentineOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SerpentineOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Serpentine oscillator: each cycle is Newton's serpentine curve x/(x^2+1), an odd rational bump that rises to a peak then decays with long tails - a smooth, sine-adjacent waveform with a rounder centre and flatter extremes, sharpening as Width grows.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SewingMachineOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SewingMachineOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sewing machine: the steady whir of an electric sewing-machine motor under a rhythmic needle-punch thump once per stitch, the domestic chug of stitching fabric; the stitch rate sets how fast it sews.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Shaker}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Shaker}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shaker / maraca: a trigger on in0 fires a soft burst of band-passed noise with a quick 'tsh' envelope - the egg-shaker and maraca sound, all air and no pitch. Tone sets the noise band (low and dark to high and crisp), Decay the burst length, Level the output. Needs only a trigger; chain two at offset times for a swung shaker groove.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Shakuhachi}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Shakuhachi}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Breath, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Japanese shakuhachi: a breathy end-blown bamboo flute with a strong air component and expressive pitch bends - a sine wrapped in heavy breath noise with a subtle attack bend, played by holding in0. Breath sets the air, Bend the attack pitch slide, Level the output. in0 = Breath gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Shamisen}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Shamisen}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Japanese shamisen: a plucked three-string lute with a percussive snap and the buzzing 'sawari' resonance at the nut - a Karplus string with a nonlinear bridge that adds the signature gritty buzz. Decay sets the ring, Buzz the sawari amount, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShankerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShankerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shanker oscillator: each cycle is the Shanker PDF proportional to (Theta+x)e^(-Theta*x), a lifetime distribution where the same Theta sets both the linear lift and the decay - giving a tightly-coupled rise/fall whose whole shape scales with one knob. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShannonWaveletOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShannonWaveletOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shannon wavelet: the sinc-derived wavelet whose spectrum is a perfect band-pass brick wall (the time-domain dual of the ideal filter), giving a ringing, sidelobe-rich waveform that is the sharpest-possible band-limited packet.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShawVdp}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShawVdp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Forced van der Pol (Shaw): a self-oscillating van der Pol resonator driven by an external sine forces it through entrainment, quasi-periodicity and chaos as the drive grows - a textbook nonlinear-oscillator route to chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SheepBaaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SheepBaaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sheep baa: the wavering bleat of a sheep, a voiced 'baa' vowel with a fast nervous vibrato (the trembling flock sound) that fades, fired at the set rate; the sound of a hillside of sheep.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShepardChordOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShepardChordOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shepard chord: a chord whose every note is octave-doubled across the whole spectrum under a fixed bell-shaped loudness window, so the chord has no clear octave register - the harmonic counterpart of the Shepard tone, an endlessly-thick pad.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShepardOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShepardOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shepard-Risset tone: seven octave-spaced partials under a fixed bell-shaped spectral window whose centre slides continuously, creating the auditory illusion of a pitch that rises (or falls) forever without ever leaving its range.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShepardTone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShepardTone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shepard tone: seven octave-spaced sines all glide upward together while a fixed bell-shaped spectral window fades in the new low octave and fades out the top, so the pitch seems to rise forever without ever getting higher - the auditory barber-pole.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShermanBurst}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShermanBurst}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sherman beta-cell: a model of insulin-secreting pancreatic cells where a slow gating variable periodically switches fast spiking on and off, producing the square-wave bursting (and chaos) of electrical activity in islet cells.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShieldHumOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShieldHumOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shield hum: the steady protective hum of a raised energy shield, a stack of harmonics gently pulsing in amplitude with a slow breathing throb and a faint high resonance, calmer and purer than the angry force field; the throb sets the shield's heartbeat.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Shilnikov}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Shilnikov}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shilnikov chaos: a flow with a saddle-focus equilibrium whose spiralling-out and looping-back form a homoclinic orbit; the Shilnikov theorem guarantees chaos nearby, giving bursts of growing spiral oscillation that reinject - a canonical route to chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShimizuOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShimizuOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shimizu-Morioka attractor: a symmetric Lorenz-like system with a simpler quadratic structure, giving a clean butterfly orbit - a tidy strange attractor for bell-like chaotic tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShinrikiOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShinrikiOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shinriki oscillator: a classic chaotic electronic circuit (a negative-resistance LC oscillator loaded by a nonlinear diode pair), one of the earliest experimentally-demonstrated chaos circuits, with a cubic-like conductance fold.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShipHornOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShipHornOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ship's horn: the deep mournful blast of an ocean-liner foghorn, a very low fundamental stacked with strong harmonics blaring in long held notes with gaps, the call that carries for miles across water; the blast rate sets the signal pattern.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ShipRoll}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ShipRoll}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ship-roll capsize model: a vessel rolling in beam waves, whose restoring moment softens and vanishes past the angle of vanishing stability; near that escape it rolls chaotically before capsizing - a nonlinear naval-architecture oscillator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Shoot}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Shoot}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Laser shot - a fast downward pitch-zap with a bright click, the arcade blaster, auto-repeating. Rate sets the fire rate, Pitch the start frequency, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Shriek}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Shriek}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Shriek - a piercing high dissonant scream of two close beating tones smeared with noise. Pitch sets the height, Tone the rasp, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SibilantOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SibilantOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sibilant oscillator: noise pushed into the high sibilance band (4-10 kHz) where the hiss of s and z lives, with an optional low voiced buzz for z versus s; the brightness knob slides the energy up toward a piercing 's'.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SidechainPumpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SidechainPumpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sidechain pump: the breathing 'pump' of a pad ducked by a kick-driven sidechain compressor, a tone whose volume drops sharply on each beat and swells back, the rhythmic heartbeat of house and EDM; the pump rate sets the tempo.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SIDPulseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SIDPulseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

SID pulse: the Commodore 64 SID chip's variable-width pulse blended with a touch of its sawtooth (the chip's 'combined waveform' trick), giving the buzzy, hollow PWM lead and bass voice that defined 8-bit demo and game music.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SIDVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SIDVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Commodore 64 SID oscillator: a gritty saw/pulse blend with optional ring modulation against a sub-octave - the lo-fi 8-bit chiptune-bass voice. Shape morphs saw to pulse, Ring sets the ring-mod against the sub, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SierpinskiChaosGame}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SierpinskiChaosGame}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sierpinski chaos game: repeatedly jump halfway from the current point toward a randomly-chosen triangle vertex; the orbit fills the Sierpinski gasket, the simplest demonstration that randomness plus contraction yields a deterministic fractal - the x-coordinate is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SigmaDeltaChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SigmaDeltaChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sigma-delta chaos: a second-order one-bit sigma-delta modulator fed a constant input produces a deterministic yet chaotic +/-1 bit-stream (the idle-tone/limit-cycle problem that plagues real converters) - structured digital noise from a feedback quantizer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sigmoid}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sigmoid}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sigmoid sine-to-square oscillator (a first as a synth block): takes a pure sine and pushes it through a tanh sigmoid, so as Shape rises the sine's curves are progressively squashed flat - rounded at low Shape, then a soft-clipped near-square, then a hard square at the top. One knob sweeps the entire continuum from sine to square WITHOUT the abrupt harmonic jumps of switching waveforms or the aliasing of a raw square, because the edges stay smoothly rounded. A continuous waveform morph distinct from the fixed geometric shapes. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SilvermanOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SilvermanOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Silverman oscillator: each cycle is Silverman's kernel 0.5*e^(-|x|/sqrt2)*sin(|x|/sqrt2+pi/4), an exponentially-damped oscillating bump that dips into negative side-lobes before settling - a higher-order kernel with real ripple, distinct from the strictly-positive smoothing kernels. Width sets how many lobes appear.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Simmons}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Simmons}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Simmons SDS electronic tom: a sine with a steep downward pitch sweep plus a noise click - the unmistakable 80s 'pew/dooo' electronic drum. Tune sets the start pitch, Sweep the pitch-drop amount, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SinaiMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SinaiMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sinai map: the Arnold cat map perturbed by a cosine coupling on the torus, a hyperbolic toral automorphism whose mixing scrambles the two coordinates into hard modular-sawtooth chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SincSquaredOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SincSquaredOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sinc-squared oscillator: each cycle is (sin(pi*x)/(pi*x))^2, the Fraunhofer single-slit diffraction intensity - a bright central lobe with strictly-positive, fast-decaying side-lobes - distinct from the bipolar sinc and the periodic Laue grating function. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SineCircleMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SineCircleMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sine-circle map: a phase that advances by a fixed rotation plus a sine coupling, the canonical model of mode-locking, Arnold tongues and the quasiperiodic-to-chaos transition - tunable from locked to chaotic.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SineCubedOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SineCubedOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sine-cubed oscillator: sin^3(theta), which expands to (3*sin(theta) - sin(3*theta))/4 - a clean fixed blend of the fundamental and a phase-inverted third harmonic, giving a slightly pinched, hollow sine with a single low overtone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SineMapOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SineMapOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sine map: the iterated map x -> r*sin(pi*x), a transcendental analogue of the logistic map with the same period-doubling road to chaos but a smoother, rounder bifurcation structure.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SinghFlow}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SinghFlow}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Singh flow: a Singh-type three-dimensional system constructed without any equilibrium point (a hidden attractor) using a single product nonlinearity and a constant term, traced here as a folded chaotic scroll.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Siren}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Siren}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rave siren oscillator: a tone whose pitch glides up and down between Low and High at Rate - the hardcore/jungle air-raid siren. Low and High set the pitch range, Rate sets how fast the pitch sweeps through the cycle, Wail skews the sweep from a smooth triangle glide toward a fast-rise / slow-fall ramp, and Level the output. A self-running source - no input needed; the glide is exponential so it tracks musically across the range.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SirenOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SirenOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Siren: a tone whose pitch sweeps up and down in a continuous wail (the rise-and-fall of an emergency siren), or, at fast rates, the rapid yelp mode; the sweep rate and depth set police-versus-air-raid character.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SIRSpatial}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SIRSpatial}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spatial SIR: a ring of cells that are susceptible, infected or recovered; infection spreads to neighbours probabilistically, the infected recover after a step, and immunity slowly wanes back to susceptible - so epidemic waves sweep round and recur. Infected fraction is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sitar}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sitar}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Plucked sitar: a Karplus-Strong string whose bridge adds the buzzing 'jawari' overtone shimmer through a nonlinear waveshaper in the feedback loop - the droning, sympathetic-buzz character of the Indian sitar. Tune sets the pitch, Buzz the bridge nonlinearity, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SitarDrone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SitarDrone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sitar/tanpura resonator: a plucked-string delay loop whose feedback passes through a curved bridge that flattens the wave peaks (the jawari), generating the shimmering, buzzing, harmonic-rich drone of a sitar bridge.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SitarOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SitarOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sitar: a plucked string whose vibration grazes the curved jawari bridge, adding a buzzing, shimmering nonlinearity that throws off rich high harmonics, the unmistakable twang of a sitar; re-plucked at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sitnikov}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sitnikov}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sitnikov problem: a light body oscillating along the axis through two equal masses on elliptical orbits; the periodically-changing gravity makes its vertical motion chaotic - a clean celestial-mechanics chaos with bursts and dwells.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SixOpFM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SixOpFM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Six-operator FM: three modulator-carrier pairs summed (a DX7-style parallel algorithm), for thick, layered FM pads, basses and electric pianos. Spread detunes the three pairs, Index the FM depth, Decay the index falloff. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SizzleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SizzleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sizzle: food frying in a hot pan, a continuous high filtered-noise hiss sprinkled with random sharp oil-spatter crackles, the appetising sound of cooking; density sets how vigorously it fries.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SkewBaker}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SkewBaker}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Skew baker's map: the dough-kneading baker's map with unequal-width strips, so it stretches and stacks asymmetrically into a multifractal attractor with a non-uniform invariant measure - lopsided mixing chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SkewLaplaceOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SkewLaplaceOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Asymmetric-Laplace oscillator: a two-sided exponential peak whose left and right sides decay at independent rates (Left, Right), so the cycle is a lopsided tent - sharp on one flank, gradual on the other. Unlike the symmetric Laplace, the asymmetry adds even harmonics and a tilted spectral character. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SkewNormalOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SkewNormalOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Skew-normal oscillator: each cycle is the skew-normal PDF 2*phi(x)*Phi(Skew*x), a Gaussian bump leaned to one side by the Skew parameter (negative leans left, positive right) - a continuously asymmetrical bell, peak-normalized and bounded, distinct from the symmetric generalized-normal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlashOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlashOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slash oscillator: each cycle is the slash distribution PDF (phi(0)-phi(x))/x^2 - a symmetric bump with a Gaussian-like centre but very heavy, polynomially-decaying tails - peak-normalized to a bounded waveform that sits spectrally between a Gaussian and a Lorentzian.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SleetOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SleetOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sleet: a cold mix of rain and ice, soft wet droplet ticks interspersed with harder, brighter frozen-pellet clicks, the rattly in-between of a freezing rain; the mix knob shifts it from rain toward ice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sleighbells}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sleighbells}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sleigh bells: a bright cluster of jingling metal bells shaken together - high inharmonic metallic noise with a fast attack and a shimmering tail. Tone sets the brightness, Decay the shimmer, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Slenthem}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Slenthem}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Gamelan slenthem: the lowest, softest tube-resonated metallophone - a mellow, round low bar with a gentle attack and a smooth decay. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlideWhistleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlideWhistleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slide whistle: the comedy swoop of a slide whistle, a pure tone that glides smoothly up then back down in pitch (the plunger sliding), the staple of cartoon falls and rises; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlinkyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlinkyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slinky: a metal coil spring sproinging, a shimmering cluster of metallic partials that glide downward in pitch with a fluttering coil resonance, fired at the set rate, the toy-spring boing; the rate sets the sproings.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlitDrum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlitDrum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slit/tongue drum: two woody, moderately-damped inharmonic modes ring when struck, the warm, hollow, pitched knock of a wooden tongue drum or log drum - excite with an impulse or gate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SloshingTank}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SloshingTank}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sloshing tank: liquid in a horizontally-rocked container whose surface mode is parametrically pumped, giving the chaotic swash of fuel tanks and tuned-liquid dampers - two coupled sloshing modes exchanging energy nonlinearly.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlurpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlurpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slurp: the noisy gurgle of sucking the last of a drink through a straw, a bubbling tone rising in pitch as the liquid runs out, choked with air-and-liquid noise, fired at the set rate; the rude end of a milkshake.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SmaleHorseshoe}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SmaleHorseshoe}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Smale horseshoe: the canonical stretch-fold-and-reinsert map that Smale proved contains a full shift on symbols - the geometric heart of why deterministic systems are chaotic, iterated here as a fractal-mixing source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SmokeAlarmOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SmokeAlarmOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Smoke alarm: a piercing ~3 kHz tone gated in the standardized Temporal-3 pattern - three half-second beeps, a pause, repeat - the internationally-mandated fire-alarm evacuation signal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SmootherStepOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SmootherStepOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Smootherstep oscillator: each cycle is Perlin's smootherstep 6t^5-15t^4+10t^3, a quintic S-ramp with zero first AND second derivatives at both ends - even flatter shoulders and a more central slope than the cubic smoothstep, a very mellow soft sawtooth with minimal edge harmonics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SN76489Osc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SN76489Osc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

SN76489 PSG: the programmable sound generator in the SEGA Master System, BBC Micro and many arcades, whose 10-bit frequency divider quantizes pitch ever more coarsely as it rises - a clean square with the chip's characteristic high-note detuning.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Snare}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Snare}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Analog snare drum: a trigger on in0 fires two detuned body tones plus a burst of band-passed noise (the snare wires). Tune sets the body pitch, Snap the noise level, Tone the body/noise balance, Decay the length, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Snare606}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Snare606}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-606 snare drum: a thin, buzzy snare - a short tonal body under a bright burst of highpassed noise, cheaper and more lo-fi than the 808, the gritty Drumatix backbeat. Tone sets the body-vs-noise balance, Decay the length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Snare707}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Snare707}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-707 snare drum: a tight, bright, slightly-digital snare - a short tonal body under a crisp highpassed noise crack, the 707's punchy backbeat. Tone sets the body-vs-noise balance, Decay the length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Snare808}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Snare808}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-808 snare drum: a trigger on in0 fires two detuned tone oscillators (the 808's shell body) plus a burst of filtered noise (the snappy wires), each with its own decay. Snappy balances the tonal body against the noise, Decay sets the length, Level the output - the dry, snappy 808 backbeat.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Snare909}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Snare909}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-909 snare drum: brighter and noisier than the 808 - a short tonal thwack under a long crack of highpassed white noise, the punchy house/techno snare. Tone sets the body-vs-noise balance, Decay the noise length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SnareRoll}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SnareRoll}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Build-up snare roll: while the gate on in0 is held, fires band-passed noise hits (snare / clap) at a rate that accelerates from Start to End over the Accel ramp - the pre-drop EDM snare roll that speeds up into the drop. Start and End set the hit rate at the beginning and end of the build (End higher = it speeds up), Accel the ramp time, Tone the noise brightness, Decay the per-hit length, and Level the output. Hold the gate over the build bar and release it at the drop.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SnareSynth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SnareSynth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Synth snare drum: a gate fires a short tuned tone mixed with a noise burst (the 'snares'), each with its own decay - a self-contained drum-machine snare voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SnareSynthOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SnareSynthOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Snare-drum voice: two detuned tone oscillators for the drum body mixed with a burst of noise for the snare wires, re-triggered at the set rate; the snappy knob balances the dry thump against the rattly hiss.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SneezeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SneezeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sneeze: the two-part 'ah-CHOO', a rising breathy tension that builds and then releases in an explosive burst of bright turbulent noise, fired at the set rate; the involuntary nasal blast.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Snore}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Snore}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Snoring - a rhythmic buzzy growl on the in-breath with a softer snort on the out-breath. Rate sets the breath pace, Tone the rasp, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SnoreOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SnoreOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Snore: the rattling buzz of snoring, a low growling tone amplitude-modulated by the soft-palate flutter that swells once per breath, the comical sawing of deep sleep; the rate sets the breathing speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SnowflakeReiter}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SnowflakeReiter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Reiter snowflake: a reaction-diffusion cellular automaton where water vapour diffuses across cells and freezes onto the crystal wherever it borders ice, growing the branching dendrites of a snowflake; this radial section reads out the advancing, ever-thickening growth front.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SoftSync}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SoftSync}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Soft-sync oscillator: when the master completes a cycle it reverses the slave saw's direction of travel rather than hard-resetting its phase, so the slave folds back on itself - the gentler, more harmonically complex cousin of hard sync that adds even harmonics and a hollow, reedy formant sweep as Ratio is detuned. Freq is the master, Ratio the slave's relative pitch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SolarRadioSweepOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SolarRadioSweepOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Solar radio sweep: a type-III solar radio burst, where electrons flung from a flare race outward through the corona emitting at ever-lower plasma frequencies, drawing a fast high-to-low diagonal streak; repeated bursts give the crackling sweep of an active Sun.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SolarWind}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SolarWind}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Solar wind - a vast shifting cosmic-noise drone with a faint pitched whistle drifting through it. Flux sets the movement, Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sonar}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sonar}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Submarine sonar - a clean periodic ping with a long ringing tail. Rate sets the ping interval, Pitch the ping frequency, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SonarPing}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SonarPing}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sonar: emits a periodic submarine ping - a descending sine chirp followed by its own fading echo return - on a slow clock, ducking the dry signal underneath. A ready-made depth/suspense bed. Period sets the seconds between pings, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SonarPingOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SonarPingOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sonar oscillator: a clean tonal ping repeated at a slow interval, each fading into a decaying echo tail as the sound returns from the deep, the unmistakable submarine 'ping... ping...'; the interval and pitch set the vessel and depth.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SparkOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SparkOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Electric spark: random sharp broadband crackle bursts (the snapping of an electric arc jumping a gap), each a very short high-passed noise transient; the density knob ramps from an occasional pop to a continuous frying arc.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SpatialPrisoners}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SpatialPrisoners}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spatial prisoner's dilemma (Nowak-May): cooperators and defectors on a ring each play their neighbours, then copy the strategy of whichever neighbour scored highest; cooperation survives in shifting clusters instead of dying out - the cooperation fraction ebbs and flows.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SpectralTiltOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SpectralTiltOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spectral-tilt oscillator: a complete harmonic series whose partial amplitudes follow a single tunable tilt, sweeping continuously from a dark sine-like tone (steep down-tilt) through sawtooth to a bright, buzzy up-tilted spectrum - one knob across the brightness axis.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Speech}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Speech}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vowel/speech synthesizer (Mutable Instruments / SAM-style): a buzzing saw source is shaped by three formant band-passes tuned to vowel sounds, so it sings. Vowel morphs through A-E-I-O-U; Speak, when above zero, auto-advances the vowel at that rate for talking/chanting textures; Tune sets the pitch. A formant voice for choirs, robots and vocal pads. Distinct from the Formant filter (which colours external audio) and Vosim (a single grain pair) - this sweeps a vowel sequence over its own oscillator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SpellCastOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SpellCastOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spell cast: the magical flourish of casting a spell, a cluster of detuned partials sweeping upward in pitch and sparkle with a shimmering tremolo, building to a release; the rate sets how often the spell fires.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SphericalPendulum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SphericalPendulum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spherical pendulum: a pendulum free to swing in two angles under a driving force, whose azimuthal and polar motions couple nonlinearly into chaotic, precessing, never-repeating swings.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SpinningTopOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SpinningTopOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spinning top: a toy top spinning on a table, a whirring hum that rises in pitch as it speeds up then wobbles and wavers as it loses momentum, the hypnotic top spin; the cycle rate sets how often it is spun.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SpinTorque}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SpinTorque}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spin-torque oscillator: a nanomagnet whose magnetization is driven into steady precession by a spin-polarized current (Slonczewski torque against Gilbert damping), and into chaos under field modulation - the heart of spintronic RF oscillators.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Spire}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Spire}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spire-style multimode oscillator: sums up to 9 unison voices, each rendered by the selected Mode (Classic saw, Noise, FM, AMSync, SawPWM, HardFM, Vowel) with the sum normalized by the voice count. Mode picks the algorithm, Shape is the per-mode control (FM index, sync ratio, pulse width, or vowel position), Voices sets unison count (up to 9, matching Spire), Detune the fine +/-3% spread, and Interval fans the voices out over a musical interval (0-12 semitones, e.g. octave/fifth stacks). The Vowel mode adds three morphing formant band-passes over the summed saw for talking-synth timbres.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SpirographOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SpirographOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spirograph oscillator: the hypotrochoid traced by a pen held inside a small gear rolling within a ring, whose radius ratio and pen offset weave the looping rosette patterns of the toy; the x-coordinate makes an intricate multi-loop waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SplashOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SplashOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Splash: an object hitting water, a sharp broadband impact transient that decays into a brief scatter of high spray-droplet noise, fired at the set rate; the cannonball plunge and its spray.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SplitMixNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SplitMixNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

SplitMix noise: advances a Weyl counter by a fixed odd constant and runs it through a strong xor-shift-multiply mixing finalizer (the SplitMix algorithm), giving fast, well-distributed white noise with a long period and good statistics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SpringSproingOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SpringSproingOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spring sproing: the twangy metallic 'sproing' of a released coil spring, a cluster of inharmonic partials with a fast downward pitch glide and a buzzy rattle, the cartoon spring-launch; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sprott2DQuadratic}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sprott2DQuadratic}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott 2-D quadratic map: a general two-variable map whose update is a full quadratic polynomial (the family Sprott computer-searched for elegant chaotic attractors), tuned here to one of its strange-attractor parameter sets.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottBOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottBOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case I flow: one of Sprott's catalogue of algebraically-simplest chaotic systems (dx=-0.2y, dy=x+z, dz=x+y^2-z), a clean low-cost strange attractor with its own folding character.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottConsA}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottConsA}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott-style minimal flow A: a near-conservative three-term chaotic system (dx=y, dy=-x+yz, dz=1-y^2 scaled) that preserves phase-space volume almost exactly, so its chaos has the dense, non-attracting texture of a conservative system.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottConsB}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottConsB}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott-style minimal flow B: a second near-conservative chaotic system (dx=yz, dy=x-y, dz=1-xy variant with weak damping) whose orbit fills its phase space densely, giving a noisy, non-settling chaotic texture.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottConsC}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottConsC}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott-style minimal flow C: a third low-dissipation chaotic system (dx=y, dy=z, dz=-x-y^2+ small drive) that nearly conserves phase-space volume, giving the dense, space-filling texture of conservative chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottConsD}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottConsD}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott-style minimal flow D: a fourth low-dissipation chaotic system (dx=-y, dy=x+z, dz=x z + 3 y^2, weakly damped) whose volume contraction is tiny, giving the dense, fat, space-filling orbit characteristic of near-conservative chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottCOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottCOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case C flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=yz, dy=x-y, dz=1-x^2) closely related to but distinct from case B, giving a compact, smoothly-folding attractor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottD}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottD}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case D flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=-y, dy=x+z, dz=xz+3y^2) whose orbit spirals through a tilted attractor, another distinct entry in Sprott's elegant-equations catalogue.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottEOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottEOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case E flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=yz, dy=x^2-y, dz=1-4x) whose squared term gives a sharply-folded orbit - another elegant low-complexity strange attractor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottGOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottGOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case G flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=0.4x+z, dy=xz-y, dz=-x+y) with a self-driving linear term, producing a steadily-circulating chaotic orbit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottHOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottHOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case H flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=-y+z^2, dy=x+0.5y, dz=x-z) whose squared feedback folds a compact attractor, another of Sprott's algebraically-simplest chaotic systems.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottJerk}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottJerk}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott jerk system: the simplest chaotic 'jerk' equation (a single variable with its first two derivatives and one nonlinearity), the most elementary route to chaos in one differential equation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottJOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottJOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case J flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=2z, dy=-2y+z, dz=-x+y+y^2) tracing a smoothly-circulating attractor, a distinct member of Sprott's elegant-equations catalogue.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottKOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottKOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case K flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=xy-z, dy=x-y, dz=x+0.3z) whose product and self-driving terms give a steadily-circulating attractor, another entry in Sprott's elegant-equations catalogue.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottLinz}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottLinz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott-Linz F flow: another of Sprott's algebraically minimal chaotic systems (dx=y+z, dy=-x+0.5y, dz=x^2-z), a low-cost smooth attractor with a character of its own.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottLOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottLOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case L flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=y+3.9z, dy=0.9x^2-y, dz=1-x) blending a squared term with linear drives into a distinctive folded orbit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottMegastable}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottMegastable}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Megastable oscillator: a damped resonator with a spatially-periodic (sinusoidal) restoring force, so it has a countable infinity of nested coexisting limit cycles - which orbit it lands on depends on the energy, giving amplitude-dependent timbres.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottMOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottMOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case M flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=-z, dy=-x^2-y, dz=1.7+1.7x+y) with a squared term that folds the orbit sharply, a distinct low-complexity strange attractor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottNOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottNOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case N flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=-2y, dy=x+z^2, dz=1+y-2z) with a squared feedback, a distinct compact strange attractor from Sprott's catalogue.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottOOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottOOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case O flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=y, dy=x-z, dz=x+xz+2.7y) whose product term twists the orbit into a distinct attractor shape.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
