\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott flow: one of Sprott's minimal three-term chaotic systems (dx=yz, dy=x-y, dz=1-xy), the simplest equations that still produce chaos - a clean, low-cost strange-attractor source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottParabolicJerk}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottParabolicJerk}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Parabolic jerk: a third-order jerk circuit whose restoring force is a parabola in position (dz=-a z - y + x^2 - offset), folding an asymmetric single-scroll chaotic attractor - a distinct member of the elegant-jerk family.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottPOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottPOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case P flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=2.7y+z, dy=-x+y^2, dz=x+y) mixing a squared term with simple linear couplings into another elegant chaotic attractor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottQOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottQOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case Q flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=-z, dy=x-y, dz=3.1x+y^2+0.5z) whose squared feedback and self-driving z term yield a distinct circulating attractor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottROsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottROsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case R flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=0.9-y, dy=0.4+z, dz=xy-z) with mostly constant drives, producing a smoothly-looping attractor distinct from the product-heavy cases.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottSE}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottSE}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott SE flow: an entry from Sprott's 'Elegant Chaos' catalogue (a jerk system dz=-a z + y^2 - x driven slightly differently), one of the algebraically simplest dissipative chaotic systems with a single quadratic term.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottSOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottSOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott case S flow: a minimal chaotic system (dx=-x-4y, dy=x+z^2, dz=1+x) closing out Sprott's elegant-equations set with another compact, squared-feedback attractor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottSQ}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottSQ}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sprott SQ flow: another 'Elegant Chaos' system whose jerk uses a signum nonlinearity (dz=-z - a y - sgn(x) + ...), reducing the chaos generator to comparators and integrators - hard-switching minimal chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SprottThomasSym}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SprottThomasSym}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Symmetric cyclic flow: a cyclically-symmetric chaotic system (each axis driven by a sine of the next plus a bias) generalizing the Thomas attractor with an adjustable symmetry-breaking bias, sweeping from a tidy lattice walk to dense chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SpruceBudworm}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SpruceBudworm}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spruce-budworm model: a fast insect population on a slowly-growing forest, whose fold-catastrophe outbreak-and-collapse hysteresis gives slow relaxation oscillations - the classic fast-slow ecological boom-bust.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SquareArmy}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SquareArmy}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

A wall of detuned pulse waves with movement: Voices sets how many, Detune spreads them, and Width is the pulse-width (square at 0.5) that an internal LFO slowly drifts for a hollow PWM ensemble.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SquareLead}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SquareLead}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Square-wave lead: the classic hollow chiptune/synth square lead with a gentle vibrato. Vibrato sets the wobble, Tone the brightness, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SquareNumOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SquareNumOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Square-number oscillator (a first as a synth block): an additive tone (NOT a square wave) whose overtones sit on the perfect squares - the 1st, 4th, 9th, 16th, 25th... harmonics. Because the partials thin out quadratically, the few that remain are widely and unevenly spaced, giving a clear, glassy, almost-pure tone with a faint metallic ring - the spectral fingerprint of the squares. Partials sets how many square overtones to sound, alias-free up to Nyquist. A figurate spectrum distinct from the triangular and prime oscillators. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SqueakyToyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SqueakyToyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Squeaky toy: a rubber dog toy squeezed, a high reedy squeak that bends up sharply then sags back down on each squeeze, fired at the set rate, the comic squeak-toy sound; the rate sets the squeezes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SquidOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SquidOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

SQUID oscillator: a superconducting quantum-interference loop whose trapped flux sits in a tilted double-well potential and is driven by an external field, hopping chaotically between flux states - a sensitive magnetometer pushed into chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SquishOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SquishOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Squish: a wet squishy splat or squelch, a burst of noise swept by a fast-closing lowpass with a gloopy pitch droop, the cartoon mud-step or slime-squish; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SSTVOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SSTVOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

SSTV oscillator: the warbling audio of slow-scan television, where each image line is sent as an FM sweep across the 1500-2300 Hz video band bracketed by sync tones, the burbling sound of pictures over ham radio; the line rate sets the warble speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Stab}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Stab}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chord stab: a trigger on in0 fires a detuned-saw triad (root, third, fifth) through a fast amp-and-filter pluck envelope - the staccato house/trance organ-stab voice. Tune sets the root pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Chord picks the voicing (0 major / 1 minor / 2 sus4 / 3 power-octave), Detune spreads paired saws per note for width, Decay sets the stab length, Cutoff the low-pass brightness, and Level the output. Distinct from the MIDI Chord block: a self-contained audio voice, not a note processor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StadiumBilliard}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StadiumBilliard}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stadium billiard: a point bouncing inside a Bunimovich stadium (a rectangle capped by semicircles), whose defocusing curved walls make every trajectory chaotic - a textbook example of hard chaos, read as the ball's position.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StadiumCheerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StadiumCheerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stadium cheer: the roar of a packed stadium erupting, a huge wall of broadband crowd noise that swells from a murmur to a deafening peak and falls back, the surge of a goal being scored; the surge rate sets the cheer waves.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StampedeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StampedeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stampede: a running crowd or herd, a low rolling rumble of many overlapping footfalls thickening into a continuous thunder with random pounding accents, building as the mass approaches; the density sets the size of the rush.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StaplerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StaplerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stapler: a desk stapler punched down, a sharp two-part mechanical ka-chunk (a spring-loaded press click then the metallic staple punch), fired at the set rate, the paperwork sound; the rate sets the stapling.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StarterPistolOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StarterPistolOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Starter pistol: the crack of a track-race starting gun, a very sharp broadband transient followed by a quick slap-back echo off the stadium, fired at the set rate; on your marks, get set, go.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Static}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Static}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Radio static - white-noise hiss with random crackle bursts and a faint drifting carrier whine, the between-stations sound. Noise sets the hiss level, Tune the whine pitch, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StaticShockOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StaticShockOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Static shock: the sharp snap of an electrostatic discharge zapping your finger to a doorknob, a very short bright broadband crack with a tiny high ring, fired at the set rate; the dry winter-carpet zap.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Steam}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Steam}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Steam / pressure release: a trigger on in0 vents a burst of band-passed noise - the hydraulic 'psssh' / hiss of escaping gas, valves and pneumatics - with a sharp attack and a decaying tail whose filter falls as the pressure drops. The mech, robot-joint and steam-pipe sound of film and game design. Tone sets the hiss brightness, Hole the resonance (how whistling / pitched the leak is), Decay the vent length, Sweep how far the filter falls as it decays, and Level the output. Trigger per vent.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SteamHissOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SteamHissOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Steam oscillator: high-passed turbulent noise (the hiss of escaping steam) with an optional narrow whistle resonance riding on top, from a gentle radiator leak to a screaming kettle; the whistle knob mixes in the pitched peak.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SteamTrainOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SteamTrainOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Steam train: the chuffing of a steam locomotive, a low thump and a burst of steam hiss on each piston exhaust repeating at the chuff rate that speeds up with the train; the rate sets how fast it rolls.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Steeldrum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Steeldrum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Struck steelpan (steel drum): a bright metallic note from partials near 1 : 2 : 2.8 : 4 with a medium shimmering decay - the sunny Caribbean steelpan timbre. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Steelpan}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Steelpan}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal steelpan (steel drum): a trigger on in0 strikes a tuned note on an oil-drum face - a bright shimmering tone with near-harmonic partials (1 : 2 : 3 : 4) and a metallic edge, medium sustain. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Shimmer the upper-partial brightness, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SteelpanOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SteelpanOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Steel pan: a struck note-area of a Caribbean steel drum, a bright stack of mostly-harmonic partials (fundamental, octave, twelfth) with a fast metallic attack and ringing sustain, re-struck at the set rate; the sunny pannist tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StepperMotorChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StepperMotorChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stepper-motor chaos: a permanent-magnet stepper driven near its mid-frequency resonance, where the sinusoidal detent and reluctance torques make the rotor angle lose lock and oscillate chaotically - the cause of stepper stall and missed steps.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StickSlip}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StickSlip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stick-slip oscillator: a spring-held block dragged by a moving belt whose velocity-weakening friction makes it alternately stick and slip, the relaxation mechanism behind earthquakes, brake squeal and bowed-string sound.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Stinger}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Stinger}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Horror stinger - a sudden dissonant cluster stab with a sharp attack and fast decay, the jump-scare hit, auto-repeating. Rate sets the interval, Tone the harshness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StingerHitOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StingerHitOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stinger hit: the sudden violent musical stab of a jump scare, a dense high dissonant cluster slammed in with a sharp attack and a fast decay (the screeching-strings shock chord), fired at the set rate; the gotcha moment.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StochasticGrainOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StochasticGrainOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stochastic grain: a Xenakis-style grain field where grain onsets follow a Poisson process and pitches are drawn from a band, the granular-cloud realization of stochastic music; the density governs the statistical mean rate, not a fixed clock.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StochResonance}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StochResonance}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stochastic-resonance switch: a particle in a double-well potential nudged by a weak periodic force plus a chaotic 'noise' term, so it hops between wells nearly in step with the drive - the counterintuitive noise-aided switching effect, deterministically rendered.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StommelBox}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StommelBox}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stommel box model: two ocean boxes exchanging heat and salt whose density-driven flow can suddenly flip direction (the thermohaline-circulation switch behind abrupt climate change), giving slow bistable, relaxation-oscillator dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Stream}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Stream}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Babbling brook - bright filtered noise with random water gurgles for a flowing-stream ambience. Flow sets the gurgle activity, Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StretchOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StretchOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stretched-harmonic oscillator (a first as a synth block): models the inharmonicity of a real struck or plucked string, where stiffness makes each overtone sharper than a perfect multiple - partial k sits at k*sqrt(1 + Stretch*k^2) instead of exactly k. That tiny upward stretch, growing with each partial, is what gives a piano its warm, slightly detuned brilliance and a struck metal bar its character. Stretch sets the amount (0 = perfectly harmonic, up = ever more bell-like). Partials sets how many to stack. A physically-modelled inharmonic spectrum distinct from the golden and integer oscillators. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StringMachine}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StringMachine}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vintage divide-down string ensemble: a full sawtooth chord run through a triple-chorus for the lush, shimmering 70s string-synth pad. Ensemble sets the chorus depth and Tone the brightness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StringPartialOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StringPartialOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stiff-string oscillator: harmonics stretched by the inharmonicity law f_n = n*f0*sqrt(1 + B*n^2), where bending stiffness pushes the upper partials progressively sharp - the slight overtone stretch that gives a piano (especially its bass) its characteristic shimmer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Strings}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Strings}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Orchestral string section: a wide ensemble of detuned saws with a slow bowed attack, section vibrato and a warm low-pass body - the sustained legato strings of cinematic and Zimmer-style scoring. Distinct from the Solina BBD ensemble effect (which colours an input) and the single Bowed string: this is the full section as a voice. Tune sets the pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Ensemble the detune spread (section width and beating), Attack the bow swell time, Vibrato the section pitch waver, Tone the brightness, and Level the output. A gate on in0 bows the note; hold for long legato.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StruckTube}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StruckTube}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Struck open tube / pipe: a hollow, woody-metallic pitched 'tonk' from striking an open pipe, ringing on its odd harmonics (1 : 3 : 5 : 7). Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StudentTNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StudentTNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Student-t noise: a Gaussian divided by sqrt(chi-squared/dof), whose degrees-of-freedom knob morphs the tails from heavy (Cauchy-like at dof=1) to light (Gaussian as dof grows) - a continuously tail-tunable noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Stylophone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Stylophone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stylophone: the buzzy, lo-fi stylus-organ - a bright square with a fast vibrato and a slightly unstable pitch, the 60s pocket-synth toy, played by holding in0. Vibrato sets the wobble, Tone the brightness, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SuarezSchopf}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SuarezSchopf}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Suarez-Schopf delayed oscillator: the canonical El-Nino model T' = T - T^3 - a T(t-tau), where a delayed negative feedback (the ocean's Rossby-wave return) sustains an irregular interannual oscillation - delay-driven climate cyclicity.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sub}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sub}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dedicated sub oscillator: runs a single phase at Freq dropped by Octave, outputting either a sine or a square, then scaled by Level. Octave shifts down 0, 1, or 2 octaves, Wave selects sine or square, and Level sets the output gain. Use it to add low-end weight under a main oscillator, with sine for clean sub and square for a harder, more present low end.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sub808}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sub808}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

808 sub-bass: a tuned sine sub with a short downward pitch-drop attack (the TR-808 bridged-T 'damped sine' punch), portamento glide between notes for the trap-style slide, an octave-up layer for midrange, and parallel saturation that adds upper harmonics so the sub translates on small speakers. Tune sets the pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Glide the portamento time, Punch the pitch-drop attack, Decay the note length, Drive the parallel saturation, and Level the output. A gate on in0 strikes the note; lives in the 30-80 Hz sub range.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SubBoom}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SubBoom}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Devastating sub-bass oscillator: a sine fundamental reinforced with two subharmonics an octave and two octaves below, then driven into saturation. Sub sets the lower-octave weight and Drive the harmonic grit so it cuts on small speakers.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SubGrowl}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SubGrowl}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sub-growl bass: a deep sine sub blended with a driven, growling harmonic layer - clean weight at the bottom with grit on top. Growl sets the harmonic drive, Sub the sub level, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SubharmonicOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SubharmonicOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Undertone-series oscillator (a first as a synth block): the mirror image of an ordinary tone - instead of stacking overtones ABOVE the fundamental it stacks the UNDERTONE series below it (f, f/2, f/3, f/4...), the descending ratios that Harry Partch and the old harmoniums explored. The fundamental sits at the TOP and the partials descend, giving a deep, hollow, slightly unsettling body quite unlike any overtone-based saw or square. Partials sets how many undertones to stack. A subharmonic spectrum distinct from every overtone oscillator. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SubHarmonicSeriesOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SubHarmonicSeriesOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Undertone-series oscillator: the mirror image of the harmonic series, stacking partials at 1, 1/2, 1/3, 1/4 ... of the set frequency (the subharmonic series of Mixtur-Trautonium and just-intonation theory) - a dark, bass-heavy, minor-feeling spectrum.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SubOscillator}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SubOscillator}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sub oscillator: the main pitch reinforced by square waves one and two octaves below it, the classic bass-thickening sub-osc found on Minimoog-style synths; the Sub1 and Sub2 knobs blend in the lower octaves for weight.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Subway}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Subway}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Subway train - rhythmic rail-joint clatter over a rolling rumble, with brake squeals. Speed sets the clatter tempo, Tone the metallic brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SubwayOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SubwayOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Subway: an electric metro train, a rising-and-falling traction-motor whine over a low rolling-stock rumble with an occasional metallic rail screech on the curves; the speed knob sets the motor pitch and rumble.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SuccessChimeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SuccessChimeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Success chime: the bright rising arpeggio of a completed task or level-up, three ascending bell tones (a major triad) ringing in quick succession, the satisfying done/achievement sound; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SujathaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SujathaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sujatha oscillator: each cycle is the Sujatha PDF proportional to (1+x+x^2)e^(-Theta*x), a lifetime distribution whose full quadratic numerator gives the broadest hump of the Lindley family before the exponential tail - a wide, gently-skewed bump. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SummonOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SummonOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Summon: calling forth a creature or boss, a deep minor chord of voices that builds from a low rumble, swelling in volume and adding upper octaves as the entity materialises, the dramatic conjuring crescendo; the rate sets the summon cycle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SundarapandianFlow}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SundarapandianFlow}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sundarapandian flow: a representative three-dimensional quadratic chaotic system from the chaos-synchronization/control literature, with two product nonlinearities folding a compact double-scroll attractor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Suona}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Suona}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Suona: the Chinese double-reed shawm - a conical-bore waveguide driven by a hard double reed (two reeds beating together, a sharper, more aggressive reed table than a single-reed clarinet/sax) into a flared metal bell, giving the loud, piercing, nasal blare of Chinese wedding and folk bands. Reed sets the double-reed bite, Bright the bore.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SuperellipseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SuperellipseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Superellipse oscillator: the Lame curve |x|^n + |y|^n = 1 whose exponent morphs the cross-section from a pinched astroid (n<1) through a circle (n=2) to a near-square 'squircle' (n large); sweeping the exponent fattens a sine toward a square.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SuperformulaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SuperformulaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Superformula oscillator: Gielis's single polar equation that generalizes the circle into starfish, flowers, polygons and gear shapes through its symmetry and exponent parameters; the radius read around the circle becomes a hugely-variable waveform from one formula.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SuperGaussOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SuperGaussOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Super-Gaussian oscillator: each cycle is a super-Gaussian bump exp(-(x^2)^Order), so Order morphs the pulse from an ordinary Gaussian (1) to an increasingly flat-topped, steep-edged near-rectangle (high) - a smoothly band-limited pulse with controllable edge sharpness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SuperPWM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SuperPWM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Supersaw built from detuned pulse waves: stacks several pulse oscillators spread symmetrically in tuning and averages them for a thick unison. Freq sets the center pitch, Detune spreads the voices apart in tuning, Width sets the pulse duty cycle (timbre), Voices sets how many oscillators stack, and Level scales the output. High detune and voice counts give wide, rich pad and lead tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SuperSawStack}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SuperSawStack}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Supersaw: seven sawtooth oscillators detuned around the centre pitch and summed (the Roland JP-8000 trance-lead sound), where the Detune knob fans the partials apart into the thick, shimmering chorused saw that defines a whole genre.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SuperSquare}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SuperSquare}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Super-square: seven detuned pulse oscillators stacked into one huge unison voice - the square-wave cousin of the supersaw, giving a wide, hollow, chorused wall of sound for hardstyle leads and fat basses. Detune spreads the seven copies, Mix balances the centre against the detuned pack. in0 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SuperTri}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SuperTri}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Detuned triangle stack: up to 9 triangle voices spread by Detune for a soft, hollow, flute-like unison with none of a saw's bite. Voices sets the thickness. A mellow alternative to the supersaw.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SupplyChain}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SupplyChain}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Supply-chain bullwhip: three echelons (retailer, wholesaler, factory) ordering to correct their inventory and pipeline with a delay, so small demand changes amplify into wild oscillating orders up the chain - the bullwhip effect as a chaotic cycle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Surdo}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Surdo}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal surdo: a trigger on in0 strikes the deep Brazilian samba bass drum - a very low membrane (modes near 1 : 1.8) with a long booming decay and a slow pitch drop, the heartbeat under a samba bateria. Tune sets the low pitch, Decay the long boom, Mute tightens it toward a damped stroke, Level the output. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Susaw}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Susaw}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Massive X super-saw: sums 7 saws spread across a detune range, with a slow random drift added to the base frequency, then scaled by Level. Detune sets the +/-3% spread between voices, Blend lowers the 6 side voices relative to the loud centre voice, and Drift sets how much the wandering frequency offset is applied. Higher Blend gives a wider unison wash; lower Blend keeps the centre pitch dominant for a tighter lead.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SuspensionQuarterCar}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SuspensionQuarterCar}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Quarter-car suspension: the sprung mass, tyre and a hardening/asymmetric damper of one wheel rolling over a periodic road, whose nonlinear damping and tyre lift-off make the body motion chaotic - vehicle ride-dynamics chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SvenssonAttr}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SvenssonAttr}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Svensson attractor: a trigonometric iterated map (a relative of the de Jong family) tracing tight, looping fractal filaments - another distinctive strange-attractor sound source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SwellPopOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SwellPopOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Swell pop: a balloon or bubble inflating and bursting, a tone that swells up in pitch and volume then snaps with a sharp pop transient, the cartoon inflate-and-burst; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SwiftHohenberg}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SwiftHohenberg}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Swift-Hohenberg mode: the amplitude equation for the convective-roll pattern-forming instability, a bistable cubic-quintic mode whose growth and saturation model the snap into and out of striped patterns.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SwingCreakOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SwingCreakOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Playground swing: a child swinging back and forth, a filtered creak of the chain that swells at each end of the arc where the swing reverses, the lazy-afternoon playground sound; the rate sets the swing tempo.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SwingEquation}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SwingEquation}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Power-grid swing: a synchronous generator's rotor angle obeying the pendulum-like swing equation against a stiff grid; a fluctuating mechanical input drives the angle through pole-slips into chaotic loss-of-synchronism - electrical-grid stability dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sword}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sword}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sword / blade SFX: a trigger on in0 fires a bright metallic swish - a band-passed noise whoosh as the blade cuts the air, layered with ringing inharmonic metal partials (the steel 'shing') that shimmer and decay. The unsheathe, swing and parry of film and game sound design. Tone sets the swish brightness, Ring the metallic-resonance level, Pitch the ring fundamental, Decay the ring length, and Level the output. Trigger per swing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SymIconMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SymIconMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Symmetric icon: an iterated map with built-in rotational symmetry (Field-Golubitsky 'symmetry in chaos'), whose orbit fills a kaleidoscopic n-fold-symmetric attractor - structured, crystalline chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sync}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sync}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hard-sync oscillator: a slave saw runs at Sync times the master frequency and is reset to zero each time the master phase wraps, producing the classic sync-sweep timbre, then scaled by Level. Freq sets the master pitch and Sync sets the slave ratio (1 to 6). Higher Sync ratios add brighter, more formant-like harmonics from the forced phase resets.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SyncGrainOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SyncGrainOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sync grain: a synchronous granular stream firing one windowed grain per period at the grain rate, each grain a short burst of an internal tone, so the grain rate sets the pitch while the internal frequency colours the timbre - classic synchronous granular synthesis.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SyncLead}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SyncLead}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hard-sync lead: a slave saw reset by a master each cycle, swept by Sync for the bright, vocal, tearing sync sweep - the classic aggressive synth lead. Sync sets the slave ratio, Tone the brightness, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SznajdModel}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SznajdModel}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sznajd model: a pair of agreeing neighbours convinces both of their outer neighbours of their opinion ('united we stand, divided we fall'), the sociophysics rule whose outward persuasion drives the ring toward one of two consensus states or a stalemate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tabla}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tabla}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal tabla: a trigger on in0 strikes a tuned Indian hand drum whose loaded membrane gives near-harmonic modes (1 : 2 : 3 : 4) plus its signature pitch-bend - the fundamental swoops as it rings, the singing 'tun' of the dayan. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Bend how far the pitch glides during the hit, Level the output. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TablaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TablaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tabla: the pitched ringing stroke of a North-Indian tabla, whose loaded drumhead is uniquely tuned so its membrane modes line up into a near-harmonic series (a rare pitched drum), with a sharp attack and a singing decay; re-struck at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TacomaFlutter}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TacomaFlutter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bridge torsional flutter: a suspension-bridge deck whose cables pull only in tension (a one-sided nonlinear restoring force) couples vertical and torsional modes under wind, the Lazer-McKenna mechanism blamed for the Tacoma Narrows collapse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Taiko}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Taiko}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cinematic taiko drum: a trigger on in0 strikes a deep membrane - a sine body with a fast downward pitch bend (the skin tensioning), an inharmonic mode for the skin 'thwack', and a wooden stick-attack click - decaying with the round boom of a big Japanese drum. The epic ensemble percussion of trailer and Zimmer-style scoring. Tune sets the pitch (in1 = Pitch CV), Bend the attack pitch-drop depth, Decay the boom length, Stick the wooden attack-click level, Skin the inharmonic body character, and Level the output. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer; layer several for an ensemble.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TaikoBig}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TaikoBig}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Big taiko drum: a deep, thunderous Japanese barrel drum - a low pitch-swept membrane boom with a hard wooden attack and a long resonant decay. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the boom, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TaikoOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TaikoOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Taiko: a large Japanese drum, a deep low membrane tone built from inharmonic circular-mode ratios with a slight downward pitch bend and a noisy skin-slap transient, re-struck at the set rate; thunderous and primal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TamaseviciusOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TamaseviciusOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tamasevicius oscillator: a compact four-element chaotic circuit (the TCMNL: two capacitors, an inductor, and a nonlinear conductance) designed as a simple, robust chaos generator for secure-communication experiments.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tambourine}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tambourine}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal tambourine: a trigger on in0 shakes a ring of jingles - a cluster of high, bright, inharmonic metallic partials that shimmer and ring, struck with a noise transient. Tune sets the jingle pitch, Decay the shimmer length, Jingle the metallic-partial richness, Level the output. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer for tambourine hits.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TanClipOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TanClipOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clipped-tangent oscillator: each cycle is tan(x) over a clipped argument range, so the waveform sits nearly flat then rips steeply through the centre - low Sharp gives a soft ramp, high Sharp approaches a square with a vertical zero-crossing, a transition curve no sine or saw has.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TangStatzLaser}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TangStatzLaser}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two-mode laser model: the Tang-Statz-deMars rate equations for two competing laser modes sharing a gain medium, whose intensity cross-saturation makes the modes flicker and exchange energy chaotically - antiphase laser dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TanhWindowOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TanhWindowOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tanh-window oscillator: each cycle is a flat-topped pulse built from the difference of two tanh edges, 0.5*(tanh(k(u+R))-tanh(k(u-R))) - a plateau of width Width with smoothly-saturating skirts whose steepness Steep sets, a distinct edge profile from the cosine, Planck and Fermi flat-tops.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tannerin}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tannerin}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tannerin / electro-theremin: a controllable slide oscillator - a pure sine you glide in pitch (the 'Good Vibrations' sound), simpler to play than a true theremin. Glide sets the portamento, Tone the harmonic richness, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TapeHissOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TapeHissOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tape hiss: the gentle background noise floor of analog magnetic tape, a soft band-limited hiss modulated by a very slow wow-and-flutter amplitude drift, the warm bed under a cassette recording; the tone knob sets the hiss colour.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TapeStopOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TapeStopOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tape stop: the classic effect of a tape (or turntable) being stopped, a tone whose pitch and volume sag rapidly to a halt as the reel slows, then snaps back to full speed to loop; the rate sets how often it stops.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TargetZone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TargetZone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Target-zone exchange rate: Krugman's model of a currency held within an official band, where the rate's nonlinear S-shaped response to fundamentals plus reflection at the band edges produces bounded, irregular near-the-edge dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TausswortheNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TausswortheNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tausworthe noise: a generalized-feedback shift-register generator (shift-and-XOR taps on a 32-bit word, the basis of combined-Tausworthe RNGs) producing fast maximal-length white noise with a distinct spectral fingerprint.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TaylorCouette}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TaylorCouette}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Taylor-Couette flow: a three-mode truncation of the fluid between two rotating cylinders, modelling the cascade from smooth Couette flow through Taylor vortices to wavy, chaotic vortices as the rotation speeds up.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TeardropOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TeardropOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Teardrop oscillator: the coordinate sin(t)*sin^Point(t/2) traces a teardrop - rounded on one side and drawn to a sharp point on the other - so raising Point sharpens the cusp and pushes energy into higher harmonics on just one half of the cycle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TeethChatterOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TeethChatterOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Teeth chatter: the rapid clacking of teeth chattering from cold or fear, a fast train of short hard clicks with slight random timing, fired at the chatter rate; a percussive bony rattle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Teleport}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Teleport}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Teleport - a shimmering upward sweep that dissolves into sparkle, the sci-fi beam-out, auto-repeating. Rate sets the interval, Tone the shimmer brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TeleporterOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TeleporterOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Teleporter: the dematerialisation shimmer of a transporter beam, a cluster of detuned partials sweeping rapidly upward in pitch and sparkle until they vanish, then re-trigger; the rate sets how fast the beam cycles.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Telharmonium}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Telharmonium}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Telharmonium: the 1897 additive tonewheel organ (the first electronic instrument) - a pure sum of sine harmonics with no filter, giving a clean, glassy, drawbar-organ tone. Bright mixes in the upper harmonics, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TempleBlockOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TempleBlockOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Temple block: a hollow carved-wood block struck with a mallet, a short dry pitched knock with a quick woody decay and a faint hollow second partial, re-struck at the set rate; the muted clop of orchestral temple blocks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tension}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tension}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cinematic tension drone: a slowly evolving underscore bed - a low cluster of detuned voices (root, fifth, octave, twelfth) that beat and drift against each other, swept by a slow filter LFO over a bed of filtered air noise - the sustained dread under a Zimmer-style scene. Self-running. Tune sets the root pitch (in0 = Pitch CV), Motion how fast the detuning and filter drift evolve, Width the cluster spread (dissonance), Noise the air-bed level, Tone the filter base frequency, and Level the output. Automate Tune for rising or falling tension.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TensionModString}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TensionModString}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tension-modulated string: a Karplus-Strong waveguide whose delay length SHRINKS with the string's own vibration energy, modelling how a real string tightens when plucked hard - so each attack glides sharp and settles back to pitch as it decays, the pitch-bend no fixed-delay pluck can make. It re-plucks itself to sustain. in0 is the Pitch CV; Tension sets the amplitude-to-pitch depth, Tone the damping, Sustain the decay length.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TensionPulseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TensionPulseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tension pulse: the slow throbbing pulse that builds suspense, a low tone swelling and contracting in volume with a faint dissonant overtone, the held-breath build before something happens; the pulse rate sets the heartbeat.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TentLogistic}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TentLogistic}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tent-logistic hybrid: crossfades between the angular tent map and the smooth logistic map each iteration, so one knob morphs the chaotic texture from sharp and piecewise to round and parabolic.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TentOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TentOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tent-map chaos source: the piecewise-linear tent map (x = mu * min(x, 1-x)) gives a sharp-edged chaotic stream whose character differs from the smooth logistic map - angular digital noise textures.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TentSkew}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TentSkew}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Skew tent map: an asymmetric tent whose peak sits off-centre, so the two linear branches have different slopes; it is exactly chaotic with a tunable invariant density - a fast, sharp, biasable noise source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TeslaCoilOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TeslaCoilOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tesla coil: a musical solid-state Tesla coil, where the spark fires in tight bursts at the note pitch so the plasma arc itself becomes a buzzy, square-ish singing voice; the duty knob sets how fat and bright the discharge.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TheraminOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TheraminOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

A smooth, expressive lead sine with a gentle vibrato and portamento glide between pitch changes - the theremin/whistle voice. Vibrato sets the wobble depth and Glide the portamento time.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Theremin}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Theremin}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch CV, Volume}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Theremin: a continuously-pitched sine voice with a warm vibrato, its pitch played by in0 (1V/oct) and its loudness by in1 - the hands-in-the-air theremin glide. Tune sets the base pitch, Vibrato the wobble depth, Level the output. in0 = Pitch CV, in1 = Volume CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThereminOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThereminOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Theremin oscillator: the pure heterodyne sine of the gesture-controlled instrument, gliding smoothly between pitches (portamento, since there are no discrete notes) with a gentle hand vibrato - the eerie, voice-like wail of the theremin.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Thermosyphon}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Thermosyphon}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Thermosyphon loop: fluid in a vertical ring heated below and cooled above circulates by buoyancy, but the flow can stall and chaotically reverse direction (Welander's model) - a physical realization of Lorenz-type convection chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Theta}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Theta}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Jacobi-theta oscillator (a first as a synth block): synthesises the theta function - a periodic version of the Gaussian bell, built as a cosine series whose harmonic k is weighted by q to the power k-squared. As Width (the q value) rises toward 1 those weights stay strong for many harmonics, so the bell sharpens into a narrow spike-train; as it falls the upper harmonics vanish and it rounds back toward a pure sine. A continuous sine-to-pulse morph rooted in one of the deepest functions in mathematics, distinct from a PWM or a filtered pulse. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThetaNeuron}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThetaNeuron}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Theta neuron: the canonical Type-I excitable model where the membrane state is an angle on a circle (theta'=(1-cos theta)+(1+cos theta) I); above threshold it rotates and fires a spike each lap - clean, frequency-controllable neural pulses.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThetaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThetaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}
