Jacobi theta-3: the function theta3(z,q) = 1 + 2 sum q^(n^2) cos(2 n z), whose Gaussian-decaying harmonics make a smooth bump that sharpens into a periodic spike as the nome rises - the building block of elliptic functions, here a peaked even waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThomasConservative}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThomasConservative}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Conservative Thomas flow: the Thomas cyclically-symmetric system with its damping set to zero, so it preserves phase-space volume and the orbit performs a labyrinthine, non-attracting random-walk through a sine-driven lattice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThomasOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThomasOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Thomas attractor: a cyclically-symmetric chaotic flow (each axis is sin of the next minus damping), giving a smooth, swirling, labyrinthine signal that drifts between order and chaos as the damping drops.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThreeScrollOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThreeScrollOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Three-scroll attractor: a chaotic flow whose orbit threads three separate scrolls, switching between three basins - chaos with a richer multi-region structure than the usual double-scroll.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThreeWing}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThreeWing}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Three-wing attractor: a chaotic flow whose orbit visits three lobes ('wings'), an even-richer multi-region switching than the four-wing system, for unpredictable jumpy modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Throat}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Throat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Throat singing (khoomei): a low buzzing drone with a single sharp overtone whistling above it, swept by Overtone - the Tuvan harmonic-singing voice. Overtone selects the emphasized harmonic, Drone the fundamental weight, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Thruster}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Thruster}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rocket thruster - a roaring filtered-noise blast with a low combustion body and pressure flutter. Thrust sets the power, Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThruZeroFM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThruZeroFM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Through-zero FM: the modulator drives the carrier frequency below zero and back, so the carrier momentarily runs in reverse - the clangorous, glitch-free deep-FM timbre analog VCOs cannot do but digital can.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThueOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThueOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Thue-Morse oscillator (a first as a synth block): an additive tone that keeps harmonic k only when k is a Thue-Morse 'odious' number - one whose binary digits sum to an ODD count (1, 2, 4, 7, 8, 11...). Because the keep/drop pattern is the self-similar Thue-Morse sequence, the spectrum is fractal: the same fair-share thinning repeats at every octave, producing a hollow, evolving timbre with a built-in self-similar comb. Partials sets the highest harmonic considered, alias-free up to Nyquist. A self-similar spectrum distinct from the prime and Fibonacci oscillators.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Thunder}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Thunder}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Thunderclap: a trigger on in0 fires a sharp crack (a band of noise) followed by a deep brown-noise sub rumble that swells then rolls away over several seconds - the storm thunder of film sound design. Crack sets the initial snap level, Rumble the sub-rumble depth, Roll the length of the rolling tail, Tone the low-pass darkness of the rumble, and Level the output. Trigger over a Rain bed for a storm; short Roll reads as a near strike, long Roll as distant.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThunderHit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThunderHit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Thunder: a deep, rolling thunderclap - a low rumble of filtered noise and a sub sweep that booms then tumbles away. Tune sets the depth, Decay the roll, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThunderOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThunderOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Thunder oscillator: a deep, slowly-modulated brown-noise rumble punctuated by occasional sharp cracks, the long rolling boom of distant-to-near thunder; the rumble knob sets how low and heavy the body sits.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TIAOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TIAOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TIA oscillator: the Atari 2600's TIA produced pitch only by dividing a fixed clock by small integers, so its notes land on a coarse, out-of-tune scale; this models that integer-divider quantization for the console's famously rough, detuned bleeps.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TidalHarmonicOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TidalHarmonicOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tidal oscillator: sums the principal tide-generating constituents at their real period ratios - the semidiurnal lunar M2 and solar S2 plus the diurnal K1 and O1 - so they beat into the fortnightly spring-neap cycle that tide tables predict.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TiganOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TiganOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tigan (T) system: a Lorenz-like chaotic flow whose middle equation carries the cross-term, tracing a flatter, more sheet-like twin-scroll attractor than Lorenz.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Timbale}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Timbale}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal timbale: a trigger on in0 strikes a shallow metal-shell drum - a tuned head plus the bright metallic inharmonic ring of the steel shell (modes near 1 : 2.7 : 4.2), the cutting crack of salsa and Latin percussion. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring, Ring the metallic shell brightness, Level the output. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Timpani}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Timpani}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tuned kettledrum (timpani): the prominent near-harmonic membrane modes (1 : 1.5 : 1.98 : 2.44 : 2.9) that give a kettledrum its definite pitch, with a soft attack thump and a downward pitch drop as the head settles. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the boom length, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TinkerbellMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TinkerbellMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tinkerbell map: a quadratic 2-D map tracing a delicate wing-shaped chaotic orbit, iterated at a set rate for a distinctive curling, fractal-edged chaos texture.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TinkerbirdMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TinkerbirdMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tinkerbird map: a Tinkerbell-family quadratic 2-D map at a parameter set that opens its delicate wing into a fuller, bird-like fractal orbit - a curling, fine-edged chaotic texture distinct from the classic Tinkerbell.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TippeTop}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TippeTop}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tippe top: a spun top that paradoxically flips to spin on its stem, its tilt angle climbing through a gyroscopic instability driven by sliding friction - a real toy whose inversion is modelled as a slow oscillatory rise-and-settle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TireScreechOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TireScreechOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tire screech: the squeal of skidding tires, a harsh resonant noise band that wavers in pitch as the rubber judders and grips, the cartoonish screech of a hard stop; the pitch knob sets the squeal frequency.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ToasterPopOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ToasterPopOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Toaster pop: the satisfying spring-loaded thunk of a toaster ejecting the toast, a low resonant clunk with a metallic spring ring and a faint rattle, fired at the set rate; breakfast is ready.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TodaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TodaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Toda oscillator: a resonator with the exponential restoring force of the Toda lattice (a famous integrable soliton system) which, when damped and sinusoidally driven, becomes chaotic - an asymmetric, spiky nonlinear-spring tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ToggleSwitchOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ToggleSwitchOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Toggle switch: the satisfying two-blip flip of a UI toggle, a low blip then a higher blip (the switch snapping to on), fired at the set rate; the settings-toggle feedback.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tom}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tom}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Analog tom drum: a trigger on in0 fires a sine whose pitch bends down from Tune with a gentler sweep than the Kick (no sub click), plus a short noise transient for stick attack. Tune sets the fundamental, Bend the pitch-drop amount, Decay the body length, Tone the attack-noise blend, and Level the output - the synthesized 808/909-style tom (low/mid/high by Tune). Patch a Clock, Euclid or sequencer gate into in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tom808}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tom808}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-808-style tom: a trigger on in0 fires a tuned sine with a downward pitch sweep and a medium decay - the round, pitched 808 tom/conga. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the length, Sweep the pitch-drop amount, Level the output. in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tom909}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tom909}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TR-909 tom: a punchy pitch-swept tom with a touch of noise grit, brighter and tighter than the 808 tom, the house/techno fill voice. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the length, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TomDrum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TomDrum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Synth tom: a sine whose pitch drops from the strike down to the tuned note while the amplitude decays, with a touch of noise for the head attack - the rounded pitched-drum voice of a drum machine.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TomSynthOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TomSynthOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tom-drum voice: a mid-range sine with a gentle downward pitch bend and a medium decay, the round pitched thump of a tom, re-triggered at the set rate; tune it from a high rack tom to a low floor tom.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ToppLeoneOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ToppLeoneOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Topp-Leone oscillator: each cycle is the Topp-Leone PDF 2*Nu*(1-x)*x^(Nu-1)*(2-x)^(Nu-1) on [0,1], a bounded-support shape that morphs from J-shaped (Nu<1) to a centred bump (Nu>1) - a compact waveform whose skew and peakedness Nu sets. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tornado}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tornado}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tornado - a vast roaring vortex: deep filtered-noise roar with a low rotating tone and debris. Fury sets the roar intensity, Tone the upper shriek, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TorusOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TorusOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Torus oscillator: two limit-cycle oscillators at incommensurate frequencies coupled together trace a quasiperiodic torus that, as coupling rises, wrinkles and breaks down into chaos - the Ruelle-Takens route, as an evolving two-tone source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TotalisticCA}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TotalisticCA}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Totalistic CA: a ring of three-state cells where each cell's next value depends only on the sum of itself and its neighbours through a totalistic rule code; richer than binary elementary CA, it grows nested, multi-level fractal patterns.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TractorBeamOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TractorBeamOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tractor beam: a steady humming energy beam pulsing with a slow throb, a pure low tone ring-modulated by a mid carrier and amplitude-pulsed, the sound of being slowly reeled into a mothership; the throb rate sets the beam's pulse.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TractrixOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TractrixOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tractrix oscillator: each cycle traces the tractrix horizontal coordinate x = t - tanh(t), which is nearly flat (cubic) through the centre then straightens to a ramp - a soft S with a long lazy middle, distinct from tanh or a linear saw.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Traffic}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Traffic}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

City traffic - a low layered road rumble with occasional passing-car doppler swooshes and distant horns. Density sets the activity, Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TrafficFlow}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TrafficFlow}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Traffic stop-and-go: three cars following each other by the optimal-velocity rule, where drivers accelerate toward a headway-dependent target speed; above a density threshold the line jams and surges in self-organized chaotic waves.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TrainletOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TrainletOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Trainlet: a granular stream whose grains are short band-limited impulse trains - a stack of equal-amplitude harmonics (Roads's trainlet) - giving a bright, buzzy, harmonically-dense click-tone; the harmonic count sets the brightness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TrainWhistle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TrainWhistle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Train horn - a rich chord of detuned tones blaring with a slow doppler bend, the classic locomotive whistle. Pitch sets the key, Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TransitDipOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TransitDipOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Transit oscillator: the light curve of a star with a planet crossing it - mostly flat at full brightness, then a brief U-shaped dip with ingress and egress ramps once per orbit - the signal that the Kepler mission used to discover thousands of exoplanets.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TransmutedExpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TransmutedExpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Transmuted-exponential oscillator: each cycle is lambda*e^(-lambda x)(1-Alpha+2*Alpha*e^(-lambda x)), a quadratic-rank-transmuted exponential where Alpha bends the pure decay - positive Alpha front-loads it, negative Alpha grows a small hump. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TrapezoidalOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TrapezoidalOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Trapezoidal oscillator: each cycle is the trapezoidal distribution - a flat-topped pulse with straight linear ramps up and down whose slope Ramp controls - sweeping from a near-rectangle (short ramp, buzzy) to a near-triangle (long ramp), a piecewise-linear flat-top distinct from the cosine and Planck tapers.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Trautonium}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Trautonium}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Trautonium: Oskar Sala's gliding subharmonic synthesizer - a sawtooth plus its strong subharmonics (f/2, f/3) for a rich, formant-heavy, organ-meets-string voice. Sub sets the subharmonic mix, Tone the brightness, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TriadHarmonicOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TriadHarmonicOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Triad-harmonic oscillator: boosts the harmonics that spell a just major triad (the 4th, 5th and 6th partials sound a root-third-fifth chord), so a single note rings with a built-in consonant chord - the acoustic basis of the major triad fused into one tone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Triangle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Triangle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal triangle: a trigger on in0 strikes a metal triangle - a cluster of high inharmonic metallic partials with a long, bright, shimmering ring that sits on top of a mix. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the long sustain, Shimmer the upper-partial brightness, Level the output. A clean, sustained metallic ping, unlike the short noisy shakers. Trigger from a Clock, Euclid or sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TriangleDrum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TriangleDrum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Struck triangle / metallic ping: four inharmonic high partials rung together with a long shimmering decay - the bright, bell-like triangle and finger-cymbal sparkle for percussion accents. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring time, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TriangleModeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TriangleModeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Triangle (idiophone) oscillator: the very inharmonic, high-and-dense partials of a struck metal triangle (ratios near 1, 2.71, 5.15, 8.43, 12.4), whose pitch is famously ambiguous - a bright, glittering metallic ping with no clear fundamental.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TriangularNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TriangularNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Triangular noise: the sum of two independent uniform samples, giving a triangular probability density (peaked at the middle, zero at the edges) - the classic TPDF dither noise that decorrelates quantization error, softer than white.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TriangularOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TriangularOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Triangular-number oscillator (a first as a synth block): an additive tone whose overtones land on the triangular numbers - the 1st, 3rd, 6th, 10th, 15th, 21st... harmonics (k(k+1)/2). The gaps between partials grow by one each step, so the spectrum starts dense and thins out smoothly as it climbs, giving a clear-but-airy body that sits between a full harmonic saw and a sparse sine stack. Partials sets how many triangular overtones to sound, alias-free up to Nyquist. A figurate-number spectrum distinct from the prime and Fibonacci oscillators. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TriangularQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TriangularQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Triangular-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the symmetric triangular inverse-CDF (sqrt(p/2) below the midpoint, 1-sqrt((1-p)/2) above), a square-root S that is steep at both ends and flat through the middle - the inverse of a triangle distribution, distinct from the logistic/erf sigmoids. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TricornFractal}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TricornFractal}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tricorn (Mandelbar): the Mandelbrot map with the iterate conjugated before squaring (z -> conj(z)^2 + c), whose anti-holomorphic twist gives a three-cornered fractal with characteristic cusps; the reseeded-on-escape real part is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TricubeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TricubeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tricube oscillator: each cycle is the tricube kernel (1-|x|^3)^3 (used in LOESS smoothing), a compact bump with a notably flat top and a cubic-cubed edge roll-off - a distinctly plateau-like pulse different from the (1-x^2)^n kernels.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TrigammaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TrigammaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Trigamma oscillator: each cycle is the trigamma psi'(x) ~ 1/x + 1/2x^2 + 1/6x^3 (the derivative of the digamma), a convex decay that falls faster than a plain 1/x - a steep-then-gentle falling ramp distinct from exponential and logarithmic decays. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TrillVoiceOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TrillVoiceOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Trill oscillator: a voiced tone rapidly gated open and shut at the flutter rate of a rolled or uvular trill (the tongue or uvula vibrating against the airflow), giving the rattling r of Spanish 'rr' or a French uvular r.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TriplingMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TriplingMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tripling map: the expanding circle map x -> 3x mod 1 (the base-3 shift, sibling of the doubling map), whose orbit is the ternary digits of the seed marching left - a sharp, deterministic ternary noise source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TrisectrixOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TrisectrixOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Trisectrix oscillator: the rational coordinate t(t^2 - 3)/(t^2 + 1) from the trisectrix of Maclaurin, a pole-free curve that dips, crosses and rises with a distinctive inflected shape - a smooth asymmetric waveform with a characteristic central wiggle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TritaveOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TritaveOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bohlen-Pierce (tritave) oscillator (a first as a synth block): a tone built from ODD harmonics only (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11...), with no even partials at all - the spectrum that fits the Bohlen-Pierce scale, which divides the TRITAVE (a 3:1 ratio, an octave plus a fifth) into 13 steps instead of dividing the octave. With only odd harmonics it has a hollow, clarinet-like purity, and it consonates with the strange non-octave intervals of BP music. Partials sets how many odd harmonics to stack, alias-free up to Nyquist. A non-octave timbre distinct from the usual harmonic oscillators. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TriweightOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TriweightOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Triweight oscillator: each cycle is the triweight kernel (1-x^2)^3, an even narrower, flatter-topped compact bump than the biweight - approaching a Gaussian shape but with true compact support (exactly zero at the edges) and an even softer harmonic spectrum.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Trombone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Trombone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Breath, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Trombone: the brassy, slightly-buzzy mid-brass - a sawtooth pushed through a brass formant with a slow swell and breath, played by holding in0. Brass sets the formant bite, Breath the air, Level the output. in0 = Breath gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Trumpet}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Trumpet}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Trumpet voice: a gate on in0 sounds a bright brassy tone - harmonically rich with a brass-like formant peak that opens up as the note swells in. Freq sets the note, Bright the brassiness (upper-harmonic weight), Vibrato the waver, Level the output. Hold the gate to sustain; in1 is the Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TruncNormalNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TruncNormalNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Truncated-normal noise: Gaussian samples confined to a chosen number of standard deviations by re-drawing any that fall outside (rejection), giving a bell shape with hard edges and no outliers - useful where unbounded Gaussian tails would clip harshly.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TschirnhausenOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TschirnhausenOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tschirnhausen oscillator: each cycle traces the Tschirnhausen cubic 3t - t^3 over a span, an odd S-curve that steepens at the centre and rolls over at the extremes - a cubic waveshape between a sine and a triangle, with a gentle compression at the peaks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Tuba}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Tuba}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Breath, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tuba: the deep, round, powerful low brass - a sawtooth through a low formant and lowpass with a slow swell, played by holding in0. Brass sets the bite, Breath the air, Level the output. in0 = Breath gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TubeBell}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TubeBell}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tubular-bell resonator: three long-decaying modes at bell-like inharmonic ratios ring when struck by the input, giving the shimmering, beating metallic tone of a chime or tubular bell.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TubeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TubeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Waveguide wind/tube oscillator: a delay-line resonator excited by breath noise models a flute or pipe. Breath sets the noise excitation and Tone the resonance damping - airy, organic pitched wind tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TubularBell}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TubularBell}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Struck tubular bell / chime: five inharmonic partials (1 : 2.76 : 5.4 : 8.93 : 13.3) with a long shimmering decay and a metallic strike, the orchestral tubular-bell and clock-chime voice. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring time, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TubularBellOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TubularBellOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tubular-bell oscillator: the chime partials 2, 3, 4.16, 5.43, 6.79, 8.21 whose 4:5:6-ish upper trio fuses into a clear 'strike note' an octave below the lowest partial (a partial that is not even present) - the orchestral-chime psychoacoustic trick.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TukeyLambdaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TukeyLambdaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tukey-lambda oscillator: each cycle is the Tukey-lambda quantile (p^L-(1-p)^L)/L swept over the cycle, so Lambda morphs the waveform continuously from a near-linear ramp (L=1) through an S-curve (L=2) to a steep, edge-loaded shape - one knob spanning a whole family of symmetric waves.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TumorImmune}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TumorImmune}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tumor-immune dynamics: immune effector cells hunt a growing tumor while the tumor suppresses them, a predator-prey-with-recruitment system whose competition gives dormancy, oscillatory relapse and chaotic regimes - oncology modelling as a slow source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TunnelDiode}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TunnelDiode}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tunnel-diode oscillator: an LC loop loaded by a tunnel diode whose N-shaped (cubic) current-voltage curve has a negative-resistance region, producing relaxation oscillations and, when driven, chaos - a real microwave-era circuit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TunnelDiodeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TunnelDiodeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tunnel-diode oscillator: a negative-resistance device whose N-shaped current-voltage curve makes the circuit jump astably between two stable branches, snapping quickly across the unstable region - a fast relaxation waveform with sharp switching edges and curved dwells.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TurbineBladeMistuned}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TurbineBladeMistuned}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Mistuned bladed disk: two coupled turbine blades with slightly different natural frequencies (mistuning) whose energy localizes and beats unpredictably under aerodynamic forcing - the vibration-localization that fatigues jet-engine blades.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TurkeyGobbleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TurkeyGobbleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Turkey gobble: the rapid burbling gobble of a tom turkey, a voiced tone chopped by a very fast irregular warble into the unmistakable gobble-obble-obble, fired at the set rate; the strut of the barnyard.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TweekAtmosphericOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TweekAtmosphericOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tweek: a distant-lightning sferic that has bounced in the Earth-ionosphere waveguide, dispersing near the VLF cutoff so it ends in a brief ringing 'tweek' tone instead of a flat click; the staccato pinging of nighttime VLF radio, fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TwinTOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TwinTOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaotic Twin-T oscillator: the Twin-T notch-network sine oscillator with a saturating amplifier whose extra phase shift, past the limit cycle, breaks into chaos - a classic audio-oscillator topology pushed over the edge.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Typewriter}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Typewriter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Typewriter - mechanical key clacks at a typing rhythm with the occasional carriage ding. Rate sets the typing speed, Tone the clack brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TypewriterOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TypewriterOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Typewriter: the clatter of a manual typewriter, randomly-timed sharp key-strike clacks at the typing rate with the occasional high margin-bell ding, the mechanical rhythm of typed prose.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Udu}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Udu}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Udu clay pot drum: a deep, hollow, tuned 'boop' from striking the pot's mouth, plus a finger-slap transient - a pitch-dropping sine with a noise click. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the boom, Level the output. Trigger on in0.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:UedaOscillator}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{UedaOscillator}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ueda oscillator: the forced cubic equation x'' + k x' + x^3 = B cos(t) with no linear restoring term, whose strange attractor ('Ueda's Japanese attractor') was one of the first chaotic attractors ever computed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:UFOHumOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{UFOHumOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

UFO hum: the hovering drone of a flying saucer, a mid tone deeply vibrato'd and tremolo'd by a slow wobble plus a faster shimmer, the classic theremin-flavoured sci-fi spacecraft hum; the wobble knob sets how unstable the craft.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:UnifiedChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{UnifiedChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Unified chaotic system: Lu's family that continuously interpolates between the Lorenz (alpha=0) and Chen (alpha=1) attractors as one parameter sweeps, so a single knob morphs between two classic chaotic regimes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VacuumCleanerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VacuumCleanerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vacuum cleaner: the drone of an upright vacuum, a high motor/impeller whine with harmonics riding on a broadband airflow rush, the steady household roar; the motor knob sets the suction speed and pitch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VaidyanathanFlow}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VaidyanathanFlow}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vaidyanathan flow: a representative three-dimensional quadratic chaotic system of the type catalogued for chaos-control and secure-communication research, with a single quadratic cross-term per equation tracing a compact twin-scroll.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VallisOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VallisOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vallis ENSO model: a three-variable model of wind-driven ocean temperature that produces the irregular, multi-year El-Nino oscillation chaotically - slow, organic climate-cycle modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ValueNoiseOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ValueNoiseOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Value-noise oscillator: picks a new random target at the set Rate and smoothstep-interpolates toward it, producing a continuous, gently-undulating random signal - the smooth interpolated 'value noise' of procedural graphics, distinct from stepped sample-and-hold and from gradient/Perlin noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VAnalog}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VAnalog}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Virtual-analog oscillator: saw or pulse (Wave) with adjustable pulse width (PWM), a square sub-oscillator an octave down (Sub), and slow random Drift for analog pitch instability. Level scales the output. A warm, slightly unstable VA voice - pair with the Mini/Jup-8 analog filters for a classic synth chain.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VanDeGraaffOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VanDeGraaffOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Van de Graaff: a static-charge generator, a faint rising whine of the charging belt building tension until it suddenly snaps over in a sharp crackling spark to ground, then recharges; the rate sets how often it discharges.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VanDerPolHeart}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VanDerPolHeart}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Heart-rhythm model: two coupled van-der-Pol oscillators standing in for the sinoatrial pacemaker and the ventricle; weak coupling gives a healthy beat, but detuning and forcing produce arrhythmia-like chaotic rhythms.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VarSlopeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VarSlopeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Variable-slope oscillator: a single waveform whose rising-to-falling time ratio is continuously adjustable, morphing from an up-ramp through a symmetric triangle to a down-ramp (and every asymmetric shape between) - one knob across the linear-waveform family.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VasicekProcess}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VasicekProcess}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vasicek process: dr = a(b-r)dt + sigma*dW, the Gaussian mean-reverting model of interest rates (an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process with an explicit long-run mean); unlike CIR it can go negative, giving a symmetric colored wander around a set level.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VectorOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VectorOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vector-synthesis oscillator: blends four waveforms - sine, saw, square and triangle - placed at the corners of an XY pad, with X and Y crossfading smoothly between them (the Prophet-VS / Korg Wavestation vector joystick). Sweep X/Y for evolving timbres from one oscillator. in0 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VectorSynth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VectorSynth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vector synthesis (Prophet-VS / SY22 style): four source waveforms sit at the corners of a square and a slowly-orbiting vector bilinearly blends between them, sweeping the timbre around the plane - a two-axis crossfade of sine, saw, square and triangle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VelvetGen}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VelvetGen}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Velvet noise generator: instead of a value every sample, it emits sparse, randomly-placed +/-1 impulses, the perceptually-smooth noise used in efficient reverbs and decorrelation - clickier and airier than white noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VelvetNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VelvetNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Velvet noise - sparse random impulses instead of dense hiss, giving a smooth, airy decorrelated texture used in reverb and diffusion. Density sets the impulse rate, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Vibraphone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Vibraphone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal vibraphone: a trigger on in0 strikes an aluminium bar (inharmonic partials 1 : 4 : 10, a metal cousin of the marimba) with a long shimmering sustain, plus the characteristic motor tremolo - an amplitude vibrato. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the (long) ring, Vibrato the tremolo depth, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VibraphoneOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VibraphoneOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vibraphone: a struck aluminium bar with near-octave partials and a long shimmering decay, amplitude-modulated by the spinning resonator-disc tremolo that gives the vibraphone its signature wobble; re-struck at the set rate, the tremolo knob sets the vibe motor speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Vibraslap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Vibraslap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Modal vibraslap: a trigger on in0 cracks the ball against the wooden box, then the loose pins rattle and buzz as they ring out - the sharp 'crack-brrrrr' heard at the top of so many percussion breaks. Tone sets the rattle brightness, Decay how long the pins buzz, Level the output. Needs only a trigger.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VicsekModel}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VicsekModel}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vicsek model: a swarm of self-propelled particles each steer toward the average heading of their neighbours plus angular noise; below a noise threshold they spontaneously align into collective motion (a flock), above it they scatter - the order parameter tracks that flocking transition.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VinylCrackle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VinylCrackle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vinyl crackle: sparse random pops with decaying tails layered over a faint filtered hiss, the lo-fi surface noise of an old record - instant nostalgia/texture under a mix.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VinylCrackleOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VinylCrackleOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vinyl crackle: the nostalgic surface noise of a vinyl record, a soft bed of filtered hiss sprinkled with random sharp dust pops and crackles, the lo-fi warmth of analog playback; density sets how worn the record.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VioletNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VioletNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Violet noise: white noise twice-differenced, rising +6 dB/octave so it is almost entirely high-frequency hiss - the spectrum of differentiated thermal noise, useful for sizzle and as a dither shape.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ViolinBowOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ViolinBowOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Violin: a bowed-string waveguide where a stick-slip bow-friction curve continuously pumps the delay line into a sustained sawtooth-like Helmholtz motion, the singing tone of a bowed violin; bow force and speed shape it from airy to digging.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VocalFilter}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VocalFilter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vowel / formant filter: three resonant formant peaks tuned to the vowels A-E-I-O-U, with Vowel morphing continuously between them and Reso setting the formant sharpness - turns any bright source into a talking, vocal timbre. A morphable vowel bank, distinct from the single Formant block.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VocalFoldChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VocalFoldChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaotic vocal folds: a two-mass model of the vibrating vocal folds whose collision nonlinearity, under asymmetric tension, breaks into the subharmonic and chaotic regimes heard as vocal fry, diplophonia and creak.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VocalFry}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VocalFry}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vocal fry: the low, creaky glottal rattle at the bottom of the voice - sparse, jittery pulses through a vocal formant. Rate sets the creak speed, Vowel the formant, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VonMisesNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VonMisesNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Von Mises noise: a concentration-controlled random angle on the circle (the circular analogue of the Gaussian), read out as its cosine; high concentration clusters near one phase, low concentration spreads uniformly - a wrapped, circular random source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VortexShedding}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VortexShedding}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vortex shedding: a van-der-Pol wake oscillator coupled to an elastic cylinder (the Hartlen-Currie model of vortex-induced vibration), which locks in, beats and chaotically modulates - the aeolian-tone source behind singing wires and bridge sway.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VosimSynth}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VosimSynth}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

VOSIM (Kaegi-Tempelaars): each fundamental period holds a short burst of N decaying squared-sine pulses followed by silence; the pulse rate sets a formant and the count/decay shape its bandwidth - an efficient 1970s vocal-formant synthesis.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VosimVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VosimVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

VOSIM oscillator: each fundamental period fires a short burst of amplitude-decaying squared-sine pulses (the Kaegi-Tempelaars voice-simulation method), so the pulse-rate sets a formant and the period sets the pitch - vowel-like tones from a pulse train.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VoterModel}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VoterModel}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Voter model: on a ring of two-opinion agents, each step a random agent adopts the opinion of a random neighbour; opinions drift by imitation until the whole population reaches consensus, then a perturbation reseeds it - the magnetization makes a slow random wander.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VowelFormant}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VowelFormant}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Formant oscillator that imposes vowel resonances on a buzzy source: Vowel morphs through A-E-I-O-U formant pairs and Q sharpens the peaks, turning a saw into a singing, talking timbre.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VoxPad}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VoxPad}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Synth-vox pad: a robotic, two-formant vocal pad on detuned pulses, morphing through vowels - the talking, choir-machine sound of vintage string synths. Vowel morphs the vowel, Width the detune, Level the output. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VPS}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VPS}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vector phase-shaping oscillator (Kleimola/Lazzarini/Timoney/Valimaki, DAFx 2011): the linear phase ramp is bent through a two-segment function with one movable inflection point (D, V) before a cosine - V above 1 packs extra cycles into the period for sweepable formant peaks (FM- and hard-sync-like spectra) while D shifts their position. Freq sets the pitch (in0 = Pitch CV), D the inflection time, V the cycles/formant, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VuvuzelaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VuvuzelaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vuvuzela: the relentless monotone drone of the South-African stadium horn, a single buzzy lip-reed tone around B-flat thick with harmonics and a faint breathy waver, the swarm-of-bees sound of the 2010 World Cup; the breath knob adds airiness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaldOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaldOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wald oscillator: each cycle is the inverse-Gaussian (Wald) PDF, a sharply-rising, long-right-tailed bump (a first-passage-time distribution) - Mu shifts the peak and Lambda its sharpness, peak-normalized to a bounded bipolar waveform with a distinctive asymmetric skew.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Walsh}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Walsh}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Walsh-function oscillator (a first as a synth block): builds its waveform from the Walsh functions - the SQUARE-wave orthogonal basis that does for digital signals what sines do for analogue, used in the Hadamard transform. Each one is a product of doubling square waves, so the output is a hard +/-1 pattern with exactly Sequency sign-changes per cycle (sequency being the square-wave equivalent of frequency). The result is a buzzy, bit-exact, distinctly digital timbre - a complete non-sinusoidal basis rather than a filtered square. Sequency picks which Walsh function to sound. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WalshOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WalshOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Walsh-function oscillator: products of square waves at octave-related rates (the Rademacher functions that build the Walsh-Hadamard basis) lock to the input pitch, giving hollow, purely-square spectra a sine basis can't.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WangBuzsaki}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WangBuzsaki}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wang-Buzsaki interneuron: a reduced conductance-based model of a fast-spiking cortical interneuron (the cells that pace gamma-band brain rhythms), firing narrow high-frequency spikes under injected current.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WangChenOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WangChenOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wang-Chen system: a chaotic flow that coexists with a single stable equilibrium, so its chaotic attractor is 'hidden' (its basin does not touch the equilibrium) - a notable counterexample in hidden-attractor theory.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WangSunOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WangSunOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wang-Sun attractor: a chaotic flow with mixed linear and quadratic terms tracing a folded-ribbon orbit, another distinctive strange attractor for evolving drones and modulation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Warp}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Warp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phase-warping oscillator: a master phase feeds a base waveform but is reshaped by one of several Mode warps (Neutral, Wrap, Grain, Hardsync, Hard Up-Down, Bend, Mirror, Random, Jitter) before the wave is read. Wave picks the base shape (sine, saw, square, triangle), Mode selects the warp, Amount sets warp depth, and Ratio scales the slave frequency for the sync modes. Pushing Amount drives anything from subtle phase bend to per-sample jitter noise depending on Mode.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WarpDriveOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WarpDriveOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Warp drive: a starship jumping to lightspeed, a deep rumble that accelerates in pitch and intensity, building to a bright whooshing peak before snapping back to start the cycle again; the rate sets the warp-charge time.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
