\phantomsection\label{blk:WarpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WarpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Spectral-warp oscillator (a first as a synth block): a single knob that bends the whole harmonic series. Each partial sits at k raised to the power Warp, so at Warp = 1 it is an ordinary harmonic saw; below 1 the partials crowd together (a compressed, dense, organ-like spectrum) and above 1 they spread apart into an ever-more-inharmonic, bell-to-clangorous timbre. Sweeping Warp morphs continuously from harmonic to inharmonic - a one-control spectral stretch unlike any fixed waveform. Partials sets how many to stack, alias-free up to Nyquist. Needs no input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaspOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaspOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wasp: the angrier, higher, harsher buzz of a wasp or hornet, a bright harmonic wingbeat tone with a sharper edge and aggressive amplitude surges as it lunges, more menacing than the rounder bee; the wingbeat sets the pitch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Water}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Water}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Water / bubbles: randomly-triggered bubble 'bloops' - sine pings whose pitch rises sharply as they pop (the physical bubble model A*sin(2*pi*f*t)*e^-bt with rising f) - over an optional flowing-noise wash, the stream, drip and underwater sound of film sound design. Distinct from Rain's falling droplet plinks: bubbles sweep up. Self-running. Flow sets the stream-noise level, Bubbles the pop density, Pitch the bubble base frequency, Spread the random pitch range, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaterDrumOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaterDrumOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Water drum: a struck clay udu/water-pot whose enclosed air cavity gives a deep boomy tone that bends quickly downward in pitch as the hand seals and opens the hole (the wet 'bloop' of an udu), re-struck at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaterHammer}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaterHammer}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Water hammer: the pressure surge when flow in a pipe is throttled, reflecting back and forth with valve-and-column nonlinearity and cavitation cut-off; repeated throttling gives the banging, relaxation-oscillating surge of plumbing.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Waterphone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Waterphone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Bow}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Waterphone: the eerie, bowed-metal horror-film instrument - several inharmonic metal-rod resonances with a slow random glissando, played by holding in0 as bow pressure. Density sets the resonance spread, Glide the pitch wander, Level the output. in0 = Bow gate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaterphoneRes}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaterphoneRes}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Waterphone resonator: three densely-packed, slowly-decaying inharmonic modes ring and beat against each other when excited, the eerie, sliding, horror-soundtrack timbre of a bowed waterphone or singing rods.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaterSprayOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaterSprayOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Water spray: the steady high hiss of a hose nozzle or spray bottle, a band-passed jet of fine-mist noise with a faint flutter as droplets break up, the airy sound of misting; tone sets the nozzle fineness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaterTank}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaterTank}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two-tank hydraulic oscillator: tanks filling and siphon-dumping into each other, whose threshold-triggered overflows interact into irregular, chaotic level swings - a relaxation oscillator you could build out of buckets.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveCrashOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveCrashOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wave-crash oscillator: a slow noise swell that builds, breaks into a bright broadband crash, then hisses out as foam and recedes, repeating like surf on a shore; the period knob sets the wave interval.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveFM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveFM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two-operator FM voice: a modulator (with its own feedback path) phase-modulates a carrier, the core DX-style FM cell - Ratio sets the harmonics, Index the brightness, Feedback adds sawtooth-like edge.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WavefolderOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WavefolderOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wavefolder oscillator: a sine driven hard into a triangle-folding nonlinearity that reflects peaks back on themselves, the Buchla/West-Coast technique that adds shimmering high harmonics with rising drive instead of clipping them - a fold-rich timbre source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveguideMesh}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveguideMesh}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Waveguide mesh: a 2-D membrane modelled as four delay-line waveguides meeting at scattering junctions, so a strike spreads, reflects off the rim and rings with the inharmonic modal spectrum of a small drum head or plate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveMorph}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveMorph}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Morphing waveform oscillator: locks to the input pitch and crossfades a single oscillator continuously from sine through saw to square with one knob - a sweepable waveshape in one voice.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WavePacketOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WavePacketOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wave-packet oscillator: a quantum Gaussian wave packet whose component states dephase and then periodically rephase, so its amplitude collapses to a blur and revives to a sharp peak each cycle - the quantum-revival phenomenon as a self-pulsing carrier.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveScan}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveScan}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Additive harmonic scanner: sums a series of sine partials whose amplitudes follow a power-law roll-off, then normalizes the result. Freq sets the fundamental, Harmonics sets how many partials are stacked, Scan tilts the spectrum from bright (all harmonics equal at 0) toward dark (fundamental only as it approaches 1), and Level scales the output. Sweep Scan for a sawtooth-to-sine timbral morph.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveStack}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveStack}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Octave saw stack: sums sawtooths at successive octaves above the fundamental with a roll-off, building the bright, organ/drawbar-like stacked-octave spectrum from one phase accumulator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Wavetable}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Wavetable}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wavetable oscillator: it advances a phase at Freq and reads single-cycle frames loaded from the node config, interpolating both within and between frames. Position (0 to 1) morphs across the frame stack, Warp (-1 to +1) phase-distorts the read pointer for timbral bending, Freq sets pitch (20 to 8000 Hz), and Level scales output. A pure generator (no inputs) that sweeps from one waveform to another as Position moves.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveTerrain}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveTerrain}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Orbit Pos}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wave-terrain synthesis: a Lissajous orbit traverses a 2D surface z=f(x,y) and the height is the output. Surface picks among five built-in terrains - Classic (the Mitsuhashi/Roads polynomial), Saddle, Ripple, EggCarton, Ring - or a drawn heightfield supplied in node config overrides them. Warp tilts the orbit, Ratio sets its y/x frequency, Freq the pitch, Level the output. The Orbit Pos input (in0) scrolls the orbit across the surface, so a moving CV sweeps the timbre instead of holding one static spectrum.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveTerrainScan}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveTerrainScan}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wave-terrain synthesis: an orbit travels around a 2-D landscape z = f(x,y) and the terrain height becomes the waveform; as the orbit's radius and the terrain warp change, the timbre morphs continuously - a classic but rarely-implemented technique.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveTerrainXY}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveTerrainXY}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

2-D wave terrain: two phasors at independent rates trace a path across a fixed two-variable surface, reading its height as the output - detuned X/Y rates sweep continuously evolving timbres a 1-D wavetable can't reach.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WebMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WebMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zaslavsky web map: an area-preserving kicked-oscillator map whose orbits weave an infinite crystalline 'stochastic web' of symmetric channels, a Hamiltonian chaos with a kaleidoscopic structure.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WeedWhackerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WeedWhackerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

String trimmer: a weed-whacker spinning its nylon line, a high motor whine periodically loaded by the slap of the line whipping through grass, the lawn-edging sound; the load knob sets how much grass it hits.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WeibullCdfOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WeibullCdfOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Weibull-CDF oscillator: each cycle is the Weibull cumulative 1-e^(-(x/Lambda)^K), a stretched-exponential ramp that morphs from a concave saturating rise (K<1) through exponential (K=1) to an S-curve (K>1) - one shape knob spanning ramp families. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WeibullNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WeibullNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Weibull noise: samples (-ln u)^(1/k) whose shape parameter k morphs the distribution from heavy-tailed exponential (k<1) through the bell-like k~3.6 to a sharp peak (k>1) - the reliability/failure-time distribution, here a tunable-skew source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WeibullQuantileOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WeibullQuantileOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Weibull-quantile oscillator: each cycle is the Weibull inverse-CDF (-ln(1-p))^(1/K), a ramp whose curvature K controls - concave-then-steep for small K, near-linear and then accelerating for large K - the quantile (inverse-CDF) counterpart to the Weibull family. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Weierstrass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Weierstrass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fractal-waveform oscillator (a first as a synth block): synthesises the Weierstrass function - the textbook curve that is continuous everywhere but differentiable nowhere - as a self-similar audio tone, summing eight cosine octaves whose frequencies climb by Lacunarity and amplitudes fall by Detail. The result is infinitely jagged: zoom in at any scale and the same roughness repeats. Detail sets the high-frequency weight (roughness/brightness) and Lacunarity the gap between layers. A genuinely fractal timbre, not a filtered or wavefolded one.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WeierstrassMandelbrot}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WeierstrassMandelbrot}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Weierstrass-Mandelbrot function: the Weierstrass fractal generalized with per-octave phase offsets and a fractional-dimension exponent, a statistically-self-affine waveform used to model rough surfaces and fractional Brownian texture.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WeierstrassOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WeierstrassOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Weierstrass function: a sum of cosines at geometrically-rising frequencies and shrinking amplitudes (sum a^k cos(b^k x)), the textbook continuous-everywhere yet differentiable-nowhere fractal curve, here pitch-locked as a richly-detailed waveform.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WeierstrassPOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WeierstrassPOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Weierstrass P-function: the canonical doubly-periodic elliptic function, which has a double pole each period giving an even waveform with a sharp spike whose width the Sharpness knob sets - a band-limited approximation of its characteristic spiked profile.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WeiWangOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WeiWangOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wei-Wang attractor: a chaotic flow constructed to have no equilibrium points at all (a 'hidden' attractor), so the orbit wanders chaotically with nowhere to settle - a modern example of hidden chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WelchOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WelchOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Welch oscillator: each cycle is the parabolic Welch window 1-(2p-1)^2, a single smooth quadratic arc - a rounded pulse brighter than the cosine-sum windows (a parabola has a richer even-harmonic spectrum than a raised cosine), with no discontinuities.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WestCoast}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WestCoast}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Buchla-style west-coast oscillator: a pure sine driven into a sine wavefolder. Fold sets how hard it folds (sparse to dense harmonics) and Symmetry biases the fold for even-harmonic, brassy timbres - timbre from folding, not filtering.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WGMesh}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WGMesh}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Strike}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

2-D digital waveguide mesh (Van Duyne & Smith 1993): a strike on in0 launches a wave across an 8x8 membrane grid - the finite-difference 2-D wave equation - so it rings with the dense, inharmonic modes of a struck drumhead, plate or gong rather than a 1-D string. Not a wavetable: the timbre is the physics of a vibrating surface. Speed (the propagation/Courant coefficient) sets pitch and brightness - low for a deep tom, high for a tight gong; Damp sets the decay; Level the output. in0 = Strike.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Whale}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Whale}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Whale song - slow, deep, pitch-bending moans drifting through an underwater haze. Calls sets how often, Pitch the depth, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WhaleSongOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WhaleSongOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Whale-song oscillator: long, slowly-gliding low-frequency moans that rise and fall over seconds with a few harmonics, the haunting, drawn-out calls of a humpback or blue whale; the glide rate sets how mournfully the pitch bends.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WheelShimmy}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WheelShimmy}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wheel shimmy: a trailing caster (shopping-cart or aircraft nose-gear) whose tyre side-force and castor geometry self-excite a violent steering wobble; the tyre's nonlinear slip saturation bounds it into a chaotic limit cycle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Whip}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Whip}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Whip crack / slapstick: a trigger on in0 fires a single sharp crack - a fast burst of bright band-passed noise with a quick downward zip, the slapstick thwack and whip-crack of cartoon and orchestral percussion. Tone sets the brightness of the crack, Decay its (very short) length, Level the output. Needs only a trigger.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WhiskOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WhiskOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Whisking: a metal whisk beating eggs or cream in a bowl, a fast rhythmic swish of bright metallic noise modulated by the back-and-forth stroke, the steady whisking motion; the stroke rate sets the speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Whisper}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Whisper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Whispering voice - breathy mid-band noise gated into syllable-like bursts. Pace sets the syllable rate, Tone the sibilance, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WhisperOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WhisperOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Whisper oscillator: broadband breath noise shaped by a vowel formant resonance instead of a pitched source, producing the pitchless, airy quality of a whispered vowel; the formant knob selects which vowel colour the breath takes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WhisperTextureOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WhisperTextureOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Whisper: a breathy unvoiced whisper, a band of noise shaped by slowly-shifting vocal formants with no pitch (just turbulent air forming words), the creepy disembodied whisper of horror; the formant rate sets the syllable speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Whistle}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Whistle}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Breath, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Human whistle / piccolo-pure tone: an almost-sinusoidal voice with a gentle vibrato and a faint breath hiss, played by holding in0. Tune sets the pitch, Vibrato the wobble depth, Level the output. in0 = Breath gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WhistlerWaveOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WhistlerWaveOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Whistler: a lightning-stroke radio impulse that travels along Earth's magnetic field lines and disperses, so the high frequencies arrive first and the pitch glides smoothly downward - the descending 'pheww' VLF whistler heard on a radio receiver; the rate sets how often a stroke fires.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Whoosh}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Whoosh}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Transition whoosh: a trigger on in0 fires a band-passed noise swish whose loudness and filter both swell to a peak at the mid-point then fall away - the air pass-by used for cuts, swipes and reveals in film and trailers. A doppler-style arc, not a tonal riser. Time sets the pass duration, Tone the filter centre the sweep peaks at, Reso the band sharpness, Body a low-rumble layer for weight, and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WienBridgeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WienBridgeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wien-bridge oscillator: the classic op-amp RC sine generator (the topology of the first Hewlett-Packard audio oscillator), modeled as a resonant loop with a soft amplitude-stabilizing nonlinearity so it settles to a clean, low-distortion sine.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WienChaos}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WienChaos}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaotic Wien bridge: the textbook Wien-bridge sine oscillator with a saturating amplifier nonlinearity that, past its stable limit cycle, breaks into chaos - a familiar audio circuit driven over the edge.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WienerProcess}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WienerProcess}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wiener process: the standard Brownian motion - the integral of white noise with independent Gaussian increments - reflected at +/-1 so it stays bounded; the canonical continuous random walk underlying every diffusion model, as a slow drifting CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WienRobinson}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WienRobinson}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaotic Wien-Robinson oscillator: the balanced Wien-Robinson bridge sine source with a cubic amplifier nonlinearity, whose deep notch and extra feedback path destabilize the limit cycle into chaos - a bench-oscillator route to chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WilberforcePendulum}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WilberforcePendulum}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wilberforce pendulum: a weight on a spring that can both bounce and twist, the two modes coupled through the spring. Tuned to resonance the vibration energy SLOSHES fully between bouncing and twisting - the heard (bounce) tone swells and fades as its energy pours into the silent twist mode and back, a slow self-amplitude-modulation that is genuine coupled-mode energy exchange, not an LFO multiplying the level. Detune mistunes the two modes so the exchange becomes partial and the beating richer. in0 is the Pitch CV; Coupling sets the slosh rate, Detune the mode mismatch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WillamowskiRossler}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WillamowskiRossler}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Willamowski-Rossler reaction: a mass-action chemical model with autocatalytic and cross-inhibitory steps that produces genuine chaos in concentration space - one of the few polynomial chemical systems proven to be chaotic.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WilsonCowanOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WilsonCowanOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wilson-Cowan neural mass: coupled excitatory and inhibitory neuron populations with sigmoid activation oscillate and, when driven, break into chaos - the cortical-rhythm model behind brain-wave dynamics, as a sound source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WilsonNeuron}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WilsonNeuron}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wilson neuron: Hugh Wilson's polynomial reduction of Hodgkin-Huxley to two variables (voltage + a recovery current), reproducing regular and fast spiking with simple cubic terms - an efficient biophysical spiker.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WimolBanlue}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WimolBanlue}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wimol-Banlue attractor: a chaotic flow built around a hyperbolic-tangent and absolute-value nonlinearity, tracing a symmetric butterfly orbit with smoothly-saturating dynamics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Wind}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Wind}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wind texture from filtered noise: white noise is fed through a resonant TPT band-pass whose center frequency is modulated by a slow 0.3 Hz gust LFO. Cutoff sets the band-pass center frequency, Reso sets the resonance (how whistling/pitched the noise becomes), Gust scales the LFO cutoff sweep depth, and Level scales the output. Produces ambient wind, breath and atmospheric beds.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WindChimes}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WindChimes}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wind chimes - randomly struck tuned metal tubes (FM bells on a pentatonic scale) drifting in a breeze. Activity sets how often they ring, Tone the metallic brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WindGen}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WindGen}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wind: band-passed noise swept into a howling wind, with the input signal's level driving the gusts - feed it silence for a steady breeze, or a signal to make the wind surge and moan with the music. A weather-bed generator that responds to what you play. Speed sets the base gustiness, Tone the wind colour, Mix the blend.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WindGustOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WindGustOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wind gust: broadband turbulent noise whose loudness is slowly and randomly modulated to swell into gusts and fall to lulls (a von-Karman-like gust envelope on a noise carrier), the restless rise-and-fade of real wind for atmospheres and whoosh textures.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WindmiOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WindmiOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

WINDMI attractor: a low-order model of the solar-wind/magnetosphere/ionosphere energy coupling whose exponential nonlinearity drives a chaotic geomagnetic-storm-like flow - space-weather chaos as a modulation source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WindNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WindNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Resonant wind noise: white noise driven through a tunable resonant band-pass whistles and howls like wind through a gap - a self-contained breath/surf/wind texture generator (deterministic RNG).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WindowSlideOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WindowSlideOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sliding window: a sash window or patio door sliding in its track, a low rolling rumble of filtered noise with a faint periodic judder as it rides the rollers, fired at the set rate; the rumble of opening a heavy slider.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WindPad}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WindPad}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Amount}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wind: a howling, gusting wind texture - white noise through a resonant bandpass whose pitch and level wander randomly, from a gentle breeze to a storm. Gust sets the wander, Pitch the centre, Level the output. in0 = Amount gate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WindupSpringOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WindupSpringOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wind-up spring: the runaway-then-slowing whir of a wind-up toy's spring-driven escapement, a click train that starts fast and gradually slows as the mainspring unwinds, looping back to full wind; the run length sets how long before it rewinds.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WinfreeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WinfreeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Winfree oscillators: a population of phase oscillators that influence each other through a pulse-shaped phase-response curve (the original biological-rhythm sync model, predating Kuramoto); the mean field locks, desynchronizes or beats irregularly.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WingRock}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WingRock}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wing rock: a slender delta-wing aircraft at high angle of attack whose asymmetric vortex lift gives negative roll damping, self-exciting the rolling 'wing-rock' limit cycle that, when forced, becomes chaotic - a flight-dynamics instability.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WireWorldCA}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WireWorldCA}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

WireWorld: a four-state automaton (empty, conductor, electron head, electron tail) on which electrons flow down wires - a head becomes a tail, a tail becomes wire, and wire turns to head when one or two neighbours are heads; logic gates emerge. Head density is the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WobbleBass}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WobbleBass}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dubstep wobble bass: a sawtooth through a resonant lowpass swept by a built-in LFO - the talking, wobbling bass of dubstep. Wobble sets the LFO rate, Cutoff the centre, Drive the grit. in0 = Gate, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WobbleBoardOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WobbleBoardOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wobble board: the flexing-sheet-of-metal warble (the Rolf Harris wobble board / thunder-sheet wobble), a low tone deeply modulated in both pitch and amplitude by a slow wobble, the boing-oing-oing drone; the wobble rate sets the flex speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WolfHowlOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WolfHowlOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wolf-howl oscillator: a voiced tone that swells up to a sustained pitch and then slowly falls, shaped by a moving vocal formant, the long mournful arc of a wolf's howl; the howl rate sets how drawn-out each cry is.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WolfTone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WolfTone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wolf tone: when a bowed-string pitch coincides with a strong body resonance, the two modes fight and the note stutters and beats uncontrollably; this models that coupled string-plus-body oscillator in its warbling, near-chaotic regime.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Wolves}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Wolves}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wolf pack - mournful howls that glide up to a sustained pitch and fall away, layered for a pack. Howls sets how often they call, Pitch the howl register, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WompWompOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WompWompOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sad trombone: the womp-womp-womp-wommmp of comic failure, a brassy buzzy tone stepping down through three short notes and a long final slide, the price-is-wrong loss sting; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Woodblock}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Woodblock}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Woodblock / clave: a trigger on in0 fires a short, bright resonant ping - the dry, percussive wood knock. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring length, Level the output. in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WoodblockHit}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WoodblockHit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Woodblock: two short, high, dry resonant modes ring when struck by the input, the hard hollow knock of a woodblock or temple block - feed it an impulse or gate to play.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WoodChipperOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WoodChipperOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wood chipper: branches fed into a chipper, a heavy low motor grind punctuated by violent splintering chunk impacts as the blades shatter the wood, the brutal yard-waste sound; the feed knob sets how often it chews.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WoodpeckerOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WoodpeckerOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Woodpecker drill: a fast train of sharp knocks, each a quickly-damped resonant ping at the wood pitch, fired at the Rate - the hollow, machine-gun drumming of a woodpecker on a trunk; Rate sets the drilling speed, Pitch the resonance of the wood.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WoodsSaxonOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WoodsSaxonOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Woods-Saxon oscillator: each cycle is the nuclear Woods-Saxon profile 1/(1+e^((|x|-R)/a)), a flat-topped pulse with Fermi-shaped (logistic) edges - a flat plateau falling through a sigmoid skirt, a distinct edge profile from the cosine, Planck and linear flat-tops. Soft sets the edge width.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Wormhole}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Wormhole}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wormhole - a swirling vortex of FM-swept tones spiralling through space-time. Swirl sets the sweep rate, Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WrappedCauchyOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WrappedCauchyOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wrapped-Cauchy oscillator: each cycle is the wrapped-Cauchy circular density (1-r^2)/(1+r^2-2r*cos(theta)), a periodic Lorentzian peak whose sharpness Rho sets - from a gentle cosine bump (Rho~0) to a needle-like spike (Rho near 1). Naturally band-limited and periodic.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WrappedExpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WrappedExpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wrapped-exponential oscillator: each cycle is e^(-Lambda*theta) over theta in [0,2pi), a sawtooth whose ramp is exponential rather than linear - it starts high and decays to a floor before snapping back, Lambda setting the decay curvature. A curved saw distinct from the linear ramp.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WrappedNormalOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WrappedNormalOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wrapped-normal oscillator: each cycle is a Gaussian wrapped around the circle (summed over neighbouring turns), a periodic bell whose width Sigma sets - from a sharp central peak to nearly flat as it wraps fully. The Gaussian-peak counterpart of the wrapped-Cauchy/von Mises. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WrapStackOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WrapStackOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wrap stack: the phase is multiplied by each integer 1..N and wrapped to a bipolar ramp, then the wrapped ramps are summed with a 1/k weighting - a sawtooth-like spectrum built purely from modulo phase-wrapping, brightening as you add stacks.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Wurlitzer}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Wurlitzer}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wurlitzer electric piano: a reedy tine voice - a sine fundamental with an odd-harmonic bark on the attack and a warm, woody decay, the bluesy 200A timbre. Tone sets the bark brightness, Decay the note length, Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:XgammaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{XgammaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Xgamma oscillator: each cycle is the xgamma PDF proportional to (1 + (Theta/2)x^2)e^(-Theta*x), a mixture of an exponential and a gamma component - the quadratic weight scales with Theta, so the hump grows and shifts as the decay sharpens. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:XorShiftNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{XorShiftNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Xorshift noise: Marsaglia's xorshift generator (three shift-and-XOR operations on a 32-bit word) produces fast, long-period high-quality white noise with a different spectral signature than a linear-congruential source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Xylophone}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Xylophone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger, Pitch CV}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Struck wooden bar (xylophone): a bright modal voice from three inharmonic partials tuned 1 : 3 : 6 like a rosewood bar, with the high partials decaying fastest for the characteristic hard, woody 'tock'. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the ring (short = staccato), Level the output. Trigger on in0, in1 = Pitch CV.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:YangChenOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{YangChenOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Yang-Chen attractor: a Lorenz-family chaotic system with two coexisting attractors depending on initial conditions, tracing an energetic twin-scroll orbit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:YangMills}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{YangMills}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Yang-Mills mechanics: two coupled particles in the x^2 y^2 potential of classical gauge-field mechanics, an energy-conserving system that is chaotic at essentially all energies - a famously fully-chaotic Hamiltonian.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:YangSystem}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{YangSystem}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Yang system: a chaotic flow lying between the Lorenz and Chen attractors in parameter space, where a small change in one coefficient switches the topology of the twin-scroll - a bridge system in the generalized Lorenz family.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:YawnOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{YawnOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Yawn: the long drawn-out 'aaah' of a yawn, a breathy voiced vowel whose pitch and brightness arch up and then sink as the jaw opens and closes, fired at the slow yawn rate; the sound of sleepiness.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:YukawaOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{YukawaOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Yukawa oscillator: each cycle is the screened-Coulomb potential e^(-Screen*r)/r, a sharp spike at the origin that decays faster than a bare 1/r tail - Screen sets how quickly the tail is cut off, from a long Coulomb-like decay to a tight pulse. Peak-normalized, bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:YuleProcess}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{YuleProcess}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Yule process: a pure-birth Markov chain where each individual independently spawns at a constant rate, so the population grows exponentially with super-exponential bursts (the model of preferential-attachment and species radiation) - a log-scaled accelerating ramp, reseeded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:YuWangOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{YuWangOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Yu-Wang attractor: a chaotic flow with an exponential nonlinearity that produces a sharply-folded, asymmetric orbit - an unusual strange attractor with a distinctive crackling character.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Zap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Zap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Psytrance zap: a trigger on in0 fires a fast descending pitch sweep - a bright saw that drops from Top times the pitch down to Tune in a few milliseconds with a short decay, the classic laser/zap blip and FX accent. Tune sets the landing pitch, Top the start-pitch multiplier (sweep height), Fall the sweep speed, Decay the blip length, and Level the output. Distinct from Impact: a bright high descending laser, not a deep sub boom.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZaslavskyMap}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZaslavskyMap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zaslavsky map: a dissipative kicked-rotor map whose attractor shows a stretched, striped fractal structure - a richly-textured 2-D chaos different from the smooth-flow attractors.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZeemanMachine}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZeemanMachine}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zeeman catastrophe machine: a bistable elastic system whose state sits in a double-well potential and snaps abruptly between wells (a cusp catastrophe) as a control parameter is swept - sudden hysteretic jumps, not smooth chaos.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZernikeOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZernikeOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zernike oscillator: each cycle is a Zernike radial polynomial R_n^0 (the optical-aberration basis) - 2r^2-1, 6r^4-6r^2+1 or 20r^6-30r^4+12r^2-1 by Order - a bipolar polynomial that oscillates with increasing lobes across the cycle, distinct from the Chebyshev/Legendre bases.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZeroCrossFM}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZeroCrossFM}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zero-cross-tracked FM: an internal sine carrier locks to the input's pitch (from its zero crossings) and is phase-modulated by the input itself, generating FM sidebands that follow whatever you feed it.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZhouChenOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZhouChenOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zhou-Chen attractor: a chaotic flow with two quadratic cross-terms and a constant drive that traces a compact, fast-spinning twin-lobed orbit, a distinct entry in the Lorenz-family zoo.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZhuChenFlow}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZhuChenFlow}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zhu-Chen flow: a Lorenz-family chaotic system with a constant drive in its third equation, shifting the attractor off-centre into an asymmetric twin-scroll - another systematically-derived member of the generalized Lorenz zoo.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZipfNoise}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZipfNoise}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zipf noise: picks discrete levels with probability proportional to 1/rank^s (the Zipf/zeta law of word frequencies and city sizes), so a few low levels dominate and high ones are rare - a heavy-headed quantized random source distinct from uniform noise.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZipperOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZipperOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zipper: the brrrip of a zip fastener, a very fast dense train of tiny tooth clicks whose rate (and so pitch) rises as the slider accelerates; the speed knob sets how fast it is yanked.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZipUpOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZipUpOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Zip: a fast zipper or whoosh-up, a buzzy tone that races rapidly upward in pitch with a granular zipping texture, the cartoon quick-exit or zip-up; fired at the set rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

Violin (physical model): a self-oscillating bowed string - an STK two-delay waveguide

## Pitch (28)

\phantomsection\label{blk:Doppler}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Doppler}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Trigger}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Doppler pass-by: makes the input sound like a source moving past the listener - on a trigger the pitch bends up as it approaches then down as it recedes (a varispeed delay line), the level swells to a peak at the closest point, and a low-pass opens at the pass then closes with distance. The car / jet / projectile fly-by of film sound design. Speed sets the pass duration, Amount the pitch-bend depth (how fast the source moves), Distance the closest approach (level and tone contrast), and Mix blends against dry. in0 = Audio, in1 = Trigger.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DriftPitch}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DriftPitch}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Analog pitch drift: a quasi-random slow modulator (two incommensurate LFOs) sweeps a fractional delay so pitch wanders like a detuning analog oscillator - organic, never-repeating instability.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FreqDouble}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FreqDouble}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Frequency doubler: squaring the signal generates a component at twice the frequency; a DC blocker removes the rectification offset, leaving an octave-up tone that tracks the input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FreqShift}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FreqShift}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bode-style single-sideband frequency shifter: splits the input at in0 into in-phase and 90-degree branches via a polyphase IIR Hilbert allpass pair, then heterodynes against an internal carrier to shift every partial by a fixed Hz amount. Shift sets the offset (-1000 to +1000 Hz) and Mix blends the shifted signal against the dry input. Because the shift is in Hz rather than ratio, harmonic relationships break, giving inharmonic, metallic, clangorous tones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GranPitch}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GranPitch}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Granular pitch shifter: two delay read-heads sweep at the pitch-shift ratio and crossfade with overlapping windows, transposing the input up or down in real time without changing its length - classic time-domain pitch shifting.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:H910}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{H910}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Harmonizer: a pitch shifter feeding a feedback delay, so each repeat is pitched again into a rising or falling cascade (the classic H910 fused pitch+delay).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Harmonizer}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Harmonizer}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Single-voice harmonizer that pitch-shifts the input by a chosen musical interval using overlapping windowed delay-line reads, then adds it back to the dry signal. Interval sets the transposition (+/-24 semitones), Detune adds up to 30 cents of offset for a less rigid blend, and Level sets the harmony voice gain. Mix scales the added voice into the output; useful for parallel harmonies and octave doubling.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HarmonizerFixed}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HarmonizerFixed}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Diatonic harmonizer: granular-pitch-shifts the input by a fixed musical interval (third, fourth, fifth, octave) and mixes it under the dry signal, adding a parallel harmony voice without a separate sequencer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:InstFreq}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{InstFreq}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Instantaneous frequency: differentiates the unwrapped phase of the analytic signal (input plus its quadrature) to estimate the signal's frequency continuously - a fast pitch/movement CV that responds within a cycle.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MicroShift}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MicroShift}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Micro-pitch doubler that generates two delay-line voices detuned slightly up and slightly down, then sums them with the dry signal to widen the source. Detune sets the symmetric pitch offset (0-50 cents) and Delay sets the base tap time (0-40 ms) before the shifted voices. Mix sets dry/wet; small Detune thickens, larger Detune broadens into a chorus-like spread.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Octaver}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Octaver}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sub-octave generator: a flip-flop toggled at each rising zero crossing produces a half-rate square tracked by the input envelope, low-passed by Tone for warmth and mixed under the Dry signal at Sub. Monophonic, analog-pedal style - best on clean single notes and bass. It glitches on chords, as expected of the technique.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Octavox}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Octavox}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

A cloud of four pitch-shifted voices at diatonic intervals, each on its own delay tap, summed into a shimmering harmonized pitch-delay (Octavox/Quadravox class).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OctUp}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OctUp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Octave-up doubler: full-wave rectification (which doubles the fundamental) followed by a DC blocker yields an octave-up tone tracking the input, blended back with the dry signal.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PhaseMult}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PhaseMult}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Harmonic phase multiplier: tracks the input period and emits a clean saw at an exact integer multiple of its pitch, generating a precise upper harmonic locked to the source (octaves, fifths-as-3x, etc.).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Pitch2Midi}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Pitch2Midi}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Monophonic pitch tracker that measures the period between rising zero-crossings, converts it to a MIDI note number, and emits it as a control value. Glide smooths the tracked pitch over time for portamento, while Out switches the output between the normalized pitch CV and a simple gate that goes high whenever the input is active. Intended to drive other modules from a played signal rather than to process audio.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PitchCorrect}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PitchCorrect}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Rough auto-tune that detects the input period via zero-crossings, finds the nearest semitone, and applies a crossfaded two-tap windowed pitch shift to pull the note onto pitch. Amount scales how far the signal is moved toward the target semitone, from no correction to full snapping, and Mix blends the corrected voice against the dry input. Best on clean monophonic sources; artifacts increase with stronger correction.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PitchCV}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PitchCV}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pitch-to-CV converter: tracks the input's fundamental from its zero crossings and outputs the frequency as a 0..1 control signal between two limits - a monophonic pitch follower for driving filters or other oscillators.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PitchQuant}\blockicon{pitch}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PitchQuant}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Pitch quantizer: tracks the input pitch from zero crossings, snaps it to the nearest equal-tempered semitone, and emits a clean saw at the quantized pitch - a monophonic auto-tune-to-grid oscillator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
