Scale sets the sensitivity and Smooth low-passes the result to tame noise on fast signals. Pair it with Compare to fire a trigger on a rising or falling edge. The inverse complement of the Integrator.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlopeDetect}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlopeDetect}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Slope detector: outputs a smoothed derivative of the input, positive while it rises and negative while it falls - a movement/transient CV for triggering on direction changes or driving slew-dependent effects.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SmoothMin}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SmoothMin}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: In A, In B}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Polynomial smooth minimum: blends toward min(a,b) but rounds the crossover with a quadratic over a window K (the computer-graphics smin) - a compact-support soft-min distinct from the exponential log-sum-exp, for gentle kink-free combining or ducking of two signals.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Smoothstep}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Smoothstep}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Hermite smoothstep ramp: maps in0 across the [Edge0, Edge1] range through

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Spread}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Spread}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dispersion of three inputs: outputs (max - min) of in0, in1, in2 times Gain -

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Stochastic}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Stochastic}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stochastic-resonance detector (a first as a synth block): the real phenomenon where adding the RIGHT amount of noise lets a weak, sub-threshold signal cross a detector it otherwise never could - too little and the signal stays buried, too much and it drowns. Noise sets the injected level, Threshold the detector, Smooth the output ballistic, and the output is the recovered crossing envelope. Sweep Noise to find the resonance sweet spot where a rhythm hidden below the threshold suddenly emerges.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StolarskyMean}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StolarskyMean}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: In A, In B}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Stolarsky mean: outputs ((a^p-b^p)/(p(a-b)))^(1/(p-1)) of the two input magnitudes, a difference-based family that interpolates the logarithmic, geometric, identric and power means as Power varies - a richly-tunable two-source combiner. Bounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Subtract}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Subtract}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Difference node: in0 minus in1 minus in2, plus a DC Offset. Feed two

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Sum}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Sum}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: A, B, C, D}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Summing node: adds inputs in 1..4 into a single output with no per-input gain - the quick way to merge oscillators, parallel chains or control signals without wiring up a full Mixer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Surprise}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Surprise}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Adaptive novelty detector (a first as a synth block): keeps a self-tuning one-tap linear predictor of the input and outputs the magnitude of its prediction error - a spike whenever the signal does something the recent past did not predict, near silence while it stays predictable. Unlike Slope (the raw derivative, which stays high through any steady ramp) Surprise LEARNS the trend and fires only on genuine novelty: a new note, a sudden jump, a broken pattern. Rate sets how fast it adapts, Smooth the output ballistic.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Switch}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Switch}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: In A, In B, In C, In D}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clocked sequential switch: routes one of the signal inputs in0..in2 to the output and advances to the next on every rising edge of the in3 clock - an addressed router (Doepfer A-151 style) that steps through your sources in time. Steps sets how many inputs are in the rotation (2 or 3). Where Mux selects an input by a manual index and Select chooses between two by level, Switch cycles automatically on a clock.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TapeBrake}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TapeBrake}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: In A, In B}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Tape stop: when the gate goes high the playback speed of a short buffer ramps down to a standstill (pitch and tempo falling together), then spins back up when released - the DJ/turntable brake effect.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Threshold}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Threshold}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Unipolar threshold gate: outputs High when in0 is at or above Threshold and

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TrackAndHold}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TrackAndHold}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: In A, In B}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Track and hold: follows the signal input while the gate is open and freezes the last value when the gate closes - a glitch-free way to freeze a moving signal or build sample-and-hold with a variable aperture.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TrackHold}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TrackHold}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Signal, Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Track-and-hold (Doepfer A-148): while the gate at in1 is high the output follows the in0 signal; when the gate goes low it freezes and holds the last value until the gate returns. Unlike Sample-and-hold (which grabs one instant on a trigger), it tracks continuously through the whole gate - use it to freeze a slow LFO or envelope, glide-and-hold pitch, or window a control signal. A core CV utility.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TrigToGate}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TrigToGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Trigger-to-gate: each rising edge of the in0 trigger opens a gate that stays high for a fixed Length, then closes - turning a momentary pulse (a clock tick, a Compare edge) into a sustained gate of predictable duration. Retriggering restarts the timer, so a stream of pulses keeps the gate open. Drive envelopes, VCAs or effects from short triggers.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TriShape}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TriShape}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phasor-to-triangle shaper: treats in0 as a phase and outputs a triangle that

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WaveSetRepeat}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WaveSetRepeat}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Waveset repeat: detects each cycle between rising zero crossings (a 'waveset') and replays it a number of times before moving on, the Trevor-Wishart waveset-distortion technique that drops pitch and adds buzzy sub-harmonics.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Window}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Window}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Window comparator: outputs High when in0 sits inside the [Lo, Hi] band and

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WindowComp}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WindowComp}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Signal}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Window comparator: outputs a high gate whenever the in0 voltage sits inside the Low..High window, and low when it strays outside - a band detector for triggering events when a CV enters a range, or for extracting a slice of a modulation curve.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WindowGate}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WindowGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Window comparator: outputs a high gate only while the input sits between a lower and upper bound, the analog window-detector used to fire triggers when a control voltage passes through a specific range.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Wrap}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Wrap}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Phase-style wrap: folds in0 back into the [-Range, Range] window by

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:XFade}\blockicon{utility}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{XFade}}\thinspace\textcolor{textDim}{\footnotesize[UTL]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: In A, In B}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Equal-power crossfade: blends two inputs with a constant-power (sine/cosine) law so the perceived loudness stays even across the whole sweep - the correct way to morph or A/B between two signals.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

# Synth (zpwr-synth) (309 blocks)

## Effect (6)

\phantomsection\label{blk:Crusher}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Crusher}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bit-depth reduction plus downsampling (bitcrush).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Folder}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Folder}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Wavefolder: west-coast sine folding with bias.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SynthDelay}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SynthDelay}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Feedback Mod, Time Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Delay line with feedback and a modulation input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SynthDrive}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SynthDrive}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Saturation/overdrive with output trim.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SynthRingMod}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SynthRingMod}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Carrier}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Ring and amplitude modulation of two inputs.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SynthWaveshaper}\blockicon{fx}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SynthWaveshaper}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FX]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Waveshaper: tanh/arctan/polynomial/sine transfer curves.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Envelope (1)

\phantomsection\label{blk:Env}\blockicon{envelope}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Env}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[ENV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Gate}\quad\textcolor{neonGreen}{out: CV}

DAHDSR envelope generator triggered by the gate input. Delay waits before the attack; Hold sustains the peak before the decay (both 0 = classic ADSR).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Filter (2)

\phantomsection\label{blk:DiodeLadder}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DiodeLadder}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Reso Mod, Cutoff Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Diode-ladder low-pass filter (TB-303 lineage) with resonance.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SynthFilter}\blockicon{filter}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SynthFilter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[FLT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio, Reso Mod, Cutoff Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

State-variable filter (low/high/band) with resonance and pitch mod.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Modulation (4)

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoiseLFO}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoiseLFO}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Noise modulation source (white/pink/brown) with optional sample-and-hold.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StepLFO}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StepLFO}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: —}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Step LFO: descending stair or per-step random sample-and-hold, smoothed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SynthLFO}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SynthLFO}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Low-frequency oscillator (sine/tri/saw/square) modulation source. A trigger into in0 (e.g. the note Gate) resets the phase (retrigger); leave it unpatched to free-run. Phase offsets the start point; Smooth slews the output for a softer shape.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SynthSampleHold}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SynthSampleHold}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Signal}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Sample-and-hold of the input at a clocked rate, with glide.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Oscillator (291)

\phantomsection\label{blk:AccordionVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AccordionVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Accordion voice: two or three free reeds sounding the same note slightly detuned (musette tuning) under steady bellows, the warm wavering chorus of an accordion/bandoneon as a note-driven polyphonic voice. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave/Fine tune it, Musette the detune wetness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AcidVCO}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AcidVCO}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM, Pitch Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

TB-303-style acid voice: a single oscillator switchable between the 303's bright sawtooth and square, with the characteristic sub-heavy low end, built to be slammed through a resonant lowpass and envelope for squelchy acid lines. in0 = note, in1 = FM, in2 = pitch mod. Octave/Fine tune it, Wave selects saw or square, Sub the sub-octave level, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AcousticGuitarVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AcousticGuitarVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Acoustic-guitar voice: a steel-string Karplus-Strong pluck with a bright attack and a resonant wooden-body colour, the warm ringing tone of a strummed acoustic. in0 = note, in1 = gate (pluck). Octave/Fine tune it, Damping the decay, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AgogoVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AgogoVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Agogo voice: the gate strikes one of two pitched metal agogo bells (alternating high/low on each strike) with a bright clangy attack and short ring, the Brazilian samba bell. in1 = gate. HiPitch/LoPitch set the two bells, Decay the ring, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AltoFluteVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AltoFluteVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Alto-flute voice: an alto flute, a mellow sine a fourth below the concert flute with a warm breathy air wash and a smooth attack, the haunting middle-register woodwind. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Vibrato the waver, Breath the air noise, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AltoSaxVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AltoSaxVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Alto-sax voice: an alto saxophone, a reedy sawtooth pushed through a resonant bandpass formant with a breathy edge, a quick attack and an expressive vibrato, the soulful jazz lead. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Vibrato the waver, Honk the formant bite, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AnvilVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AnvilVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Anvil voice: a struck iron anvil, a bright clanging cluster of dense inharmonic metal partials with a sharp attack and a noisy ring, the blacksmith's hammer-on-anvil clang. in0 = note, in1 = gate (strike). Octave tunes it, Decay the ring, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ArpVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ArpVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Arpeggiator voice: from one held note it generates a built-in arpeggio, cycling through a chord shape at the set rate in up, down, up-down or random order - an instant single-finger arp. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Rate the arp speed, Chord the triad shape, Mode the direction, Octaves the range, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BagpipeVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BagpipeVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bagpipe voice: a fixed low drone reed sounding continuously beneath a melody chanter reed at the note pitch, both buzzy and slightly detuned, the relentless skirl of Highland pipes. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave tunes the chanter, Drone the drone level, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BalafonVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BalafonVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Balafon voice: a West-African gourd-resonated xylophone, a struck wooden bar whose gourd resonators add a buzzy nasal rattle, the warm buzzing tone of the balafon. in0 = note, in1 = gate (strike). Octave tunes it, Buzz the gourd rattle, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BalalaikaVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BalalaikaVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Balalaika voice: a thin bright triangular-bodied Russian lute, a lightly-damped Karplus pluck with a sharp trebly attack and quick decay, often strummed fast; the plinky tone of the balalaika. in0 = note, in1 = gate (pluck). Octave/Fine tune it, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BandoneonVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BandoneonVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bandoneon voice: the concertina of tango, two or three free reeds slightly detuned under bellows for a wheezy, expressive, vibrato-laden chord tone, more reedy and tremulous than an accordion. in0 = note, in1 = gate (bellows). Octave/Fine tune it, Detune the reed spread, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BanjoVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BanjoVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Banjo voice: a bright lightly-damped Karplus string coupled into a quick drum-head resonance, the twangy plinky fast-decaying attack of a 5-string banjo. in0 = note, in1 = gate (pluck). Octave/Fine tune it, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BaritoneSaxVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BaritoneSaxVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Baritone-sax voice: the big low saxophone, a fat reedy sawtooth through a deep bandpass formant with a heavy breathy growl, a soft attack and vibrato, the honking bass of the sax section. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Vibrato the waver, Honk the formant bite, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BassClarinetVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BassClarinetVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bass-clarinet voice: a low single-reed cylindrical-bore waveguide whose phase-inverting reflection gives the hollow odd-harmonic woody growl an octave below the clarinet. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Breath the pressure, Dark the bore filter, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BassFluteVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BassFluteVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bass-flute voice: a bass flute, a soft sine tone two octaves below the concert flute with a thick breathy air column and a slow attack, the dark velvety low woodwind. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Vibrato the waver, Breath the air noise, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BassoonVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BassoonVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bassoon voice: the low double-reed, a buzzy sawtooth through a dark lowpass that leaves a reedy growl in the bass, with a soft attack and slow vibrato, the woody foundation of the woodwind section. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Vibrato the waver, Reed the growl, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BassoonVoiceWG}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BassoonVoiceWG}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bassoon voice: a double-reed low conical bore, woody and round in the bass with all harmonics, self-oscillating on breath, the comic baritone of the orchestra. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Breath the pressure, Dark the bore filter, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BellTowerVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BellTowerVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Church-bell voice: the gate strikes a large cast bell, voiced from its named partials - hum, prime, tierce (minor third), quint and nominal - with the long swelling-and-fading sustain of a tower bell. in0 = note, in1 = gate. Octave tunes it, Decay the toll length, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BerimbauVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BerimbauVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Berimbau voice: a Brazilian musical bow (the capoeira instrument), a single buzzy plucked string whose pitch toggles with the coin pressure, over a gourd resonance and a faint caxixi rattle. in0 = note, in1 = gate (pluck). Octave/Fine tune it, Buzz the gourd buzz, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlownBottleVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlownBottleVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Blown-bottle voice: noise blown across the mouth of a Helmholtz resonator (a bottle) excites a breathy near-sine tone at the cavity resonance tracking the note, the hooting whistle of blowing over a bottle. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Noise the breath amount, Resonance the cavity Q, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BongoVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BongoVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bongo voice: the gate strikes a small high-pitched hand drum, a bright tight membrane tone with a fast pitch snap and a quick decay, the chattering high partner of the conga. in0 = note (transposes), in1 = gate. Tune sets the head pitch, Decay the ring, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BoobamVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BoobamVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Boobam voice: a tuned tubular drum, a struck membrane over a pitched tube giving a clean focused mid-pitched 'boo' with a quick pitch drop, the melodic boobam. in0 = note, in1 = gate (strike). Octave tunes it, Decay the body, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BouzoukiVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BouzoukiVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bouzouki voice: a Greek long-neck lute, a bright trichordo Karplus pluck layered with an octave course for the metallic ringing sustain of rebetiko. in0 = note, in1 = gate (pluck). Octave/Fine tune it, Octave-course level, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BowedPadVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BowedPadVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bowed-pad voice: a sustained string-section pad built from several bowed-saw layers detuned and gently chorused, with a slow bow-pressure swell on the gate, the lush legato bed of a string ensemble. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave/Fine tune it, Spread the section width, Swell the attack, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BowVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BowVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bowed-string voice: a pitch-tuned delay-line string driven by a stick-slip bow-friction curve while the gate holds, so it self-oscillates into a sustained, singing Helmholtz tone (the bowed cousin of PluckString). in0 = note, in1 = gate (bow on). Octave/Fine tune it, BowForce the grip, Bright the string filter, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrassFallVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrassFallVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brass-fall voice: a brass note that ends with a doit-fall, a sawtooth that sounds steady then bends its pitch downward across the note like the falling tail of a big-band brass hit; the fall depth sets how far it drops. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave tunes it, Fall the drop in semitones, Bright the blare, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrassLeadVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrassLeadVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brass-section lead voice: a stack of detuned saws through a lowpass whose cutoff swells open on the gate (the slow brass attack) then settles, the punchy synth-brass of 80s pop. in0 = note, in1 = gate. Octave/Fine tune it, Swell the attack-opening time, Tone the cutoff ceiling, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrassSectionVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrassSectionVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brass-section voice: a stacked horn section, several detuned saws through a lowpass whose cutoff swells open on the gate (the brass attack) for a punchy ensemble fanfare. in0 = note, in1 = gate. Octave tunes it, Swell the attack-opening, Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BrassSwellVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BrassSwellVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Brass-swell voice: a whole brass section in a slow crescendo, several detuned sawtooth voices through a lowpass that opens as the section swells from nothing up to a full blare, the cinematic brass hit-build. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave tunes it, Swell the crescendo speed, Spread the section width, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Buzz}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Buzz}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM, Pitch Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Buzzer / band-limited impulse train: sums Harmonics equal-amplitude harmonics of the note in closed form (the Dirichlet kernel sin(N*pi*phase)/(N*sin(pi*phase))), giving a bright brassy pulse with a flat harmonic series and no aliasing - the classic Csound buzz generator. Harmonics sets the partial count (brightness), Octave and Fine tune it, and Level scales the output. A rich raw source to feed subtractive filters or formant shaping. The harmonic count is auto-capped so the top partial stays below Nyquist.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Bytebeat}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Bytebeat}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM, Pitch Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Bytebeat oscillator: an algorithmic 8-bit generator that runs a bitwise integer formula over a pitch-driven sample counter, the low byte becoming the waveform - the chiptune/demoscene 'bytebeat' technique that turns shift-and-mask arithmetic into gnarly, ever-evolving digital timbres no analog or wavetable model can make. Formula selects the expression, Octave and Fine drive the counter rate (pitch), and Level scales the output. A world of aliased, glitchy, harmonically rich tones from one line of integer maths.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CajonVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CajonVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cajon voice: the gate strikes a box drum, switching by note register between a deep bass thud (low notes) and a snare-wire buzz against the front face (high notes), the flamenco/acoustic box drum. in0 = note (selects bass vs slap), in1 = gate. Tune sets the bass pitch, Snare the wire buzz, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CastanetsVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CastanetsVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Castanets voice: the gate clicks a pair of hardwood shells, a very short sharp wooden double-click with a high resonant tick, the flamenco castanet snap. in1 = gate. Tone sets the pitch, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CelestaVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CelestaVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Celesta voice: a felt-hammer-struck metal bar over a wooden resonator, a soft sweet bell-like tone with gentle inharmonic overtones and a medium decay, the celesta of orchestral magic. in0 = note, in1 = gate (strike). Octave tunes it, Decay the ring, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CelesteVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CelesteVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Celeste voice: a felt hammer striking a small steel plate over a wooden resonator, the gate triggering a soft sweet bell-like tone with a gentle attack and pure near-octave partials, the celesta. in0 = note, in1 = gate. Octave tunes it, Decay the ring, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CelloVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CelloVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cello voice: the tenor/bass bowed string, a rich sawtooth Helmholtz motion through a deep resonant body with a slow bow attack and a warm vibrato, the soulful cello. in0 = note, in1 = gate (bow). Octave/Fine tune it, Vibrato the waver, Body the resonance, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CelloVoiceWG}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CelloVoiceWG}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cello voice: the bowed-string waveguide tuned to the cello's low range with a darker bow filter, giving a deep woody resonant sustain. in0 = note, in1 = gate (bow). Octave/Fine tune it, BowForce the grip, Dark the bow filter, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChamberOrganVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChamberOrganVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chamber-organ voice: a small soft pipe organ, gentle stopped-flute sine ranks with a slow breathy speak and an instant release, the intimate baroque continuo organ. in0 = note, in1 = gate. Octave tunes it, Ranks the added octave pipes, Tone the cutoff, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChaosVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChaosVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chaotic-oscillator voice: a saw whose pitch and amplitude are continuously perturbed by a logistic-map chaotic generator, so the tone wobbles and shimmers unpredictably (deterministic chaos as modulation) around the played note. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave/Fine tune it, Chaos the logistic r (order->chaos), Depth the modulation amount, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CharangoVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CharangoVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Charango voice: a small bright Andean lute with five paired courses, a high lightly-damped Karplus pluck with a fast shimmering double-course beating, plinky and trebly. in0 = note, in1 = gate (pluck). Octave/Fine tune it, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChiptuneLead}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChiptuneLead}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

NES-style chiptune lead: a band-limited pulse with a stepped duty cycle (12.5/25/50/75%) and a fast internal arpeggio that retriggers a chord shape, the 8-bit square-lead sound of an NES/Famicom. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave/Fine tune it, Duty the pulse width step, Arp the arpeggio speed, Chord the arpeggiated interval shape, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChoirAahVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChoirAahVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Choir 'aah' voice: a bright open-vowel choir, a sawtooth shaped by the bright 'aah' vocal formants with a slow ensemble vibrato across a few detuned voices, the lush massed sound of a choir on aah. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave/Fine tune it, Spread the section width, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChoirOohVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChoirOohVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Choir 'ooh' voice: a dark rounded-vowel choir, the same massed-voice approach but shaped by the low 'ooh' formants for a warm hooting pad, the soft backing of a choir on ooh. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave/Fine tune it, Spread the section width, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChoirVox}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChoirVox}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM, Pitch Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Choir voice: a sawtooth shaped by three fixed vocal formants into a sustained 'aah'/'ooh' choir vowel, with a gentle ensemble vibrato for a sung, breathy pad. in0 = note, in1 = FM, in2 = pitch mod. Octave/Fine tune it, Vowel morphs aah->ooh, Vibrato the pitch waver, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChordOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChordOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Chord oscillator: one note drives a detuned chord of saws.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChordStab}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChordStab}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Paraphonic chord-stab voice: from a single note it generates a whole triad (major, minor or sus) of detuned saws in one voice, the instant rave/house stab you play with one finger. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave/Fine tune it, Chord selects the triad quality, Detune the stack width, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CimbalomVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CimbalomVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cimbalom voice: a Hungarian concert hammered dulcimer, a metal string struck hard (bright quick-attack Karplus) with a resonant box body and long ringing sustain, the dramatic cimbalom of folk and orchestral music. in0 = note, in1 = gate (strike). Octave/Fine tune it, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClarinetVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClarinetVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clarinet voice: a single-reed clarinet, a square wave (odd harmonics only) through a soft lowpass that gives the hollow woody tone of the cylindrical bore, with a smooth attack and light vibrato, the agile mellow woodwind. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Vibrato the waver, Tone the brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClavesVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClavesVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Claves voice: the gate strikes two resonant hardwood sticks, a clean high-pitched woody 'tok' with a quick decay, the crisp clave click that carries a Latin rhythm. in0 = note (transposes), in1 = gate. Tone sets the pitch, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClaveVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClaveVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clave voice: a struck wooden clave or woodblock, a high resonant near-sine ping with a fast woody decay, the bright Latin-percussion click. in1 = gate (trigger). Tune the pitch, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClavichordVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClavichordVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clavichord voice: a string struck and stopped by a metal tangent, a quiet bright Karplus pluck with a hard metallic attack and a tiny pitch-bend wobble (Bebung) from key pressure, the intimate medieval keyboard. in0 = note, in1 = gate (strike). Octave/Fine tune it, Bebung the pressure vibrato, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClavVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClavVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Clavinet voice: a bright struck-string Karplus pluck with a hard attack click and a tight, percussive decay run through a comb-like pickup colour, the funky electric clavinet of 70s funk. in0 = note, in1 = gate (pluck). Octave/Fine tune it, Damping the decay, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ColLegnoVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ColLegnoVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Col-legno voice: striking the string with the wood of the bow, a short percussive woody tick with a faint pitched component and a dry rattle, the eerie col-legno effect. in0 = note, in1 = gate (strike). Octave tunes it, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CombOrganVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CombOrganVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Combo-organ voice (Vox/Farfisa): a bright reedy additive organ summing the first few harmonics into the buzzy, slightly-hollow combo-organ tone of 60s garage rock, distinct from the smoother tonewheel organ. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave tunes it, Bright the upper-harmonic level, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CombResonVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CombResonVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Comb-resonator voice: a pitch-rate impulse train fed into a tuned feedback comb filter, whose evenly-spaced resonant peaks ring out a hollow, metallic, plucked-flanger tone distinct from the noise-burst Karplus pluck. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave/Fine tune it, Feedback the resonance/decay, Bright the damping, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CongaVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CongaVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Conga voice: the gate strikes a hand-drum, a clear pitched membrane tone with a slight upward pitch snap and a short skin decay, the warm open-hand hit of a conga. in0 = note (transposes), in1 = gate. Tune sets the head pitch, Decay the ring, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ContrabassoonVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ContrabassoonVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Contrabassoon voice: the deepest double-reed, a low conical-bore waveguide an octave below the bassoon, a dark rumbling foundation reed. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Breath the pressure, Dark the bore filter, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ContrabassVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ContrabassVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Contrabass voice: the lowest bowed/plucked string, a deep heavily-damped Karplus pluck (pizzicato) with a round woody body and a long fundamental, the orchestral double bass. in0 = note, in1 = gate (pluck). Octave/Fine tune it, Damping the decay, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CornetVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CornetVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cornet voice: a conical-bore cousin of the trumpet, the same buzzy sawtooth but rounder and sweeter through a softer lowpass, the mellow lyrical lead of the brass band. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Vibrato the waver, Bright the blare, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CowbellVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CowbellVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cowbell voice: the gate strikes two square oscillators a non-harmonic interval apart (the 808 cowbell ratio) gated by a sharp decay, the clanky percussion staple. in0 = note (transposes), in1 = gate. Tune sets the pitch, Decay the length, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrossModVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrossModVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Cross-modulation voice: two oscillators each frequency-modulate the other (mutual cross-FM), a feedback timbre generator that breaks into rich, chaotic, metallic spectra a one-way FM operator cannot reach. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave tunes it, Ratio the second oscillator's pitch, Amount the cross-FM depth, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CrotaleVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CrotaleVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Crotale voice: a small tuned bronze disc (antique cymbal), a very high bright ping with widely-stretched inharmonic partials and a long shimmering ring, the crystalline crotale. in0 = note, in1 = gate (strike). Octave tunes it, Decay the ring, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CS80Voice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CS80Voice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

CS-80 voice: the Yamaha CS-80 brass-lead character (Vangelis's Blade Runner sound), two detuned oscillators with a ring-modulated edge through a resonant lowpass, fat and expressive. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave tunes it, Ring the ring-mod amount, Tone the cutoff, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DCO}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DCO}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM, Pitch Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Analog-modeled DCO (Roland Juno digitally-controlled oscillator character): rock-stable pitch (no drift) with a band-limited (PolyBLEP) saw and variable-width pulse blended by Pulse, plus a square Sub oscillator one octave down - the clean, punchy Juno foundation that sits perfectly under a chorus. Octave/Fine tune it, PWM sets the pulse width, Pulse blends saw->pulse, Sub the sub-oscillator level, and Level the output. in1 = FM, in2 = pitch mod.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DidgeridooVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DidgeridooVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Didgeridoo voice: a low lip-buzzed drone under a slowly-moving vocal-tract formant carving the wah overtones, plus a breath rhythm, the meditative drone of a didgeridoo as a playable voice. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave tunes the drone, WahRate the formant motion, Wah the depth, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DistortedGuitarVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DistortedGuitarVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Distorted-guitar voice: an overdriven electric, a high-sustain Karplus string slammed through heavy tanh distortion for a singing, harmonically-saturated rock lead with long feedback-like sustain. in0 = note, in1 = gate (pluck). Octave/Fine tune it, Drive the distortion, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DjembeVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DjembeVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Djembe voice: the gate strikes a West-African goblet drum, a deep bass membrane tone for an open hit blended with a sharp skin-slap noise transient, the rich tone-and-slap of a djembe. in0 = note (transposes), in1 = gate. Tune sets the bass pitch, Slap the slap-noise amount, Decay the ring, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DoubleBassVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DoubleBassVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Double-bass voice: the lowest bowed string, a dark sawtooth Helmholtz motion through a deep heavy body with a slow bow attack, the foundation of the string section. in0 = note, in1 = gate (bow). Octave/Fine tune it, Vibrato the waver, Dark the body, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DownLifterVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DownLifterVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Downlifter FX voice: while the gate is held a tone sweeps steadily downward in pitch over the fall time, the tension-release 'downlifter' that follows a drop; releasing resets it. in1 = gate. Start/End set the sweep range, Time the fall duration, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DrawbarOrganVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DrawbarOrganVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Drawbar-organ voice: a generic additive drawbar organ, eight sine footages you blend from mellow flutes to bright reeds with an instant gate, the all-purpose organ palette. in0 = note, in1 = gate. Octave tunes it, Lower the 16'/8' fundamentals, Upper the bright harmonics, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DroneVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DroneVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Evolving-drone voice: a wide stack of detuned saws whose detune slowly breathes under a triple-rate LFO, building a thick, ever-shifting sustained drone for ambient beds. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave/Fine tune it, Spread the base detune, Motion the LFO depth, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DualFMVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DualFMVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM, Pitch Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dual-modulator FM voice: a sine carrier phase-modulated by two stacked modulator operators at independent ratios, building richer, more evolving FM spectra than a single 2-op pair (a compact 3-op stack). in0 = note, in1 = FM in, in2 = pitch mod. Octave tunes it, RatioA/RatioB the two modulator pitches, Index the FM depth, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DualVCO}\blockicon{pedal}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DualVCO}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[CIRCUIT]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM, Pitch Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Analog-modeled dual VCO (the fat two-oscillator lead/bass, Moog/Prophet character): two band-limited (PolyBLEP) oscillators - osc2 offset by Interval semitones and Detune cents - summed for the classic thick two-VCO tone, with slow analog drift. Distinct from the 7-voice Susaw: this is the tuned 2-oscillator voice. Octave tunes both, Interval sets osc2's pitch offset, Detune the fine beating, Shape sweeps triangle->saw->pulse, Mix the osc1/osc2 balance, and Level the output. in1 = FM, in2 = pitch mod.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DustVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DustVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV)}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Dust voice: a stochastic generator firing random unit impulses at a mean Density (the granular 'dust' source of Xenakis/Roads), a crackling sparse-to-dense impulse field for percussion textures and excitation. Density sets the mean impulses per second, Tone a gentle lowpass colour, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ElectricGrandVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ElectricGrandVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Electric-grand voice (CP-70 style): a Karplus struck-string piano fed through a magnetic pickup, giving an acoustic body with a focused, slightly-electric comb colour, the stage-piano of 70s ballads. in0 = note, in1 = gate (strike). Octave/Fine tune it, Pickup the comb depth, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ElectricGuitarVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ElectricGuitarVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Electric-guitar voice: a clean electric pluck, a Karplus string with a magnetic-pickup comb colour and a tight medium sustain, the bell-clean Strat tone before any amp drive. in0 = note, in1 = gate (pluck). Octave/Fine tune it, Tone the pickup brightness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EnglishHornVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EnglishHornVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

English-horn voice: the alto oboe, a double-reed conical-bore waveguide warmer and rounder than the oboe, the plaintive cor anglais. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Breath the pressure, Warmth the bore filter, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ErhuVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ErhuVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Erhu voice: a bowed two-string Chinese fiddle, a bowed-string waveguide with a narrow nasal bandpass body and a singing vibrato, the expressive crying tone of the erhu. in0 = note, in1 = gate (bow). Octave/Fine tune it, BowForce the grip, Vibrato the waver, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EuphoniumVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EuphoniumVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Euphonium voice: a tenor-tuba, a broad warm sawtooth through a rounded lowpass with a smooth legato attack, the singing baritone of the brass band sitting between trombone and tuba. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Vibrato the waver, Warmth the body, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FairlightVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FairlightVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fairlight voice: the gritty low-bit-rate digital sampler character of the 1980s CMI, a sawtooth deliberately bit-crushed and sample-rate-reduced for the famous lo-fi digital sheen of early sampling. in0 = note, in1 = pitch mod. Octave/Fine tune it, Bits the quantization, Crush the downsample, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FarfisaVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FarfisaVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Farfisa voice: a 1960s transistor combo organ, a bright thin reedy tone from a fixed stack of square-ish harmonics with a fast gate, the buzzy garage/ska organ. in0 = note, in1 = gate. Octave tunes it, Bright the upper-harmonic edge, Vibrato the waver, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FifeVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FifeVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Fife voice: a high marching fife, a shrill near-sine tone an octave above the flute with a strong air hiss and a fast attack, the piercing whistle of the fife-and-drum corps. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Vibrato the waver, Breath the air noise, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FlugelhornVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FlugelhornVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Flugelhorn voice: a mellow conical-bore lip-reed brass, darker and rounder than a trumpet, the warm flugelhorn of jazz ballads. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Breath the pressure, Mellow the bell filter, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FluteVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FluteVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Flute voice: a concert flute, a nearly pure sine tone coloured by a wash of breath noise across the embouchure with a quick attack and a light vibrato, the airy lyrical woodwind. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Vibrato the waver, Breath the air noise, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FluteVoiceWG}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FluteVoiceWG}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Flute waveguide voice: an open-bore delay line excited by an air-jet cubic nonlinearity while the gate (breath) is held, giving the breathy, pure tone of a flute or whistle - the jet-driven counterpart to the reed-driven ReedVoice. in0 = note, in1 = gate (breath). Octave/Fine tune it, Breath the pressure, Noise the air hiss, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FMBassVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FMBassVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

FM-bass voice: a punchy two-operator FM bass with a tight pitch-tracked modulator and a fast index decay for a clicky attack over a deep body, the snappy digital bass of FM synths. in0 = note, in1 = gate. Octave tunes it, Ratio the modulator pitch, Click the attack index, Decay the body, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FMBellVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FMBellVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Two-operator FM bell voice: the gate strikes a sine carrier phase-modulated by a non-integer-ratio sine operator whose index decays, so the spectrum starts bright and clangorous and settles to a pure tone - the classic DX bell/marimba. in0 = note, in1 = gate. Octave tunes it, Ratio the modulator pitch, Index the initial FM depth, Decay the ring/index time, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FMStackVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FMStackVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM, Pitch Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Six-operator FM voice: six sine operators at harmonic ratios wired by a selectable algorithm (a serial modulator chain, three 2-op pairs, or all-parallel additive), the full DX-style FM engine in one block. in0 = note, in1 = FM in, in2 = pitch mod. Octave tunes it, Algorithm picks the wiring, Index the FM depth, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FormantGrowl}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FormantGrowl}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM, Pitch Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Growl-bass voice: a sawtooth driven hard through tanh distortion then a resonant bandpass formant, giving the snarling, vocal, talkbox-like growl of a dubstep/neuro bass. in0 = note, in1 = formant sweep (added to Formant), in2 = pitch mod. Octave/Fine tune it, Drive the distortion, Formant the vowel peak, Reso its sharpness, Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FormantOsc}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FormantOsc}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM, Pitch Mod}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}

Vowel oscillator: a sawtooth driven through two resonant formant band-passes whose centre frequencies morph across the A-E-I-O-U vowels (Vowel), giving a singing/talking tone. Unlike FOF granular synthesis this is a buzz-through-resonators model; Res sets formant sharpness and Level the output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FormantSeqVoice}\blockicon{wave}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FormantSeqVoice}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentLight}{\footnotesize[OSC]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonMagenta}{\footnotesize[SYNTH]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Pitch (CV), FM}\quad\textcolor{neonCyan}{out: Audio}
