Modal shift: brightens or darkens the key by raising the fourth toward Lydian or lowering the third, sixth and seventh toward natural minor, recolouring a part's mode without retransposing it.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NegativeHarmony}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NegativeHarmony}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Negative harmony: reflects every note about the axis halfway between the tonic and dominant (Ernst Levy's mirror), swapping major for minor and turning a progression into its tonal shadow while keeping the same harmonic function.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NeoRiemann}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NeoRiemann}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Neo-Riemannian triad transforms: builds a major/minor triad on each note and applies P (parallel), L (leading-tone) or R (relative) - the maximally-smooth chord moves of late-Romantic and film harmony, each sharing two common tones with the input.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Octave}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Octave}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Doubles each note up and/or down by whole octaves.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OctaveClamp}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OctaveClamp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Octave clamp: folds every incoming note into the single octave starting at Center by collapsing its pitch class, flattening wide-ranging input into one register - a pitch-class monitor or tight-range re-voicer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OctaveFoldGate}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OctaveFoldGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Octave fold: collapses every note into a single octave above a base note (keeping only its pitch class), squashing a wide-ranging part into one register - useful for bass-monitoring or extreme register clamping.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OctaveStackAdd}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OctaveStackAdd}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Octave stack: layers each note with a copy an octave above and/or below, fattening a thin line into a multi-octave stack (organ-style); choose which octaves to add.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OvertoneStack}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OvertoneStack}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Overtone stack: layers quieter harmonic-series voices (octave, octave+fifth, two octaves) above each note with a geometric Rolloff, thickening a single note toward an organ-like harmonic chord.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PitchClassGate}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PitchClassGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Pitch-class gate: passes only notes whose pitch class belongs to the chosen Root and Mode set (chromatic, major, minor, pentatonic or whole-tone), filtering a stream down to a key without transposing anything.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PitchMirror}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PitchMirror}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Pitch mirror: reflects every note around a fixed axis pitch, so ascending lines become descending and the melody is turned upside-down about a chosen centre (a literal pitch inversion, key-independent).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PowerChordAdd}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PowerChordAdd}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Power chord: doubles every note with a perfect fifth above (and optionally the octave on top), instantly turning a single line into the root-fifth power chords of rock guitar.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RangeCompress}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RangeCompress}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Range compress: linearly squeezes the played pitch range toward a center note, narrowing a wide-ranging part into a tighter register (or, near ratio 1, leaving it alone) - a melodic-ambitus compressor.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RangeExpand}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RangeExpand}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Range expand: stretches the played pitch range away from a center note (the inverse of range-compress), exaggerating a cramped melody into a wider, more dramatic ambitus; near ratio 1 it leaves the part alone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RangeGate}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RangeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Range gate: passes only notes whose pitch lies inside a Low..High window, masking a register in or out of the part; Invert mutes the window instead of keeping it.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ReflectOctave}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ReflectOctave}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Reflect octave: mirrors each note's pitch class about a pivot while keeping it in its own octave, inverting the melodic contour within every register at once - octave-preserving interval inversion.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RepeatedNoteGate}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RepeatedNoteGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Repeated-note gate: drops a note-on when it repeats the previous kept pitch, passing only when the melody actually changes note - a de-stutter that removes hammered repeats; Invert keeps only the repeats.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Scale}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Scale}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Quantizes incoming notes to the nearest degree of the chosen root and scale.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SubHarmonicAdd}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SubHarmonicAdd}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Sub-harmonic add: reinforces every note with undertone voices an octave (Sub1) and an octave-plus-fifth (Sub2) below at adjustable levels - the subharmonic weight of a Trautonium or organ bourdon.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SubharmonicShift}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SubharmonicShift}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Subharmonic shift: drops each note to the nearest undertone (subharmonic) of a ceiling pitch, the mirror of the harmonic series, building dark, minor-tilted lines beneath an upper anchor note.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Transpose}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Transpose}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Shifts every note by a fixed semitone amount plus a modulatable offset.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TwelveToneRow}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TwelveToneRow}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Twelve-tone row: enforces Schoenberg's serial rule - no pitch class may sound again until all twelve have been played - dropping early repeats so the stream is forced into complete chromatic rows.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:UnisonDetuneAdd}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{UnisonDetuneAdd}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Unison detune: layers a second copy of each note slightly detuned (via per-note microtuning), thickening a mono line into a wide chorused unison without a separate effect.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VoiceLead}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VoiceLead}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Smooth voice leading: places each note's pitch class in the octave nearest the previous output, so a line or chord progression moves by the smallest interval. Range caps the leap; past it the original octave is kept.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WholeToneSnap}\blockicon{harmony}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WholeToneSnap}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[HRM]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Whole-tone snap: quantizes every note to the nearest degree of the six-note whole-tone scale from Root, dissolving tonality into the dreamy, rootless ambiguity that scale is famous for.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Modulation (60)

\phantomsection\label{blk:AvgPitchCC}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AvgPitchCC}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Average-pitch CC: emits a controller that follows the mean pitch of all held notes (the chord's centroid), a smooth register-position modulation source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BendFromVelocity}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BendFromVelocity}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Bend from velocity: emits a pitch-bend on each note-on scaled by how hard you played, so harder hits bend up further (an expressive velocity-to-pitch gesture); Depth sets the range.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BluesInflect}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BluesInflect}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Blues inflect: bends the third, fifth and seventh of a major key downward toward the blue notes (the cracks between the piano keys that a blues singer or guitarist bends into), adding microtonal blues feel without changing the notes played.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BohlenPierceTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BohlenPierceTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Bohlen-Pierce tune: retunes the keyboard to the Bohlen-Pierce scale, which divides not the octave but the 3:1 'tritave' into 13 equal steps, giving an alien but surprisingly consonant tuning built on odd-harmonic ratios.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ByzantineTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ByzantineTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Byzantine tune: retunes to a Byzantine chant scale, whose soft-chromatic genus uses neutral intervals absent from Western tuning, the sound of Eastern Orthodox liturgical melody.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CarlosAlphaTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CarlosAlphaTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Carlos Alpha tune: retunes to Wendy Carlos's Alpha scale of 78-cent equal steps, a non-octave temperament engineered to nail pure major thirds and fifths at the cost of ever repeating at the octave - a shimmering, otherworldly intonation.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CarlosBetaTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CarlosBetaTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Carlos Beta tune: retunes to Wendy Carlos's Beta scale, built from a ~63.8-cent step that deliberately does not repeat at the octave, giving the uncanny non-octave harmony of her Beauty in the Beast.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CarlosGammaTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CarlosGammaTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Carlos Gamma tune: retunes to Wendy Carlos's Gamma scale, an ultra-fine ~35.1-cent micro-step tuning that renders both perfect fifths and major thirds nearly pure, the smoothest of her invented scales.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CCGlide}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CCGlide}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Portamento for a CC: smooths the watched CC toward each newly received value over Time, emitting interpolated CC each block - de-zippers a stepped controller, where MidiSlew only glides the internal scalar.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CCLFO}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CCLFO}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Continuous CC LFO generator (sine/triangle/saw/square) emitted on a chosen CC at a free Rate - automates a synth parameter over MIDI, where MidiLFO drives only the internal mod-matrix scalar.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CCSeq}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CCSeq}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Tempo-synced step CC sequencer: one CC value per synced step cycling an 8-step shape (overridable via the node's config list) - a modulation sequencer in the CC domain, where StepSeqMidi sequences notes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChordSpanCC}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChordSpanCC}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Chord-span CC: emits a controller that reflects the interval span (highest minus lowest) of the held notes, full at Range semitones - wide voicings push it up.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ExpressionSwell}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ExpressionSwell}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Expression swell: emits a CC11 expression value that ramps up from zero after each note-on over the swell time, an automatic volume fade-in / crescendo per note for strings and pads.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GamelanDetune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GamelanDetune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Gamelan detune: tunes alternate keys slightly apart so paired notes shimmer with the slow acoustic beating of a gamelan's deliberately-mistuned instrument pairs (ombak) - an ensemble-wide chorused waver.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Generative}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Generative}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Probabilistic melodic random walk synced to a rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GoldenAngleDetune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GoldenAngleDetune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Golden-angle detune: scatters each successive note's microtuning by the golden angle, the maximally-even irrational spacing, so repeated notes never land on the same detune - an organic, never-repeating pitch shimmer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HarmonicSeriesSnap}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HarmonicSeriesSnap}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Harmonic-series snap: detunes each note to the nearest member of the natural harmonic series above a root, replacing tempered intervals with the pure, beatless ratios of just intonation as a brass or didgeridoo would sound them.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HarmonicSeriesTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HarmonicSeriesTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Harmonic-series tune: retunes every note to the nearest member of the natural overtone series of a root (the just-intonation pitches a vibrating string actually produces), so chords lock into pure, beatless ratios that no equal scale can reach.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HighNoteCC}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HighNoteCC}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

High-note CC: emits a controller that tracks the pitch of the highest held note, so the top voice of your playing steers a synth parameter (filter, level, ...).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HirajoshiTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HirajoshiTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Hirajoshi tune: retunes to the Japanese hirajoshi pentatonic, the dark, koto-flavoured five-note scale of traditional Japanese music, snapped from the played notes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:IntervalCC}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{IntervalCC}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Interval CC: emits a controller on each note-on from how far it leaps from the previous note, so stepwise lines read low and big jumps read high, full at Range semitones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JustMinorTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JustMinorTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Just-minor tune: retunes to a 5-limit just-intonation natural minor (pure 6/5 third and 3/2 fifth), giving beatless minor chords impossible in equal temperament.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LowNoteCC}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LowNoteCC}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Low-note CC: emits a controller that tracks the pitch of the lowest held note, so the bass voice of your playing steers a synth parameter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MaqamRastTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MaqamRastTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Maqam Rast tune: retunes to the Arabic maqam Rast, whose neutral third and sixth sit a quarter-tone below the Western major, giving the characteristic in-between intervals of Middle-Eastern melody.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MeantoneTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MeantoneTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Meantone tune: retunes to quarter-comma meantone, the Renaissance/Baroque temperament with pure (beatless) major thirds bought at the price of a howling 'wolf' fifth, the sound of early-music keyboards.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MicrotuneSpread}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MicrotuneSpread}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Microtune spread: gives every note a small random pitch detune up to +/-Cents via its microtuning offset, loosening rigid tuning into a chorused, slightly-out ensemble shimmer.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MidiEnv}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MidiEnv}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

ADSR envelope triggered by the note gate, on the scalar control output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MidiLFO}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MidiLFO}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Low-frequency modulation source (sine/tri/saw/square) on the scalar control output.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MidiRandom}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MidiRandom}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Generates random notes within a pitch range at a synced rate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MidiSampleHold}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MidiSampleHold}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Samples and holds the incoming control value on each note.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MidiSlew}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MidiSlew}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Smooths (glides) the control value over a set time.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NEdoRetune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NEdoRetune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

N-EDO retune: detunes every note from 12-tone equal temperament to the nearest step of an N-equal-divisions-of-the-octave scale, opening up 19, 24, 31 or any microtonal grid without changing the played note numbers.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoteOnTrigCC}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoteOnTrigCC}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Note-on trigger CC: fires a CC pulse that jumps to full on every note-on and decays away, a built-in trigger envelope you can route to a synth parameter; Decay sets the fall time.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoteToPressure}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoteToPressure}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Note-to-pressure: emits a channel-aftertouch value scaled from each note's velocity, so how hard you strike also drives any pressure-mapped destination (vibrato, filter, swell) - turning a non-aftertouch keyboard into an expressive one.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PartchTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PartchTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Partch tune: retunes to an 11-limit just-intonation subset in the spirit of Harry Partch's 43-tone tonality, the ratio-based microtonality of his hand-built instruments.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PelogTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PelogTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Pelog tune: retunes to a Javanese pelog scale - seven unequal steps with characteristic narrow and wide intervals - the more tense, expressive counterpart to slendro in gamelan music.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PersianTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PersianTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Persian tune: retunes to a Persian dastgah scale with its characteristic koron (quarter-flat) degrees, the microtonal intervals of classical Persian music played on the tar and santur.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PitchBendLFO}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PitchBendLFO}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Pitch-bend LFO: emits a free-running pitch-bend sine that wobbles the tuning continuously whether or not notes are playing - a global vibrato/auto-bend source independent of any single note; Rate and Depth shape the sweep.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PitchClassCC}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PitchClassCC}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Pitch-class CC: emits a controller on each note-on from the note's pitch class (its position 0-11 within the octave, octave-independent), a harmonic-colour modulation source.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PitchDrift}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PitchDrift}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Nudges each note's pitch by a small random detune up to +/-Cents (per-note, polyphonic) - the gentle oscillator instability of analog gear, applied to pitch where Humanize only scatters timing and velocity.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PitchScoop}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PitchScoop}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Pitch scoop: emits a pitch-bend that starts bent below each new note and swoops up to true pitch over the scoop time, the expressive slide-into-note of a fretless or vocal attack.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PolyCountCC}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PolyCountCC}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Poly-count CC: emits a controller that reflects how many notes are held (chord density), reaching full value at Max voices - play thicker chords to open a parameter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PressSwell}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PressSwell}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Generates a channel-pressure (aftertouch) swell that rises to Max over Rise while any note is held, snapping back to zero on release - an automatic crescendo for pads and strings, where ATProc only transforms incoming aftertouch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PythagoreanTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PythagoreanTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Pythagorean tune: retunes to 3-limit Pythagorean intonation built from a spiral of pure fifths, giving brilliant, wide major thirds and the bright, slightly-sharp leading tones of medieval and string-ensemble tuning.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:QuarterToneShift}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{QuarterToneShift}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Quarter-tone shift: detunes every note by up to a quarter-tone via its microtuning offset, sliding the whole performance off the 12-tone grid into 24-tone territory without changing the note numbers.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:QuarterToneTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{QuarterToneTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Quarter-tone tune: retunes to 24-tone equal temperament, snapping every note to the nearest 50-cent quarter-tone, the standard grid of Arabic maqam notation and much 20th-century microtonal music.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RagaBhairavTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RagaBhairavTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Raga Bhairav tune: retunes to the just-intonation shrutis of the North-Indian morning raga Bhairav (with its flat second and flat sixth), bringing the pure, beatless intervals of Indian classical tuning.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RandOctave}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RandOctave}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Randomly shifts notes by up to +/- Range octaves with a chance.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RandomCC}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RandomCC}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Random CC: fires a random value on a chosen controller number with every note-on, an instant source of evolving per-note modulation (filter, pan, anything) that never repeats; Amount sets the spread around center.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SeptimalTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SeptimalTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Septimal tune: retunes to a 7-limit just scale including the harmonic seventh (7/4), the deep, locked-in dominant-seventh sound prized in barbershop and blues harmony.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SlendroTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SlendroTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Slendro tune: retunes the keyboard to a Javanese gamelan slendro scale - five near-equal steps spanning the octave - giving the floating, anchorless quality of Indonesian slendro tuning, snapped from the nearest played note.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StretchTuning}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StretchTuning}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Stretch tuning: applies a Railsback-style octave stretch, tuning notes progressively sharp as they rise and flat as they fall, the psychoacoustic tuning real pianos use so octaves sound in tune to the ear.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SubharmonicTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SubharmonicTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Subharmonic tune: retunes every note to the nearest member of the undertone (subharmonic) series descending from a top pitch, the mirror image of the overtone series that gives eerie, hollow, just-intoned minor sonorities.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SwellPressure}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SwellPressure}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Swell pressure: emits a channel-pressure that rises the longer notes are held and falls once they release, an automatic aftertouch swell for sustained pads; Rate sets the speed.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThaiTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThaiTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Thai tune: retunes to the Thai 7-tone equal temperament (seven equal steps to the octave), the tuning of the Thai ranat and pi phat ensemble that sits between the Western diatonic pitches.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TurkishMakamTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TurkishMakamTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Turkish makam tune: retunes to a Turkish makam built on Holdrian (53-comma) steps, whose microtonal neutral seconds and sevenths give the characteristic intervals of Ottoman classical melody.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VelToCC}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VelToCC}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Turns each note's velocity into a CC value emitted just before the note, so a synth's filter or level opens with how hard you played - dynamics routed to expression, where Note2CC sends pitch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VelToDetune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VelToDetune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Velocity-to-detune: maps how hard each note is played to a microtuning detune, so dynamics bend pitch slightly sharp or flat - the pitch-pulls-with-force behaviour of acoustic instruments and analog drift.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WerckmeisterTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WerckmeisterTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Werckmeister tune: retunes to Werckmeister III, the famous 1691 well-temperament whose unequal fifths give each key its own colour (the 'well-tempered' tuning of Bach's era) - subtle, key-dependent detuning from equal temperament.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:YoungTune}\blockicon{lfo}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{YoungTune}}\thinspace\textcolor{neonGreen}{\footnotesize[MOD]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Young tune: retunes to Thomas Young's 1799 well-temperament, where each key has its own subtly different colour - near-pure common keys, spicier remote ones - the way Classical-era keyboards actually sounded.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Probability (180)

\phantomsection\label{blk:AbundantGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AbundantGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Abundant gate: passes note-ons only on abundant counts - numbers whose proper divisors sum to more than themselves (12, 18, 20, 24...) - thinning the stream onto the divisor-rich integers; Invert keeps the deficient ones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AchillesGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AchillesGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Achilles gate: passes note-ons on Achilles counts - powerful numbers (every prime factor squared) that are not themselves a perfect power (72,108,200,288,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AdditivePrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AdditivePrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Additive-prime gate: passes note-ons on additive-prime counts that are prime and whose digit sum is also prime (2,3,5,7,11,23,29,41,43,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AlmostPrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AlmostPrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Almost-prime gate: passes note-ons on k-almost-prime counts having exactly K prime factors counted with multiplicity (K=1 primes, K=2 semiprimes, K=3 ...), one knob spanning the whole almost-prime family; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AntiprimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AntiprimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Antiprime gate: passes note-ons on highly-composite (antiprime) counts that have more divisors than every smaller number (1,2,4,6,12,24,36,48,60,...), the most-divisible records; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AntiRepeatGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AntiRepeatGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Anti-repeat gate: drops a note when it repeats the immediately-previous pitch, forcing the line to keep moving instead of hammering one note - an automatic de-stutter for melodic variety.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AscendingDigitGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AscendingDigitGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Ascending-digit gate: passes note-ons whose decimal digits strictly increase from left to right (1..9,12,13,...,123,124,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:AutomorphicGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{AutomorphicGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Automorphic gate: passes note-ons on automorphic counts whose square ends in the number itself (5,6,25,76,376,625,...), a curious self-reproducing-under-squaring set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BellNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BellNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Bell-number gate: passes note-ons on Bell-number counts (1,2,5,15,52,203,877,...), which count the ways to partition a set into groups; their fast growth thins the stream quickly. Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BinaryPalindromeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BinaryPalindromeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Binary-palindrome gate: passes note-ons whose count reads the same in binary forwards and backwards (1,3,5,7,9,15,17,21,...), a self-mirroring bit pattern distinct from decimal palindromes; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BinaryWeightGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BinaryWeightGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Binary-weight gate: passes note-ons whose count has exactly the chosen number of 1-bits in binary (its Hamming weight), a bit-pattern sieve that thins to sparser, more clustered hits as the weight rises; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:BlumIntegerGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{BlumIntegerGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Blum-integer gate: passes note-ons on Blum counts that are the product of two distinct primes both congruent to 3 mod 4 (21,33,57,69,77,...), the moduli behind Blum-Blum-Shub cryptography; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CakeNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CakeNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Cake-number gate: passes note-ons on cake counts (the most pieces a cake splits into with k planar cuts: 1,2,4,8,15,26,42,...), the 3-D analogue of the lazy-caterer numbers; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CarmichaelGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CarmichaelGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Carmichael gate: passes note-ons on Carmichael counts - squarefree composites that fool the Fermat primality test (561,1105,1729,...) - via Korselt's criterion; an extremely sparse set. Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CatalanGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CatalanGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Catalan gate: passes note-ons only on Catalan-number counts (1,2,5,14,42,...), the combinatorial sequence counting trees and bracketings, which thins out fast as the values explode; Invert keeps the others.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CatalanParityGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CatalanParityGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Catalan-parity gate: passes note-ons when the Catalan number C(count) is odd - which happens only when the count-plus-one is a power of two - giving a sparse, exponentially-spaced pattern; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CenteredCubeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CenteredCubeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Centered-cube gate: passes note-ons on centered-cube counts (1,9,35,91,189,...), the dots in a cube grown shell by shell around a center; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CenteredDecagonalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CenteredDecagonalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Centered-decagonal gate: passes note-ons on centered-decagonal counts (1,11,31,61,101,...), a ten-sided figure grown ring by ring around a center; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CenteredHexGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CenteredHexGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Centered-hexagonal gate: passes note-ons on centered-hexagonal counts (1,7,19,37,61,...), the numbers of dots in a growing hexagonal grid (and the rows of Pascal-triangle hex packing); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CenteredNonagonalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CenteredNonagonalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Centered-nonagonal gate: passes note-ons on centered-nonagonal counts (1,10,28,55,91,...), a nine-sided figure grown ring by ring around a center, which are also every third triangular number; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CenteredPentagonalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CenteredPentagonalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Centered-pentagonal gate: passes note-ons on centered-pentagonal counts (1,6,16,31,51,76,...), a pentagon grown ring by ring around a center dot; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CenteredSquareGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CenteredSquareGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Centered-square gate: passes note-ons on centered-square counts (1,5,13,25,41,...), the dots in a square diamond grown ring by ring; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CenteredTriangularGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CenteredTriangularGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Centered-triangular gate: passes note-ons on centered-triangular counts (1,4,10,19,31,...), a triangular grid grown outward from a central dot; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Chance}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Chance}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Probabilistic gate: lets each note through with the set probability.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CircularPrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CircularPrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Circular-prime gate: passes note-ons on circular-prime counts where every cyclic rotation of the decimal digits is itself prime (2,3,5,7,11,13,17,37,79,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CollatzGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CollatzGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Collatz gate: runs the 3n+1 process on each note's count and passes it when the number of steps to reach 1 is even - an erratic but deterministic gate straight from the unsolved Collatz conjecture; Invert takes the odd-length ones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CollatzPeakGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CollatzPeakGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Collatz-peak gate: passes note-ons whose count sends its Collatz (3n+1) trajectory soaring above Mult times the starting value, spotlighting the counts that take the wildest hailstone excursions; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ContraryGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ContraryGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Contrary gate: passes a note only if it moves in the opposite direction to the previous melodic step, forcing the line into constant zig-zag contrary motion and filtering out runs that keep climbing or falling.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoprimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoprimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Coprime gate: passes a note only when its count shares no common factor with the modulus (gcd = 1), thinning the stream onto the totatives of the modulus - a number-theoretic sieve; Invert keeps the non-coprime counts.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CoprimeStepGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CoprimeStepGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Coprime-step gate: passes a note only when its running count shares no common factor with the previous kept count (their gcd is 1), a number-theoretic thinning that favours relatively-prime spacings; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CousinPrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CousinPrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Cousin-prime gate: passes note-ons on counts in a cousin-prime pair (two primes four apart: 3,7,13,17,19,23,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CubeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CubeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Cube gate: passes note-ons on perfect-cube counts (1,8,27,64,125,...), an exponentially-widening figurate spacing; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:CullenGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{CullenGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Cullen gate: passes note-ons on Cullen-number counts (n*2^n+1: 3,9,25,65,161,385,...), the +1 companions of the Woodall numbers; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DeficientGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DeficientGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Deficient gate: passes note-ons on deficient counts whose proper divisors sum to less than the number (1,2,3,4,5,7,8,9,10,11,...; the majority of integers); Invert keeps the abundant/perfect ones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DelannoyGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DelannoyGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Delannoy gate: passes note-ons on central-Delannoy counts (1,3,13,63,321,1683,...), the king-move lattice paths from corner to corner of a grid; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DescendingDigitGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DescendingDigitGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Descending-digit gate: passes note-ons of 2+ digits whose decimal digits strictly decrease from left to right (10,20,21,30,31,32,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DigitalRootGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DigitalRootGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Digital-root gate: passes note-ons whose count has a chosen digital root (the single digit reached by repeatedly summing digits), giving a clean period-9 repeating pattern; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DigitalRootPrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DigitalRootPrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Digital-root-prime gate: passes note-ons whose digital root is a prime digit (2, 3, 5 or 7), a clean period-9 repeating pattern; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DigitCountGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DigitCountGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Digit-count gate: passes note-ons whose running count has exactly the chosen number of decimal digits, opening for a block of counts then closing - a coarse decimal-magnitude window; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DigitFactorialGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DigitFactorialGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Factorion gate: passes note-ons on the rare factorion counts equal to the sum of the factorials of their digits (1,2,145,40585); mostly silent, firing only on those four. Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DigitGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DigitGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Digit-sum gate: passes a note unless the decimal digit-sum of its count is divisible by Modulo, carving a self-similar arithmetic rhythm out of a steady stream; Invert keeps only the divisible ones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DigitProductGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DigitProductGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Digit-product gate: passes a note only when the product of its count's decimal digits exceeds a threshold, so counts containing a zero or small digits drop out - a digit-driven thinning with a tunable density. Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DigitProductPrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DigitProductPrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Digit-product-prime gate: passes note-ons where the product of the nonzero decimal digits is prime - exactly one prime digit (2,3,5,7) among ones (2,3,5,7,12,13,15,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DigitSumGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DigitSumGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Digit-sum gate: passes note-ons whose decimal digit sum equals the chosen target, a repeating digit-driven pattern whose density you set with the target; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DigitSumSquareGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DigitSumSquareGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Digit-sum-square gate: passes note-ons whose decimal digit sum is a perfect square (1,4,9,16,...), so counts summing to 1, 4, 9 or 16 pass; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DivisorCountGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DivisorCountGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Divisor-count gate: passes note-ons whose count has exactly the chosen number of divisors (tau=2 selects primes, tau=3 prime squares, and so on), a divisor-structure sieve; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DivisorParityGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DivisorParityGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Divisor-parity gate: passes note-ons on counts with an odd number of divisors, which are exactly the perfect squares (1,4,9,16,25,...) - a gate that fires on an ever-widening square spacing; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DoubleFactorialGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DoubleFactorialGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Double-factorial gate: passes note-ons on double-factorial counts n!! - the product of integers of the same parity down to 1 (1,2,3,8,15,48,105,384,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:DudeneyGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{DudeneyGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Dudeney gate: passes note-ons on Dudeney counts that are perfect cubes whose digit sum equals the cube root (1,512,4913,5832,17576,19683); an extremely rare set. Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EmirpGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EmirpGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Emirp gate: passes note-ons on emirp counts - primes that turn into a different prime when their digits are reversed (13,17,31,37,71,73,79,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EquidigitalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EquidigitalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Equidigital gate: passes note-ons on equidigital counts that take the same number of digits to write as their prime factorization does (1,2,3,5,7,10,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EvenDigitGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EvenDigitGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Even-digit gate: passes note-ons whose count is written with only even decimal digits (2,4,6,8,20,22,24,...), a simple digit-pattern thinning; Invert passes counts containing an odd digit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:EvenDigitSumGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{EvenDigitSumGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Even-digit-sum gate: passes note-ons whose decimal digit sum is even, a near-even-split digit pattern; Invert keeps the odd-digit-sum counts.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ExtravagantGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ExtravagantGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Extravagant gate: passes note-ons on extravagant counts whose prime factorization takes MORE digits to write than the number itself (4,6,8,9,12,18,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FermatPrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FermatPrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Fermat-prime gate: passes note-ons on the five known Fermat primes (2^(2^k)+1: 3,5,17,257,65537), the primes behind constructible polygons; an exceedingly rare set. Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FibGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FibGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Fibonacci gate: passes a note unless the Fibonacci number of its ordinal is divisible by Modulo, carving a golden, self-similar rhythm out of a steady stream; Invert keeps the divisible ones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FibonacciGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FibonacciGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Fibonacci gate: passes note-ons on Fibonacci-number counts (1,2,3,5,8,13,21,34,...), the golden-ratio recurrence whose gaps grow geometrically; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FibonacciPrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FibonacciPrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Fibonacci-prime gate: passes note-ons on Fibonacci counts that are also prime (2,3,5,13,89,233,1597,...), a doubly-special sparse set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FrugalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FrugalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Frugal gate: passes note-ons on frugal counts that take fewer digits to write than their prime factorization does (125=5^3, 128=2^7, ...), an economical-number sieve; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GapGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GapGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Gap gate: passes a note only after at least Gap notes have gone by since the last one it let through, a refractory thinner that guarantees a minimum spacing between surviving notes regardless of input density.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
