\phantomsection\label{blk:GeneralizedPentagonalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GeneralizedPentagonalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Generalized-pentagonal gate: passes note-ons on generalized-pentagonal counts (1,2,5,7,12,15,22,26,...), the exponents in Euler's pentagonal-number-theorem partition product; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GoldenGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GoldenGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Golden gate: passes notes by a golden-ratio low-discrepancy sequence against Density, so the kept notes spread far more evenly through time than random chance ever would - a deterministic, clump-free thinning.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:GolombGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{GolombGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Golomb gate: passes note-ons on Golomb-sequence counts (1,2,3,4,5,...; the non-decreasing self-describing sequence where term n says how often n appears), a gently-climbing staircase set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HammingNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HammingNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Hamming-number gate: passes note-ons on regular (5-smooth) counts whose only prime factors are 2, 3 and 5 (1,2,3,4,5,6,8,9,10,12,15,16,...), the Hamming/Humble numbers of computing lore; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HappyGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HappyGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Happy gate: passes notes on happy-number counts - those whose repeated sum-of-squared-digits reaches 1 - and drops the sad ones that fall into the 4-16-37 cycle; Invert flips which set plays.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HeptanacciGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HeptanacciGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Heptanacci gate: passes note-ons on heptanacci counts where each term is the sum of the previous seven (1,2,4,8,16,32,64,127,...), approaching a doubling sequence; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HexagonalPyramidalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HexagonalPyramidalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Hexagonal-pyramidal gate: passes note-ons on hexagonal-pyramidal counts (1,7,25,63,129,...), the cannonballs in a hexagon-based pyramid; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HexanacciGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HexanacciGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Hexanacci gate: passes note-ons on hexanacci counts where each term is the sum of the previous six (1,2,4,8,16,32,63,125,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HighlyAbundantGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HighlyAbundantGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Highly-abundant gate: passes note-ons on highly-abundant counts whose divisor sum beats that of every smaller number (1,2,3,4,6,8,10,12,16,18,20,24,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HighlyCompositeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HighlyCompositeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Highly-composite gate: passes note-ons only on counts that set a new record for number of divisors (1,2,4,6,12,24,36,48,60,...), the maximally-factorable integers; a rare, widening-gap accent pulse. Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HoaxNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HoaxNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Hoax-number gate: passes note-ons on hoax counts whose digit sum equals the summed digit sums of their DISTINCT prime factors (22,58,84,...), the squarefree-distinct cousin of Smith numbers; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:HofstadterQGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{HofstadterQGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Hofstadter-Q gate: passes note-ons on values that occur in the chaotic Hofstadter Q meta-sequence (Q(n)=Q(n-Q(n-1))+Q(n-Q(n-2))), an erratic self-referential set from Godel, Escher, Bach; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:IntervalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{IntervalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Interval gate: passes a note only if its leap from the previous kept note lies between Min and Max semitones, filtering a line down to small steps or to wide jumps - a melodic-contour sieve.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:JacobsthalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{JacobsthalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Jacobsthal gate: passes note-ons on Jacobsthal-number counts (1,3,5,11,21,43,...; J(n)=J(n-1)+2J(n-2)), a Fibonacci-like sequence whose ratio tends to 2; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KaprekarGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KaprekarGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Kaprekar gate: passes note-ons on Kaprekar counts whose square can be split into two parts that add back to the number (1,9,45,55,99,297,703,999,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KeithNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KeithNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Keith-number gate: passes note-ons on Keith (repfigit) counts - numbers that reappear in the Fibonacci-like sequence seeded by their own digits (14,19,28,47,...) - an extremely rare self-referential set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LazyCatererGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LazyCatererGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Lazy-caterer gate: passes note-ons on central-polygonal (lazy-caterer) counts (1,2,4,7,11,16,22,...), the maximum pieces a pancake splits into with n straight cuts; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LeonardoGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LeonardoGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Leonardo gate: passes note-ons on Leonardo-number counts (L(n)=L(n-1)+L(n-2)+1: 1,3,5,9,15,25,41,...), the sizes behind Dijkstra's smoothsort heaps; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LeylandGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LeylandGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Leyland gate: passes note-ons on Leyland-number counts (x^y+y^x for x,y>=2: 8,17,32,54,57,100,145,...), a sparse two-exponent set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LiouvilleGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LiouvilleGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Liouville gate: passes note-ons when the Liouville function is +1 (the count has an even number of prime factors with multiplicity), an almost-balanced parity stream from analytic number theory; Invert keeps the odd-factor counts.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LucasGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LucasGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Lucas gate: passes note-ons only on Lucas-number counts (2,1,3,4,7,11,18,...), the Fibonacci companion sequence, thinning the stream onto golden-ratio-spaced ordinals; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LucasPrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LucasPrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Lucas-prime gate: passes note-ons on Lucas counts that are also prime (2,3,7,11,29,47,199,521,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:LuckyNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{LuckyNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Lucky-number gate: passes note-ons on Ulam's lucky counts, the survivors of a Josephus-style sieve (1,3,7,9,13,15,21,25,...) that share many properties with the primes; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MagicConstantGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MagicConstantGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Magic-constant gate: passes note-ons on magic-square constant counts n(n^2+1)/2 - the common row/column/diagonal sum of an n x n magic square (1,5,15,34,65,111,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MersenneGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MersenneGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Mersenne gate: passes note-ons on Mersenne-number counts (one less than a power of two: 1,3,7,15,31,63,127), the all-ones binary numbers; their exponential spacing thins the stream fast. Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MersennePrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MersennePrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Mersenne-prime gate: passes note-ons on Mersenne-prime counts (2^p-1 that are prime: 3,7,31,127,...), an extremely sparse, exponentially-spaced set tied to the perfect numbers; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MertensGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MertensGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Mertens gate: passes note-ons while the Mertens function (the running sum of the Moebius function up to the count) is positive, a slow random-walk-like sign whose growth is tied to the Riemann hypothesis; Invert keeps the negative stretches.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MoebiusGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MoebiusGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Moebius gate: passes a note only when its count is squarefree (the Moebius function is non-zero), dropping any note whose ordinal is divisible by a perfect square - a sieve-flavoured aperiodic gate; Invert keeps the square-divisible ones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MoranGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MoranGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Moran gate: passes note-ons on Moran counts - Harshad numbers whose quotient (number over its digit sum) is prime (18,21,27,42,45,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MotzkinGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MotzkinGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Motzkin gate: passes note-ons on Motzkin-number counts (1,2,4,9,21,51,127,...), the combinatorial sequence counting non-crossing chords and lattice paths; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MultiplicativePersistenceGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MultiplicativePersistenceGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Multiplicative-persistence gate: passes note-ons whose multiplicative persistence - the number of times you replace the count by the product of its digits before hitting a single digit - equals the chosen target; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NarayanaGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NarayanaGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Narayana gate: passes note-ons on Narayana's-cows counts (a(n)=a(n-1)+a(n-3): 1,2,3,4,6,9,13,19,28,...), a slow recurrence whose ratio tends to the supergolden ratio; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NarcissisticGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NarcissisticGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Narcissistic gate: passes note-ons on Armstrong (narcissistic) counts that equal the sum of their own digits each raised to the number-of-digits power (1,153,370,371,407,...), a rare self-referential set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NivenGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NivenGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Niven (Harshad) gate: passes note-ons on counts divisible by the sum of their own digits (1..10,12,18,20,21,24,...), a moderately-dense digit-driven set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NontotientGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NontotientGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Nontotient gate: passes note-ons on nontotient counts that are never the output of Euler's totient for any number (14,26,34,38,50,...; all even), an unreachable set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NudeNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NudeNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Nude-number gate: passes note-ons on nude counts that are divisible by each of their own nonzero digits (1..9,11,12,15,22,24,...), a polydivisible-by-digit set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OctahedralGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OctahedralGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Octahedral gate: passes note-ons on octahedral counts (k(2k^2+1)/3: 1,6,19,44,85,...), the 3-D figurate numbers counting points in an octahedron; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OddDigitGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OddDigitGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Odd-digit gate: passes note-ons whose count is written with only odd decimal digits (1,3,5,7,9,11,13,15,...), the digit-pattern complement of the even-digit gate; Invert passes counts containing an even digit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OdiousGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OdiousGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Odious gate: passes note-ons on odious counts with an odd number of 1-bits in binary (1,2,4,7,8,11,13,14,...), the Thue-Morse complement of the evil numbers; Invert keeps the evil counts.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:OreNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{OreNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Ore-number gate: passes note-ons on Ore (harmonic-divisor) counts whose divisors have an integer harmonic mean (1,6,28,140,496,...), a divisor-rich set overlapping the perfect numbers; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PadovanGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PadovanGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Padovan gate: passes note-ons on Padovan-number counts (1,2,3,4,5,7,9,12,16,...; P(n)=P(n-2)+P(n-3)), the plastic-number sequence; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PalindromeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PalindromeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Palindrome gate: passes note-ons only on counts that read the same forwards and backwards (1,2,...,9,11,22,...,101,...), an irregular, self-mirroring thinning; Invert keeps the non-palindromes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PalindromicPrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PalindromicPrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Palindromic-prime gate: passes note-ons on counts that are both prime and a decimal palindrome (2,3,5,7,11,101,131,151,...), the intersection of two famous sparse sets; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PalindromicSquareGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PalindromicSquareGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Palindromic-square gate: passes note-ons whose square reads the same forwards and backwards (1,2,3,11,22,26,101,111,...), the roots of palindromic squares; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PandigitalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PandigitalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Pandigital gate: passes note-ons on 1-to-k pandigital counts whose digits are exactly a permutation of 1..(digit count), using each once with no zeros (1,12,21,123,132,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PartitionGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PartitionGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Partition gate: passes note-ons on integer-partition counts p(k) (1,2,3,5,7,11,15,22,30,...), the number of ways to write k as a sum, which thins out as it climbs; Invert keeps the others.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PartitionParityGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PartitionParityGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Partition-parity gate: passes note-ons when the integer-partition number p(count) is odd, a famously-irregular parity pattern (the subject of Ramanujan's congruences); Invert keeps the even-p counts.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PellGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PellGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Pell gate: passes note-ons on Pell-number counts (1,2,5,12,29,70,...; P(n)=2P(n-1)+P(n-2)), the silver-ratio sequence behind the best rational approximations to root-2; Invert keeps the others.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PellLucasGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PellLucasGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Pell-Lucas gate: passes note-ons on companion-Pell counts (Q(n)=2Q(n-1)+Q(n-2): 2,6,14,34,82,198,...), the Lucas-style partner of the Pell numbers; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PentagonalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PentagonalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Pentagonal gate: passes note-ons on pentagonal-number counts (1,5,12,22,35,...; k(3k-1)/2), the figurate numbers from Euler's pentagonal-number theorem; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PentanacciGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PentanacciGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Pentanacci gate: passes note-ons on pentanacci counts where each term is the sum of the previous five (1,2,4,8,16,31,61,120,...), a fast-growing Fibonacci generalization; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PerfectNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PerfectNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Perfect-number gate: passes note-ons on perfect counts equal to the sum of their proper divisors (6,28,496,8128), an exceedingly rare and ancient set; mostly silent, firing only on those landmarks. Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PerfectPowerGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PerfectPowerGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Perfect-power gate: passes note-ons on counts that are an exact power a^b with b>=2 (1,4,8,9,16,25,27,32,...), merging the squares, cubes and higher powers into one set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PerrinGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PerrinGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Perrin gate: passes note-ons on Perrin-sequence counts (3,2,5,5,7,10,12,...; P(n)=P(n-2)+P(n-3)), famous because n almost always divides P(n) exactly when n is prime; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PoliteGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PoliteGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Polite gate: passes note-ons on polite counts that can be written as a sum of two or more consecutive integers (everything except the powers of two); Invert keeps just the powers of two.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PolydivisibleGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PolydivisibleGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Polydivisible gate: passes note-ons on polydivisible counts where the first k digits form a number divisible by k for every prefix (12,15,24,...,120,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PolygonalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PolygonalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Polygonal gate: passes note-ons on s-gonal figurate-number counts for any chosen number of Sides - triangular at 3, square at 4, pentagonal at 5, and so on - one knob covering the whole figurate family; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PowerfulNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PowerfulNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Powerful-number gate: passes note-ons on powerful counts where every prime factor occurs at least squared (1,4,8,9,16,25,27,32,36,...), the dense-factor opposite of squarefree; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PowerOfTwoGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PowerOfTwoGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Power-of-two gate: passes note-ons only on counts that are exact powers of two (1,2,4,8,16,32,...), an octave-spaced exponentially-thinning pattern; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PracticalNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PracticalNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Practical-number gate: passes note-ons on practical counts, where every smaller integer is a sum of distinct divisors of the count (1,2,4,6,8,12,...; all powers of two and more), a divisor-rich set close to the abundant numbers; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PrimeDigitGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PrimeDigitGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Prime-digit gate: passes note-ons whose count is written with only the prime digits 2, 3, 5 and 7 (2,3,5,7,22,23,25,...), a digit-pattern sieve; Invert passes counts containing a non-prime digit.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Number-theory gate: counts incoming notes and lets one through only when its ordinal is a prime number (2,3,5,7,11,...), so the texture thins out unpredictably as primes spread; Invert keeps the composites instead.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PrimeIntervalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PrimeIntervalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Prime-interval gate: passes a note only when its leap from the previous one spans a prime number of semitones (2,3,5,7,11,...), favouring the seconds, thirds, fourths and fifths while blocking octaves and tritones - a number-theoretic melodic filter.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PrimePowerGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PrimePowerGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Prime-power gate: passes note-ons only when the count is a power of a single prime (2,3,4,5,7,8,9,...), a sparse self-similar pattern from analytic number theory; Invert keeps everything else.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PrimeTripletGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PrimeTripletGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Prime-triplet gate: passes note-ons on counts belonging to a prime triplet - a prime with two more primes within a span of six (5,7,11,13,17,19,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PrimorialGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PrimorialGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Primorial gate: passes note-ons on primorial counts - products of the first k primes (2,6,30,210,2310,...) - an extremely sparse, fast-growing set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ProbabilityByPitch}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ProbabilityByPitch}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Probability by pitch: the chance a note passes rises or falls with its keyboard position, so you can thin out the bass while keeping the top (or the reverse) - register-weighted probabilistic gating.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ProbabilityRamp}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ProbabilityRamp}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Probability ramp: the pass chance sweeps from Start to End across a repeating cycle of notes, so phrases can fade in (sparse to dense) or thin out (dense to sparse) on a loop - an evolving probabilistic gate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:PronicGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{PronicGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Pronic gate: passes note-ons on pronic (oblong) counts n(n+1): 2,6,12,20,30,..., the products of consecutive integers; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ProthGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ProthGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Proth gate: passes note-ons on Proth-number counts (k*2^n+1 with k odd and k below 2^n: 3,5,9,13,17,25,...), the numbers with a fast dedicated primality test; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RandomGateWalk}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RandomGateWalk}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Random-gate walk: the pass probability itself drifts on a bounded random walk, so the music breathes between dense and sparse passages instead of thinning uniformly like a fixed-chance gate.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RecamanGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RecamanGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Recaman gate: passes note-ons on counts that appear in Recaman's sequence (jump back by n if new and positive, else forward), the famously erratic jumping sequence; its darting coverage thins the stream unpredictably. Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RefactorableGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RefactorableGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Refactorable gate: passes note-ons on refactorable (tau) counts whose own number of divisors divides the number (1,2,8,9,12,18,24,36,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RepdigitGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RepdigitGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Repdigit gate: passes note-ons on repdigit counts whose decimal digits are all identical (1..9,11,22,...,99,111,...), a sparse uniform-digit pattern distinct from the all-ones repunits; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RepunitGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RepunitGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Repunit gate: passes note-ons on repunit counts whose decimal digits are all ones (1,11,111,1111,...), an extremely sparse, widely-spaced pattern; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RhombicDodecahedralGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RhombicDodecahedralGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Rhombic-dodecahedral gate: passes note-ons on rhombic-dodecahedral counts (1,15,65,175,369,...), the 3-D figurate numbers of that crystal shape; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:RoughNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{RoughNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Rough-number gate: passes note-ons whose count's smallest prime factor is at least Bound (a B-rough number with no small factors), the complement of smoothness; raising Bound makes the gate fire only on prime-like counts. Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SafePrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SafePrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Safe-prime gate: passes note-ons on safe-prime counts - primes p for which (p-1)/2 is also prime (5,7,11,23,47,59,...), the partners of the Sophie-Germain primes used in cryptography; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SchroederGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SchroederGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Schroeder gate: passes note-ons on large-Schroeder counts (1,2,6,22,90,394,1806,...), the lattice-path numbers counting subdivisions and super-Catalan structures; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SelfNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SelfNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Self-number gate: passes note-ons on self (Colombian) counts that cannot be written as some smaller number plus that number's digit-sum (1,3,5,7,9,20,31,...), a self-referential sieve; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SemiperfectGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SemiperfectGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Semiperfect gate: passes note-ons on semiperfect (pseudoperfect) counts where some subset of the proper divisors sums to the number (6,12,18,20,24,28,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SemiprimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SemiprimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Semiprime gate: passes note-ons on semiprime counts - numbers that are the product of exactly two primes (4,6,9,10,14,15,21,...) - a moderately-sparse, multiplicatively-defined pattern; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SexyPrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SexyPrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Sexy-prime gate: passes note-ons on counts in a sexy-prime pair (two primes six apart: 5,7,11,13,17,23,...), so named from the Latin sex for six; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SmithNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SmithNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Smith-number gate: passes note-ons on Smith counts whose digit sum equals the digit sum of their prime factorization (4,22,27,58,85,...), a quirky composite set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SmoothNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SmoothNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Smooth-number gate: passes note-ons whose count's largest prime factor stays at or below Bound (a B-smooth number, the easy-to-factor integers behind sieve algorithms); raising Bound opens the gate on more counts. Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SophieGermainGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SophieGermainGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Sophie-Germain gate: passes note-ons on Sophie-Germain-prime counts - primes p where 2p+1 is also prime (2,3,5,11,23,...), the primes behind safe-prime cryptography; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SphenicGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SphenicGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Sphenic gate: passes note-ons on sphenic counts - squarefree products of exactly three distinct primes (30,42,66,70,78,...) - a multiplicatively-defined, moderately-sparse set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SquarefreeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SquarefreeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Squarefree gate: passes note-ons on squarefree counts with no repeated prime factor (1,2,3,5,6,7,10,11,13,...), about 61 percent of integers; Invert keeps the square-divisible counts.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SquareGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SquareGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Square gate: passes note-ons only on perfect-square counts (1,4,9,16,25,...), so notes get steadily sparser as the gaps between squares widen; Invert plays everything except the squares.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SquarePyramidalGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SquarePyramidalGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Square-pyramidal gate: passes note-ons on square-pyramidal counts (1,5,14,30,55,...), the number of cannonballs in a square-based pyramid; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SquareTriangularGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SquareTriangularGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Square-triangular gate: passes note-ons on the rare counts that are simultaneously a perfect square and a triangular number (1,36,1225,41616,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StarNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StarNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Star-number gate: passes note-ons on centered star (hexagram) counts (1,13,37,73,121,...), the dots in a growing six-pointed star; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StellaOctangulaGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StellaOctangulaGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Stella-octangula gate: passes note-ons on stella-octangula counts (k(2k^2-1): 1,14,51,124,245,...), the points of a growing star-tetrahedron; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:StrobogrammaticGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{StrobogrammaticGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Strobogrammatic gate: passes note-ons on counts that read the same when rotated 180 degrees (0,1,8,11,69,88,96,...), a visual digit-symmetry set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SunnyNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SunnyNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Sunny-number gate: passes note-ons on sunny counts where the number plus one is a perfect square (3,8,15,24,35,48,...), one less than each square; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SuperabundantGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SuperabundantGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Superabundant gate: passes note-ons on superabundant counts whose ratio sigma(n)/n beats every smaller number (1,2,4,6,12,24,36,48,60,120,...), the densest-divisor records; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:SylvesterGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{SylvesterGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Sylvester gate: passes note-ons on Sylvester's-sequence counts (2,3,7,43,1807,...; each one more than the product of all previous), a doubly-exponential set so sparse it fires only a handful of times; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TetrahedralGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TetrahedralGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Tetrahedral gate: passes note-ons on tetrahedral-number counts n(n+1)(n+2)/6 (1,4,10,20,35,56,...), the 3-D figurate numbers counting stacked spheres; their cubic spacing thins fast. Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TetranacciGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TetranacciGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Tetranacci gate: passes note-ons on tetranacci counts where each term is the sum of the previous four (1,2,4,8,15,29,56,108,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThabitGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThabitGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Thabit gate: passes note-ons on Thabit (321-) number counts (3*2^n-1: 2,5,11,23,47,95,191,...), the medieval numbers behind amicable-pair constructions; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ThueMorseGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ThueMorseGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Thue-Morse gate: passes notes where the cube-free Thue-Morse sequence (the bit-parity of the note count) is 0, an aperiodic-yet-structured pattern that never settles into a short loop; Invert flips which half plays.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TrailingZeroGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TrailingZeroGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Trailing-zero gate: passes note-ons whose count has exactly the chosen number of trailing binary zeros (its 2-adic valuation), selecting one rung of the ruler sequence - odd counts at 0, doubly-even at higher; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TriangularGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TriangularGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Triangular gate: passes note-ons on triangular-number counts (1,3,6,10,15,...), thinning the stream on the handshake-number sequence; Invert keeps the non-triangular notes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TribonacciGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TribonacciGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Tribonacci gate: passes note-ons on Tribonacci-sequence counts (1,2,4,7,13,24,44,...; each the sum of the previous three), spreading wider than Fibonacci; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TrimorphicGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TrimorphicGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Trimorphic gate: passes note-ons on trimorphic counts whose cube ends in the number itself (1,4,5,6,9,24,25,49,...), the cube-analogue of automorphic numbers; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TruncatablePrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TruncatablePrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Right-truncatable-prime gate: passes note-ons on primes that stay prime as each trailing digit is chopped off (2,3,5,7,23,29,31,37,53,...), a famously finite set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:TwinPrimeGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{TwinPrimeGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Twin-prime gate: passes note-ons on counts belonging to a twin-prime pair (a prime with another prime two away: 3,5,7,11,13,17,19,...), thinning onto the famously-clustered twin primes; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:UlamGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{UlamGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Ulam gate: passes note-ons on Ulam-number counts (each the smallest integer that is a sum of two earlier terms in exactly one way: 1,2,3,4,6,8,11,...), a mysteriously near-periodic set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:UndulatingGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{UndulatingGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Undulating gate: passes note-ons on undulating counts whose digits zig-zag high-low-high (121,132,231,1212,...), a wave-like digit pattern; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:UndulatingNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{UndulatingNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Undulating-number gate: passes note-ons on counts of 3+ digits that undulate in an alternating ababab pattern with two different digits (121,131,212,232,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:UntouchableGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{UntouchableGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Untouchable gate: passes note-ons on untouchable counts that can never be the sum of the proper divisors of any number (2,5,52,88,96,...), an unreachable set in the aliquot map; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VelocityFloorGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VelocityFloorGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Velocity floor gate: drops note-ons whose velocity falls below a floor, filtering out the quietest notes (off events for dropped notes are removed too to avoid stuck notes); Invert keeps only the quiet ones.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VelocityProbGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VelocityProbGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Velocity-probability gate: passes each note with a probability that rises with its velocity, so soft notes thin out and accents survive - a dynamics-driven random thinning; Bias lifts the baseline pass chance.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:VelRandomGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{VelRandomGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Velocity-random gate: passes each note with a probability that rises with its velocity, so accented hits survive while ghost notes thin out - dynamics-weighted probabilistic gating.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WedderburnEthringtonGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WedderburnEthringtonGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Wedderburn-Etherington gate: passes note-ons on the counts of unordered binary trees (1,2,3,6,11,23,46,98,...), a combinatorial sequence from chemistry and phylogenetics; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WeirdNumberGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WeirdNumberGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Weird-number gate: passes note-ons on weird counts that are abundant yet have no subset of proper divisors summing to the number (70,836,4030,...), a rare paradoxical set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WoodallGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WoodallGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Woodall gate: passes note-ons on Woodall-number counts (n*2^n-1: 1,7,23,63,159,383,...), an exponentially-sparse set partnered with the Cullen numbers; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:WythoffGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{WythoffGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Wythoff gate: passes note-ons on the lower-Wythoff counts floor(n*phi) (1,3,4,6,8,9,11,...), the golden-ratio Beatty sequence that, with its complement, partitions the integers; Invert keeps the upper-Wythoff notes.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZuckermanGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZuckermanGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Zuckerman gate: passes note-ons on Zuckerman counts divisible by the product of their own (nonzero) decimal digits (1..9,11,12,15,24,36,...); Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ZumkellerGate}\blockicon{dice}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ZumkellerGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[PRB]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonCyan}{\footnotesize[CV]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Zumkeller gate: passes note-ons on Zumkeller counts whose divisors can be split into two sets of equal sum (6,12,20,24,28,30,...), a balanced-divisor set; Invert keeps the rest.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

## Routing (29)

\phantomsection\label{blk:Channel}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Channel}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Reassigns notes to a fixed MIDI channel.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChannelByPitch}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChannelByPitch}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Channel-by-pitch: routes notes to different MIDI channels by register, splitting the keyboard into Zones equal bands so a single part can drive several multitimbral instruments stacked across the range.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ChannelByZone}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ChannelByZone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Channel by zone: routes notes below the Split point to channel ChanLo and notes at or above it to ChanHi, splitting one keyboard across two MIDI channels (multitimbral split).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:ClampToRange}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{ClampToRange}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Clamp to range: pulls every out-of-range note onto the nearest boundary of [Low,High], folding a wide part flat against the edges of a fixed register.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FixedNote}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FixedNote}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Forces every note to a single fixed pitch (drum / trigger).

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:FoldToRange}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{FoldToRange}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Fold to range: shifts every note up or down by whole octaves until it sits inside the [Low,High] window, packing a wide-range part into a fixed register while preserving each note's pitch class.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KeyboardSplit3}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KeyboardSplit3}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Three-zone split: divides the keyboard at two split points into low, mid and high zones, each transposed by its own amount - a quick way to layer a bassline, a pad and a lead from one part across three regions.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KeySwitch}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KeySwitch}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Splits the keyboard at a note; one zone passes, the other is filtered.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:KeyZoneGate}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{KeyZoneGate}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Key-zone gate: passes only notes whose pitch sits inside [Low,High] and drops the rest (their note-offs are removed too to avoid stuck notes); Invert keeps only the notes outside the zone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MaxPolyphony}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MaxPolyphony}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Max polyphony: caps how many notes may sound at once, dropping any note-on past the limit until a voice frees up - a voice-stealing-free polyphony clamp for thinning dense chords.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:Merge}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{Merge}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Passes both note-stream inputs through, merged into one stream.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MidiLatch}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MidiLatch}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Holds notes after release until the next note arrives.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MirrorZone}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MirrorZone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Mirror zone: reflects the notes inside [Low,High] around the zone centre (a melodic inversion confined to one key zone), leaving notes outside the zone alone.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:MuteZone}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{MuteZone}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Mute zone: drops every note whose pitch sits inside [Low,High] while letting the rest of the keyboard through, punching a silent notch into the range.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoteFilter}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoteFilter}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Passes only notes inside a key and velocity window.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoteLimit}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoteLimit}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Polyphony cap: never lets more than Max notes sound at once, stealing the oldest (FIFO note-off) when a new note would exceed the limit - tames dense chords and runaway generators.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}

\phantomsection\label{blk:NoteRangeTranspose}\blockicon{routing}\textcolor{neonCyan}{\textbf{NoteRangeTranspose}}\thinspace\textcolor{accentPink}{\footnotesize[RTE]}\thinspace\textcolor{neonYellow}{\footnotesize[MIDI]}\quad\textcolor{textDim}{in: Audio}\quad\textcolor{neonYellow}{out: MIDI}

Note-range transpose: transposes by Semitones only the notes whose pitch sits inside [Low,High], leaving everything outside the zone at its original pitch.

\smallskip\noindent\textcolor{neonCyan}{\rule{0.32\linewidth}{0.6pt}}
